<a id="paper-2605.01645"></a>
### [Long-range correlation and the spin conductivity in the XXZ chain from ballistic macroscopic fluctuation theory](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01645v1)

**Authors:** Shinya Ae  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01645v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `non-equilibrium dynamics` **4/5** · 🔥 `non-equilibrium universality` **4/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01645_figures/2605.01645_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: σ0 calculated for various numbers of the anisotropy parameter; (a) p0 = 2, 3, · · · , 10, 20, · · · , 100, (b) p0 = 2 + 1/ν with ν = 2, 3, · · · , 10, (c) p0 = 3 + 1/ν with ν = 2, 3, · · · , 10 and (d) p0 is given by the continued fraction 1 p0 = 1|</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper uses Ballistic Macroscopic Fluctuation Theory to show that superdiffusive spin transport in the XXZ chain is driven by long-range spin correlations. It demonstrates that spin conductivity scales with 1/T at high temperatures and diverges at the isotropic point. This provides a theoretical link between microscopic correlations and macroscopic transport properties.

**Why it may be interesting.** This paper is highly relevant to many-body dynamics as it provides a fundamental link between long-range correlations and anomalous (superdiffusive) transport in integrable systems, which is a central topic in non-equilibrium statistical mechanics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether superdiffusive spin transport in the XXZ chain is driven by 1/N-scaled long-range spin correlations and analyzing spin conductivity in the critical regime.

**Main result.** The study shows that spin conductivity is proportional to 1/T in the high-temperature limit and that its proportionality constant diverges as the system approaches the isotropic point, providing evidence for superdiffusive transport.

**Method.** The author employs Ballistic Macroscopic Fluctuation Theory (BMFT) combined with Generalized Hydrodynamics (GHD) and the Thermodynamic Bethe Ansatz (TBA) to analyze fluctuations and correlations.

**Model / system.** The spin-1/2 XXZ integrable spin chain in the critical regime (0 <= Delta < 1), characterized by an anisotropy parameter Delta = cos(theta).

**Key observables.** Spin conductivity, spin correlation functions, spin current density, magnetization density, and Drude weight.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature (T), inverse temperature (beta), anisotropy parameter (Delta), and the thermodynamic limit (N -> infinity).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes the system is in the critical regime, relies on the integrability-driven existence of infinitely many conserved charges, and assumes local thermal equilibrium within fluid cells.

**Figures summary.** The figures illustrate the behavior of the conductivity constant sigma_0 as a function of the anisotropy parameter Delta, showing its decay at the free fermion point and its divergence as it approaches the isotropic point.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the model and the problem of long-range correlations, moves through the application of BMFT and GHD to calculate the evolution of correlations, derives the relationship between conductivity and correlation functions, and concludes with the high-temperature limit and scaling at the isotropic point.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Based on the ballistic macroscopic fluctuation theory, the integration of the spin correlation function (spin conductivity) is analyzed for the spin-1/2 XXZ chain in the critical regime. In the time when the magnetization of an infinite spin chain fluctuates from an initial state with a wavelength as long as the infinite length $N$, the equal-time two-point spin correlation function is scaled up to $O(1/N)$. In the state where the ballistic spin transport decays at high temperature $T$, the diffusive transport remains on a large scale. We show that the spin conductivity is proportional to $1/T$ in the limit $T\to\infty$ and its high temperature proportionality constant diverges in the case where one-quasiparticle magnetization is infinitely large. This analysis informs that the superdiffusive spin transport is driven by the $1/N$-scaled long-range spin correlation and sheds a light on the dynamic scaling in spin transport at the isotropic point.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.01239"></a>
### [Memory-assisted multimode microwave-to-optical transduction](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01239v1)

**Authors:** Ujjwal Gautam, Nasser Gohari Kamel, Sourabh Kumar, Daniel Oblak  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01239v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · 🔥 `quantum optics experiment` **4/5**

**Summary.** The researchers have demonstrated the first microwave-to-optical transducer that uses a quantum memory to enable on-demand signal retrieval. By using a Yb-doped crystal, they successfully reduced noise from pump pulses and showed that multiple modes can be transduced simultaneously. This advancement is crucial for building scalable quantum networks connecting superconducting processors.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and quantum networks as it provides a solution for synchronizing qubits in quantum repeater protocols and increases entanglement generation rates through multimode capacity.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of eliminating noise caused by intense pump pulses during microwave-to-optical transduction, which is essential for connecting superconducting quantum devices.

**Main result.** Demonstration of the first on-demand microwave-to-optical transducer assisted by a quantum memory, achieving low noise levels (0.3-0.4 photons) and multimode transduction capacity.

**Method.** Integration of a quantum memory protocol with transduction within a three-level atomic system using a rare-earth-ion-doped crystal.

**Model / system.** A low-doping concentration 171Yb3+:Y2SiO5 crystal operated at a temperature of 30 mK, utilizing zero-first-order Zeeman transitions and GHz-range hyperfine splitting.

**Key observables.** Noise photon counts in the detection window, storage duration, interference patterns from varying input microwave pulse phase/frequency, and multimode transduction capacity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Storage durations of 460 and 620 microseconds; noise levels of 0.4 and 0.3 photons; 30 mK operating temperature.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the challenge of noise in transduction, describes the implementation of a memory-assisted protocol in Yb-doped crystals, demonstrates on-demand retrieval and coherence via interference, and concludes with the demonstration of multimode capacity.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Microwave-to-optical quantum transducers will enable coherent interconnection between distant superconducting quantum devices. Ongoing explorations with several platforms have shown promising results at single-photon levels. However, in all these demonstrations, elimination of noise due to the concurrence of the weak transduced signal with intense pump pulses remains a challenge, requiring high suppression filtering setups. A memory-assisted transducer, on the other hand, offers a versatile approach that not only mitigates the noise but also enables the on-demand retrieval of the transduced signal. Here, we integrate a quantum memory protocol with transduction in a three-level atomic system to demonstrate on-demand retrieval of transduced signals. Due to the zero-first-order Zeeman transitions at zero magnetic fields, providing long optical and spin coherence times, and GHz range hyperfine splitting, we use a low-doping concentration $^{171}{\rm Yb}^{3+}$:${\rm Y}_2{\rm SiO}_5$ crystal at 30\,mK temperature. We achieve on-demand transduction assisted by memory with $0.4\ (\text{and }0.3)$ noise photons in the detection window at a storage duration of $460\ (\text{and }620) \, μ\textrm{s}$. To demonstrate the coherent nature of the protocol, we show interference patterns resulting from transduced signals due to varying phase or frequency of the input microwave pulses. Further, multimode transduction capacity is demonstrated, utilizing the spin and optical inhomogeneous broadening. The on-demand capability of the protocol allows synchronizing qubits in a quantum repeater protocol, while multimode capacity increases the entanglement generation rate. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of an on-demand microwave-to-optical transducer assisted by memory.

</details>

The channel is connected to source and drain contacts and at each terminal the influx is described by the electron in-scattering function Σin, which injects contact electrons into hole states G&gt; in the channel, while the outflow is governed by the out-scattering function Σout, which...</figcaption>

The energy levels satisfy energy conservation, E4 −E3 = E2 −E1.</figcaption>
</figure>

The comparison shows how the reduced tight- binding model captures the low-energy band features needed for transport while remaining compact enough for NEGF calculations. (b) In the orbital character of the silicon tight- binding band structure, red and blue curves show dominant p- and s-orbital character, where conduction/valence band states correspond to sp3-bonding/antibonding states.</figcaption>
</figure>

(b) Irreducible 1st Brillouin zone (IBZ), capturing the ⃗k = (ky, kx) momentum degrees of freedom of electrons propagating orthogonal to the device axis. (c) Visualization of the device unit cell of a 25-nm depletion region in a Si APD device, constructed with 45 silicon cells and p+- and n+-doped semi-infinite leads. Periodicity orthogonal to the device axis was acccounted for by sampling the Brillouin zone in (b) with a uniform mesh.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a quantum transport framework using NEGF to model impact ionization in silicon avalanche photodiodes. By treating carrier multiplication as a multi-particle self-energy, it provides an atomistic way to predict detector performance for quantum networks. This approach overcomes the limitations of traditional semiclassical models in nanoscale devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous many-body approach (NEGF/Keldysh) to modeling non-equilibrium, inelastic scattering processes that are critical for the hardware layer of quantum communication.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The lack of a microscopic, atomistic description of impact ionization in nanoscale semiconductor avalanche photodiodes, which is necessary to predict detector performance for quantum networking.

**Main result.** The development of an NEGF-based framework that captures carrier multiplication as a multi-particle self-energy, establishing a transport baseline for predicting device metrics like dark counts and timing jitter.

**Method.** An atomistic quantum transport simulation using the Non-Equilibrium Green's Function (NEGF) formalism and the Self-Consistent Born Approximation (SCBA) implemented in PyTorch.

**Model / system.** A silicon p-i-n-like junction avalanche photodiode modeled with an equivariant tight-binding Hamiltonian (s+p basis) and semi-infinite p-type and n-type leads.

**Key observables.** Carrier multiplication, spectral function, local density of states (LDOS), transmission spectrum, and nonequilibrium charge distributions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** 25-nm depletion region, high electric fields, reverse bias voltage (e.g., -5V), and silicon doping concentrations.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of a compressed s+p basis limits high-energy conduction band accuracy; the electrostatic profile was imposed rather than solved self-consistently with Poisson; and the model lacks phonon/defect scattering.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a quantum network block diagram; Figure 2 illustrates the quantum transport model and self-energy components; Figure 4 compares tight-binding to DFT bands; Figure 5 shows the device geometry; Figure 8 displays the spatial tilt of the LDOS and the occupied DOS under bias.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the importance of detectors in quantum networks, identifies limitations in semiclassical modeling, presents the NEGF/SCBA mathematical framework, validates the tight-binding model against DFT, demonstrates the emergence of carrier multiplication, and discusses implications for device performance.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present an atomistic quantum transport simulation framework based on the Non-Equilibrium Green's Function (NEGF) formalism to model impact ionization in semiconductor avalanche devices, with direct relevance to near-term quantum networking applications. Conventional descriptions of avalanche breakdown rely predominantly on semiclassical simulation methods, such as local ionization coefficients, semiclassical carrier trajectories, or Monte Carlo sampling, all of which implicitly assume weak correlations and mean-field electronic interactions. These assumptions break down in nanoscale, high-field junctions where carrier multiplication emerges from strongly non-equilibrium, energy-resolved scattering processes.   Our approach formulates impact ionization as a multi-particle self-energy within NEGF, enabling a non-perturbative, energy- and atomic orbital-resolved description of carrier multiplication directly from the device spectral function. This formulation captures strongly inelastic scattering processes beyond semiclassical approximations and is implemented in a matrix-based real-space representation suitable for nanoscale device modeling. Using a model semiconductor structure under high electric fields, we demonstrate the emergence of carrier multiplication and analyze its dependence on energy-resolved transport and nonequilibrium charge distributions. The framework provides insight into microscopic mechanisms governing avalanche processes and their impact on device performance. Our results establish a transport baseline for self-consistent calculations of the impact-ionization self-energy and carrier multiplication. By resolving the available and occupied states that underlie avalanche onset, this framework provides a route toward predictive modeling of silicon single-photon avalanche detectors and avalanche photodiodes used in quantum-network receivers.

</details>

**Authors:** Chaorong Guo, Hongzheng Wu, Zenong Zhou, Ai-Xi Chen, Xiaobing Luo

(4a) and (4b). (b) Average transfer fidelity ¯F versus on-site disorder W/ ¯J for N = 3, 5, 10. (c) ¯F versus hopping disorder δJ/ ¯J. Here, W and δJ denote the maximum on-site and hopping disorder strengths. **Summary.** The paper presents a universal theory for achieving perfect, unidirectional quantum state transfer around any N-site ring by designing a Hamiltonian with an equidistant energy spectrum. It demonstrates that this can be physically realized using Floquet engineering in driven chains or through the intrinsic statistics of anyons. This provides a robust, analytical framework for creating quantum routers in diverse experimental platforms.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a deterministic 'quantum router' design that is highly relevant for quantum information processing, offering a scalable framework for unidirectional state transfer in various platforms like superconducting circuits and photonic synthetic dimensions.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Establishing a universal analytical design for perfect chiral circulation—the sequential transfer of a quantum state around an N-site ring with unit fidelity—and overcoming the lack of a general theory for rings larger than three sites.

**Main result.** The authors prove that an equidistant energy spectrum is the necessary and sufficient condition for perfect chiral circulation in translationally invariant rings and provide an exact closed-form Hamiltonian construction for arbitrary N.

**Method.** The study uses spectral analysis of the evolution operator, Magnus expansion for Floquet engineering, and second-order perturbation theory for anyonic doublon dynamics to derive and validate the required Hamiltonian parameters.

**Model / system.** The model consists of translationally invariant N-site rings with periodic boundary conditions, specifically realized through Floquet-engineered driven open chains and anyon-Hubbard models involving correlated doublon dynamics.

**Key observables.** Site populations (Pn(t)), average transfer fidelity (F_bar), energy spectrum, and group velocity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of sites (N), total synthetic flux (Phi), hopping amplitudes (J), driving frequency (omega), drive amplitude (A), drive phase (phi), and anyonic statistical angle (theta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Floquet analysis assumes the high-frequency limit (J << omega), and the anyonic model assumes the strong-interaction regime (U >> J).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of the N=3 chiral ring and state transfer direction; Figure 5 compares numerical results of the driven Hamiltonian against analytical effective models.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by establishing the universal spectral criterion for chiral circulation, then provides the analytical Hamiltonian construction, followed by specific physical realizations via Floquet engineering and anyonic physics, and concludes with scalability and robustness tests.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Perfect chiral circulation-the sequential transfer of a quantum state around a closed loop with unit fidelity-has been achieved in specific few-site systems, yet a general theory remains absent. We prove that for translationally invariant $N$-site rings, an equidistant energy spectrum is the necessary and sufficient condition for such circulation, and we derive an exact closed-form Hamiltonian valid for arbitrary $N$. In the minimal three-site ring, we demonstrate two physically distinct realizations: Floquet engineering in a driven open chain, and correlated doublon dynamics in an anyon-Hubbard model where fractional statistics intrinsically provide the requisite gauge flux. Grounded in this universal spectral criterion and an explicit Hamiltonian construction, we provide an analytical, transparent design framework for perfect chiral circulation applicable to diverse platforms, such as superconducting circuits, classical electrical circuits, and photonic synthetic dimensions.

</details>

**Authors:** Seyed Mohsen Moosavi Khansari  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02155v1>

**Summary.** This paper explores how using GHZ-entangled coins in a quantum walk can enhance the correlation between the coin and position degrees of freedom. While entanglement initially causes a delay due to destructive interference, it ultimately leads to significantly higher mutual information and faster spatial spreading. These findings suggest that pre-existing entanglement can be a powerful tool for engineering information dynamics in quantum protocols.

**Why it may be interesting.** It demonstrates how multipartite entanglement can be used as a resource to control information flow and accelerate correlations, which is highly relevant for designing efficient quantum state transfer and quantum sensing protocols.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how initial tripartite entanglement in a three-coin system affects the dynamics of mutual information and spatial spreading in a discrete-time quantum walk.

**Main result.** GHZ-type entangled states exhibit a 'delay-and-rise' non-monotonic dynamic that eventually leads to up to 18% higher mutual information between coin and position by the tenth step compared to separable states.

**Method.** Analytical calculations for early-time interference mechanisms and numerical simulations of the quantum walk evolution on a 1D lattice.

**Model / system.** A discrete-time quantum walk on a 1D lattice with an 8-dimensional coin Hilbert space (three qubits) and a 'unanimous-coin' displacement rule where movement only occurs if all three coins are in the same state.

**Key observables.** Quantum mutual information between coin and position, von Neumann entropy of reduced subsystems, and position entropy (spatial spreading).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Initial entanglement type (separable, bipartite, or GHZ), number of walk steps, and the dimensionality of the coin space.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The global state remains pure (no decoherence or environmental coupling) and the position space is truncated to a finite lattice.

**Figures summary.** The notes refer to a Table 1 showing the evolution of mutual information for different initial states and a Figure 1 (description incomplete in notes) likely illustrating the dynamics of the walk.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the triple-coin model and displacement rule, compares different initial entanglement configurations, provides analytical derivations for short-time interference, presents numerical results for mutual information and spatial spreading, and discusses implications for quantum protocols.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This work investigates a discrete-time quantum walk on a one-dimensional lattice driven by three entangled coins, each initialized via a Hadamard operator. The walker moves only when all three coins yield identical outcomes (HHH or TTT), coupling an 8-dimensional coin Hilbert space to the position degree of freedom. We analyze fully separable, fully entangled, and intermediate initial coin states, computing the von Neumann entropy of reduced subsystems to derive the mutual information between coin and position over successive steps. Our results demonstrate that initial tripartite entanglement significantly accelerates the growth of mutual information and enhances coin-position correlations compared to separable initial conditions. Notably, GHZ-type entangled states exhibit non-monotonic short-time dynamics due to interference, yet ultimately yield up to 18% higher mutual information by the tenth step. These findings underscore the role of pre-walk entanglement as a resource for controlling information flow and spatial spreading in quantum-walk-based protocols, with implications for quantum transport, state transfer, and correlation engineering.

</details>

The insets present the same data as in the main panels but for higher frequencies.</figcaption>

**Summary.** This paper explores how an external magnetic field can manipulate the propagation of electromagnetic waves through a quantum spin chain. By calculating the dispersion relation, the authors show that the wave velocity and attenuation can be precisely controlled, especially in the THz frequency range. The findings provide a theoretical foundation for using quantum magnetic media as tunable components for electromagnetic wave control.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is relevant to quantum optics and many-body dynamics as it demonstrates a mechanism for controlling light-matter interaction and wave propagation using tunable quantum many-body states.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how the propagation, velocity, and attenuation of electromagnetic waves can be controlled in a one-dimensional magnetic crystal using an external magnetic field.

**Main result.** The study identifies that the dispersion relation and refractive index are highly tunable via the magnetic field, revealing specific frequency regimes of anomalous dispersion and damping, particularly at characteristic frequencies related to the field strength.

**Method.** The authors use Jordan-Wigner fermionization to solve the spin Hamiltonian exactly and apply Maxwell's equations in matter to derive the electromagnetic dispersion relation.

**Model / system.** A 1D spin-1/2 isotropic XY chain in a transverse magnetic field, representing a quasi-1D magnetic crystal with nearest-neighbor exchange coupling J.

**Key observables.** Complex wavevector k(omega), dispersion relation, magnetic susceptibility tensor, refractive index, wave velocity, and attenuation coefficient.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic field strength (B/J), frequency (omega/J), temperature (T), and exchange interaction (J).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The medium is assumed to be nonconducting and nonpolarizable; the interatomic distance is much smaller than the wavelength; the model is limited to an exactly solvable free-fermion system.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of EM wave propagation and field orientations; Figure 2 displays the dynamic susceptibility; Figure 3 plots wave velocity and damping across different magnetic field strengths.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the physical model, establishes the mathematical framework for EM propagation in magnetic media, performs exact calculations using fermionization, analyzes results in strong and weak field regimes, and discusses future generalizations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We consider a simple model of one-dimensional magnetic crystal and examine the propagation of an electromagnetic wave through such a medium. Calculating the dispersion relation ${\bf k}(ω)$ allows us to illustrate how the spread of the electromagnetic wave can be controlled by an external magnetic field. Our rigorous calculations should be useful for more realistic (and less tractable mathematically) models of magnetic media.

</details>

**Authors:** Nguyen Truong Thu Ngo, Väinö Mehtola, Jérome Lenssen, Peiyong Wang, Francesco Cosco, Tien-Fu Lu, James Q. Quach  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01716v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Schematic of the CartPole environment. The task consists of balancing the inverted pendulum mounted on the movable cart by applying discrete left or right forces. The observation states set ˜s = [s1, s2, s3, s4] is described by the cart position x and velocity ˙x, as well as the pole angle φ and angular velocity ˙φ respectively.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01716_figures/2605.01716_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Classical actor network (left) and critic network (right) illustrations. Both networks share an identical internal structure consisting of an input layer for observations followed by two hidden layers of 128 and 256 neurons, respectively. The input layer received the raw observations. While the actor network contained the Softmax function to calculate the action probability distribution, the critic outputs a single state value judging how well the current environment states is. By comparing this value to actual reward, the agent determines whether the actor’s chosen action was better or worse than expected.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01716_figures/2605.01716_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Hybrid quantum neural network for the actor (top) and critic (bottom). The functions of both actor and critic remain the same as its classical counterpart. The NN has been replaced by PQCs to approximate policy function (actor) and value function (critic). Each PQC output is copied 32 times before fully connected to two outputs (actor) and one output (critic). Soft is also used to output the final probability distribution.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01716_figures/2605.01716_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4: Experimental setup for measurements on Qubit 3 of the VTT Q5 device. The qubit flux bias is provided by an SRS SIM928 source (orange), which sets the qubit frequency and remains constant throughout the experiment. Microwave drive pulses are generated by the HDAWG (green), while readout pulses are produced by the UHFQA. Qubit drive pulses are implemented via frequency up-conversion, where narrow-band intermediate-frequency in-phase (I) and quadrature (Q) signals are mixed with a high-frequency local oscillator using an IQ mixer.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01716_figures/2605.01716_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5: Average reward scores over 50 runs (5a). The single-qubit QRL model (blue) achieves the maximum reward threshold significantly faster than the Classical RL baseline (green), validating the higher sample efficiency observed in simulation. The orange curve represented the same QRL model with manual backward propagation process implemented. The dashed lines show the average episodes taken to solve the CartPole task. Average episode counts needed for the single-qubit QRL and classical RL models to reach the success criterion of 100 consecutive perfect episodes (5b). The Single-Qubit model (blue) demonstrates a 2.7x improvement in sample efficiency (161.6 ± 56.3 episodes) compared to...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates a method for real-time control of a CartPole system using a hybrid quantum-classical reinforcement learning agent on a physical superconducting QPU. By bypassing standard software layers, the researchers achieved significant increases in execution speed and identified the optimal balance between measurement precision and control frequency for stable operation.

**Why it may be interesting.** It addresses the critical intersection of quantum control and real-time feedback loops, providing insights into how hardware-induced noise and latency affect the stability of quantum-classical hybrid dynamical systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Bridging the gap between idealized quantum reinforcement learning simulations and practical, real-time deployment on physical NISQ hardware by addressing latency, noise, and the trade-off between control frequency and measurement precision.

**Main result.** A single-qubit hybrid agent demonstrates superior sample efficiency compared to classical networks and, by bypassing high-level software stacks to program electronics directly, achieves a >40x speedup in iteration rates on a superconducting QPU.

**Method.** A hybrid quantum-classical actor-critic framework using a single-qubit variational quantum circuit (VQC) trained via the parameter-shift rule and optimized with the Adam optimizer.

**Model / system.** The system uses the CartPole-v1 benchmark task implemented on the VTT Q5, a 5-qubit superconducting quantum processor, utilizing Zurich Instruments readout electronics programmed via low-level command tables.

**Key observables.** Expectation value of the Pauli-Z measurement, control-loop inference frequency, measurement shot budget, and episode return (stability).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Control frequency (20-100 Hz), shot count (128-1024), qubit coherence times (T1, T2), and gate durations (120 ns).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The cart position is discarded from the state vector to simplify single-qubit encoding, and the study assumes the use of a specific 3D feature vector.

**Figures summary.** Figures include the CartPole environment schematic, neural network architectures (classical and hybrid), learning efficiency trajectories, heatmaps of stability vs. frequency/shot count, and a comparison of hardware execution latency.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the control problem, describes the hybrid agent architecture and hardware setup, presents simulation-based learning efficiency results, analyzes the impact of frequency and shot-count trade-offs, and concludes with a low-latency hardware implementation demonstration.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The application of quantum reinforcement learning (QRL) to real-time control systems faces significant challenges regarding hardware latency, noise susceptibility, and learning convergence. This work presents an end-to-end investigation of a minimal hybrid quantum-classical agent applied to the CartPole benchmark, addressing the gap between idealized simulation and execution on a physical superconducting quantum processing unit (QPU). We demonstrate that a single-qubit agent acts as an effective learning model, solving the environment in substantially fewer episodes than a comparable classical actor-critic network even when the training of the hybrid agent is restricted to use parameter-shift for its quantum circuit component. To connect learning to deployment constraints, we map the inference-time trade-off between control-loop rate and measurement shot budget to provide guidance for an eventual real-time control demonstration. The resulting performance matrices show that both inference control frequency and shot count strongly affect balancing stability: higher inference frequencies consistently improve performance, and increasing the shot budget lowers the minimum inference frequency required to achieve near-maximal balancing. These results highlight the importance of finding an optimal medium between shot count and control frequency and developing circuits that are e.g. initial-state invariant. Lastly, we address the critical bottleneck of control latency on NISQ hardware by bypassing the standard high-level software stack and programming the Zurich Instruments readout electronics directly via command tables. These results quantify some of the current boundaries of quantum-assisted control and provide a start for achieving the tens-of-hertz throughput required for real-time closed-loop control feedback.

</details>

Both beams are circularly polarized and focused by a lens (L) into the Rb cell, where nonlinear processes generate emission at 420 nm and 5.2 𝜇m. The 420 nm light is isolated using a band-pass filter (BPF) and detected by PD2. Detection of the 5.2 𝜇m emission is achieved by optical chopping and lock-in demodulation of the PD3 signal. For CBL linewidth measurements, an external Fabry-Perot cavity is placed after the cell, and the transmitted spectrum is detected by PD2.</figcaption>

(b) CBL power versus vapor density-length product for micromachined cells with 15 mm (red) and 150 mm (dashed blue) lenses, and for a glassblown cell with a 150 mm lens (dashed orange). (c-e) Optimal detuning of the (c) 780 nm laser, (d) the 776 nm laser, and (e) their sum versus density-length product for three configurations: a micromachined cell (FL = 15 mm; blue curve), a micromachined cell (FL = 150 mm; green curve), and a glassblown cell (FL =...</figcaption>

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The study demonstrates efficient nonlinear frequency conversion in tiny, micromachined Rubidium vapor cells. By optimizing optical overlap and atomic density, the researchers achieved higher efficiency for blue light generation than traditional large-scale cells, providing a foundation for integrated quantum sensors and light sources.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and AMO physics as it demonstrates a scalable, chip-integrated platform for generating coherent quantum light sources and frequency-translated photons, essential for quantum sensing and integrated quantum networks.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Achieving efficient nonlinear frequency translation (four-wave mixing) in miniaturized, chip-scale alkali-metal vapor cells where the reduced interaction length typically lowers conversion efficiency.

**Main result.** Demonstrated efficient generation of coherent blue (420 nm) and mid-IR (5.2 um) light in micromachined Rb cells, achieving higher blue-light efficiency than conventional glassblown cells due to optimized optical overlap and higher atomic densities.

**Method.** The researchers used resonant four-wave mixing in a diamond-type four-level scheme in 85Rb, utilizing micromachined Si-Pyrex cells and varying temperature and laser detuning to optimize the nonlinear process.

**Model / system.** A chip-scale Rubidium (Rb) vapor platform consisting of micromachined cells (internal cavity ~2x2x1.4 mm^3) using a diamond four-level atomic scheme involving 780 nm and 776 nm transitions.

**Key observables.** Coherent blue light (CBL) power at 420 nm, coherent mid-IR light (CMIRL) power at 5.2 um, spectral linewidth of CBL, and temperature/power scaling of emissions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Atomic density-length product (N*L), laser detuning, cell temperature, pump laser power, and AC Stark shifts.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Use of the non-depleted pump regime analytical solution where efficiency scales as sinh^2(gamma*L).

**Figures summary.** The figures include a transmission profile of the Fabry-Perot cavity mode for linewidth measurement and data regarding the efficiency and scaling of the nonlinear processes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the challenge of miniaturization, describes the micromachined platform and atomic configuration, details the experimental setup for blue and mid-IR detection, presents results on temperature and power scaling, compares the chip-scale performance to glassblown cells, and concludes with potential applications.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The exceptional nonlinearity of alkali-metal vapors enables highly efficient nonlinear optical processes even at relatively low optical intensities. However, such processes have traditionally relied on centimeter-scale vapor cells. Here, we utilize a versatile chip-scale Rb vapor platform to generate coherent blue and mid-IR light in continuous-wave mode by means of resonant four-wave mixing. Optimized optical overlap with the atomic medium enables blue light generation of $\sim$20 $μ$W over a very short interaction length, while maintaining a directly measured linewidth of $\sim$1 MHz, which is presently limited by the measurement apparatus. Comparison with a conventional glassblown vapor cell further shows that the micromachined platform can achieve higher coherent blue-light generation efficiency despite its substantially shorter interaction length. Moreover, an anodically bonded Si window enables to detect coherent mid-IR emission with collected powers of $\sim$50 nW. We further characterize the temperature dependence and input-power scaling of the blue emission, confirming efficient nonlinear conversion within these compact vapor cells. This chip-scale platform provides a versatile foundation for a range of nonlinear optical functions, from precise wavelength references and quantum light sources to next-generation quantum sensors.

</details>

### [Observable measures of multipartite entanglement](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01375v1)

**Authors:** Francois Payn, Davide Girolami  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01375v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01375_figures/2605.01375_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 2. We study the scaling with the particle number N of the lower bound to the genuine N-partite entanglement of formation in symmetric pure states. While, as expected, the GHZ state has always one bit of N-partite entanglement, the Dicke states display peak entanglement when N is twice the number of excitations. We conjecture this behaviour is universal, i.e., it may hold for any N.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01375_figures/2605.01375_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 1. The yellow line represents the total entanglement of formation E2↔N F (ρ(p)) in Eq. (24). The blue line shows that the lower bound of the total entanglement of formation increases as p varies from 0.8 to 1, where the state becomes an ideal N-partite GHZ state. The red line is the upper bound of E2↔N−1 F (ρ(p)), and it is always zero, matching the analytical value.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01375_figures/2605.01375_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. We investigate the entanglement structure of ran- dom five-qubit pure states, as constructed in (34). In this case, the parameter of the Bernoulli distribution (33) is set to p = 0.85. For this choice, we calculate the (averaged) lower bounds to the k-partite entanglement of formation and squashed entanglement, for k = 2, 3, 4, 5.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents a new method to estimate multipartite entanglement using only observable quantities like state purities and correlations. It avoids the exponential cost of full state tomography and provides a scalable way to certify entanglement in large-scale quantum systems. The approach is particularly useful for characterizing GHZ, W, and Dicke states in the presence of noise.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for experimentalists in AMO and quantum optics as it provides a way to certify complex multipartite entanglement using low-depth measurements (purity and correlations) without the need for full state tomography.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Quantifying multipartite entanglement in laboratory settings is extremely difficult because it typically requires full state tomography or computationally intractable calculations for mixed states.

**Main result.** The authors developed a framework of experimentally accessible upper and lower bounds for multipartine entanglement (including entanglement of formation and squashed entanglement) using only local and global state purities and correlation functions.

**Method.** The approach uses the entropy strong subadditivity inequality and the Koashi-Winter monogamy relation to derive bounds, and employs a dynamic programming strategy to efficiently compute these bounds for large systems.

**Model / system.** The framework applies to arbitrary-sized multipartite quantum systems, with specific analytical and numerical tests performed on GHZ, W, and Dicke states, as well as random pure states.

**Key observables.** Local and global state purities, correlation functions of local observables, and operator norms.

**Important parameters / regimes.** System dimension (d), number of particles (N), entanglement degree (k), and noise/purity parameters (p).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The bounds are most effective when the global state is nearly pure; the method assumes that higher-order purities are measurable, though higher-order expansions increase measurement complexity.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows how bounds certify N-partite entanglement in dephased GHZ states; Figure 2 illustrates the scaling of entanglement bounds for GHZ, W, and Dicke states; Figure 3 shows the entanglement structure of random pure states.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of entanglement quantification, derives bipartite bounds using information-theoretic inequalities, extends these to multipartite entanglement using a recursive method, and validates the results through analytical tests on symmetric states and numerical simulations of random states.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Multipartite entanglement is the premier resource for quantum technologies. Yet, its exact quantification in the laboratory is notoriously challenging, typically requiring the full knowledge of high dimensional quantum states. Here, we construct observable bounds to multipartite entanglement for systems of arbitrary size, which are functions of the local and global state purities, and correlation functions. First, we derive experimentally accessible upper and lower limits to both the bipartite entanglement of formation and the squashed entanglement of bipartite systems, by leveraging cornerstone results of quantum information theory: the entropy strong subadditivity inequality and the Koashi-Winter monogamy relation. Then, we convert them into bounds to the entanglement up to degree k for arbitrary states, and to the genuine k-partite entanglement, by employing a recently proposed method. Finally, we analytically and numerically test these results, by bounding the multipartite entanglement of several relevant states and mixtures, including the important classes of GHZ, Dicke, W states, and random pure states.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper evaluates how effectively quantum error detection can mitigate noise on real IBM quantum hardware. It shows that while larger codes can exponentially improve accuracy, the practical benefits are limited by a pseudothreshold where hardware overheads and circuit depth begin to outweigh error suppression.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the scalability and performance of quantum error detection (QED) on real hardware, specifically addressing the trade-offs between error suppression and the overheads of sampling and classical processing.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that QED can exponentially improve expectation value accuracy as code distance increases, though it identifies a pseudothreshold where increased circuit depth and SWAP gate overhead can eventually degrade performance.

**Method.** The authors perform a detailed benchmarking study using both real IBM quantum computers and noisy simulations, employing repetition and triangular color codes for memory and logical Bell state experiments.

**Model / system.** The experiments were conducted on IBM quantum hardware (including IBM Kyiv, Fez, and Boston) using the heavy-hex lattice topology and a global depolarizing noise model.

**Key observables.** Error-mitigated expectation values (e.g., <Z> and <ZZ>), logical Bell state fidelity, and the fraction of measured codewords (post-selection rate).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Code distance (d), number of physical qubits (n), circuit depth (D), physical noise rate (p), and classical processing time.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes that all occurring errors are correctable and that the operator commutes with the code projector; assumes a depolarizing noise model for certain derivations.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the exponential scaling of classical processing time, the convergence of expectation values with increasing code distance, the crossover effect where larger codes perform worse at high depths, and the estimation of the pseudothreshold decay.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the benefits and scaling bottlenecks of QED, moves to experimental benchmarks on repetition and color codes, analyzes the impact of hardware topology and circuit depth, and concludes with a mapping of the error detection frontier (pseudothresholds).

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum error detection can produce unbiased expectation values that exponentially converge to noiseless results as the code distance is increased. Despite this, its performance as an error mitigation technique is relatively understudied on quantum hardware because of its two main drawbacks: (i) the number of samples increases exponentially in the circuit depth/noise level, and (ii) the classical processing generally grows exponentially in the code distance, though exceptions exist. Additionally, the constant (but often large) overhead of embedding the code and logical operations on hardware can make accuracy worse instead of better. In this work, we seek to provide a clear picture of these opportunities and challenges for scaling quantum error detection on hardware. We do so by performing a detailed benchmarking study on real and simulated noisy quantum computers, using the repetition code and triangular color code for memory experiments and logical computations with up to $74$ physical qubits. In addition to these benchmarks, we estimate the pseudothreshold of codes to map the frontier of error detection on current and future quantum computers. Despite the challenges, our results show strong promise for scaling quantum error detection on hardware.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

Each point corresponds to a...</figcaption>

**Summary.** This paper presents a novel quantum-classical hybrid algorithm designed to solve stochastic optimization problems used in insurance underwriting. By combining a specialized QAOA-based circuit with a robust classical post-processing repair scheme, the authors demonstrate that quantum hardware can outperform traditional classical heuristics on large-scale, high-constraint problems.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Solving the Chance-Constrained Knapsack Problem (CCKP), a stochastic combinatorial optimization problem where item weights are random variables and constraints must be met within a specific risk tolerance.

**Main result.** The proposed quantum-classical hybrid scheme achieves solution quality comparable to classical heuristics for small instances and outperforms them for larger, more complex instances (e.g., n=75, M=5).

**Method.** A hybrid framework using a QAOA-based variational circuit (LD-DA) on IBM Heron processors, combined with a parameter transfer strategy to mitigate barren plateaus and a self-consistent classical recovery scheme for post-processing noisy samples.

**Model / system.** A variational quantum circuit using a phase separator Hamiltonian (encoding values and mean weights) and a mixer Hamiltonian (using X and XX rotations) acting on a specific Hamming-weight sector of qubits.

**Key observables.** Measurement probability P(T) of target subsets, solution quality metric (closeness kappa to Gurobi), and constraint residual.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of items (n), number of constraints (M), weight variance (sigma^2), risk tolerance (epsilon), and circuit depth (p).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The weight distributions are assumed to be closed under convolution (specifically focusing on Normal distributions) to allow for deterministic reformulation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the QAOA circuit architecture; Figure 2 compares Gurobi runtime and solution quality against heuristics; Figure 3 shows the CDF of loss values and the impact of the recovery algorithm; Figure 4 illustrates the evolution of profit distributions during fine-tuning.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the CCKP problem, proposes a deterministic reformulation via SOCP, details the quantum-classical hybrid architecture (ansatz, mixer, and parameter transfer), describes the classical post-processing recovery algorithm, presents experimental results on IBM hardware and MPS simulations, and compares performance against classical benchmarks.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The presence of stochastic elements in combinatorial optimization problems makes them particularly challenging, as such problems quickly become intractable for classical computers even at relatively small sizes. In this work, we propose a novel quantum-classical hybrid scheme for solving a class of stochastic optimization problems known as chance-constrained knapsack problems, in which item weights follow probability distributions and constraints may be violated within a specified risk tolerance. Our method employs knapsack-specific QAOA-based circuits to generate samples which, when combined with a self-consistent classical recovery scheme introduced in this work, produce high-quality solutions. Experiments carried out on IBM Heron processors, using circuits with depths up to 177 and comprising 3443 gates acting on as many as 150 qubits, yield solutions that indicate improvement over classical optimization schemes. The proposed quantum-classical scheme paves the way to tackling such problems, with the potential to outperform approaches that rely solely on classical computation.

</details>

**Authors:** Wuttichai Pankeaw, Teparksorn Pengpan, Pruet Kalasuwan  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02581v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that entanglement entropy can act as a quantitative fingerprint to resolve the internal bond structure of frustrated spin chains. By analyzing different bipartitions, the authors show that the partially dimerized phase exhibits a unique multi-band signature in pairwise entropy that distinguishes it from the fully dimerized phase.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether von Neumann entanglement entropy can serve as a robust diagnostic tool to distinguish between different dimerized bond architectures (fully vs. partially dimerized) in frustrated spin chains.

**Main result.** The study establishes that entanglement entropy provides a clear fingerprint for bond patterns, specifically identifying a unique multi-band structure in pairwise entropy for the partially dimerized phase and specific saturation values for the fully dimerized phase.

**Method.** The authors use exact Matrix Product State (MPS) representations of valence-bond ground states and perform finite-size scaling analysis to extract thermodynamic limits.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on the spin-1/2 Majumdar-Ghosh model and the spin-3/2 J1-J2-J3 chain, which supports both fully dimerized (FD) and partially dimerized (PD) phases.

**Key observables.** von Neumann entanglement entropy (half-chain, single-site, and pairwise), entanglement spectrum, and correlation length.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Spin magnitude (S=1/2 and S=3/2), bond multiplicity, dimerization patterns, and boundary conditions (Periodic vs. Open).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is based on exact valence-bond ground states, which are specific types of gapped, non-critical states.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate dimer patterns and boundary configurations, plots of half-chain entropy convergence for MG, FD, and PD phases, single-site entropy showing edge effects, and pairwise entropy showing multi-band structures and distance-dependent correlations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of identifying bond architecture, describes the MPS construction for the studied spin models, presents entanglement entropy results for various bipartitions and boundary conditions, and concludes with a diagnostic comparison of scaling behaviors.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The von Neumann entanglement entropy of exact valence-bond ground states is studied in two frustrated one-dimensional spin chains: the spin-1/2 Majumdar-Ghosh (MG) model and the spin-3/2 J1-J2-J3 chain in its fully dimerized (FD) and partially dimerized (PD) phases. Using matrix-product-state representations, the entropy is computed as a function of system size for three complementary bipartitions - half-chain, single-site, and pairwise - under both open and periodic boundary conditions. In all cases, the entropy saturates to a finite constant in the thermodynamic limit, confirming area-law behavior. The saturation values, extracted via finite-size scaling, are directly related to the underlying virtual-spin bond structure. The MG model and FD phase exhibit similar entanglement behavior, differing primarily in saturation magnitude determined by spin value and bond multiplicity, and both display even-odd oscillations and exponential convergence with system size. In contrast, the PD phase shows qualitatively distinct signatures, including multiple half-chain saturation values depending on the bond type at the cut, asymmetric edge contributions in the single-site entropy, and a multi-band structure in the pairwise entropy reflecting the coexistence of single- and double-singlet bonds. These results establish entanglement entropy as a robust signature of frustrated bond architecture, enabling clear distinction among dimerized phases with different spin magnitude, bond multiplicity, and dimerization patterns.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper presents the design of a low-cost, open-hardware DAC System-on-Module for precise control of ion-trap electrodes. It addresses the challenges of scalability and supply-chain security by using widely available components to achieve ultra-low noise performance. The resulting prototype is a viable, extensible platform for large-scale quantum computing applications.

**Why it may be interesting.** This provides the essential high-precision hardware infrastructure required to implement complex ion-shuttling and transport protocols in large-scale QCCD architectures, which is critical for scaling up ion-trap quantum computers.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need for a scalable, low-cost, and ultra-low noise DC electrode control system for ion-trap quantum technologies that avoids proprietary ecosystems and supply-chain vulnerabilities.

**Main result.** The development of the 'Vanguard DAC,' an open-hardware System-on-Module that achieves ultra-low noise performance (comparable to ground plane noise) and high-precision voltage control suitable for ion-trap applications.

**Method.** A mixed-signal PCB design utilizing a Texas Instruments DAC81416 and an AMD Xilinx Spartan-7 FPGA, featuring a two-stage linear voltage regulation strategy and an 8-layer stack-up for EMI mitigation.

**Model / system.** An open-hardware Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) System-on-Module (SoM) designed for controlling DC electrodes in trapped ion quantum computing, simulators, and sensors.

**Key observables.** Output noise density, power spectral density (PSD), voltage resolution (LSB), slew rate, settling time, and crosstalk between channels.

**Important parameters / regimes.** 16-bit resolution, +/-10 V output range, 50 MHz clock frequency, 48.22 kHz RC filter cutoff, and 1.76 V/us measured slew rate.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The design assumes that using OEM components and open-hardware architecture can effectively mitigate risks of component obsolescence and proprietary lock-in.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows design requirements; Figure 2 presents CAD models of the SoM; Figure 3 shows PCB layout details; Figure 4 illustrates the control infrastructure; Figure 5 and 6 show voltage accuracy and inter-device offsets; Figure 7 displays the power spectral density; Figure 8 shows voltage transition waveforms.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the design requirements, describes the hardware architecture and power management, details the PCB engineering and implementation, presents performance characterization (noise, accuracy, and transient response), and concludes with future improvements.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

A new design for an open-hardware Digital-to-Analog Converter System-on-Module is presented for low-noise ion-trap electrode control. The design specifications were established to fill the technical needs of a modular, scalable DC electrode control platform with sufficient bandwidth, noise characteristics and control flexibility. Critically, a priority was placed on supply-chain management considerations and cost effectiveness for scaling. The system is based upon the Texas Instruments DAC81416 and AMD Xilinx Spartan-7 FPGA for the analog signal and compute architecture respectively. Performance characterization of a prototype device suggests the design is suitable for a variety of ion-trap physics experiments and quantum computing applications.

</details>

### [Measuring Accuracy and Energy-to-Solution of Quantum Fine-Tuning of Foundational AI Models](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02798v1)

**Authors:** Oliver Knitter, Sang Hyub Kim, Maximilian Wurzer, Jonathan Mei, Claudio Girotto, Karen Horovitz, Chi Chen, Masako Yamada, Frederik F. Flöther, Martin Roetteler  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02798v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<img src="2605.02798_figures/2605.02798_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1: Diagram of the fully-differentiable QML classification head added to the base LLM, reproduced and modified from [12]. It is composed of two sub-modules: a quantum-inspired multi-head encoder to classically reduce the dimension of the SetFit latent vectors, and a data-reuploading parameterized quantum ansatz operating on a QPU. Note that in contrast to the classification head used in [12], our variation of this architecture does not incorporate a final classical linear layer.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02798_figures/2605.02798_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2: Average energy consumption, shown on a semilog plot, for 600-shot debiased inference circuits on QPU (IonQ Forte Enterprise) vs GPU (NVIDIA L4). Least squares fits strongly indicate linear and exponential scaling for QPU and GPU runs, respectively, and indicate break-even of energy-to-solution at around 34 qubits. The exact crossover point is specific to choices of hardware, circuits, and hyperparameters.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02798_figures/2605.02798_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3: Per-job average power draw readings for one slate of inference tests, increasing from 10 to 28 qubits. Fluctuations have been normalized and shifted vertically for readability, and are shown for both the total system and the two macroscale components with greatest power draw.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02798_figures/2605.02798_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 4: Test accuracy on SST2 binary classification as a function of qubit count. QPU+DNL results use debiasing and non-linear filtering for error mitigation. Also shown are noise- less and noisy (gate noise model) simulations, and classical baselines (SVC and logistic regression). The filtered QPU results approach simulation accuracy at scale, consistently exceeding classical baselines starting at 14 qubits. At 18 qubits, results show a reduction in the classical baseline error of 23.9% and 15.7% respectively when executing our model on a noiseless simulator and on a QPU with error mitigation.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents an experimental study measuring the actual power consumption of a trapped-ion quantum processor during AI fine-tuning tasks. It demonstrates that while classical simulations scale exponentially in energy, quantum processors show linear scaling, suggesting a clear energy advantage for quantum computing at around 34 qubits. Furthermore, it shows that quantum-enhanced models can outperform classical counterparts in text classification accuracy.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Evaluating the energy-to-solution (ETS) and classification accuracy of hybrid quantum-classical pipelines for fine-tuning foundational AI models, specifically comparing QPU efficiency against GPU-based classical simulations.

**Main result.** The study identifies a break-even point for energy-to-solution at approximately 34 qubits, where QPUs become more efficient than GPUs, and demonstrates that quantum fine-tuned models can achieve a 24% improvement in classification error over classical baselines.

**Method.** The researchers used direct electrical instrumentation of an IonQ Forte Enterprise processor to measure power draw and applied a hybrid pipeline involving a Sentence Transformer and a parameterized quantum circuit (PQC) with error mitigation techniques like debiasing and non-linear aggregation.

**Model / system.** The experimental platform is an IonQ Forte Enterprise trapped-ion quantum processor. The computational model is a hybrid QML architecture using a BERT-based encoder (SetFit) followed by a PQC with angle encoding and data re-uploading.

**Key observables.** Classification accuracy (error rate), Energy-to-Solution (ETS), power draw (kW), and Z-basis expectation value <Z>.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Qubit counts (10 to 28), circuit depth, number of re-uploads (R=4), and power draw of the QPU (~5 kW) vs GPU (~0.072 kW).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes that the energy-to-solution metric is a scalable metric for future applications; assumes gate counts scale linearly with qubit number; assumes training on classical simulators is a valid precursor to hardware execution.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the hybrid architecture; Figure 2 is a semilog plot of energy scaling (linear QPU vs exponential GPU); Figure 3 shows QPU power draw stability; Figure 4 compares classification accuracy across different qubit counts and noise regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of energy efficiency in QML, describes the hybrid architecture and error mitigation pipeline, presents the experimental setup for energy and accuracy measurements, compares results against classical baselines (SVM, LR) and simulations (MPS, statevector), and concludes with scaling analysis.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present an experimental study of energy-to-solution (ETS) of hybrid quantum-classical applications, enabled by direct instrumentation of power consumption of a Forte Enterprise trapped-ion quantum processor. We apply this methodology to a hybrid quantum-classical pipeline for quantum fine-tuning of foundational AI models, and validate the approach end-to-end on quantum hardware. Despite noise and limited qubit counts, the resulting models achieve accuracy competitive with and exceeding classical baselines such as logistic regression and support vector classifiers. Our results show that QPU energy consumption scales approximately linearly with qubit number for shallow circuits, while classical simulation exhibits exponential scaling, indicating a break-even for ETS around 34 qubits. The classification error improvement of the best quantum fine-tuned model over the best classical fine-tuned model considered in this study is around 24%. We further contextualize these findings with comparisons to tensor network methods. This work establishes energy-to-solution as a measurable and scalable metric for evaluating quantum applications and provides experimental evidence of favorable energy-accuracy trade-offs.

</details>

### [Permutation Routing on Ramanujan Hypergraphs with Applications to Neutral Atom Quantum Architectures](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02498v1)

**Authors:** Joshua M. Courtney  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02498v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `Rydberg arrays` **4/5**

</figure>

**Summary.** The paper demonstrates that using Ramanujan hypergraphs as connectivity overlays can reduce the time required to rearrange atoms in a quantum processor from polynomial to logarithmic scaling. It introduces several advanced routing protocols, including hybrid greedy-Valiant and entanglement-assisted methods, to optimize large-scale neutral-atom architectures.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous mathematical blueprint for designing the connectivity of next-generation quantum processors, specifically showing how expander-like hypergraph topologies can overcome the scaling bottlenecks of 2D/3D grids.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Optimizing the permutation routing of atoms in reconfigurable neutral-atom quantum architectures to minimize routing depth and latency.

**Main result.** Proves that routing on Ramanujan (d,r)-regular hypergraphs achieves a logarithmic depth of Theta(log N), providing a significant speedup over standard grid-based routing.

**Method.** Uses hypergraph theory, spectral analysis of the clique expansion graph, and Valiant's two-phase randomized routing algorithm.

**Model / system.** Reconfigurable neutral-atom arrays (e.g., optical tweezers, AOD, or AOL architectures) where qubits are encoded in atoms and entangling gates are performed via Rydberg blockade.

**Key observables.** Routing number (rt(H)), routing depth (T), spectral ratio (beta), and displacement energy (Phi_t).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of atoms (N), hypergraph degree (d) and uniformity (r), capacity (k), and the crossover threshold for entanglement-assisted routing (R_break approx 4).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes the effectiveness of covering towers relies on a uniform spectral bound; assumes a matching-model where edge-disjoint swaps can be performed simultaneously.

**Figures summary.** Tables comparing routing regimes, routing bounds for various hypergraphs, and trade-offs between AOL capacity and routing depth.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the routing problem in neutral atom arrays, develops a mathematical framework using Ramanujan hypergraphs, analyzes specific architectural models (2D AOD and 3D AOL), explores algebraic constructions and their limits (Abelian Alon-Boppana barrier), and evaluates advanced strategies like hierarchical and entanglement-assisted routing.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We consider the routing of neutral atoms on a reconfigurable lattice in terms of hypergraph transformations. We prove the routing number of a Ramanujan $(d,r)$-regular hypergraph on $N$ vertices satisfies $\mathrm{rt}(H) = Θ(\log N)$, where routing is via matchings in the clique expansion graph $G_{\mathrm{cl}}(H)$. Hypergraphs reframe the qubit routing problem by replacing Nenadov's two-sided spectral gap hypothesis with a one-sided condition based on eigenvalue centering. Song--Fan--Miao (SFM) coverings scale for Ramanujan families of every uniformity. A virtual overlay theorem establishes a capacity--depth tradeoff for 3D acousto-optic lens (AOL) architectures, with multi-layer stacking achieving $Θ(\log N)$ routing with $L = O(\log N)$ independent overlay layers. An abelian Alon--Boppana barrier shows that fixed-degree Cayley graphs on $\mathbb{Z}_n^2$ cannot be Ramanujan and affine derandomization on such graphs achieves 15--30% congestion reduction. Towers of $k$-fold Ramanujan coverings yield $\mathrm(H_L) = O(\log N)$ by recursive routing lift. Entanglement-assisted routing by pre-distributed Bell pairs achieves $O(\log N)$ teleportation depth with a stable crossover at $\sim\!4$ routing rounds. Displacement energy analyzes greedy adaptive routing, identifying stalling and a hybrid greedy--Valiant protocol achieving $\sim\!3\times$ speedup at practical scales. Hierarchical multi-scale routing achieves $O(\log^2 N / \log b)$ depth with boundary-only transfers at capacity $k = O(\sqrt{N} \log N)$, and $O(\log N)$ depth with optimal block size $b = Θ(\sqrt{n})$.

</details>

**Authors:** Chen-Te Ma, Ma-Ke Yuan  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02097v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5**

</figure>

**Summary.** This paper establishes new, computable criteria for determining the full separability of multipartite quantum states using third-order negativity. It provides a complete classification of entanglement measures for four-qubit systems and demonstrates how these algebraic invariants can be applied to study entanglement in Conformal Field Theory.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides fundamental tools for characterizing multipartite entanglement, which is essential for understanding complex correlations in many-body dynamics and quantum information processing.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the difficulty of classifying multipartite entanglement and establishing necessary and sufficient criteria for full separability in multi-qubit and multi-qudit systems.

**Main result.** The authors prove that the third-order negativity (I5) is a necessary and sufficient criterion for the full separability of tripartite pure states and develop a complete set of 18 independent entanglement measures for four-qubit pure states.

**Method.** The study employs invariant theory, the replica trick, convex-roof extensions for mixed states, and algebraic decomposition of hyperdeterminants.

**Model / system.** The research focuses on multipartite quantum systems, specifically 3-qubit and 4-qubit pure and mixed states, as well as general q-partite qudit systems and applications to 2D Conformal Field Theory.

**Key observables.** Third-order negativity (I5), three-tangle, two-tangle, four-tangle, and the hyperdeterminant.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Central charge (c) in CFT, number of qubits/qudits (q), and the large-c limit.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the use of local unitary transformations for classification and relies on the compact nature of the parameter simplex for finding extrema.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides graphical notation for tripartite wavefunctions and the construction of the 2nd Renyi multi-entropy.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by establishing separability criteria for tripartite states, moves to the classification of 4-qubit entanglement measures, generalizes these findings to q-partite qudit systems, and concludes with applications to Conformal Field Theory.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We show that the third-order negativity provides a necessary and sufficient criterion for full separability of tripartite pure states, and extend this to mixed states beyond bipartite diagnostics such as negativity. As a minimal nontrivial example, a four-qubit pure state has three-qubit mixed reductions; its complete characterization requires six bipartite, eight tripartite, and four quadripartite measures, with the third-order negativity serving as a key separability criterion. We further generalize these separability criteria to multipartite qudit systems and discuss an application to conformal field theory.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.02051"></a>
### [Sub-Cubic Quantum Gate Synthesis via Stochastic Commutator Decomposition](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02051v1)

**Authors:** Yevgen Kotukh  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02051v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5**
