### [Probing Quantum Entanglement in $τ^+τ^-$ Pairs via the $ππ$ Channel at STCF](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01233v1)

**Authors:** Xiaokang Li, Chentao Bao, Hai Chen, Mingyi Liu, Dayong Wang  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01233v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2: Distributions of cos θ± i = −h± i (top two rows) and cos θ− i cos θ+ j = h− i h+ j (bottom one row) along the diagonal {ˆn, ˆr, ˆk} directions in the ππ channel, com- pared among the full-PHSP truth sample (red histogram), the in-acceptance truth sample (blue histogram), and the corresponding good-solution reconstruction (green histogram).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents a feasibility study for detecting quantum entanglement in tau-lepton pairs at the future Super Tau-Charm Facility. By simulating the pi-pi decay channel, the authors demonstrate that the facility can accurately reconstruct quantum correlations and validate the quantum-tomography framework. This establishes STCF as a competitive platform for high-energy particle physics studies involving quantum information observables.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Assessing the feasibility of using the proposed Super Tau-Charm Facility (STCF) to probe quantum entanglement and Bell-inequality violation in tau-lepton pairs.

**Main result.** The study successfully established a consistency chain for reconstructing entanglement, achieving a reconstructed concurrence of 0.279 ± 0.007 in the pi-pi channel, demonstrating STCF's potential for precision quantum correlation studies.

**Method.** Full Monte Carlo simulations using the OSCAR framework, including Geant4-based detector simulation, and quantum state tomography via the polarimeter-vector method.

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of tau-lepton pairs (tau+tau-) produced via electron-positron annihilation at a center-of-mass energy of 7 GeV at the proposed Super Tau-Charm Facility (STCF).

**Key observables.** Concurrence (C), Bell-inequality violation parameter (m12), Spin Density Matrix (SDM) elements, and spin-analyzing power (kappa).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Center-of-mass energy sqrt(s) = 7 GeV; peak luminosity of 0.5 x 10^35 cm^-2s^-1; tau decay length of approximately 140 micrometers.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study uses a 'good-solution' approach to handle the two-fold ambiguity in tau momentum reconstruction, effectively isolating detector effects from reconstruction errors.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows SM predictions for Concurrence and the Horodecki condition as functions of energy and scattering angle; Figure 2 displays angular distributions of decay products along specific axes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scientific motivation, describes the experimental platform (STCF) and simulation framework, details the reconstruction methodology and the consistency chain, presents results for the pi-pi and rho-rho channels, and discusses limitations regarding vertex resolution.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum entanglement and Bell-inequality violation in $τ^+τ^-$ pairs provide a sensitive probe of quantum correlations in high-energy interactions. We present a feasibility study of $e^+e^- \to τ^+τ^-$ at the proposed Super Tau-Charm Facility based on full Monte Carlo simulation at $\sqrt{s} = 7$ GeV, focusing on the $ππ$ channel ($τ^\pm \to π^\pmν$), which offers the maximal spin-analyzing power $|κ| = 1$ and the simplest final-state topology for validating the quantum-tomography framework. We establish a consistency chain from the tree-level QED prediction through truth-level and detector-level reconstruction, yielding a reconstructed concurrence of $0.279 \pm 0.007$ with the good-solution approach. A complementary full-simulation study of the $ρρ$ channel is also briefly reported. These results demonstrate that the STCF can provide a competitive platform for precision studies of quantum correlations in $τ$-lepton pairs.

</details>

**Authors:** Abir Mukherjee, Kajal Sharma, Ajit K Katiyar, Saranya Das, Samit K Ray, Samaresh Das  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02646v1>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates the fabrication of a CMOS-compatible resonant tunneling device using monolayer MoS2 that exhibits high peak-to-valley ratios. By combining experimental transport measurements with DFT and NEGF simulations, the authors show how valley-selective tunneling can be controlled. This work provides a foundation for developing room-temperature quantum technologies and spin-valley qubits.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper explores the manipulation of valley-selective transport and spin-valley states via gate voltage, which is a fundamental concept for developing 2D-material-based quantum information processing and spin-valley qubits.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to develop CMOS-compatible 2D-based Resonant Tunneling Devices (RTDs) and investigate valley-selective tunneling density of states in monolayer MoS2 for potential use in spin-valley qubit technology.

**Main result.** The researchers demonstrated a high Peak-to-Valley Ratio (PVR) of 178 at 4K and 24 at room temperature, showing that gate-voltage-induced manipulation of momentum-conserved and non-conserved tunneling is possible.

**Method.** The work combines experimental fabrication (CVD growth, e-beam evaporation, and transfer) and characterization (Raman, AFM, XPS, CL, and I-V measurements) with theoretical modeling using Density Functional Theory (DFT) and Non-Equilibrium Green's Function (NEGF) frameworks.

**Model / system.** The physical system is a double-barrier resonant tunneling device consisting of a CVD-grown monolayer MoS2 quantum well sandwiched between Al2O3 tunneling barriers, integrated with highly n-doped Si reservoirs.

**Key observables.** Peak-to-Valley Ratio (PVR), Negative Differential Resistance (NDR), Tunneling Density of States (TDOS), and I-V characteristics.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature regimes from 4K to 300K, MoS2 effective mass (0.46m0), Si effective mass (0.19m0), and sulfur vacancy concentrations.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The theoretical model utilizes an effective mass approximation and a mode space approach to reduce computational burden.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows Raman spectra, Raman maps, micro-PL, CL characterization, and AFM height estimation; Figure 2 displays high-resolution XPS spectra for elemental composition and valence band analysis.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the fabrication of MoS2 RTDs, details the experimental characterization of material quality and defects, presents the electrical transport measurements (I-V, NDR, PVR), provides theoretical validation via DFT and NEGF simulations of the tunneling density of states, and concludes with applications in spin-valley qubit technology.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The present work experimentally demonstrates the fabrication of CVD grown monolayer MoS2 ultra thin quantum well based double barrier resonant tunneling device (RTD) architecture well compatible with conventional CMOS fabrication technology. The strongly quantized electronic states from multiple valleys in the momentum space in such ultra 2D sheet along the c-axis sandwiched in between Al2O3 tunneling barriers exhibit multiple resonant tunneling peaks thereby enhancing the FWHM of the NDR region as derived from experimental I-V characteristics as well as theoretical joint invision through Density Functional Theory (DFT) and Non-Equilibrium Greens function (NEGF) visualized via Tunneling Density of States (TDOS). Understanding extended to S-vacancies not only change the bandgap, as evaluated through nanoscale Cathodoluminescence (CL) spectroscopy, but also alters the effective mass hence the mobility as investigated here within the high symmetry path in the k-space. Electrical performances of fabricated RTD, starting from cryogenic to room temperatures, show a significant milestone via exhibiting huge PVR values of 178 at 4K and 24 at RT with more possible improvement in the field of room temperature quantum technology. Momentum conserved and non conserved tunneling from highly n-doped Si through multiple valleys of 1L-MoS2 provides a tremendous opportunity in gate-induced manipulation in Spin-Valley Qubit technology operational at deep cryogenic temperatures (mK).

</details>

### [A general thermodynamic approach for diffusion on a lattice](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02579v1)

**Authors:** Matías A. Di Muro, Miguel Hoyuelos  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02579v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

At i = 0 the type-A and B particle coverage is fixed at parameters cA and cB, respectively, while for i = L is fixed at dA and dB. The slopes are equal, given by Eq. (66). In this particular case, we have Nm = 600 and a mean coverage ¯θ = 0.4. The results were obtained from Monte Carlo simulations.</figcaption>

(70) and the symbols correspond to the Monte Carlo simulation results. The statistical error of the data is less than 1%.</figcaption>
</figure>

Linear, cuadratic and cubic chemical potentials are shown: µe = θk; k = 1 (circles), k = 2 (boxes), k = 3 (triangles). The dashed line represents Eq. (75) and the symbols correspond to the MC simulations. The statistical error of the data is less than 1%.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper provides a general method to calculate diffusion coefficients in interacting systems by relating them to known ideal-system values through thermodynamic geometry. It offers a computationally efficient alternative to expensive molecular dynamics for predicting transport properties in solids and surfaces.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a general thermodynamic framework to determine the transport coefficients (Onsager matrix) for interacting particle diffusion on a lattice.

**Main result.** The authors derive a fundamental relationship where the Onsager matrix of an interacting system is the ideal-system matrix scaled by the ratio of the determinants of their covariance matrices. This framework successfully reproduces the Darken equation for solids and the Zhdanov model for surface diffusion.

**Method.** The study employs classical irreversible thermodynamics, Einstein's fluctuation theory, and information geometry (using Massieu functions and Hessian matrices). Results are validated using Monte Carlo simulations.

**Model / system.** A lattice-gas model where each site is treated as an open thermodynamic system. The system includes binary mixtures of species A and B with various interaction potentials, including soft-core and power-law chemical potentials.

**Key observables.** Onsager matrix (L), transport coefficients, diffusion currents (intrinsic, tracer, and collective), and thermodynamic factor (Gamma).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Coverage (theta), chemical potential (mu), temperature (T), and the degree of particle interaction (represented by the excess chemical potential).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes local thermal equilibrium, small thermodynamic gradients (linear response regime), and that the range of interactions is much smaller than the cell size. It also neglects higher-order terms in the entropy expansion.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of particle transfer between cells; Figure 2 displays steady-state coverage profiles on a 1D lattice; Figures 3 and 4 show diffusion currents as a function of coverage for various interaction models, demonstrating agreement between theory and simulation.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the thermodynamic framework and the relationship between transition rates and entropy; it then develops the scaling relation for the Onsager matrix using the covariance matrix determinant; finally, it applies this theory to specific models (Darken and Zhdanov) and validates it via Monte Carlo simulations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This work presents a general thermodynamic approach to describe particle diffusion on a lattice, a model used to study transport processes in solids and on surfaces. By treating each lattice site as an open thermodynamic system, the effects of microscopic particle interactions are represented through the chemical potential. A fundamental relationship between the Onsager matrix ($L$) and its ideal-system counterpart ($L_\text{id}$, where interactions are neglected) using the determinant of the covariance matrix is demonstrated. This framework allows for the calculation of transport coefficients using the combination of their ideal values and thermodynamic properties. The general result is successfully applied to reproduce the Darken equation for substitutional diffusion in solids and to derive the non-diagonal diffusion matrix of the Zhdanov model for surface diffusion of Langmuir particles. In the last case, analytical predictions are further validated through numerical simulations across various interaction potentials.

</details>

(a) Absolute en- ergy errors as a function of iteration for the ground state. (b) Same as (a) but for the lowest 13 states using state averaging. All three algorithms start from the same random initial TTNS. The state aver- aged optimizations use the same bond dimensions as the ground state optimizations, thus the larger error. Shown are DMRG energies as a function of the inverse max. bond di- mensions 1∕𝐷max (blue points), and a linear fit that uses two of these energies (purple line). Its vertical intercept yields a lower bound of the energy and a corresponding error estimate. In practice, the fit is done for two energies with the largest bond dimensions. Here, it is shown for energies with smaller 𝐷max values, giving an error esti- mate that is larger than necessary. The blue line connects and extrap- olates DMRG energies using cubic spline interpolation. Reprinted from Ref. [65], CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 license.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.00998_figures/2605.00998_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Zundel ion IR spectrum computed using two TTNS ap- proaches: time-propagation (TDSE, red curves) and eigenstate opti- mization (TISE; blue curves and sticks) for different basis sizes/bond dimensions. The total propagation time is 𝑇= 2000 fs. Both curves use the same cos[𝜋𝑡∕(2𝑇)] kernel for convolution, leading to a scal- ing factor of 4𝑇∕𝜋for the stick spectrum.</figcaption>

First row: Exci- tation energies taken from the peaks of the time-propagation-based spectrum in Fig. 4 (red points), from the eigenstate computation af- ter (blue points) and before (purple points) diagonalization. The last point is based on extrapolation, compare with Fig. 3. Second row: Same as first row but for the IR intensities. Missing data for the peak at 1040 cm−1 means that the simulation did not lead to a significant peak in this region.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This perspective paper details how Tree Tensor Network States (TTNS) can overcome the curse of dimensionality in molecular simulations. By optimizing the tensor network structure, the authors show it is possible to compute thousands of accurate vibrational eigenstates for large, complex clusters. This approach bridges the gap between condensed matter tensor network techniques and molecular quantum dynamics.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it demonstrates how tensor network architectures (originally from condensed matter) can be optimized to solve high-dimensional quantum problems in molecular physics, providing a scalable alternative to traditional wavepacket methods.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Simulating the vibrational and vibronic structures of large, high-dimensional, and fluxional molecules is hindered by the exponential scaling of the direct-product basis, known as the curse of dimensionality.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that Tree Tensor Network States (TTNS) combined with DMRG can accurately compute thousands of eigenstates for complex systems, such as the 33-dimensional Eigen ion, with significantly higher efficiency and accuracy than traditional methods.

**Method.** The method utilizes Tree Tensor Network States (TTNS) and the Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) algorithm, incorporating an inexact Lanczos method for targeting specific states and automated tree structure optimization.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on high-dimensional molecular systems, specifically fluxional protonated water clusters like the Zundel ion and the 33-dimensional Eigen ion, using Sum-of-Products (SoP) Hamiltonians.

**Key observables.** Vibrational/vibronic energies (eigenvalues), infrared (IR) intensities, and molecular dynamics.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Bond dimension (D_max), target energy (sigma), and the dimensionality of the system.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The accuracy is highly dependent on the quality of the Potential Energy Surface (PES) and the choice of coordinate systems; the Hamiltonian must be representable in a Sum-of-Products (SoT) form.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares MPS vs TTNS diagrams and shows the canonicalization procedure; Figure 2 shows DMRG convergence vs ML-MCTDH; Figure 3 demonstrates energy extrapolation via 1/D_max; Figure 4 compares TISE and TDSE IR spectra; Figure 7 shows tree structure optimization for the Eigen ion.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scaling problem, presents the TTNS/DMRG framework, discusses implementation details like canonicalization and state-shifting, demonstrates scalability on water clusters, evaluates error estimation and tree optimization, and concludes with future directions in automation and data analysis.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Tree tensor network states (TTNSs) combined with the density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) are emerging as powerful tools for vibrational and vibronic structure simulations in molecules with strong coupling and fluxionality. In this Perspective, we discuss how TTNS methods enable accurate, full-dimensional computations of thousands of eigenstates for molecular systems ranging from quartic-force-field benchmarks to molecules with strong vibronic coupling and protonated water clusters as large as the 33-dimensional Eigen ion, H$_3$O$^+$$\cdot$(H$_2$O)$_3$. We emphasize the close connection and interoperability between DMRG-based TTNS methods and the multilayer multiconfiguration time-dependent Hartree method (ML-MCTDH), which share the same underlying ansatz. We also highlight practical challenges of predictive simulations, including robust error estimation, convergence of observables such as infrared intensities, and optimization of tensor network tree structures. Finally, we outline recent advances toward direct targeting of excited states and discuss opportunities for broader applications in molecular spectroscopy and quantum dynamics.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.01319"></a>
### [Barren Plateaus as Destructive Interference: A Diagnostic Framework and Implications for Structured Ansatzes](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01319v1)

**Authors:** Pilsung Kang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01319v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01319_figures/2605.01319_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Variance bridge across ansatz families and circuit conditions. Point annotations indicate the corresponding (n, d) settings, where n is the number of qubits and d is the circuit depth. The close agreement between E[B2 eff,kQk] and its factorized approximation shows that the proposed diagnostics remain quantitatively consistent with the standard variance language of BP theory.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01319_figures/2605.01319_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Finite-size gradient-variance scaling for HEA and HVA across circuit depths. The plotted quantity is the mean parameter-wise variance of ∂k⟨H⟩, computed across random seeds and then averaged over parameters for each (n, d, variant) condition. HEA shows a clear decrease in gradient variance as the number of qubits increases, whereas HVA instead shows increasing variance over the tested range. This provides a standard variance-based check that the HEA settings exhibit BP-relevant gradient suppression, while HVA retains substantially larger gradient signal.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01319_figures/2605.01319_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Mean interference-quality measure Beff for HEA and HVA across system size and circuit depth. HEA remains near a stable low-Beff band over the tested range, consistent with persistent random-sign cancellation. By contrast, HVA systematically exhibits larger Beff, indicating escape from the random-sign regime.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01319_figures/2605.01319_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4: Cancellation ratio Rk versus 1/pNeff,k across parameters for (a) HEA and (b) HVA. The dashed line indicates the random-sign baseline, Rk ≈ p</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01319_figures/2605.01319_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5: Variance-bridge validation across ansatz families. (a) Factorization ratio across tested conditions. Values near 1 indicate accurate approximate factorization. (b) Correlation between B2 eff,k and Qk. HEA remains closer to an ideal factorized random-sign regime, whereas HVA exhibits slightly larger residual structure.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes that Barren Plateaus in quantum circuits are caused by destructive interference between different terms in the Hamiltonian gradient. By introducing new diagnostic metrics, the authors show that structured ansatzes avoid these plateaus by organizing the signs of gradient contributions to prevent cancellation.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a microscopic, mechanistic interpretation of a fundamental problem in quantum computing (Barren Plateaus) by framing it as a wave-like interference phenomenon, which is highly relevant to understanding the trainability of many-body quantum simulations.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to understand the microscopic mechanism behind Barren Plateaus (BPs) in variational quantum circuits, specifically whether gradient vanishing is caused by a reduction in active terms or by destructive interference between terms.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that BPs can be understood as a regime of destructive interference and that structured ansatzes like HVA avoid BPs by improving sign organization (coherence) rather than just reducing the number of active terms.

**Method.** The study introduces a diagnostic framework using cancellation ratios and effective term counts, establishing a 'variance bridge' identity to link these metrics to standard gradient variance theory.

**Model / system.** The research uses the Transverse-Field Ising Model (TFIM) and its longitudinal extension as a testbed, comparing Hardware-Efficient Ansatz (HEA) and Hamiltonian Variational Ansatz (HVA).

**Key observables.** Cancellation ratio (R_k), effective term count (N_eff,k), interference-quality measure (B_eff,k), and gradient variance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** System size (n), circuit depth (d), and the random-sign model baseline.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes a random-sign model as a null baseline and focuses on the regime where gradient contributions can be decomposed into individual Hamiltonian terms.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the variance bridge identity; Figure 2 compares gradient variance scaling for HEA and HVA; Figure 3 shows mean B_eff across scales; Figure 4 shows the relationship between cancellation ratio and effective term count; Figure 5 validates the variance bridge factorization.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the mechanistic gap in BP theory, introduces a new diagnostic framework for gradient decomposition, establishes a mathematical bridge to existing variance theory, and then uses numerical simulations of TFIM with HEA and HVA to demonstrate how structured ansatzes escape the destructive interference regime.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Barren plateaus (BPs) are usually described by the exponential suppression of gradient variance, but the mechanism by which gradient signal disappears remains unclear. We show that this phenomenon can be understood as destructive interference among termwise gradient contributions. To make this perspective operational, we introduce a diagnostic framework based on the cancellation ratio $R_k$, the effective term count $N_{\mathrm{eff},k}$, and the interference-quality measure $B_{\mathrm{eff},k}=R_k\sqrt{N_{\mathrm{eff},k}}$. Under a random-sign model, $B_{\mathrm{eff},k}$ remains near a stable baseline, defining a random-sign cancellation regime. For the transverse-field Ising model (TFIM), we find that the hardware-efficient ansatz (HEA) remains close to this regime across system sizes and depths, whereas the Hamiltonian variational ansatz (HVA) systematically escapes it. In particular, HVA exhibits larger $B_{\mathrm{eff},k}$ not merely because $N_{\mathrm{eff},k}$ is larger, but because $R_k$ also remains systematically larger despite the broader term participation. This pattern indicates improved sign organization rather than simple term suppression. We further establish an exact identity that connects the proposed interference diagnostics directly to the standard variance-based theory of BPs. These results position destructive interference as a mechanistic interpretation of BP-like behavior in the regimes studied here, but they do not imply that BPs and destructive interference are universally interchangeable across all architectures and settings.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.02679"></a>
### [Computational Methods towards Ultrastable Glasses](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02679v1)

**Authors:** Fabio Leoni, Misaki Ozawa, John Russo, Taiki Yanagishima, Andrea Ninarello  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02679v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

**Summary.** This review paper surveys the computational landscape of generating ultrastable glasses through advanced numerical algorithms. It evaluates different methods for accessing low-energy amorphous states, providing a guide for understanding the current state of the field and future opportunities in material design.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of computationally realizing ultrastable glasses, which possess exceptionally low-energy states and enhanced stability, by reviewing the algorithms used to access these deeply supercooled states.

**Main result.** The authors provide a comprehensive review and comparative analysis of the most effective algorithms for generating ultrastable glasses, discussing their efficiency, limitations, and the resulting stability achieved.

**Method.** The paper reviews various numerical algorithms that utilize unphysical moves to bypass conventional cooling protocols and access non-equilibrium glassy states.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on amorphous solids and ultrastable glasses, specifically looking at deeply supercooled and non-equilibrium glassy states.

**Key observables.** Kinetic, thermodynamic, and mechanical stability of the glass states.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Energy levels of the glassy states, degree of supercooling, and the efficiency of various computational algorithms.

**Paper structure.** The paper outlines key algorithmic steps, discusses the efficiency and physical interpretation of individual approaches, and concludes with a comparative analysis of stability across different methods.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Ultrastable glasses, amorphous solids with exceptionally low-energy states and enhanced kinetic, thermodynamic and mechanical stability, have long been a subject of intense experimental interest. Over the past decade, their computational realization has emerged as a major goal in condensed matter physics, as numerical methods can exploit unphysical moves to access deeply supercooled and nonequilibrium glassy states far beyond the reach of conventional cooling protocols, thereby providing key insights into the nature of the glass transition and amorphous states and enabling the design of mechanically robust glassy materials. In this review, we outline the key steps underlying the most effective algorithms developed across the field. For each approach, we discuss its efficiency, limitations, and physical interpretation. We finally present a comparative analysis of the stability achieved across these methods, with the aim of equipping both newcomers and experts with an intuitive and comprehensive understanding of the field's current state and the opportunities it presents.

</details>

**Authors:** Krzysztof Lider, Marek Góźdź  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02293v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **2/5**

**Summary.** This paper applies a novel 'projection evolution' model to the Deutsch algorithm, treating time as a quantum observable. It provides a framework for calculating how gate errors propagate, finding that the algorithm can exhibit a self-correcting behavior that reduces overall error.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a physical model to describe quantum gate dynamics and predict error propagation in the Deutsch algorithm using a projection evolution framework.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that the total error of the Deutsch algorithm can be smaller than the error of a single Hadamard gate, revealing a self-correcting effect under certain error conditions.

**Method.** The authors use second quantization formalism (creation and annihilation operators) and density matrix evolution within a Projection Evolution (PEv) model where time is treated as a quantum observable.

**Model / system.** The system is a two-qubit quantum register modeled as a two-level harmonic oscillator within the second quantization formalism, implementing the Deutsch algorithm.

**Key observables.** Probability of correct and incorrect results (Prob(|0>) and Prob(|1>)), density matrices, and the evolution parameter tau.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The single-gate success probability alpha^2 and the discrete evolution steps tau_0 through tau_4.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The wave function depends on all four spacetime coordinates, evolution operators are represented by an orthogonal resolution of unity, and the phase alpha is set to zero.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the Deutsch algorithm circuit; Figure 2 illustrates the evolution timeline; Figure 3 plots success/failure probabilities against gate error; Figure 4 compares algorithm error to single-gate error; Figure 5 shows the ratio of these errors.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the PEv model and its application to the Deutsch algorithm, derives evolution operators for gates using second quantization, performs error propagation analysis for bit-flip errors, and concludes with a comparison of algorithm-level vs. gate-level error rates.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This work demonstrates that the Deutsch algorithm can be effectively modelled using a two-level harmonic oscillator within the second quantization formalism. By adopting this framework, evolution operators are derived. We present a projection evolution model that accurately characterizes the physical state transformation within quantum gates. This approach provides a systematic method for finding evolution operators, enabling the complete description and prediction of state evolution - including projection errors - in quantum algorithms.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.02840"></a>
### [Entanglement cost in non-local quantum computation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02840v1)

**Authors:** Alex May  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02840v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

**Summary.** This paper provides an extensive review of non-local quantum computation, focusing on the amount of entanglement needed to perform operations between distant systems. It connects the entanglement cost of these operations to broader topics like quantum cryptography and quantum gravity.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the entanglement cost required to implement non-local quantum computation (NLQC) using a single round of communication and shared entanglement.

**Main result.** The work provides a comprehensive review of the upper and lower bounds on the entanglement cost for NLQC and explores its connections to various fields.

**Method.** The paper is a book-length theoretical treatment and review of the bounds and applications of non-local quantum computation.

**Model / system.** The system consists of two spatially separated quantum systems that interact via a single round of classical communication and shared entanglement.

**Key observables.** entanglement cost

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes a single round of communication and the availability of shared entanglement.

**Paper structure.** The paper serves as a book-length review, covering the definition of NLQC, bounds on entanglement cost, and its applications in cryptography, complexity, and quantum gravity.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This is a book-length treatment of the subject of non-local quantum computation (NLQC). NLQC is a method for implementing quantum operations that interact two systems without directly bringing the systems together. Instead, a single round of communication and shared entanglement is used. NLQC has appeared in the context of quantum cryptography, computational complexity, communication complexity, quantum gravity, and other applications. The understanding of entanglement cost in NLQC is closely tied to questions in all of these areas. We review upper and lower bounds on entanglement cost, as well as some of the applications of NLQC and its connections to other subjects.

</details>

**Authors:** Ruimin Ke, Talha Azfar, Kaicong Huang, Shuyang Li  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01127v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `QC/QI experiment` **2/5**

</figure>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a hybrid quantum-classical method for solving large-scale transportation network partitioning problems. By using energy sensitivity to selectively optimize only the most influential variables on a quantum processor, the framework enables the handling of larger problems than current hardware would otherwise allow. It demonstrates a practical pathway for applying gate-model quantum computing to complex, dense optimization tasks in the NISQ era.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of partitioning large-scale transportation networks into balanced and spatially coherent zones, which involves dense quadratic interactions that exceed the capacity of current NISQ hardware.

**Main result.** The proposed impact-driven hybrid framework improves convergence and spatial coherence compared to classical refinement, acting as a scalable mechanism for NISQ-era hardware, though it does not outperform direct quantum optimization.

**Method.** A hybrid quantum-classical framework that uses energy sensitivity analysis to identify influential decision variables and assigns reduced sub-QUBO instances to a gate-based quantum processor.

**Model / system.** A transportation network modeled as a spatial graph using a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) formulation, implemented on the IBM Quantum System One backend using the Iskay optimizer.

**Key observables.** QUBO objective value (representing workload balance and spatial coherence) and spatial partition quality (continuity vs. fragmentation).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of traffic zones (n=64), number of operational attributes (m), and the size of the active quantum subspace (q).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The optimization difficulty is non-uniformly distributed, allowing for selective optimization; the method assumes the primary bottleneck is hardware scale rather than algorithmic complexity.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the hybrid workflow; Figure 2 displays IBM hardware connectivity and error rates; Figures 3, 4, and 6 visualize different partition results (direct, classical, and hybrid); Figures 5 and 7 show objective value trajectories for classical and hybrid methods, respectively.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the transportation partitioning problem, formulates it as a QUBO, proposes the impact-driven decomposition framework, describes the implementation on IBM hardware, presents comparative experimental results, and discusses scalability tradeoffs and future work.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Partitioning transportation networks into balanced and spatially coherent traffic zones is a fundamental yet computationally challenging task in intelligent transportation systems. The resulting optimization problem exhibits dense interactions among decision variables and can be formulated as a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) model. While quantum optimization naturally aligns with such quadratic energy representations, current noisy intermediate-scale quantum hardware imposes limitations on problem size, connectivity, and circuit reliability. This paper proposes an impact-driven hybrid quantum--classical optimization framework for traffic zone partitioning that bridges transportation-scale optimization models and practical gate-based quantum processors. Instead of static geographic decomposition, the method estimates the energy impact of decision variables and selectively assigns quantum computation to influential subproblems while a classical coordination loop maintains global feasibility. The framework is implemented using the Iskay optimizer and evaluated on the IBM Quantum System One backend. Experiments compare direct quantum optimization, classical iterative SubQUBO refinement, and the proposed hybrid approach. Results show that impact-guided decomposition improves convergence behavior and produces more coherent spatial partitions relative to classical refinement, while remaining consistent with hardware constraints. Although the hybrid method does not outperform the best direct quantum solution, it demonstrates a practical pathway toward scalable hybrid optimization for transportation applications under current quantum hardware conditions.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02156v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** This survey provides a comprehensive overview of how confinement in lattice gauge theory can be understood as a statistical-mechanical problem of surface tension and topological defects. It connects various theoretical frameworks, such as dualities and effective string models, to the behavior of Wilson loops. The paper highlights the ongoing mathematical challenge of proving the existence of a mass gap in 4D non-Abelian theories.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Understanding the mechanism of confinement in lattice gauge theory through the lens of statistical mechanics, specifically how the area law arises and the mathematical challenge of proving a continuum mass gap.

**Main result.** The survey synthesizes various viewpoints—including center vortices, monopoles, and dualities—to show that confinement is a statistical-mechanical phenomenon related to surface tension and extended topological defects.

**Method.** The paper reviews theoretical frameworks such as strong-coupling expansions, character expansions, duality transformations, and numerical Monte Carlo techniques like multilevel algorithms.

**Model / system.** Lattice Gauge Theory (LGT) formulated as a statistical mechanics of parallel transport on a Euclidean hypercubic lattice, including SU(N) Yang-Mills, Z2 gauge theory, and compact U(1) models.

**Key observables.** Wilson loops, Polyakov loops, 't Hooft loops, Creutz ratio, string tension, and the mass gap.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Coupling constant (beta), number of colors (N), temperature (T), lattice spacing (a), and N-ality.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the validity of the continuum limit and relies on the convergence of expansions like the strong-coupling or cluster/polymer expansions.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the statistical-mechanical formulation of LGT, moves through strong-coupling and duality mechanisms, discusses numerical diagnostics and error analysis, explores finite-temperature transitions and universality, and concludes with the mathematical status of the continuum limit.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Wilson loops provide the central gauge-invariant probe of confinement in lattice gauge theory. This survey reviews the statistical-mechanical formulation of lattice gauge ensembles, the strong-coupling and duality mechanisms behind area laws, finite-temperature and continuum scaling diagnostics, and the mathematical status of Wilson-loop confinement.

</details>

### [Lattice Realization of Twist Defects in a $\mathbb{Z}_2\times \mathbb{Z}_2$ Topological Order](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02039v1)

**Authors:** Gustavo M. Yoshitome  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02039v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper provides a microscopic lattice construction for twist defects in a bilayer topological system. It shows how lattice dislocations can be used to realize anyonic symmetries and how these defects interact through non-Abelian fusion rules. This work enriches the understanding of how topological orders can be manipulated and interconnected via lattice-level defects.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper explores the microscopic lattice realization of anyonic symmetries and their associated twist defects within a Z2 x Z2 topological order.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that lattice dislocations can induce twist defects that permute anyon labels, establishes non-Abelian fusion rules for these defects, and shows that the fusion of electric-magnetic duality and layer exchange symmetries results in a trivalent junction carrying an Abelian anyon.

**Method.** The authors use an algebraic approach involving the analysis of loop (Wilson) operators, K-matrix Chern-Simons theory, and the construction of branch cuts and dislocations in a lattice Hamiltonian.

**Model / system.** A Z2 x Z2 topological order constructed as a bilayer of two decoupled Wen plaquette models (a double toric code) on a square lattice.

**Key observables.** Quantum dimensions, fusion rules, double loop operator eigenvalues, and ground state degeneracy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The anyon labels in Z2^4, the K-matrix, and the specific permutations (sigma, rho, xi) of the anyonic symmetries.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation assumes the validity of the K-matrix formalism and the specific structure of the Z2 x Z2 topological order.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates a dislocation defect and anyon mutation; Figure 2 shows closed loops of e or m particles; Figure 5 depicts a branch cut with pentagon operators; Figure 6 depicts the fusion of sigma and rho defects at a trivalent junction.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the microscopic model, defines the anyonic symmetries, details the lattice implementation of twist defects via dislocations, performs algebraic characterization of defect types and fusion rules, and concludes with a detailed derivation of loop operator eigenvalues.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this work, we explore a microscopic realization of three types of anyonic symmetries in a $\mathbb{Z}_2\times\mathbb{Z}_2$ topological order, corresponding to a double toric code. These symmetries act as nontrivial permutations on the anyon labels of the parent state. We consider a setup consisting of two decoupled Wen plaquette models stacked on top of each other and introduce dislocations that modify the Hamiltonian, giving rise to localized twist defects, eventually inducing interactions between the layers. In this context, branch cuts act as sources of anyon permutations when they cross it. We characterize the defects by calculating their quantum dimensions, and we also consider double loop operators around them that allow us to determine the non-Abelian fusion rules between the defects, including when they carry different anyon permutations.

</details>

### [Many Hamiltonians Are Sparsifiable](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02211v1)

**Authors:** Arpon Basu, Joshua Brakensiek, Aaron Putterman  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02211v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that many complex quantum Hamiltonians can be significantly simplified into much smaller, sparse versions without losing their essential energy properties. By proving that classes like Pauli Hamiltonians are highly sparsifiable, the authors challenge existing 'no-go' theorems and show that quantum systems can be more efficiently compressed than classical constraint satisfaction problems.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether complex, many-term local Hamiltonians can be replaced by a much smaller, sparse set of terms (sparsifiers) that preserve the expectation value of energy for all quantum states within a $(1 \pm \epsilon)$ factor.

**Main result.** The authors prove that many classes of Hamiltonians, including Pauli strings, random operators, and certain Quantum SAT instances, are highly sparsifiable, contradicting previous 'no-go' beliefs and suggesting quantum systems may be easier to sparsify than classical ones.

**Method.** The study uses a new framework of 'non-redundancy' analysis, randomized classical algorithms, matrix concentration inequalities (Matrix Chernoff Bound), and algebraic techniques like automorphism group analysis and polynomial methods.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on $n$-qubit quantum systems governed by $r$-local, positive semi-definite (PSD) Hamiltonians, specifically examining Pauli Hamiltonians, random $r$-local operators, and $r$-partite hypergraph models.

**Key observables.** The primary observable is the expectation value of the Hamiltonian energy $\langle \psi | H | \psi angle$ across all possible states $|\psiangle$.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Key parameters include the error tolerance $\epsilon$, the locality $r$, the number of original terms $m$, the rank $R$ of the local operators, and the non-redundancy (NRD) of the Hamiltonian.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The results primarily apply to generic or random Hamiltonians and specific structured classes like Pauli strings; the sparsifiability may fail for Hamiltonians with specific tensor product structures.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of Hamiltonian sparsification, develops a theory of non-redundancy to characterize barriers to sparsification, analyzes specific classes (Pauli, random, and Quantum SAT), provides lower bounds for tensor product structures, and concludes with implications for quantum complexity and algorithms.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the problem of Hamiltonian sparsification: given a parameter $\varepsilon \in (0,1)$ and an $n$-qubit Hamiltonian $H$ which is the sum of $r$-local positive semi-definite (PSD) terms $H_1, \dots H_m$, our goal is to compute a sparse set $L \subseteq [m]$, along with weights $w: L \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ such that for every state $|ψ\rangle\in \mathbb{C}^{2^n}$, $$ \sum_{i \in L} w(i) \langle ψ| H_i | ψ\rangle \in (1 \pm ε) \sum_{i = 1}^m \langle ψ| H_i | ψ\rangle $$.   When the set $L$ is significantly smaller than $m$, this reduces the number of terms in the underlying system, while still ensuring that the behavior of the system is essentially unchanged. We show that many Hamiltonians indeed are sparsifiable to a number of terms much smaller than $n^r$, including: (a) Hamiltonians where each term is an $r$-local Pauli string, (b) Hamiltonians where each term is an $r$-local random operator of rank $R$, for $R \geq 2^{r-1}+1$, and (c) Hamiltonians where each term is an arbitrary $r$-local operator of rank $\geq 2^r -1$ (a.k.a. Quantum SAT).   Taken together, our results show that the sparsifiability of Hamiltonians is a robust phenomenon, contrary to prevailing belief (see for instance, Aharonov-Zhou ITCS 2019, QIP 2019). Our results find applications, for instance, to better (semi-)streaming algorithms for quantum Max-Cut, answering a question left open by Kallaugher and Parekh (FOCS 2022). In fact, our results even codify that quantum systems are often easier to sparsify than their classical counterparts.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.02148"></a>
### [Mobility Anisotropy Reshapes Self-Propelled Motion](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02148v1)

**Authors:** Amir Shee, P. S. Pal  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02148v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

**Summary.** This paper provides an exact solution for the dynamics of anisotropic self-propelled particles in a harmonic trap. It demonstrates that mobility anisotropy can drive particles into high-potential regions, resulting in a sub-Gaussian distribution. This work is significant for understanding the behavior of microswimmers and autonomous robots in confined environments.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how anisotropic translational mobility affects the nonequilibrium dynamics and steady-state distribution of a self-propelled particle in a harmonic trap.

**Main result.** The authors find that anisotropic mobility leads to a sub-Gaussian steady-state position distribution and a negative excess kurtosis, causing particles to be displaced into high-potential regions in the high-persistence regime.

**Method.** The authors provide an exact analytical solution to the nonequilibrium dynamics of the system.

**Model / system.** A two-dimensional self-propelled particle (relevant to rodlike microswimmers or wheeled robots) subject to anisotropic translational mobility and a harmonic confining potential.

**Key observables.** Mean displacement, Mean Squared Displacement (MSD), fourth moment, excess kurtosis, and position distribution.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Ratio of mechanical to rotational relaxation timescales, persistence regime, and the degree of mobility anisotropy.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is modeled as a self-propelled particle in a 2D harmonic trap with anisotropic mobility.

**Paper structure.** The paper presents an exact solution for the dynamics, analyzes the MSD and mean displacement, calculates the steady-state fourth moment and kurtosis, and discusses the resulting sub-Gaussian position distribution.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We exactly solve the nonequilibrium dynamics of a harmonically trapped self-propelled particle with anisotropic translational mobility in two dimensions, relevant to rodlike microswimmers and wheeled robots. The mean displacement and MSD reveal a quasi-steady plateau with vanishing fluctuations in the high-persistence regime. An exact calculation of steady-state fourth moment yields a negative excess kurtosis that varies non-monotonically with the ratio of mechanical to rotational relaxation timescales. This gives rise to a strictly sub-Gaussian steady-state position distribution, in which the particle with anisotropic mobility, in high persistence regime, is displaced into the high-potential region lying outside the stationary contour set by the activity and harmonic confinement. This is further corroborated by the relaxation of the MSD from the quasi-steady plateau to the steady-state regime.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces kA-QAOA, a new parameterization for the QAOA algorithm that groups interactions by their order. It demonstrates that this method can match the performance of more complex multi-angle approaches while being much more efficient to optimize, making it highly suitable for NISQ-era hardware with multi-qubit gates.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Finding an optimal QAOA parameterization that balances solution quality (expressiveness) with classical optimization complexity (number of parameters and function evaluations) for hypergraph-based optimization problems.

**Main result.** The proposed kA-QAOA achieves approximation ratios comparable to the highly expressive MA-QAOA but requires significantly fewer function evaluations and parameters, effectively acting as a middle ground between efficiency and quality.

**Method.** The authors introduce kA-QAOA, which groups cost function terms by their k-body interaction order, and benchmark it against SA, MA, and AA-QAOA using classical optimizers (BFGS, Powell) and TQA initialization.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) applied to combinatorial optimization problems on hypergraphs, specifically 3-uniform cyclic sign-alternating hypergraphs and random coefficient hypergraphs using k-body Ising-like Hamiltonians.

**Key observables.** Approximation Ratio (AR), Fidelity, Number of Function Evaluations (nfev), and Non-stabilizerness (Stabilizer Renyi Entropy).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of QAOA layers (p), interaction order (k), and parameter initialization strategies (e.g., 1/p, TQA0.75).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The approach assumes the availability of hardware capable of executing native multi-qubit gates (e.g., trapped ions or neutral atoms) to avoid the overhead of decomposing k-body terms into two-qubit gates.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a 4-qubit cost Hamiltonian circuit; Figure 3 compares performance metrics (magic, AR, nfev) across QAOA variants; Figures 4-7 show AR, fidelity, and nfev for specific hypergraph problem classes across different layers.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the kA-QAOA scheme, defines the hypergraph problem classes and Hamiltonians, details the benchmarking methodology and optimization strategies, presents comparative results for different hypergraph types, and discusses the implications regarding magic and resource efficiency.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The quantum approximate optimization algorithm (QAOA) has emerged as a promising candidate for demonstrating quantum advantage on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices. While various QAOA parameterization schemes exist, ranging from the original single-angle approach to the more expressive multi-angle quantum approximate optimization algorithm (MA-QAOA) and automorphic-angle quantum approximate optimization algorithm (AA-QAOA), each presents distinct trade-offs between expressiveness and classical optimization complexity. In this work, we introduce the $k$-interaction-angle quantum approximate optimization algorithm ($k$A-QAOA), a parameterization scheme that groups cost function terms by their $k$-body interaction order, providing a natural middle ground between parameter efficiency and solution quality. This approach is particularly well-suited for combinatorial optimization problems defined on hypergraphs, where multi-body interactions naturally arise in applications such as Boolean satisfiability and resource allocation with multi-party constraints. We benchmark $k$A-QAOA against standard single-angle quantum approximate optimization algorithm (SA-QAOA), MA-QAOA, and AA-QAOA on two problem classes: 3-uniform cyclic sign-alternating hypergraphs and random coefficient hypergraphs. Our results demonstrate that $k$A-QAOA achieves approximation ratios comparable to MA-QAOA while requiring significantly fewer function evaluations, thereby reducing quantum resource consumption.

</details>

**Summary.** The paper introduces an efficient numerical framework for computing the many-body density of states for identical particle systems. By leveraging the algebraic symmetries of the system's universal combinatorial properties, the authors can approximate the spectrum with tunable accuracy and significantly reduced computational complexity. This allows for the recovery of statistical distributions, such as Bose-Einstein statistics, without prior assumptions about particle statistics.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a highly efficient way to compute many-body spectra and thermodynamic properties (like temperature and occupation numbers) for large systems without the need for expensive Monte Carlo sampling, which is directly applicable to analyzing the microcanonical ensemble in cold atom or trapped ion experiments.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The combinatorial explosion of the many-body density of states (MBDoS) for identical particle systems, which makes exact calculations intractable as the number of particles and single-particle states increases.

**Main result.** A method that reduces computational cost by a combinatorial factor by separating universal combinatorial properties from system-specific quantities and using a hierarchical q-sector truncation scheme.

**Method.** The approach uses SVD to separate universal properties, Galois theory to study symmetries in cyclotomic fields, and generating functions with transfer matrices for efficient counting. It also employs simulated annealing to optimize single-particle energy permutations to minimize information loss during truncation.

**Model / system.** Isolated quantum systems of identical particles (bosons and fermions) in a finite-dimensional space with L single-particle levels and N particles.

**Key observables.** Many-body density of states (MBDoS), single-particle occupation numbers, and microcanonical inverse temperature.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of particles (N), number of single-particle states (L), occupancy limit (R), and single-particle energy spectrum (epsilon).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes the postulate of equiprobability (microcanonical ensemble) and the ergodicity hypothesis; it also relies on a kernel width (Gamma) for smoothing the spectrum.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the distribution of universal spectrum components in the complex plane; Figure 2 illustrates the hierarchical sector flow and its similarity to block-spin renormalization; Figure 3 demonstrates the generating function expansion for specific degeneracy classes; Figure 4 and 5 compare the accuracy of different energy permutations and truncation levels using Kernel Density Estimation; Figure 6 and 7 show the MBDoS, temperature estimates, and recovered particle distributions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the computational problem, presents a mathematical framework based on SVD and Galois theory to identify universal spectra, develops a generating function method for counting, proposes a hierarchical truncation scheme for efficiency, optimizes this scheme via simulated annealing, and validates the method by recovering Bose-Einstein distributions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a method for approximating the many-body density of states of a system of quantum identical particles, with a reduction of the computational cost by a combinatorial factor compared to the full calculation. This is carried out by considering an isolated quantum system of identical particles, and studying its non-interacting many-body spectrum through the use of a new approach based on a separation of universal combinatorial properties from the system-specific quantities. In this paper we focus on a practical computation method that leverages our formalism of many-body combinatorics, in order to perform an efficient numerical computation of the many-body density of states. In addition, this method provides further computational improvements by allowing most of the results to be cached in persistent storage and computed incrementally, making way for efficient use of parallelization and dynamic programming techniques. We give an extensive description of the method and provide several detailed examples of approximations of bosonic many-body density of states with tunable accuracy requirements. Lastly, we demonstrate how one such approximation can be used to recover Bose-Einstein-like distributions without any particle statistics assumptions.

</details>

**Authors:** Hyunho Cha, Chae-Yeun Park, Jungwoo Lee  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01258v1>

**Summary.** This paper identifies the optimal resource requirements for quantum autoencoders, finding a 'Goldilocks' balance of ancillas that achieves maximum reconstruction fidelity. It proves that a specific, limited number of ancillas is sufficient for the encoder, while providing a counterexample where more complex decoders are theoretically better but practically negligible.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the minimal circuit width (ancilla requirements) for optimal blind, single-copy quantum state compression in Quantum Autoencoders (QAEs).

**Main result.** The authors identify a 'Goldilocks' regime where an architecture with exactly k encoder ancillas and n decoder ancillas achieves optimal fidelity, proving that while isometric decoders are not universally optimal, they are practically near-optimal.

**Method.** The study uses mathematical frameworks involving CPTP maps, Stinespectring dilations, and Gram-matrix criteria, supplemented by numerical experiments using MNIST-encoded quantum states and Adam optimization.

**Model / system.** A Quantum Autoencoder (QAE) framework designed to compress n-qubit input states from a distribution mu into a k-qubit latent bottleneck using unitary operations and ancilla registers.

**Key observables.** Average fidelity, average infidelity, and circuit width (ancilla counts).

**Important parameters / regimes.** n (input qubits), k (latent qubits), nB (encoder ancillas), nE (decoder ancillas), and the error parameter epsilon.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study focuses on the single-copy, blind compression regime rather than the asymptotic i.i.d. regime, and some proofs regarding encoder rank are valid for sufficiently small error rates.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the QAE architecture; Figure 2 compares isometric vs. non-isometric decoder infidelity; Figure 3 shows the necessity of encoder ancillas; Figure 4 shows performance on MNIST data.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the QAE problem, defines the mathematical framework for compression, provides theoretical proofs for encoder and decoder ancilla thresholds, presents numerical validation using MNIST and phase-family distributions, and discusses limitations and open questions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum autoencoders (QAEs) are learning architectures that compress quantum data into a low-dimensional latent state while preserving the information needed for reconstruction. We study blind single-copy compression of quantum states through a $k$-qubit bottleneck and investigate the minimal circuit width required to attain the information-theoretic optimum under average infidelity. Between the conventional architecture, which is narrow but nonuniversal, and fully general \emph{completely positive and trace preserving} (CPTP) realizations, which are universal but overparameterized, we identify a \emph{Goldilocks} regime. We prove that for every distribution of pure $n$-qubit states, there exists a QAE with exactly $k$ encoder ancillas and $n$ decoder ancillas that achieves the optimal fidelity over all CPTP encoder--decoder pairs. The encoder-side statement is sharp in that we construct source families for which every optimal scheme necessarily uses at least $k$ encoder ancillas, thereby determining the universal encoder threshold exactly. On the decoder side, we show that isometric decoders are exactly optimal for several analytically tractable source families, but we also exhibit an explicit counterexample demonstrating that decoder isometry is not universally sufficient. Nevertheless, numerical experiments indicate that the performance gap is practically negligible.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that a thin film with magnetic impurities can function as a reservoir computer for image recognition. By leveraging long-range dipole-dipole interactions, the system converts spatial patterns into temporal signals, offering a potential path toward low-cost, easy-to-fabricate spintronic machine learning hardware.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a practical, easily realizable magnetic reservoir computing system that overcomes the high power consumption and fabrication difficulties of existing spintronic hardware.

**Main result.** The long-range dipole-dipole interaction effectively encodes spatial input patterns into temporal magnetization fluctuations, achieving up to 75% accuracy in MNIST digit recognition using spatially averaged outputs.

**Method.** Numerical simulations of magnetization dynamics using the OOMMF framework and the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation, combined with training a linear output layer via Stochastic Gradient Descent.

**Model / system.** A thin film (1um x 1um x 0.1um) embedded with randomly distributed magnetic impurities (Ni or YIG) subject to external magnetic fields, uniaxial anisotropy, exchange interaction, and long-range dipole-dipole interaction.

**Key observables.** Spatially averaged magnetization components and their time derivatives.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Impurity concentration (1% to 100%), saturation magnetization (Ms), and external magnetic field strength.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The input data is prepared by taking a spatial average in the sample; the system relies on the presence of long-range dipole-dipole interactions for complex dynamics.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of the thin film with magnetic impurities; Figure 4 compares classification accuracy using raw magnetization versus its time derivative for Ni impurities.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of magnetic reservoir computing, describes the physical model of a thin film with impurities, details the simulation methodology and MNIST task, presents results regarding the role of dipole interactions and material properties, and concludes with the implications for hardware implementation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The reservoir computing based on the thin film embedded with magnetic impurities in the presence of the long-range (the dipole-dipole) interaction is numerically investigated. We simulated the magnetization dynamics by taking into account the dipole-dipole interaction and performed the handwritten-digit recognition task. Although the training data is prepared by taking spatial average in the sample, the high classification accuracy is achieved. Our result demonstrates that the long range interaction effectively encodes the complex spatial input pattern into the time domain, even when only a spatially averaged output is accessible. The proposed system paves the way for easily realizable magnetic reservoir computing.

</details>

En- tanglement distribution is restricted to hub–spoke links and ring links among the neighboring stations, rather than forming a fully connected clique. **Summary.** This paper proposes architectural design principles for a global quantum internet using LEO satellites. It demonstrates how optimizing satellite orbits and ground station placement can overcome the lack of quantum buffering to enable reliable, concurrent global entanglement distribution.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Designing an efficient satellite-serviced quantum network backbone that supports concurrent global connectivity while addressing the unique constraints of entanglement (no-cloning) and limited quantum buffering.

**Main result.** Identified that anisotropic ground-station placement, multi-inclination LEO constellations, and multi-point satellite service policies significantly reduce connectivity latency and improve global entanglement availability.

**Method.** A discrete-time simulator using a Gaussian-beam optical link-efficiency model and renewal theory to analyze connectivity statistics and waiting times.

**Model / system.** A global hybrid network consisting of a Low-Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite constellation distributing photonic Bell pairs to a lattice of optical ground stations connected via terrestrial fiber.

**Key observables.** Time-to-connectivity (latency), latency-conditioned average active-link strength, entanglement-generation rates, and largest connected component (LCC) fraction.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Orbital altitude (h), orbital inclination (i), anisotropy parameter (alpha) for ground-station spacing, and number of optical terminals per satellite (T).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes a conservative regime with minimal reliance on long-lived quantum memories and excludes physical-layer impairments like atmospheric turbulence, cloud cover, and detector noise.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows orbital geometry parameters; Figure 2 illustrates bipartite connectivity in a specific region; Figure 3 shows ground-station density variations and lattice layouts; Figure 4 depicts the hub-spoke-ring service model.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the architectural problem, defines the geometric and orbital models, presents a simulation framework based on renewal theory, evaluates various architectural configurations (ground-station, constellation, and service policy), and concludes with a discussion of scalability and future directions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Satellite-serviced quantum networks pose an architectural problem distinct from classical satellite networking: because entanglement cannot be copied, and long-lived buffering is technologically constrained for near-term devices, useful end-to-end service requires fixed optical ground infrastructure and simultaneous multi-hop path availability. We investigate the design of a satellite-serviced quantum backbone aimed at supporting concurrent global connectivity across a traffic matrix of major population and financial centers under finite waiting-time constraints. Using a discrete-time simulator, we evaluate performance using two architecture-level metrics: (i) time-to-connectivity, and (ii) latency-conditioned average active-link strength. Across a broad parameter sweep, we identify three dominant architectural effects. First, anisotropic ground-station lattices reduce time-to-connectivity relative to longitudinally collapsed and isotropic baselines by aligning ground infrastructure with latitude-dependent satellite access. Second, multi-inclination LEO constellations reduce waiting times for strong connectivity compared to single-inclination constellations at fixed satellite budgets by providing additional visibility for a diverse latitude set. Third, multi-party satellite service policies alleviate per-satellite concurrency bottlenecks and substantially reduce time-to-connectivity at stringent traffic-matrix thresholds. We further show that satellite altitude is the dominant physical lever shaping the visibility--loss trade-off, strongly affecting both connectivity latency and achievable link strength, while orbital plane count and satellite packing provide secondary refinements at fixed altitude. Together, these results delineate the architectural conditions required for scalable, concurrent entanglement connectivity in satellite-serviced quantum networks.

</details>
