<a id="paper-2605.02732"></a>
### [Geometric Formulation of Power-Efficiency Bounds in Carnot-like Engines](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02732v1)

**Authors:** R. X. Zhai  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02732v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02732_figures/2605.02732_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Isosceles trapezoidal attainable region for σh and σc.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02732_figures/2605.02732_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Linear-programming formulation of the power- eﬀiciency constraint.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a geometric method to calculate the exact limits of the power-efficiency trade-off in heat engines. By treating dissipation as a geometric optimization problem, it provides new analytical bounds for engines where heat loss scales with time via power laws, covering both sublinear and superlinear regimes.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the exact power-efficiency trade-off constraints for Carnot-like heat engines operating under non-standard dissipation regimes.

**Main result.** The authors derive a unified geometric framework that provides exact power-efficiency frontiers and closed-form constraints for specific power-law dissipation exponents.

**Method.** The problem is reformulated as a geometric optimization and linear programming problem in the plane of normalized branch dissipations.

**Model / system.** A Carnot-like heat engine operating between two reservoirs at temperatures Th and Tc, featuring a branch-resolved power-law dissipation model where irreversible heat scales with branch duration as tau^(-alpha).

**Key observables.** Efficiency (eta), power (P), and dimensionless irreversible heats (sigma_h, sigma_c).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Carnot efficiency (eta_C), dissipation exponent (alpha or epsilon = 1/alpha), and the dissipation asymmetry parameter (xi).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes irreversible heat transfer follows a power-law decay with branch duration and focuses on branch-resolved dissipation rather than microscopic engine mechanisms.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the isosceles trapezoidal attainable region for dimensionless irreversible heats; Figure 2 illustrates the linear programming formulation of the power-efficiency constraint.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the power-efficiency problem, defines the power-law dissipation model, presents a geometric optimization framework, applies it via linear programming to find efficiency bounds, and provides analytical solutions for specific dissipation exponents.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We formulate the power-efficiency constraint of Carnot-like heat engines as a geometric optimization problem in the plane of normalized branch dissipations. Efficiency contours are straight lines in this plane, so maximizing efficiency at fixed power reduces to bounding the slope of an admissible line. We apply this framework to branch-resolved power-law dissipation, where the irreversible loss on each isothermal branch decays with the branch duration with a common exponent rather than following the standard inverse-time law. After optimizing over the dissipation-asymmetry parameter, the fixed-power attainable set becomes a two-dimensional region, and the resulting slope-bound problem reduces to linear programming. The framework yields the exact power-efficiency frontier within this model and gives closed-form constraints for representative dissipation exponents, including the maximum-power limit.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper introduces SQD-AA, a new quantum algorithm that uses amplitude amplification to solve the sampling bottleneck in sample-based diagonalization. It demonstrates that this approach can significantly reduce the number of required quantum measurements and circuit depth, making molecular electronic structure calculations more feasible in the early fault-tolerant era.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The Sample-Based Quantum Diagonalization (SQD) algorithm suffers from high measurement overhead because dominant bitstrings are sampled repeatedly, while the sub-dominant bitstrings needed for accuracy are rarely observed.

**Main result.** The proposed SQD-AA algorithm achieves a quadratic advantage in query complexity for exponentially decaying distributions and reduces total T-gate complexity by up to an order of magnitude compared to standard SQD, while maintaining significantly shallower circuits than iQPE.

**Method.** The authors introduce SQD-AA, which integrates Amplitude Amplification (AA) into the SQD framework to sequentially rotate the quantum state and reduce the probability of previously measured bitstrings.

**Model / system.** The method is evaluated on electronic structure Hamiltonians for molecules such as cyclopentadiene, Cr2, H2O, and Mo2, using both RPA and UCJ/ASP state preparation models.

**Key observables.** Ground state energy (GSE), T-count, T-depth, total query complexity, and total number of shots.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Target fidelity (Ft), convergence threshold (tau), target chemical accuracy (epsilon), and decay rates (alpha and gamma) for bitstring distributions.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes an early fault-tolerant computing regime and requires approximate knowledge of bitstring probabilities to implement the amplification steps.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 visualizes the AA rotation process; Figure 2 shows the SQD-AA algorithm flowchart and circuit schematic; Figure 3 illustrates query complexity ratios for algebraic decay; Figure 4 and 5 compare T-count and T-depth across different molecular systems.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the SQD sampling problem, presents the SQD-AA algorithm and its analytical complexity, evaluates performance on model distributions (algebraic/exponential), benchmarks against SQD and iQPE using molecular systems, and discusses parameter optimization and hardware implications.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recently, sample-based quantum diagonalization (SQD) has emerged as a promising approach to compute ground and excited states of problem Hamiltonians.This method classically diagonalizes a Hamiltonian in a subspace that is spanned by samples obtained from a quantum computer. However, by its nature, SQD suffers from a fundamental sampling problem, as some basis states that are required for a targeted accuracy may only be sampled extremely rarely. To alleviate this limitation, we introduce the SQD-AA algorithm that combines SQD with amplitude amplification (AA). SQD-AA uses AA to sequentially reduce probabilities of already measured bitstrings, thus making the observation of new ones more likely. We observe a reduction in the total query complexity of more than a factor 100 for algebraically and exponentially decaying model distributions, and analytically show a quadratic advantage for the latter. Moreover, we evaluate real molecules in an early fault-tolerant scenario and compare SQD-AA to SQD and iterative quantum phase estimation (iQPE). For all considered examples, we observe the lowest total number of T-gates for SQD-AA while only requiring circuits that are 3-4 orders of magnitude shallower than those needed for iQPE. Given this substantial reduction in circuit depth compared to iQPE while saving 2 orders of magnitude in total runtime compared to SQD, we expect a significant regime in early fault-tolerance where SQD-AA runs feasibly, but iQPE circuits are too deep to execute confidently.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.02845"></a>
### [The Complexity of Stoquastic Sparse Hamiltonians](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02845v1)

**Authors:** Alex B. Grilo, Marios Rozos  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02845v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02845_figures/2605.02845_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Complexity classification of Hamiltonian problems under locality, sparsity, and stoquasticity constraints.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02845_figures/2605.02845_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Complexity classification of Hamiltonian problems with separable witness under locality, sparsity, and stoquasticity constraints.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper establishes that the problem of finding the ground state energy of a sparse stoquastic Hamiltonian is complete for the complexity class StoqMA. It also demonstrates that the power of a stoquastic verifier does not increase with the number of unentangled provers beyond two, providing a significant result for quantum complexity theory.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to determine the computational complexity of the Stoquastic Sparse Hamiltonian problem (StoqSH) and investigate the power of the complexity class StoqMA when multiple unentangled proofs are provided.

**Main result.** The authors prove that StoqSH is StoqMA-complete and that the separable version of this problem is StoqMA(2)-complete, effectively showing that k unentangled Merlins can be simulated by two.

**Method.** The work employs a Kitaev-style circuit-to-Hamiltonian construction, a sparse decomposition of Hamiltonians into 1-sparse terms, and a product test using SWAP tests to simulate multiple unentangled provers.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on Stoquastic Sparse Hamiltonians, which are matrices where off-diagonal elements are non-positive, ensuring the ground state has non-negative amplitudes, and where each row has at most polynomial non-zero entries.

**Key observables.** The ground state energy (specifically the minimum eigenvalue) and the acceptance probability of the quantum verifier.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The energy gap (alpha - beta) which is assumed to be at least 1/poly(n), the sparsity d, and the completeness and soundness parameters (a, b, c, s).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The proof assumes the existence of oracles to access sparse Hamiltonian entries and relies on the ability to decompose the Hamiltonian into 1-sparse terms.

**Figures summary.** Figure 3 shows a plot in the completeness vs. soundness plane illustrating the region where the simulation of k Merlins by two Merlins holds.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the complexity classes and the StoqSH problem, then proceeds to prove the StoqMA-completeness of StoqSH, followed by an investigation into the separable version and the simulation of multiple unentangled provers (StoqMA(k) to StoqMA(2)).

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Despite having an unnatural definition, $\mathsf{StoqMA}$ plays a central role in Hamiltonian complexity, e.g., in the classification theorem of the complexity of Hamiltonians by Cubitt and Montanaro (SICOMP 2016). Moreover, it lies between the two randomized extensions of $\mathsf{NP}$, $\mathsf{MA}$ and $\mathsf{AM}$. Therefore, understanding the exact power of $\mathsf{StoqMA}$ (and hopefully collapsing it with more natural complexity classes) is of great interest for different reasons. In this work, we take a step further in understanding this complexity class by showing that the Stoquastic Sparse Hamiltonians problem ($\mathsf{StoqSH}$) is in $\mathsf{StoqMA}$. Since Stoquastic Local Hamiltonians are $\mathsf{StoqMA}$-hard, this implies that $\mathsf{StoqSH}$ is $\mathsf{StoqMA}$-complete. We complement this result by showing that the separable version of $\mathsf{StoqSH}$ is $\mathsf{StoqMA}(2)$-complete, where $\mathsf{StoqMA}(2)$ is the version of $\mathsf{StoqMA}$ that receives two unentangled proofs.

</details>

(b) Renormalization-group flow in the λep-λbd plane at λ∗ ee = 0, calculated from Eq. (7) with C1 = 0.43, C2 = 0.89, and C3 = 2.11. (c) Evolution of λee/λep with ℓ, obtained for λep(ℓ= 0) = 0.02, λbd(ℓ= 0) = 0.5, with four initial values of λee indicated in the legend. (d) Phase diagram in the λep-λee plane, obtained from Eq. (7) with λbd(ℓ= 0) = 1. The black curve denotes the phase boundary between the electron-electron-driven and Peierls-transition-driven phases. The arrow labeled λbd indicates that, as λbd(ℓ= 0) increases, the phase boundary shifts to the...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper provides a theoretical explanation for the unexpected second Hall plateau in the 3D quantum Hall effect of HfTe5. It demonstrates that a magnetic-field-induced Lifshitz transition triggers a spin-density-wave that creates a global gap and a metal-insulator transition. This work highlights how the tunability of 3D Landau bands allows for richer physics than the 2D quantum Hall effect.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to explain the origin of the second Hall plateau and the associated metal-insulator transition observed in the 3D quantum Hall effect of HfTe5.

**Main result.** The authors attribute the second plateau to an insulating ground state driven by a spin-density-wave (SDW) order, which arises from a magnetic-field-driven Lifshitz transition.

**Method.** The study employs mean-field analysis, renormalization-group (RG) analysis, and the Kubo formula to calculate transport properties.

**Model / system.** The system is the 3D weak topological insulator HfTe5 in the ultraquantum limit, modeled by a generic Dirac Hamiltonian describing 3D band dispersion.

**Key observables.** Hall conductivity (sigma_xy), longitudinal resistivity (rho_xx), and the density wave gap (Delta).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic field (B), carrier density (n0), Lifshitz transition field (BL), and quantum limit field (BQ).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes a screened Yukawa potential for electron-phonon interaction and uses a linearization of the Landau bands near Fermi points.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the evolution of Landau bands and the transition between the first and second Hall plateaus; Fig 2 illustrates the evolution of 0th Landau bands with magnetic field; Fig 3 shows the calculated magnitude of the density wave gap.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the experimental puzzle, presents a Dirac model for the 3D bands, identifies the Lifshitz transition and resulting SDW instability via mean-field and RG analysis, calculates transport signatures, and discusses experimental implications for materials like ZrTe5.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The recent discovery of the 3D quantum Hall effect in $\mathrm{HfTe_5}$ has also revealed puzzling signatures of possible 3D fractionalization. Beyond the first plateau associated with the lowest Landau band, Hall conductivity exhibits a second plateau with a value of about $3/5$ of the first, accompanied by a suppressed longitudinal resistivity. Here, we attribute this second plateau to an insulating ground state arising from spin-density-wave order. We show that a magnetic-field-driven Lifshitz transition causes the spin-down holelike zeroth Landau band to cross the Fermi energy and that the resulting nesting between the lowest spin-up and spin-down Landau bands induces a spin-density wave. We calculate the Hall and longitudinal resistivity and reproduce the experimental behaviors. Our renormalization-group analysis further supports this insulating ground state. Our work reveals that the tunability of Landau bands along the magnetic-field direction endows the 3D quantum Hall effect with a broader phenomenology than its 2D counterpart and merits further exploration.

</details>

**Authors:** Daniel Báscones, Arshpreet Singh Maan, Valentin Savin, Francisco Garcia-Herrero  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01035v1>

**Summary.** This paper presents a new FPGA-based hardware architecture designed for the real-time decoding of quantum LDPC codes. By utilizing the GARI method to handle correlated errors, the design significantly reduces hardware resource consumption and enables the deployment of multiple decoder cores on a single FPGA, paving the way for scalable, energy-efficient quantum error correction.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Achieving low-latency, scalable, and resource-efficient real-time decoding for Quantum LDPC codes, specifically addressing the hardware bottlenecks caused by correlated errors and increasing code distance.

**Main result.** The authors developed an FPGA architecture that achieves an average decoding latency of 596 ns for a [[144, 12, 12]] code, using six times fewer resources than previous GARI-based proposals and enabling multiple decoder cores on a single device.

**Method.** A scalable FPGA architecture using controlled parallelism and systematic resource reuse, implementing a message-passing (Belief Propagation) decoder based on the GAI (Graph Augmentation and Rewiring for Inference) framework.

**Model / system.** The implementation targets Quantum Low-Density Parity-Check (QLDPC) codes, specifically the [[144, 12, 12]] bivariate bicycle code, under a correlated error model (X, Y, and Z errors).

**Key observables.** Decoding latency, hardware resource utilization (LUTs, REGs, BRAMs), and decoding accuracy under correlated errors.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Code parameters [[144, 12, 12]], clock frequency of approximately 274-300 MHz, and 6-bit to 10-bit LLR quantization.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The routing efficiency assumes a uniform distribution of tags in the crossbar interconnect; the impact of reduced bit-width quantization on error floors is noted as an unknown.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the GARI architecture overview; Figure 2 details the DX, DZ decoder unit; Figure 4 illustrates the crossbar interconnect structure; Figure 5 shows the full decoder mapping; Figures 7-9 illustrate timing diagrams and physical placement on the FPGA.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the decoding problem and GARI framework, describes the proposed hardware architecture (DX, DZ and U, V units), details the interconnect and routing strategy, presents implementation results on Xilinx FPGAs, and discusses scalability and future work.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this work, we introduce a new hardware architecture for decoding correlated errors in quantum LDPC codes. The decoder is based on message passing and exploits the structure of the detector error model obtained through the recently introduced Graph Augmentation and Rewiring for Inference (GARI) method. The proposed architecture enables flexible scaling and can, in principle, adapt to any quantum LDPC codes using the GARI framework. It leverages resource reuse while maintaining a modest degree of parallelism, thereby reducing power consumption and area requirements, while preserving low decoding latency.   As a case study, the architecture was implemented on a VCU19P FPGA as an ensemble of three decoder cores targeting the [[144,12,12]] bivariate bicycle code, achieving an average latency of 596 ns per decoding round. This implementation consumes six times fewer resources than the previous GARI-based proposal, being the first reported implementation of multiple decoder cores for correlated errors on a single FPGA device. This enables better energy-conscious scaling of the quantum error correction layer on the classical side, reducing overall power consumption while meeting real-time constraints without compromising decoding accuracy under correlated errors.

</details>

### [A Theory of Saddle Escape in Deep Nonlinear Networks](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01288v1)

**Authors:** Divit Rawal, Michael R. DeWeese  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01288v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

(b) Same at fixed L = 6 with r layers at bottleneck scale: diamonds track the ε−(r−2) law of Theorem 6. Theoretical prediction and experiment diverge at large ε.</figcaption>

</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5: Three-mode tanh cascade and escape-time decomposition. Black: training loss; blue: mode-1 alignment ∥W1v1∥2/ √</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper provides a theoretical framework for understanding why deep neural networks experience long training plateaus followed by sudden feature acquisition. The authors prove that the time spent in these plateaus is governed by the number of layers at the small-scale 'bottleneck' rather than the total depth of the network. By classifying activation functions into universality classes, they show that the escape dynamics follow a predictable power-law scaling. The theory is robust, applying to both symmetric and asymmetric initializations and extending to multi-mode teacher scenarios.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Understanding the dynamical mechanism behind 'saddle escape' in deep nonlinear networks, specifically how the time required to escape training plateaus scales with network depth and initialization scale.

**Main result.** The discovery of a critical-depth escape law where escape time scales as epsilon^-(r-2), where r is the number of bottleneck layers, rather than the total network depth L.

**Method.** Derivation of an exact identity for weight matrix norm imbalance, reduction of high-dimensional matrix gradient flow to a scalar ODE via a symmetric balanced ansatz, and use of Hermite-moment expansions.

**Model / system.** Deep nonlinear feedforward networks with L layers, smooth activation functions (classified into four universality classes), and Gaussian inputs, trained via gradient flow on a population squared loss.

**Key observables.** Escape time (tau* or t_esc), layer weight Frobenius norms, signal energy (gamma), and layer scale imbalance (Delta_l).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Initialization scale (epsilon), bottleneck depth (r), total network depth (L), and nonlinearity order (q).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Small-scale initialization (epsilon -> 0), gradient flow dynamics, Gaussian input distribution, and the use of a permutation-symmetric submanifold for reduction.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares reduced ODE loss to full-parameter GD; Figure 3 shows universality collapse of different activations; Figure 4 shows log-log plots of escape time vs epsilon confirming the power-law scaling; Figure 5 illustrates multi-mode escape dynamics.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by deriving an exact identity for weight imbalance, introduces a reduction method to a scalar ODE, classifies activation functions, proves the critical-depth escape law for single-mode and multi-mode teachers, and validates the theory via numerical simulations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In deep networks with small initialization, training exhibits long plateaus separated by sharp feature-acquisition transitions. Whereas shallow nonlinear networks and deep linear networks are well studied, extending these analyses to deep nonlinear networks remains challenging. We derive an exact identity for the imbalance of Frobenius norms of layer weight matrices that holds for any smooth activation and any differentiable loss and use this to classify activation functions into four universality classes. On the permutation-symmetric submanifold, the identity combines with an approximate balance law to reduce the full matrix flow to a scalar ODE, giving a critical-depth escape time law $τ_\star = Θ(\varepsilon^{-(r-2)})$ governed by the number $r$ of layers at the bottleneck scale rather than the total depth $L$. We find that this same $r-2$ exponent is recovered under He-normal initialization with $r$ bottleneck layers rescaled by $\varepsilon$, where the symmetry manifold is preserved by the flow but not attracting. We find close agreement between our theory and numerical simulations.

</details>

Stage II (PIDN Surrogate Training) uses these samples to train a neural surrogate F by minimizing a com- posite objective consisting of value-matching loss and gradient-consistency loss, thereby reconstructing the effective ZNE-induced optimiza- tion vector field. The PIDN takes two inputs: the noisy expectation value obtained from the quantum circuit and the historical parameter trajectory encoded via a GRU branch. These representations are fused to produce two outputs: a denoised expectation value approximating the ZNE- corrected cost and a prediction of the next-step parameters. The network is trained using a composite loss function combining supervised...</figcaption>

(a-c) Loss landscapes and optimization trajectories for (a) noiseless VQA, (b) noisy VQA, and (c) ZNE-assisted VQA under depolarizing noise. (d-f) Corresponding results under dephasing noise, arranged in the same order as (a-c). The optimization trajectories are shown as black and colored connected dots, with the blue dots being the starting points and red ones being ending points. **Summary.** This paper introduces a neural network approach to accelerate error-mitigated quantum algorithms by learning to predict denoised optimization trajectories. By replacing expensive multi-noise evaluations with a physics-informed surrogate, it significantly reduces the circuit execution overhead for NISQ-era algorithms.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs) on NISQ hardware are limited by high computational costs from hardware noise and the significant sampling overhead required by error mitigation techniques like Zero-Noise Extrapolation (ZNE).

**Main result.** The proposed Physics-Informed Denoising Network (PIDN) achieves a 4-6x speedup in circuit executions compared to ZNE while maintaining comparable accuracy and gradient fidelity.

**Method.** A neural network surrogate model (PIDN) is trained using a hybrid loss function that combines data-matching with a physics-informed loss to enforce gradient consistency with ZNE-mitigated trajectories.

**Model / system.** The method is benchmarked on QAOA for MaxCut (3-regular graphs), Sherrington-Kirkpatrick, and Transverse-Field Ising models, as well as VQE for LiH, BeH2, and H2O molecules.

**Key observables.** Expectation values, gradient directions, approximation ratio (AR), energy error, and Relative Low-Frequency power ratio (RLF).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Noise levels (depolarizing and dephasing), noise strength (up to 1e-1), and the physics-loss weight (beta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes the optimization dynamics can be captured by learning a trajectory tube and that the loss landscape retains sufficient low-frequency structure.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the three-stage workflow; Figure 2 illustrates the PIDN architecture; Figure 3 visualizes loss landscape deformation; Figure 6 shows energy convergence; Figure 9 shows the correlation between gradient similarity and landscape smoothness.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the noise problem in VQAs, proposes the PIDN framework and its three-stage training/inference workflow, details the physics-informed loss function, presents benchmarks on optimization and chemistry tasks, and concludes with robustness and landscape analysis.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Variational quantum algorithms are promising for near-term quantum computing, but are severely limited by hardware noise and the substantial circuit overhead required for error mitigation methods such as Zero-Noise Extrapolation (ZNE). We propose a Physics-Informed Denoising Network (PIDN) that reduces the cost of ZNE by learning a surrogate model of its optimization dynamics. By viewing the variational update as a trajectory in the parameter space, PIDN is trained to reproduce ZNE-mitigated expectation values and gradient directions while incorporating a physics-informed loss that preserves the gradient descent dynamics. Once trained, PIDN replaces repeated multi-noise evaluations with denoised expectation and gradient estimation directly from the current noisy observation and the historical trajectory, significantly reducing circuit executions. We benchmark the approach on the quantum approximate optimization algorithm for 3-regular graphs, Sherrington-Kirkpatrick, and transverse-field Ising models, as well as the variational quantum eigensolver for LiH, BeH$_2$ and H$_2$O. Across all tasks, PIDN attains performance comparable to ZNE, while reducing the number of circuit executions by a factor of approximately 4 to 6. Gradient cosine similarity with ZNE remains above 0.95 throughout training. Robustness analysis shows that PIDN fails only when ZNE itself becomes unreliable, and ablation studies confirm the necessity of the physics-informed loss for maintaining directional consistency. We further find that PIDN tracks optimization dynamics most accurately when the effective loss landscape retains strong low-frequency structure. These results establish PIDN as a scalable, resource-efficient strategy for noise-resilient variational optimization in the noisy intermediate-scale quantum regime.

</details>

### [ADaPT: Adaptive-window Decoding for Practical fault-Tolerance](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01149v1)

**Authors:** Tina Oberoi, Joshua Viszlai, Frederic T. Chong  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01149v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

A confidence score is computed; if confidence is high we commit correction C and proceed to the next window, otherwise the window is enlarged to d and retried. Concurrently, the observed retry rate robs is tracked to dynamically adjust Qthreshold. (Bottom right) Decoding with a reduced window size (d′ &lt; d). The reaction time with d′</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01149_figures/2605.01149_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2: Decoding time decreases superlinearly with smaller window sizes. The decoding time for toric codes with distance d = 7 using the BP+LSD decoder under depolarizing noise. The y-axis shows the decoding time for a window of size W, normalized by decoding time when W = d. The x-axis shows the window size W. Decoding time decreases superlinearly as W is reduced.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01149_figures/2605.01149_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3: Detector separation statistics in fixed window decoding of the d = 7 toric code. Window decoding with W = d is performed on the toric code under depolarizing noise for different p. Over all shots and windows, we record the maximum nearest-neighbour distance among triggered detectors — measured in space (2D toric Manhattan, top) and in time (|∆t|, bottom). In ≈90% of cases this maximum is ≤d/2, yet a fixed-W decoder incurs the full overhead of W = d in every window.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01149_figures/2605.01149_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 4: Correlation of logical error rate (LER) with cluster- based confidence metric (Q). This is a memory experiment on the toric code under depolarizing noise with physical error rate p = 0.005, decoded using the BP-LSD. Here Q is calculated globally over the whole experiment. The plot shows that the logical error probability increases as Qllr increases, demonstrating the inverse dependence of LER on Q.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01149_figures/2605.01149_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 5: Effect of logical error rate on changing commit size This figure shows that the logical error rate (LER) remains essentially unchanged for commit sizes c ∈{1, 2, 3} when the buffer size is fixed at d −1, for sliding-window decoding of the toric code at distance d = 7.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes ADaPT, an adaptive-window decoding technique that reduces the computational latency of quantum error correction. By dynamically adjusting the decoding window based on decoder confidence, it maintains high accuracy while significantly lowering the decoding time overhead. This is crucial for achieving the real-time processing speeds required for scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computing.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The high computational latency and decoding overhead caused by using fixed-size windows in real-time quantum error correction (QEC) decoding.

**Main result.** The ADaPT technique achieves target logical error rates while reducing average decoding time overhead to 0.4-0.6x of the standard fixed-window approach.

**Method.** An adaptive-window decoding algorithm that uses a confidence metric derived from decoder soft information to expand the window size only when necessary, paired with a dynamic hypertuner to maintain a target retry rate.

**Model / system.** The study evaluates performance on Toric codes and Bivariate Bicycle (BB) QLDPC codes under various noise models, including depolarizing noise and hardware-inspired models for neutral atoms and superconducting qubits.

**Key observables.** Logical Error Rate (LER), decoding time/latency, decoder confidence score (Q), and window retry rate (r_obs).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Code distance (d), window size (W), commit size (C), and physical error rate (p).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes the availability of cluster statistics/soft information from the decoder and that the hypertuner can adjust thresholds based on observed retry rates.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the ADaPT workflow; Figure 2 shows super-linear scaling of decoding time with window size; Figure 3 shows error cluster distances; Figure 4 shows the correlation between confidence and LER; Figure 6 and 8 demonstrate LER and latency performance across different codes and noise models.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of decoding latency, proposes the ADaPT algorithm and confidence metric, describes the dynamic hypertuner control law, presents benchmarking results on Toric and BB codes under various noise models, and concludes with future integration opportunities.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Window decoding, first proposed to reduce decoding complexity for real-time decoding, is an essential component to realize scalable, universal-fault tolerant computation. Prior work has focused on improving throughput through parallelization and reducing reaction time via speculation on window boundaries. However, these methods use a fixed window size d, paying a fixed decoding time overhead for each window. In practice, we find this overhead of a fixed window size unnecessary in many cases due to the sparsity of average-case errors in QEC. Leveraging this insight, in this paper we propose an adaptive window decoding technique based on decoder confidence. This technique reduces the overhead in decoding time thus reducing reaction time without compromising on logical error rates. We benchmark adaptive window decoding across different codes and hardware inspired noise models. Our results show that this adaptive technique reaches the target error rate while maintaining a low decoding time overhead across different codes, and under different noise models.

</details>

### [Axion electron-electron interaction in the RaOH molecule to search for Dark matter](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02775v1)

**Authors:** Mikhail Reiter, Anna Zakharova  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02775v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02775_figures/2605.02775_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Feynman diagram for the axion electron-electron interaction, the dotted line corresponds to the axion</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02775_figures/2605.02775_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: RaOH</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper calculates the sensitivity of the RaOH molecule to axion-mediated electron-electron interactions. By using advanced relativistic electronic structure methods, the authors provide a theoretical basis for using heavy-atom molecules to search for dark matter signatures through parity and time-reversal symmetry violation.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is relevant to AMO physics as it utilizes precision molecular spectroscopy and relativistic molecular modeling to probe fundamental physics and search for new particles.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the suitability of the RaOH molecule for detecting axion-mediated electron-electron interactions as a signature of dark matter.

**Main result.** The study establishes a theoretical framework to calculate the sensitivity parameter (W_ee) for axion-mediated interactions in RaOH, providing comparative values against other molecules like YbOH and BaF.

**Method.** Relativistic ab initio electronic structure calculations using the DIRAC19 package, combining Generalized Relativistic Effective Core Potentials (GRECP) with a one-center restoration technique.

**Model / system.** The system is the triatomic RaOH molecule, modeled as a linear rotator with a heavy radium atom and a rigid OH ligand, subject to P and T-violating axion-mediated potentials.

**Key observables.** The sensitivity parameter (E_ax or W_ee) representing the expectation value of the P, T-odd Hamiltonian, and the energy shift in an external electric field.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Axion mass range of 10^-12 to 10^3 eV; coupling constants g_e,S and g_e,P; molecular angular momentum quantum numbers J, l, and s.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The OH ligand is treated as a rigid rotor; the Yukawa potential is approximated by a 1/r potential; the Born-Oppenheimer and tree approximations are used.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a Feynman diagram of the axion-mediated electron interaction; Figure 2 provides a structural representation of the RaOH molecule.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from the astrophysical context of dark matter to the specific quantum mechanical interaction model, followed by the description of relativistic computational methods and concluding with numerical results and comparisons.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Axions are promising candidates for the role of Dark matter particles. In this paper, the question of the suitability of the RaOH molecule for the experimental detection of electron-electron interactions through the exchange of axions is studied. To take into account an impact of the rotations and vibrations a computation must be performed for a large number of molecular configurations. In this work we study this problem using a combination of the Generalized relativistic effective core potential and One-center restoration technique of the correct four-component spinors.

</details>

**Authors:** Rod Rofougaran, Neil Mehta, Katherine Klymko, Pooja Rao, J. Wayne Mullinax, Sam Stein, Norm M. Tubman, Ermal Rrapaj  
**Type:** both · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02056v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The depth of the multilayer ansatze (3-LUCJ, 6-UpCCGSD, and 7-HVA) were chosen such that the total number of variational parameters is approximately equal to that of UCCSD.</figcaption>

Trials are constructed by truncating the full quantum-prepared state, retaining the largest-magnitude coefficients, and renormalizing the resulting state.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02056_figures/2605.02056_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">

Each point corresponds to a trial wavefunction saved every ten ADAPT-VQE iterations and subsequently used in ph-AFQMC. Horizontal dashed lines indicate the corresponding AFQMC(UCCSD) reference energies.</figcaption>
</figure>

The vertical axis shows energy error in mHa on a logarithmic scale. Dashed lines denote mean-field RHF and UHF errors, crosses indicate VQE(UCCSD) results initialized on RHF and UHF references, and solid circles show the corresponding AFQMC energies using these trials. Encircled data points denote non-variational results for which E −EFCI &lt; 0.</figcaption>
</figure>

**Summary.** The paper benchmarks various quantum-circuit-based trial wavefunctions used as inputs for the phaseless auxiliary-field quantum Monte Carlo method. It demonstrates that adaptive quantum circuits can provide superior accuracy in strongly correlated regimes and reveals that standard variational metrics like energy do not always predict the quality of the resulting Monte Carlo projection.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and molecular physics as it provides a roadmap for using near-term quantum hardware to improve the accuracy of stochastic many-body methods in the presence of strong correlation.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Benchmarking the performance of various quantum-prepared trial wavefunctions (ansatze) within the phaseless auxiliary-field quantum Monte Carlo (ph-AFQMC) framework to minimize phaseless bias.

**Main result.** The study finds that variational energy and fidelity are not always reliable indicators of ph-AFQMC accuracy, and that adaptive ansatze like ADAPT-VQE can outperform fixed-ansatz counterparts in strongly correlated regimes using more compact circuits.

**Method.** A hybrid quantum-classical approach using VQE to optimize quantum circuits (UCCSD, LUCJ, HVA, etc.) and ph-AFQMC to project these states into accurate ground states, implemented via the CUDA-Q platform.

**Model / system.** Linear hydrogen chains (H8 and H10) in an STO-6G basis, subjected to bond stretching to explore transitions from weakly to strongly correlated regimes.

**Key observables.** VQE energy, ph-AFQMC energy, absolute energy error relative to FCI, state fidelity, spin-projected fidelity, and spin contamination (S^2).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of variational parameters, circuit depth (k), bond length (R), and number of spatial orbitals (N).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Use of the phaseless approximation to control the fermion sign problem; use of a Multi-Slater Determinant (MSD) expansion to represent quantum statevectors; use of Trotterization for UCC mapping.

**Figures summary.** Figures show absolute energy errors for H10 across different ansatze, circuit depth scaling, and energy error as a function of ansatz depth (k) for various bond lengths.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the QC-AFQMC framework, details the construction of various quantum ansatze (UCC, LUCJ, HVA, ADAPT), describes the ph-AFQMC implementation on GPUs, presents benchmarking results on hydrogen chains, and concludes with a discussion on optimization and scalability.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The phaseless auxiliary-field quantum Monte Carlo (ph-AFQMC) method is a stochastic imaginary-time projection technique for computing ground-state properties of strongly correlated quantum systems, with accuracy that depends critically on the choice of trial wavefunction. Here, we investigate ph-AFQMC with trial states prepared using parameterized quantum circuits. In this work, we present a comprehensive benchmarking study of quantum trial wavefunctions spanning unitary coupled-cluster, Hamiltonian-informed, Jastrow-inspired, and adaptively constructed ansatze. The benchmarking evaluates accuracy, expressibility, and scalability of these ansatze within the QC-AFQMC framework. We test these ansatze on linear hydrogen chains under bond stretching and find that several ansatz families produce chemically accurate ph-AFQMC energies across the dissociation curve. We have performed simulations using the CUDA-Q quantum development platform on the GPU partition of the Perlmutter supercomputer. When comparing ansatze at similar numbers of variational parameters, we find that different ansatz families yield comparable ph-AFQMC results despite exhibiting substantially different variational energies, optimization costs, and circuit depths. Our results indicate that the variational energy of an ansatz is not always a reliable indicator of its quality for ph-AFQMC and reveal instances of over-parameterization. In the strongly correlated regime, trial wavefunctions obtained from adaptive ansatze, exemplified here by ADAPT-VQE with the UCCSD operator pool, can outperform their fixed-ansatz counterparts (UCCSD) in terms of projected energies while using substantially more compact circuits, providing a flexible route to optimize quantum resources within the ph-AFQMC framework.

</details>

**Authors:** Akshat Mishra, Hugo U. R. Strand, Erik G. C. P. van Loon  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02717v1>

**Summary.** This paper provides a rigorous benchmark of the Dual Fermion method against exact Monte Carlo simulations using the Falicov-Kimball model. It demonstrates that while DF captures non-local correlations and momentum-dependent physics much better than DMFT, it still faces challenges in certain doped regimes. This work is crucial for validating diagrammatic extensions used to study real materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to rigorously benchmark the Dual Fermion (DF) approach, specifically the ladder approximation, against exact Monte Carlo results to understand its ability to capture non-local correlations and momentum-dependent self-energy.

**Main result.** The Dual Fermion approach significantly outperforms DMFT in capturing the momentum structure of the Green's function and susceptibility, though it unexpectedly shows less accuracy in describing the relationship between orbital density and chemical potential in doped systems.

**Method.** The authors compare the ladder Dual Fermion and Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) approximations against a numerically exact classical Monte Carlo solution for the Falicov-Kimball model.

**Model / system.** The Falicov-Kimball model, which describes a lattice of itinerant c-electrons and immobile, classical f-electrons interacting via an onsite Coulomb potential U.

**Key observables.** Single-particle Green's function, self-energy, electron densities (nc, nf), dynamical susceptibility (chi_cc), and compressibility.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Interaction strength (U), temperature (beta), chemical potential (mu), and lattice size (16x16).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Dual Fermion method uses a ladder approximation and assumes the bosonic Matsubara frequency contribution is dominated by the zero frequency; the study focuses on the paramagnetic regime and excludes the ordered phase.

**Figures summary.** Phase diagrams (T-U), comparisons of Green's functions and self-energy across DMFT, DF, and MC, momentum-resolved spectral data, and real-space/momentum-space susceptibility plots.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the Falicov-Kimball model and the need for benchmarking, details the DF and DMFT methodologies, presents results for single-particle and two-particle observables, and concludes with an analysis of convergence and finite-size effects.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Strong electronic correlations generally require non-perturbative treatment. Local correlations are captured by dynamical mean-field theory while nonlocal correlations can be treated with diagrammatic extensions such as the Dual Fermion approach. Dual Fermion is built on physically motivated, but in principle uncontrolled approximations, so careful benchmarking is needed to understand the strengths and limitations of the method. In this work, we benchmark ladder Dual Fermion and dynamical mean-field theory for the Falicov-Kimball model with the exact classical Monte Carlo solution. We focus on the thermodynamics, electronic structure and susceptibility, especially at the combined frequency and momentum structure, and find that Dual Fermion clearly outperforms dynamical mean-field theory. Somewhat surprisingly, Dual Fermion is not as accurate for the relation between orbital density versus chemical potential in the doped system. These results demonstrate the need for rigorous benchmarking of diagrammatic extensions of dynamical mean-field theory for models with inequivalent orbitals, which is essential for modelling materials.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper provides a complete mathematical classification of the energy spectrum for a 1D Dirac particle interacting with two symmetric point-like barriers. It details how different types of relativistic interactions, such as scalar and electrostatic, lead to distinct phenomena like bound state absorption and the Klein effect.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the existence and properties of bound states, resonances, critical, and supercritical states in a 1D relativistic system with two symmetric point interactions.

**Main result.** The authors classify the energy spectrum for various parity-even and parity-odd arrangements, finding that scalar interactions admit bound states and resonances, while electrostatic interactions are non-confining and pseudoscalar interactions only admit resonances.

**Method.** The paper uses a distributional approach and self-adjoint extensions to define contact interactions, employing transfer matrices and S-matrix pole analysis to determine the energy spectrum.

**Model / system.** A one-dimensional Dirac equation for a particle of mass m, featuring two singular (contact) interactions located at x = +/- l/2, characterized by scalar, electrostatic, vector, and pseudoscalar parameters.

**Key observables.** Bound state energies, scattering resonances (S-matrix poles), transmission and reflection amplitudes, and critical/supercritical energy thresholds.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Interaction strengths (B, A0, A1, W), barrier separation (l), particle mass (m), and the parity (even/odd) of the arrangement.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is restricted to one dimension and the interactions are assumed to be supported exactly at two symmetric points.

**Figures summary.** Plots showing the trajectory of resonances in the complex energy plane, the relationship between interaction strength and bound-state energies, and the movement of energies as parameters vary.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes a mathematical framework for two-point relativistic interactions, analyzes parity-symmetric arrangements, investigates specific interaction types (scalar, electrostatic, etc.), and examines the limits of vanishing separation and non-relativistic behavior.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We consider the one-dimensional Dirac equation with the most general relativistic contact interaction supported on two points symmetrically located with respect to the origin. In order to determine the shape of the interaction, we use a distributional method, which in the present case is equivalent to the standard method of defining contact interactions by self-adjoint extensions of symmetric operators. The interaction on each of these two points depends on four parameters, each one having a clear physical meaning. We are interested in the scattering and confining properties of this model. We focus our attention on even or odd interactions under parity transformations and investigate the existence of critical and supercritical states, bound states, confinement and scattering resonances for some particular interactions of special interest.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.01629"></a>
### [Brain criticality through nonadditive entropic analysis of electroencephalograms](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01629v1)

**Authors:** Henrique Santos Lima, Constantino Tsallis, Dimitri M. Abramov  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01629v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable

**Summary.** This paper applies nonadditive statistical mechanics to analyze EEG data from children. It demonstrates that brain activity follows q-Gaussian distributions and that ADHD is characterized by higher levels of complexity, potentially providing a path toward objective diagnostic tools.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the complexity and critical behavior of brain activity in typical versus ADHD children using nonadditive entropic frameworks.

**Main result.** The study finds that EEG amplitudes follow q-Gaussian distributions and that ADHD subjects exhibit higher complexity than typical subjects, suggesting potential biomarkers for psychiatric diagnosis.

**Method.** Nonadditive entropic analysis of electroencephalogram (EEG) amplitudes using q-Gaussian probability distributions.

**Model / system.** The system consists of brain activity as measured by EEG amplitudes in typical and ADHD children, modeled through nonadditive statistics.

**Key observables.** EEG amplitudes, entropic index (q), and inverse width (beta).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The entropic index q, which characterizes complexity, and the parameter beta.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The brain activity can be appropriately described by nonadditive entropies and q-Gaussian distributions.

**Paper structure.** The paper presents an investigation of EEG amplitudes using nonadditive entropy, compares typical and ADHD subjects, and discusses the implications for psychiatric biomarkers.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

On the grounds of nonadditive entropies -- appropriate for complex systems -- we investigate the electroencephalogram amplitudes of typical and ADHD children. The corresponding probability distributions are $q$-Gaussians, i.e., $ρ(x) \propto e_q^{-βx^2} \equiv [1+(q-1) βx^2]^{1/(1-q)}$, where $(q,β)$ are, respectively, the entropic index characterizing complexity and the inverse width. We show that $q$ tends to monotonically vary with $β$ for both typical and ADHD subjects, thus revealing critical behavior of the brain. Moreover, we verify that ADHD subjects have a higher complexity than the typical ones. Consistently, biomarkers for objective phychyatric diagnosis could emerge along this path. We show that $q$ tends to monotonically vary with $β$ for both typical and ADHD subjects, thus revealing critical behavior of the brain. Moreover, we verify that ADHD subjects have a higher complexity than the typical ones. Consistently, biomarkers for objective phychyatric diagnosis could emerge along this path.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper uses neutron diffraction to prove that the kagome magnet Nd3Ru4Al12 has a collinear hexagonal ferromagnetic ground state rather than a previously proposed orthorhombic one. This finding provides a microscopic basis for the large Hall and Nernst responses observed near the transition temperature. The result resolves a long-standing discrepancy in the magnetic modeling of this material.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Resolving the discrepancy regarding the magnetic ground state of the kagome lattice magnet Nd3Ru4Al12, specifically whether it follows a previously reported orthorhombic 'ortho-FM' state or a simpler hexagonal state.

**Main result.** The study identifies the ground state as a collinear hexagonal ferromagnetic (hex-FM) state with uniform moment length, effectively refuting the existence of the previously proposed orthorhombic state.

**Method.** Single-crystal neutron diffraction (unpolarized and polarized) was used to analyze magnetic structure factors and flipping ratios, supplemented by DFT calculations.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the kagome lattice magnet Nd3Ru4Al12, characterized by a staggered Nd sublattice and a hexagonal ferromagnetic (hex-FM) magnetic space group.

**Key observables.** Magnetic structure factors, magnetic intensity at (100) and (200) reflections, flipping ratio (P_obs), and ordered magnetic moment (mc).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Critical temperature Tc approx 41 K, magnetic moment mc approx 2.1-2.3 uB/Nd, and ordering vector Q = 0.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the validity of the nuclear structure refinement to isolate the magnetic signal.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Kagome lattice and fluctuation mechanism; Fig 2: Unpolarized neutron scattering intensities; Fig 3: Comparison of observed vs calculated structure factors; Fig 4: Comparison of different magnetic models (hex-FM, AOAO, ortho-FM); Fig 5: Polarized neutron scattering geometry and flipping ratio results.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scientific conflict, describes the experimental neutron diffraction setup, presents the structural refinement and symmetry analysis, compares competing magnetic models, and concludes with the implications for understanding thermal spin fluctuations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We determine the magnetic ground state of the kagome lattice magnet Nd3Ru4Al12 by single-crystal neutron diffraction, supported by experiments with polarized neutrons. We identify this material as a collinear ferromagnet ("hex-FM") with uniform moment length mc = 2.1 μB/Nd and ordering vector Q = 0, in contrast to a previous, seminal report that proposed unequal moment lengths on two Nd sites, here called the "ortho-FM" state. Our analysis of the flipping ratio in polarized neutron scattering is consistent with the hex-FM state. The results provide a microscopic basis for understanding the large fluctuation-induced Hall and Nernst responses near TC = 41 K, as previously reported for Nd3Ru4Al12.

</details>

**Authors:** Akshaya Kumar Jena, Aashima Aashima, Pritam Kumar Jana, Bortolo Matteo Mognetti

**Summary.** The paper presents a method to control the thickness and composition of colloidal layers by engineering the kinetics of DNA strand-displacement reactions. By using reaction kinetics to suppress unwanted equilibrium interactions, the researchers achieved a system that assembles into predictable, alternating layers. This provides a new way to program the morphology of complex soft-matter aggregates.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Controlling the morphology, thickness, and compositional order of DNA-mediated colloidal aggregates by overcoming kinetic limitations and undesired equilibrium interactions.

**Main result.** Demonstrated that engineering reaction kinetics via toehold-mediated strand displacement can act as a digital filter to suppress undesired interactions, enabling the assembly of multi-layered crystals with alternating particle types.

**Method.** A hybrid simulation approach combining Brownian dynamics for particle positions and the Gillespie algorithm for reaction-diffusion dynamics, validated against theoretical free-energy calculations.

**Model / system.** A binary suspension of self-protected colloids (Types A and B) and a functionalized surface, where DNA oligomers facilitate reversible formation of two-strand and three-strand complexes.

**Key observables.** Number of layers in the aggregate, compositional order, number of DNA complexes (loops, bridges), and effective particle-surface/particle-particle interaction potentials.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of ligands per particle (N_L), chemical potential (mu), reaction timescale (tau_0), and hybridization free energies (delta G).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Neglects non-specific ligand-ligand interactions, assumes ligands are thin rods, and assumes lateral diffusion of tethered linkers is much faster than the self-assembly timescale.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the binary system and DNA design; Figure 2 compares kinetic vs. equilibrium interaction potentials; Figure 3 shows steady-state layer configurations and scaling with N_L and density; Figure 4 tracks complex dynamics over time.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of kinetic limitations in DNA-assembly, presents a design for controlled layer deposition, derives the mathematical forces and free energy of the system, and uses reaction-diffusion simulations to validate the kinetic control mechanism.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We design a system with a binary suspension of colloids and a surface that triggers the self-assembly of crystallites with a finite thickness. The proposed design allows controlling the number of layers forming the aggregate and constrains the two types of particles to lie on different planes. These functionalities are achieved by decorating the colloids and the surface with multiple DNA oligomers featuring specific interactions. The surface triggers a chain of reactions between DNA oligomers, leading to localized self-assembly. Equilibrium principles control the thickness of the aggregates. Instead, compositional order is achieved by engineering the reaction kinetics between DNA oligomers in a way that limits interactions between colloids of the same type. We validate our design using theory and reaction-diffusion simulation algorithms, which capture the multibody nature of the interactions. This work demonstrates how engineering the kinetics provides a new avenue for controlling the morphology of aggregates assembled by DNA.

</details>

### [Combinatorial Analysis of Dyadic and Quasi-Dyadic Codes](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01942v1)

**Authors:** Anthony Gómez-Fonseca, Gretchen L. Matthews, Kirsten D. Morris, Tefjol Pllaha  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01942v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Subfigure (a): Two dyadic matrices with signature weight 4 and ℓ= 5. Subfigure (b): Construction 3.11 with ℓ= 3 and v = 4 using only the first three rows. Subfigure (c): Comparison of Construction 3.10 versus Construction 3.11.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper provides a mathematical toolkit for constructing and optimizing QLDPC codes by using the algebraic properties of dyadic matrices to minimize structural defects like short cycles. By controlling these cycles, the authors show that decoding performance can be substantially improved even when the girth cannot be increased.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Improving the performance of Quantum Low-Density Parity-Check (QLDPC) codes under iterative decoding by controlling short-cycle structures and harmful subgraphs like absorbing sets.

**Main result.** The authors developed an algebraic framework using dyadic and quasi-dyadic matrices to efficiently enumerate and control 4-, 6-, and 8-cycles, demonstrating that reducing cycle multiplicity can significantly improve decoding gains.

**Method.** The paper utilizes an algebraic framework of dyadic matrices (forming a commutative ring) and TBC (Tanner-Based Code) walk analysis to relate cycles in lifted Tanner graphs to walks in a protograph, alongside PEG-style construction algorithms.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on the CSS (Calderable-Shor-Steane) framework for quantum error correction, using dyadic and quasi-dyadic matrices as the basis for constructing parity-check matrices $H_X$ and $H_Z$ that satisfy the commutation constraint.

**Key observables.** Girth, short-cycle multiplicity (4-, 6-, and 8-cycles), permutation shift, and absorbing sets.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Lifting factor $N=2^\ell$, signature weight $\omega$, and the girth of the protograph.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the order of the permutation shift $m$ is 1 and that the girth of the lifted graph is bounded by the properties of the underlying protograph.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare random versus optimized dyadic matrices to show decoding gains from 4-cycle reduction, and illustrate performance comparisons between different construction strategies and girth levels.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces an algebraic framework for dyadic matrices, develops methods for cycle enumeration and construction algorithms (PEG-style), analyzes absorbing sets, and concludes with performance simulations and 6/8-cycle counting theorems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum low-density parity-check (QLDPC) codes offer a promising route to scalable fault-tolerant quantum computation, but their performance under iterative decoding is strongly influenced by short-cycle structure and other harmful subgraphs in the associated Tanner graphs. This paper develops an algebraic framework for constructing and analyzing (Q)LDPC codes from dyadic and quasi-dyadic matrices-translation-invariant $2^\ell \times 2^\ell$ binary matrices specified compactly by a signature row and forming a commutative ring with recursive block structure. Leveraging this structure, we relate cycles in lifted Tanner graphs to tailless backtrackless closed walks in the protograph and derive efficient, implementable methods to enumerate and control short cycles (notably $4$-, $6$-, and $8$-cycles). We introduce dyadic-aware PEG-style construction algorithms that use forbidden sets of shifts to maximize attainable girth when possible and otherwise minimize the multiplicity of the shortest cycles at the target girth. Motivated by error-floor phenomena, we further characterize and explicitly enumerate absorbing sets in key dyadic layout boundary cases, identifying configurations that induce abundant $(a,0)$-absorbing sets. Finally, we propose CSS-oriented dyadic constructions that satisfy commutation constraints by design and demonstrate via belief-propagation simulations that reducing short-cycle multiplicity can yield substantial decoding gains even when girth cannot be increased.

</details>

### [Comment on `On computing quantum waves exactly from classical action'](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02621v1)

**Authors:** Gabor Vattay  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02621v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figure class="paper-figure-card">

</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02621_figures/2605.02621_page3.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Low-resolution page preview, page 3</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02621_figures/2605.02621_page4.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Low-resolution page preview, page 4</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper is a formal rebuttal to a claim of an exact equivalence between classical and quantum dynamics. The author proves that the proposed method is actually a semiclassical approximation because it fails to account for the quantum potential term.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper critiques a claim that the Schrödinger equation can be solved exactly using only classical action and density, arguing that the proposed method is actually a semiclassical approximation.

**Main result.** The author demonstrates that the original derivation neglects the spatial derivatives of the probability density, which omits the essential quantum potential term.

**Method.** The author uses mathematical derivation via the Madelung hydrodynamic formulation, applying the Laplacian to the wave function ansatz and comparing the resulting real and imaginary components to the standard Schrödinger equation.

**Model / system.** The discussion focuses on the time-dependent Schrödinger equation for a scalar potential V(x) within the Madelung hydrodynamic framework.

**Key observables.** Quantum potential (Q), probability density (rho), and classical action (phi).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The semiclassical (WKB) limit where the quantum potential is neglected.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The critique targets the assumption in the original paper that the spatial variation of the density amplitude is negligible.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by identifying the error in the cited work, provides a mathematical derivation showing the omission of the quantum potential, and then analyzes specific examples (particle in a box, double-slit, etc.) to show how the error was masked.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

A recent article by Lohmiller \& Slotine (Proc.\ R.\ Soc.\ A \textbf{482}: 20250413) claims that the Schrödinger equation can be solved exactly using only classical least action and classical fluid density, asserting that this formulation avoids semiclassical approximations. We show that their mathematical derivation contains a foundational error. By neglecting the spatial derivatives of the probability density amplitude, the authors inadvertently omit the quantum potential -- the term originally identified by Madelung and later emphasised by Bohm. Consequently, their proposed equivalence is not exact but rather constitutes the standard semiclassical approximation. We further demonstrate that each of the paper's illustrative examples either belongs to a class where the quantum potential vanishes identically due to the geometry of the problem, or recovers the correct quantum result by importing quantum eigenfunctions through the initial conditions, thereby concealing the error.

</details>

**Authors:** Abhishek Awasthi, Maximilian Hess, Salome Lomadze, Francesco Bär, Christian Biefel  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02465v1>

(20). The plot shows results for δt ∈{0.01, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3}, using standard X-mixers and the XY -mixers with full connectivity. In each plot the x-axis shows the number of Trotter steps used in the respective experiment and the y-axis shows the probability of measuring the optimal solution which was determined classically beforehand.</figcaption>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the success of using constraint-preserving XY-mixers in quantum adiabatic evolution depends heavily on whether the constraints are global or local. It shows that while global constraints cause high Trotter errors that negate the benefits of XY-mixers, local constraints allow these mixers to significantly outperform standard methods.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how Trotterization errors in XY-mixers affect the performance of Trotterized Adiabatic Evolution (TAE) for combinatorial optimization problems with equality constraints.

**Main result.** The effectiveness of XY-mixers depends on constraint locality: global constraints lead to significant Trotter errors that favor standard X-mixers, whereas local/disjoint constraints allow XY-mixers to outperform X-mixers by several orders of magnitude.

**Method.** Theoretical analysis of Trotter error scaling combined with numerical statevector simulations using Qiskit on various optimization problems (Portfolio, Paint Shop, Multi-Commodility Flow).

**Model / system.** Quantum optimization frameworks using Trotterized Adiabatic Evolution (TAE) with XY-mixers (which preserve Hamming weight) and standard Pauli-X mixers, applied to Ising Hamiltonians encoding $k$-hot constraints.

**Key observables.** Probability of measuring/sampling the optimal solution.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Trotter step size ($\delta t$), Trotter depth ($p$), constraint locality (global vs. local), and Hamming weight ($k$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes linear, unweighted equality constraints and uses a first-order Suzuki-Trotter expansion for the implementation.

**Figures summary.** Figures compare success probabilities of XY vs. X mixers across different problem types (Portfolio, MCPS, MCFP) and varying Trotter step sizes and depths, highlighting the performance degradation in global vs. local constraint regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of constraint handling, provides a theoretical analysis of Trotter error scaling in XY-mixers, presents numerical validation using three distinct optimization problems, and concludes with a discussion on constraint locality as a design principle.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Constraint handling is a central challenge for quantum algorithms applied to combinatorial optimization. Standard penalty-based approaches increase problem size, distort energy landscapes, and often degrade performance. Constraint-preserving mixers, such as XY-mixers, restrict quantum evolution to feasible subspaces, but their implementation on gate-based hardware requires Trotterization, which introduces approximation errors. In this work, we systematically investigate the interplay between constraint-preserving XY-mixers and Trotterized Adiabatic Evolution (TAE). We present a theoretical analyses of the origin and scaling of Trotter errors in XY-mixers and show that the dominant contribution depends on the size and structure of individual constraints rather than on the total problem size. Our findings are validated through extensive numerical simulations on three representative problems: Portfolio Optimization, the Multi-Car Paint Shop problem, and a Multi-Commodity Flow problem. For problems with a single global equality constraint spanning all variables, Trotter errors significantly impair XY-mixer performance, making standard Pauli-X mixers more robust under realistic implementations. In contrast, for problems whose constraints decompose into multiple disjoint local blocks, XY-mixers outperform X-mixers by several orders of magnitude even under Trotterized evolution. These results identify constraint locality as the key criterion for the effective use of XY-mixers and demonstrate that TAE combined with structure-aware mixer design provides a robust and theoretically grounded alternative to variational quantum optimization methods. We further present a dedicated mixer Hamiltonian for TSP-like 2-way-1-hot constraints.

</details>
