Within each module, local two-qubit gates can be applied between all qubits according to the available connectivity (physical coupling edges).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02389_figures/2605.02389_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2: DAG of a quantum circuit encoding precedence con- straints of quantum operations. Operations in the same layer can be executed in parallel.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02389_figures/2605.02389_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3: Panel (a) shows the qubit mapping: Annotated values {0, . . . , n −1} denote physical qubit indices, while the value inside a node indicates the virtual qubit mapped onto the physical qubit; −1 implies a non-initialized physical qubit. In (b), the generate action creates an EPR pair on a pair of non- initialized remote communication qubits. Panel (c) visualizes a tele-gate execution and (d) visualizes a tele-qubit action.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02389_figures/2605.02389_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 1. The edge set En denotes the coupling connections in a module enabling local operations. We assume that all local operations take some constant duration to execute, which we denote tlocal. We note that remote entanglement generation over a quantum channel is inherently stochastic: each attempt succeeds with probability pgen &lt; 1 and otherwise must be repeated. We adopt a standard abstraction [14], [24], [28] and treat entanglement generation as deterministic with a fixed latency tgen, approximating the mean waiting time of a repeat-until-success protocol, tgen := t0/pgen, where t0 is the duration of a single heralded entanglement generation attempt.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02389_figures/2605.02389_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 4: Example of a state vector.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents an improved reinforcement learning framework for compiling quantum circuits in distributed architectures. By re-engineering the RL action space to use routing chains rather than single edges, the authors significantly speed up both the training process and the resulting circuit execution time. This is a crucial step toward managing the complexity of scaling quantum computers via modular interconnects.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Optimizing quantum circuit routing and compilation in distributed quantum computing (DQC) architectures to minimize total execution time.

**Main result.** The proposed RL agent using action-space engineering achieves up to a 35% reduction in modeled execution time and a 64% reduction in training wall-clock time compared to existing baselines.

**Method.** A Reinforcement Learning approach using Deep Q-Networks (DQN) with a novel action-space formulation (routing via precomputed shortest paths), action-masking strategies, and a low-dimensional Q-value approximation.

**Model / system.** A distributed quantum computing architecture consisting of multiple interconnected modules (QPUs) with local intra-module connectivity and remote inter-module links for EPR pair generation and tele-operations.

**Key observables.** Total circuit execution time (measured in timesteps), cumulative reward, and training wall-clock time.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Entanglement generation probability (p_gen = 0.95), gate execution latencies (SWAP vs. remote operations), and circuit size (number of CNOT gates).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes classical channels are available for heralding, neglects single-qubit gate routing, and approximates stochastic entanglement generation as a deterministic process with fixed latency.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows remote entanglement generation; Figure 2 depicts a circuit DAG; Figure 3 visualizes qubit mapping and remote actions (tele-qubit, tele-gate); Figure 4 shows state vector encoding; Figures 5-6 show hardware topologies; Figures 11-12 show execution time distributions via boxplots.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the DQC challenge, describes the hardware and circuit models, details the RL agent's novel action-space and masking innovations, presents numerical comparisons against a baseline, and discusses scalability and training dynamics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

As it becomes increasingly difficult to monolithically scale a quantum processor, distributed quantum computing (DQC) offers an alternative by distributing qubits across multiple smaller interconnected quantum processor modules. In such an architecture, the challenge of quantum circuit compilation shifts from placing and routing qubits within one module to placing, routing and using the qubits efficiently across modules. In order to optimize circuit execution time, the right state-dependent networking decisions must be found, such as when and where to generate shared remote quantum states to support remote operations.   Reinforcement learning (RL) provides a natural framework for this problem, generating a compilation policy that can generalize across different circuits. Building on the framework of Promponas et al. (2024), we introduce an agent that combines a novel action-space formulation with effective action-masking strategies. A comprehensive numerical comparison of the two approaches under different coupling constraints shows that our agent achieves improved training and inference performance with a relative reduction in the modeled execution time of up to 35\%.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.02588"></a>
### [S-CAD: Selective Classical Advantage Distillation for Quantum Conference Key Agreement](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02588v1)

**Authors:** Trevor Thomas, Walter O. Krawec, Bing Wang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02588v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable

**Summary.** This paper presents a new protocol called S-CAD for Quantum Conference Key Agreement that allows users to selectively use classical advantage distillation. It provides a new security proof against coherent attacks that is more efficient than previous work and demonstrates through simulations when the protocol is most effective.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the optimal use of Classical Advantage Distillation (CAD) in Quantum Conference Key Agreement (QCKA) and establishing whether previous performance bounds were asymptotically tight.

**Main result.** The authors introduce S-CAD, a new protocol that allows for selective activation of CAD, and provide an asymptotic proof of security against general coherent attacks that outperforms previous methods.

**Method.** The authors design a new protocol (S-CAD) that generalizes existing QCKA+CAD methods and use asymptotic security proofs and simulations of star network topologies to evaluate performance.

**Model / system.** The protocol utilizes GHZ states to establish shared group keys among multiple parties in a star network topology.

**Key observables.** Secret key rate and security against coherent attacks.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The decision to enable or disable CAD and the network topology (star network).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The security proof is derived against general coherent attacks.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the S-CAD protocol, provides an asymptotic security proof, and concludes with a numerical evaluation of various star network topologies.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum conference key agreement (QCKA) protocols utilize GHZ states to establish shared group keys between multiple parties. While previous work has shown that standard Classical Advantage Distillation (CAD) protocols can sometimes benefit QCKA performance, it was unknown if past results were asymptotically tight. In this work, we design a new CAD protocol, "Selective Classical Advantage Distillation (S-CAD)", for QCKA, which generalizes prior QCKA+CAD work and allows the parties to selectively enable or disable CAD. We derive an asymptotic proof of security against general coherent attacks, which outperforms prior work. Finally, we evaluate in a variety of simulated star network topologies, showing when S-CAD can help, and when it is best to disable CAD entirely.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.01008"></a>
### [Semantics-Based Verification of an Implemented Shor Oracle for ECDLP in Qrisp](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01008v1)

**Authors:** Lei Zhang, Zhiyuan Chen  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01008v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01008_figures/2605.01008_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. Oracle composition in the implementation. The accumulator R is updated by two scalar-multiplication-and-add routines, controlled bitwise by x1 and x2, yielding R ⊕x1G ⊕x2P. Here ⊕denotes the elliptic-curve group operation under the imple- mentation’s point encoding, not bitwise XOR.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a method for verifying that quantum software implementations of ECDLP oracles correctly match their mathematical specifications. It demonstrates that even when individual components work correctly, the compilation process in frameworks like Qrisp can introduce errors in controlled operations. This highlights the need for semantic auditing in the development of trustworthy quantum algorithms.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The discrepancy between abstract mathematical oracles for the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP) and their actual implementation in quantum programming frameworks like Qrisp, which can lead to invalid algorithmic conclusions.

**Main result.** The paper establishes a semantics-first verification framework and identifies a specific case where a compiled controlled-gate implementation fails to satisfy the expected control law, despite passing standalone tests.

**Method.** The authors use a refinement-style verification approach, defining correctness obligations across multiple layers of implementation and using a well-formedness predicate to handle exceptional inputs.

**Model / system.** The study uses the Qrisp quantum programming framework to implement a Shor-style oracle for ECDLP over a prime field Fp, specifically utilizing Short Weierstrass curves.

**Key observables.** Circuit complexity metrics including qubit count, circuit depth, T-count, and CX-count, as well as the verification of the control law and point-update accuracy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The field size parameter m = ceil(log2 p) and the specific small-scale instance parameters p=5, a=3, b=3.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The complexity analysis assumes that underlying modular arithmetic and inversion primitives compile to uniform polynomial-size reversible circuits.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the composition of the oracle, showing how the accumulator register is updated via sequential bitwise-controlled updates.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the semantic gap problem, proposes a refinement-based verification framework, provides a complexity argument for the oracle family, and concludes with a case study demonstrating a control-law violation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Shor-style quantum algorithms for the elliptic-curve discrete logarithm problem (ECDLP) are highly sensitive to the exact semantics of their group-operation oracles. Consequently, minor implementation choices can invalidate the intended mathematical model and lead to misleading conclusions. This paper introduces a semantics-first verification perspective for an end-to-end, compilable ECDLP implementation built on Qrisp. We specify the implemented oracle at the level of program semantics, derive refinement-style verification obligations for its key components, and provide a high-level complexity argument for the resulting oracle family. A small case study highlights that (i) the core point-update primitive agrees with a classical reference on well-formed inputs, yet (ii) controlled execution may violate the expected control law under the evaluated toolchain, despite a passing trivial control sanity check. These results position semantic auditing as a practical prerequisite for trustworthy ECDLP-oriented quantum software.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.01383"></a>
### [Sequential Learning and Catastrophic Forgetting in Differentiable Resistor Networks](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01383v1)

**Authors:** Maniru Ibrahim  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01383v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01383_figures/2605.01383_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 2: Gradient cosine similarity as a function of the task-similarity parameter α, evaluated at different checkpoints along Task A training. The endpoints are robust: α = 1 gives aligned gradients, whereas α = 0 gives antiparallel gradients. Intermediate values show larger variability, consistent with near-compromise tasks and smaller gradient norms.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01383_figures/2605.01383_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 1: Sequential training behaviour in the resistor network for a representative run. The model is first trained on Task A and then on Task B. While both tasks can be learned individually, Task A performance deteriorates substantially after Task B training, demonstrating catastrophic forgetting.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01383_figures/2605.01383_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3: Forgetting as a function of the task-similarity parameter α. Forgetting increases as the second task changes from identical to Task A (α = 1) toward the fully reversed task (α = 0).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01383_figures/2605.01383_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4: Forgetting as a function of the Task-B target contrast cB = y1 −y2 for a random ensemble of second tasks. Positive contrast corresponds to the same output ordering as Task A, near-zero contrast corresponds to compromise tasks, and negative contrast corresponds to reversed or contradictory tasks. Forgetting increases strongly as the target contrast becomes negative, showing that contradictory tasks are those that reverse the output ordering imposed by the first task.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01383_figures/2605.01383_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5: Forgetting–adaptation trade-off for uniform and gradient-weighted anchoring. Each point shows the mean over 20 random graph realisations, with error bars denoting one standard deviation. Increasing the regularisation strength reduces forgetting but increases the final Task B loss. Gradient-weighted anchoring produces stronger protection against forgetting at comparable λ, but also more strongly suppresses adaptation to Task B.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper studies how learning new tasks causes the erasure of previously learned behaviors in electrical resistor networks. It reveals that forgetting is a physical reconfiguration of the network's primary current pathways and demonstrates a fundamental trade-off between retaining old knowledge and adapting to new inputs.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the mechanisms of catastrophic forgetting and the stability-plasticity trade-off in differentiable physical networks during sequential learning tasks.

**Main result.** Forgetting is identified as a localized reconfiguration of high-current transport pathways and is strongly driven by task conflict; regularization strategies like anchoring can reduce forgetting but at the cost of reduced adaptation to new tasks.

**Method.** Gradient-based optimization of edge conductances using automatic differentiation, combined with regularization techniques such as uniform and gradient-weighted anchoring.

**Model / system.** Differentiable resistor networks governed by Kirchhoff's laws, where edge conductances are trainable parameters in various graph topologies (Erdos-Renyi, small-world, scale-free, and random-geometric).

**Key observables.** Forgetting (loss increase on Task A), Task B loss (adaptation), gradient cosine similarity, task target contrast, and conductance update magnitudes.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Regularization strength (lambda), training budget (number of gradient steps), network size (N), and graph topology.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The network is assumed to reach physical equilibrium; conductances are constrained to be positive via softplus transformation; the study uses a minimal benchmark setup.

**Figures summary.** Figures visualize the forgetting-adaptation trade-off, the impact of shortest-path distance, and the localization of conductance updates on high-current edges.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the differentiable resistor network framework, defines the sequential learning protocol and regularization methods, presents results on task conflict and physical mechanisms of forgetting, analyzes the impact of network topology, and concludes with implications for tunable matter.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Differentiable physical networks provide a simple setting in which learning can be studied through the interaction between trainable parameters and physical equilibrium constraints. We investigate sequential learning in differentiable resistor networks governed by Kirchhoff's laws. Although individual input--output mappings can be learned by gradient-based adjustment of edge conductances, sequential training on conflicting tasks produces catastrophic forgetting. We show that forgetting is controlled by task conflict and by the degree of adaptation to the new task. Uniform anchoring and normalised gradient-weighted anchoring reduce forgetting only by increasing the final loss on the new task, giving a clear forgetting--adaptation trade-off. We also show that forgetting is associated with localised conductance changes on high-current edges, giving a physical interpretation as reconfiguration of dominant transport pathways. Broader random-task ensembles show that the strongest forgetting occurs when the second task reverses the output ordering imposed by the first task. Finally, comparisons across Erdős--Rényi, small-world, scale-free, and random-geometric graph ensembles show that topology changes the forgetting--adaptation balance. These results position differentiable resistor networks as compact, physically interpretable testbeds for studying continual learning in tunable matter.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that 2D p-wave magnets can host topological superconductivity through an Ising-type s+p wave mixing mechanism. By tuning the ratio of interactions or applying an external magnetic field, one can realize robust Majorana edge modes without needing strong spin-orbit coupling.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to find a platform for topological superconductivity that does not rely on weak, relativistic spin-orbit coupling, specifically investigating the emergence of Majorana modes in 2D p-wave magnets.

**Main result.** The researchers find that an odd-parity exchange field allows for a coupled s+p Ising superconducting state, which can transition into a nodal topological phase with Majorana edge modes when the triplet component dominates.

**Method.** The study uses a Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) formulation, self-consistent linearized gap equations, and topological invariant calculations (winding numbers and Pfaffian invariants).

**Model / system.** A two-dimensional p-wave magnet on a square lattice described by an extended Hubbard model with on-site and nearest-neighbor attractive interactions and an odd-parity exchange field.

**Key observables.** Pairing amplitudes (singlet and triplet), superconducting phase diagrams, momentum-resolved winding numbers, and Majorana edge mode spectral density.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Exchange field strength (J), on-site attraction (U), nearest-neighbor attraction (V), chemical potential (mu), and transverse Zeeman field (hx).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes a square lattice with an effective two-band tight-binding structure derived from a larger four-band model via Schrieffer-Wolff transformation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1a shows the pairing mechanism and d-vector orientation; Figure 1b illustrates the real-space lattice model; Figure 1c presents superconducting phase diagrams.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the physical problem of spin-orbit coupling-independent TSC, defines the p-wave magnetic model and Hamiltonian, derives the gap equations and symmetry classification, analyzes the topological phases and Majorana modes, and discusses the effects of external Zeeman fields.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Fermi-surface spin splitting generated by non-relativistic exchange fields provides a new route to topological superconductivity without relying on strong spin-orbit coupling. Here, we study superconducting instabilities of a square-lattice $p$-wave magnet with onsite and nearest-neighbour attractive interactions. The odd-parity exchange field removes inversion symmetry in the spin-split electronic structure, allowing singlet and triplet order parameters to mix within a single $A_1$ symmetry channel. The leading instability is a coupled $s+p_x$ Ising state, whose singlet-triplet balance is continuously tunable by the relative strength of the nearest-neighbour attraction. When the triplet gap amplitude exceeds the singlet one, this Ising state undergoes a transition into a nodal topological superconducting phase with Majorana edge modes protected by momentum-resolved winding numbers. These modes extend over finite momentum intervals bounded by the surface projections of bulk point nodes. We further show that a Zeeman field perpendicular to the exchange field can induce a $Z_2$ topological superconducting phase, even in the regime where a single gap dominates.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.01145"></a>
### [When Independent Gaussian Models Break Down: Characterizing Regime-Dependent Modeling Failures in $φ^4$ Theory](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01145v1)

**Authors:** Anish Bhat, Ryo Ide, Zihan Zhao  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01145v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01145_figures/2605.01145_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: 1a: Empirically, for λ = 0.1, Kposition ≈−0.23, indicating near-Gaussian behavior. Largely independent of N, it saturates at −0.77, near the theoretical limit of −0.81 given by standard ϕ4 theory. 1b: Empirically, DKL, Fourier gradually decreases to DKL, Fourier ≈0.4. Similarly, KFourier approaches ≈0.14 with N, likewise indicating near-Gaussian marginals. Note that curves are mostly overlapped with one another.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01145_figures/2605.01145_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Spectral relative error vs. normalized coupling across all baselines. Similar trends were observed for spectral error with respect to system size N, which is consistent with our observation that coupling increased with N, as across all λ, C ranges from C(N = 32) ≈0.06 to C(N = 128) ≈0.2.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the failure of Gaussian models in interacting field theories is primarily due to complex dependencies between modes rather than the non-Gaussian nature of individual modes. By identifying specific regimes of coupling, it provides a diagnostic tool to determine when more expressive, nonlinear models are necessary.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study investigates why Gaussian and independent Fourier-mode models fail to capture the complexity of self-interacting physical systems, specifically distinguishing between failures due to marginal non-Gaussianity versus structured inter-mode dependencies.

**Main result.** The authors find a separation between marginal simplicity and joint complexity: while individual Fourier modes become approximately Gaussian as system size increases, the inter-mode coupling grows, driving the failure of Gaussian models.

**Method.** The researchers used Metropolis-Hastings MCMC to sample the 1D phi-4 theory and employed various diagnostics including a 4th-order coupling metric, spectral error, and KL divergence to compare Gaussian baselines against the true distribution.

**Model / system.** A one-dimensional phi-4 theory on a lattice with periodic boundary conditions, defined by a Hamiltonian containing kinetic, mass, and quartic interaction terms.

**Key observables.** Two-point correlation function, power spectrum, normalized coupling metric (C), spectral error (epsilon), excess kurtosis, and Kullback-Leibler divergence.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Interaction strength (lambda), system size (N), and bare mass (m^2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study is restricted to 1D lattice systems with periodic boundary conditions and focuses on 4th-order statistics, potentially overlooking higher-order cumulants.

**Figures summary.** The notes suggest figures demonstrating the scaling of Fourier mode Gaussianity (decreasing kurtosis) alongside the simultaneous growth of the coupling metric (C) and spectral error.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of modeling failures, describes the 1D phi-4 system and MCMC methodology, presents the diagnostic metrics, details the discovery of the separation between marginal and joint complexity, identifies three distinct coupling regimes, and concludes with implications for future nonlinear model design.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In practical physical systems, modeling assumptions of Gaussianity and basis independence break down due to self-interactions. We study a specific instance of one-dimensional $φ^4$ theory on a lattice, analyzing how the interaction strength and system size jointly affect the marginal and joint distributions of frequency-based representation of the field (i.e., Fourier modes). We find that models relying on Gaussian and independent Fourier modes fail primarily from structured dependencies rather than marginal non-Gaussianity, since individual modes become approximately Gaussian despite mode coupling growing with size. Based on this, we identify three distinct regimes that delineate where traditional methods remain effective and where more expressive models are needed. Our results provide a computationally simple diagnostic to establish when Gaussian models are insufficient, and establish a concrete design criterion that future nonlinear models must satisfy.

</details>

</details>

### [Coherent exciton spin dynamics and three-dimensional quantum state tomography in a single InAlAs quantum dot](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01801v1)

**Authors:** J. Njala, Y. Yamamoto, R. Kaji, S. Adachi, H. Sasakura  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01801v1>

(b) Time evolution of the spin magni- tude |S(t)|. The data are...</figcaption>

**Summary.** The study uses advanced tomography to map the 3D spin precession of an exciton in an InAlAs quantum dot. It reveals that spin coherence persists longer than the exciton lifetime itself, providing a robust platform for spin initialization and control in semiconductor nanostructures.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics as it demonstrates high-fidelity 3D reconstruction of a single-qubit-like state (exciton spin) and provides a quantitative benchmark for decoupling intrinsic dephasing from recombination-induced decay.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Achieving a complete, three-dimensional understanding of the spin trajectory of a neutral exciton in a single quantum dot and disentangling anisotropic exchange interaction from other relaxation processes.

**Main result.** The researchers demonstrated 3D quantum state tomography of exciton spin, revealing a long-lived spin coherence (1.1 ns) that exceeds the exciton lifetime and a spin trajectory that follows a spiral motion on the Bloch sphere.

**Method.** Time-resolved quantum state tomography using micro-photoluminescence in three orthogonal polarization bases (circular, diagonal, and rectilinear) combined with a global fitting procedure.

**Model / system.** A single InAlAs/AlGaAs quantum dot under two-LO-phonon quasi-resonant excitation, modeled by a Hamiltonian dominated by the anisotropic exchange interaction (AEI) and a Lindblad master equation.

**Key observables.** Fine-structure splitting (FSS), exciton lifetime, spin coherence time, initial degree of circular polarization, and the time-resolved spin vector magnitude.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fine-structure splitting of ~19.6 ueV, exciton lifetime of ~767 ps, spin coherence time of 1.1 ns, and an instrument response function of ~30 ps.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The spin evolution is dominated by the anisotropic exchange interaction with negligible influence from the local nuclear environment (no Overhauser shift).

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the micro-PL spectrum and FSS determination; Fig 2 displays differential polarization signals showing quantum beats; Fig 3 illustrates the 3D spiral spin trajectory and the decay of the spin vector magnitude.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the experimental setup and spectral characterization, presents the time-resolved polarization measurements in three bases, details the 3D tomographic reconstruction and Hamiltonian fitting, and concludes with the analysis of spin relaxation and coherence.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the coherent exciton spin dynamics in a single InAlAs/AlGaAs quantum dot using time-resolved quantum state tomography. Under two-LO-phonon quasi-resonant excitation of neutral exciton, we observe pronounced quantum beats in the circular and diagonal polarization components, reflecting the fine-structure splitting ($Δ\approx 19.6 \ μ\text{eV}$). By employing a global fitting procedure across three orthogonal polarization bases, we demonstrate that the spin evolution is consistently described by a unified Hamiltonian dominated by the anisotropic exchange interaction. While the initial degree of circular polarization is limited to $\approx 0.28$ due to fast relaxation processes during carrier cooling, the subsequent dynamics reveal a long-lived spin coherence ($1.1 \pm 0.2$ ns) that exceeds the exciton lifetime ($\sim 767$ ps). Our analysis reveals that the spin-formation time is significantly shorter than the instrument response function, and the absence of a discernible Overhauser shift confirms a negligible influence from the local nuclear environment under the present conditions. These results provide a quantitative benchmark for the three-dimensional reconstruction of spin trajectories using differential polarization signals, demonstrating the feasibility of using quasi-resonant excitation for stable spin initialization in semiconductor nanostructures.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.02265"></a>
### [The infinitesimal environmental dust as a photonic bath at infinity](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02265v1)

**Authors:** Gaomin Tang, Yuhua Ren, Jian-Sheng Wang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02265v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **4/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `free-space correlated emission` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02265_figures/2605.02265_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Schematic of thermal radiation from (a) a three-dimensional object of finite size and (b) a semi-infinite object filling the region of z &lt; 0 to the surrounding environment. Both objects are maintained at temperature T1, while the surrounding environment is at temper- ature T0. The left panels depict that the environment extending to infinity is modeled as infinitesimal “dust” with homogeneous dielec- tric properties modeled by self-energy Πr 0 (gray shading). The right panels show the equivalent theoretical representation in which the ef- fect of the distributed dust is modeled as a photonic bath at infinity with effective self-energy Πr ∞.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper simplifies the complex problem of calculating far-field thermal radiation by replacing an infinite volume of 'environmental dust' with a simple, local photonic bath at infinity. The authors derive analytical expressions for this bath in both 3D and 2D geometries, demonstrating that it correctly recovers black-body radiation properties. This new formalism provides a much more efficient way to perform both analytical and numerical calculations in radiative heat transfer.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a powerful new computational framework for calculating far-field thermal radiation and Casimir forces, which is highly relevant for researchers working on open quantum systems and quantum optics in the presence of thermal environments.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of modeling far-field thermal radiation due to the computational complexity of integrating electromagnetic wave propagation over infinite space using the 'environmental dust' model.

**Main result.** The authors successfully map the distributed environmental dust to a simplified 'photonic bath at infinity' represented by a local self-energy, proving it behaves as a black body and providing analytical expressions for 3D and 2D systems.

**Method.** The study utilizes the nonequilibrium Green's function (NEGF) formalism, specifically the Keldysh Green's function, and employs a surface Green's function framework for verification.

**Model / system.** A material object (either a 3D body or a 2D planar system) at temperature T1 surrounded by an environment at temperature T0, modeled as a medium with infinitesimal absorption (dust).

**Key observables.** Radiative energy and momentum transfer, Casimir force, far-field heat, momentum, and angular-momentum radiation, and energy density.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperatures T1 and T0, frequency omega, infinitesimal absorption parameter eta, and the vacuum permittivity and permeability.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes infinitesimal absorption (eta to 0+), time-translational invariance, and a linear-response regime.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides a schematic comparison between the traditional distributed dust model and the proposed effective photonic bath at infinity model for both 3D and 2D systems; Figure 2 shows a discretized lattice model used for the surface Green's function derivation.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the problem of far-field radiation modeling, introduces the mapping of dust to a photonic bath using NEGF, derives the effective self-energy and black-body behavior for 3D and 2D systems, and concludes with an alternative derivation using a surface Green's function framework and lattice renormalization.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In far-field thermal radiation, electromagnetic waves emitted by an object propagate to infinity, requiring the far region to be modeled as an effective thermal bath. This bath was proposed as infinitesimal environmental "dust", but explicit calculations with such distributed dust involve integrals over infinite space that are difficult to evaluate. In this work, we map this environmental dust to a photonic bath at infinity within the nonequilibrium photonic Green's function formalism. By explicitly evaluating the spatial integral over the dust, we show that its contribution reduces to a simple local self-energy, for which we derive analytical expressions for both three-dimensional objects and planar systems. We further demonstrate that the bath behaves as a black body and clarify its role in far-field thermal radiation. An alternative derivation based on the surface Green's function framework is also provided in Appendix B, demonstrating the theoretical consistency of the results without invoking the dust model. The photonic bath at infinity provides a convenient framework for both analytical and numerical calculations in far-field thermal radiation.

</details>

### [Voltage-Tunable Nonequilibrium Dispersion Interactions](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02315v1)

**Authors:** Christine M. E. Little, Daniel S. Kosov  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02315v1>

The voltage enhances the attractive dispersion interaction over an order of magnitude larger than the equilibrium values. <img src="2605.02315_figures/2605.02315_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Same as Fig. 2, but for deeper molecular orbital en- ergies ϵa = ϵb = −1.0 eV. All other parameters are identical to Fig. 2. The minima shifts to substantially higher volt- ages compared to Fig. 2, demonstrating that the position of the dispersion enhancement tracks the molecular orbital en- ergy according to the voltage dependent pole of the retarded Green’s function. The depths of the minima are nearly un- changed, indicating that the maximum attraction is set by the interplay of U, Γ, and the current-driven noise rather than by molecular orbital energies.</figcaption>

The results reveal a striking nonequilibrium enhancement of the dispersion force. At zero bias, the equilibrium dispersion interaction is weakly attractive for all three values of U, with mag- nitudes of order 10−3 eV. This small equilibrium value reflects the narrow hybridisation width (Γ = 0.05 eV) rel- ative to the orbital resonance energy (∼0.75 −0.9 eV): the charge fluctuation spectral weight on each dot is con- centrated in a narrow window around...</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 4. Repulsive dispersion interaction under population inversion. The dispersion interaction energy Eint (in eV) is plotted as a function of applied bias voltage V (in V) for the same parameters as Fig. 2, but with the lead electronic tem- perature set to T = −300 K to model a population-inverted lead distribution. The interaction is repulsive (Eint &gt; 0) at every bias.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper develops a theoretical framework to study how electrical bias affects the van der Waals-like forces between nanostructures. It reveals that applied voltage can significantly strengthen attractive forces or even turn them repulsive through population inversion. This offers a mechanism for active, voltage-controlled control of nanoscale forces and self-assembly.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a fundamental generalization of the London picture to nonequilibrium settings, showing how breaking detailed balance via voltage can fundamentally change the nature of long-range forces.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Developing a theory for dispersion (van der Waals) interactions between two nanostructures driven into a nonequilibrium steady state by an applied bias voltage, and determining how voltage can tune the magnitude and sign of these forces.

**Main result.** The authors derive a general expression for the interaction energy as a coupling between charge noise and charge dissipation, demonstrating that voltage can enhance attraction by an order of magnitude and that population inversion can induce a repulsive interaction.

**Method.** Nonequilibrium Green's function (NEGF) theory on the Keldysh contour using the second-order Born approximation.

**Model / system.** Two capacitively coupled quantum nanostructures (e.g., molecular junctions) each connected to independent electron reservoirs, interacting via a density-density Coulomb coupling without direct tunneling.

**Key observables.** Interaction energy (E_int), charge noise spectrum (S), dissipative charge response (chi), and the generalized KMS ratio (Z).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Applied bias voltage (V), electronic temperature (T), hybridization width (Gamma), and population inversion.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Green's function is assumed to be block-diagonal (no inter-system tunneling), and the second Born approximation is used for the inter-system interaction.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of the coupled nanostructures and reservoirs; Figure 2 shows interaction energy as a function of voltage showing enhancement; Figure 3 shows the shift in the voltage-induced minimum with different orbital energies; Figure 4 shows the transition to a repulsive regime under population inversion.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of nonequilibrium dispersion, develops the NEGF theoretical framework, provides a fluctuation-dissipation interpretation, presents model calculations for molecular junctions showing voltage-tunable attraction and repulsion, and discusses experimental implications.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a nonequilibrium Green's function theory for dispersion interactions between two nanostructures, each an open quantum system driven into a nonequilibrium steady state by an applied bias voltage. Starting from the two-particle nonequilibrium Green's function, we derive a general expression for the interaction energy in terms of the polarisation propagators of the individual systems. The interaction energy admits a physically transparent decomposition into charge noise and charge dissipation contributions, providing a fluctuation-dissipation interpretation that generalises the equilibrium London picture. Model calculations for coupled molecular junctions demonstrate that the applied voltage can enhance the attractive dispersion interaction by nearly an order of magnitude relative to equilibrium. In thermal equilibrium, the dispersion interaction is universally attractive, irrespective of the specific form of the nanostructure Hamiltonians or their coupling to reservoirs. Out of equilibrium, we introduce a generalised Kubo-Martin-Schwinger ratio that parametrises the departure from detailed balance. We show that, in contrast to equilibrium, nonequilibrium conditions can lead to a repulsive dispersion interaction. Finally, we discuss the conditions under which population inversion in the electronic leads can drive a sign reversal of the dispersion interaction.

</details>

**Authors:** Sai Abhishikth Dhurjati, Moritz Langer, Yared G. Zena, Ahmad Rahimi, Liesa Raith, Martin Bauer, Frank H. P. Fitzek, Riccardo Bassoli, Caspar Hopfmann

**Summary.** The researchers created a new type of semiconductor light source using free-standing circular gratings that are much easier to manufacture than previous versions. This method significantly boosts the brightness of quantum dot photon emission and reduces unwanted energy splitting, making it a promising platform for scalable quantum communication networks.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it demonstrates a scalable way to generate high-brightness, high-fidelity entangled photon sources by engineering the local density of states and relaxing mechanical strain.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The need for a scalable, fabrication-simple, and strain-free platform for efficient extraction of entangled photon pairs from GaAs quantum dots, avoiding the complexity and degradation caused by conventional multi-layer architectures.

**Main result.** The development of a monolithic, free-standing circular Bragg grating (CBG) that achieves a 700-fold increase in photoluminescence intensity, reduces fine-structure splitting from 7.3 to 1.3 ueV, and reaches integrated count rates of 45 MHz.

**Method.** A single-step top-down fabrication process using aspect-ratio-dependent etching (ARDE) for vertical asymmetry, combined with FDTD simulations for design optimization and AFM-based lithography for deterministic QD alignment.

**Model / system.** Free-standing GaAs/AlGaAs circular Bragg grating cavities embedded with deterministically positioned GaAs quantum dots within suspended membranes.

**Key observables.** Photoluminescence intensity enhancement, extraction efficiency, fine-structure splitting (FSS), coherence time (T2), Purcell factor, and integrated count rates.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Cavity Q-factor (target <= 300), membrane thickness (150 nm), grating period, and fiber numerical aperture (NA = 0.6).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of an ARDE model to predict trench depth based on width and the assumption that the 150 nm membrane thickness is a practical approximation of the ideal 134 nm design.

**Figures summary.** Schematic of the experimental setup; comparison of simulated vs. fabricated structural parameters; optical performance plots (extraction efficiency and Purcell factor); micro-photoluminescence spectra and statistical distributions of FSS, Stokes parameters, and coherence times.

**Paper structure.** Introduction of the problem (extraction efficiency and strain); description of the fabrication-minimal CBG architecture and ARDE process; FDTD design optimization; experimental results including intensity enhancement, strain reduction (FSS), and coherence preservation; and a comparison of performance against existing platforms.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Deterministic and bright quantum light sources based on scalable semiconductor technologies are a crucial building block for future quantum communication networks. While circular Bragg gratings (CBGs) are highly effective for extracting light from solid-state quantum emitters, conventional architectures rely on complex multi-layer processing or flip-chip bonding, which introduce detrimental strain and limit scalability. Here, we present a fabrication-minimal approach to realize monolithic, free-standing CBG cavities with deterministically positioned single GaAs quantum dots (QDs). By utilizing aspect-ratio-dependent etching (ARDE) in a single-step top-down process, we achieve the necessary vertical structural asymmetry for directional emission without requiring bottom reflectors. Finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations validate this geometry, predicting free-space extraction efficiencies up to $68 \, \%$ and coupling efficiencies of $40 \, \%$ into a lensed single-mode fiber ($\text{NA} = 0.6$). Experimentally, the deterministically coupled QD-CBG devices yield a photoluminescence intensity enhancement of up to $\times 700$ compared to unprocessed planar QDs, reaching integrated count rates of $45 \, MHz$. Furthermore, the suspended membrane architecture effectively relaxes residual strain, significantly reducing the average exciton fine-structure splitting from $7.3 \, μeV$ in planar QDs to $1.3 \, μeV$ in the CBGs. Interferometric measurements confirm that the fabrication process preserves the optical quality of the emitters, with average coherence times of $70 \, ps$. By bridging optimized FDTD design with precise nanofabrication and robust optical performance, these results establish free-standing GaAs CBGs as a highly scalable platform for bright and coherent entangled photon pair sources.

</details>

### [Floquet-Multiple Andreev Reflections](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02642v1)

**Authors:** Régis Mélin, Romain Danneau, Morteza Kayyalha  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02642v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper explores how time-periodic driving in multi-terminal Josephson junctions leads to new types of Andreev reflection resonances. It provides a microscopic framework to predict conductance and noise signatures for complex multi-particle transfer processes. The study offers a pathway for engineering and probing Floquet states in superconducting hybrid structures.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the emergence of Floquet-Multiple Andreev Reflection (Floquet-MAR) resonances in the transport properties of multi-terminal Josephson junctions.

**Main result.** The authors identify that higher-order multipair processes (like quartets and octets) create characteristic conductance and noise resonances, and they prove that the Fano factor for an N-order process scales as 2N.

**Method.** Keldysh Green's function formalism and a diagrammatic expansion using 'snake diagrams' to describe Andreev-tube trajectories.

**Model / system.** A three-terminal Josephson junction consisting of a ballistic 2D normal conductor connected to three superconducting leads, driven by voltage-induced periodic phase evolution.

**Key observables.** Finite-bias conductance, quantum noise cross-correlations, current susceptibility, and the Fano factor.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Bias voltages (VL, VR), electrochemical potential (mu_N), superconducting phases, and the Thouless energy.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Large-gap approximation, zero-temperature limit, large-system/ballistic limit, and lowest-order perturbation theory in tunneling amplitudes.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows schematic setups and diagrams for quartet and octet transport processes; Figure 2 and 3 use toy models to illustrate the emergence and suppression of resonances at zero bias.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the Floquet-MAR concept, describes the 3-terminal junction model, develops the Keldysh-based microscopic transport theory, analyzes specific higher-order processes (quartets/octets), and concludes with a mathematical derivation of the Fano factor scaling.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Floquet theory describes quantum systems governed by time-periodic Hamiltonians, much as Bloch theory describes spatially periodic solids. In voltage-biased multiterminal Josephson junctions, the Josephson relation causes superconducting phase differences to evolve periodically in time, thereby providing an intrinsic Floquet drive. In this Letter, we consider three-terminal Josephson junctions formed on a ballistic two-dimensional normal conductor with a continuum of electronic states. We show that the quartet and higher-order multipair processes yield characteristic Floquet-multiple Andreev reflection (Floquet-MAR) finite-bias conductance and noise resonances that are parameterized by the bias voltage and electrochemical potential. This microscopic picture opens a route toward implementing and probing Floquet-MAR physics in ballistic multiterminal Josephson junctions.

</details>

### [Triplet-assisted leakage during singlet-triplet qubit readout with a quantum point contact](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02785v1)

**Authors:** Karol Kawa  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02785v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `quantum measurements` **4/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

Panels (a)–(c) show, respectively, both electrons localized in the left dot, one electron in each dot, and both electrons localized in the right dot. Since the QPC is placed next to the right dot, increasing the right-dot occupation modifies the barrier between the source and drain leads.</figcaption>

Panel (a) shows the complete high-bias singlet graph of off-diagonal QPC-induced transitions between reduced singlet eigenstates; panel (c) shows schematic QPC-induced pathways in the triplet sector; and panels (b) and (d) show the corresponding aggregated jump-rate matrices for 𝜖= 40 meV and 𝜖= 20 meV on a common color scale within each symmetry sector. In the schematic panels, darker arrow saturations denote transitions within the same manifold, intermediate saturations denote transitions between adjacent manifolds, and the lightest saturation denotes transitions between remote manifolds;...</figcaption>

Right: 𝜖= 20 meV, i.e., below the QPC bias. Each panel shows the corresponding occupation dynamics together with the detector current record for a single representative trajectory in the singlet and triplet branches. The plotted QPC current is shown as a time histogram — counted QPC events are summed in 120 equal-width bins and divided by the bin width, corresponding to Δ𝑡≃0.11 ns per displayed current bin.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">

The singlet branch (solid vermilion line) and the triplet branch (dashed blue line) are shown together. The dark-gray dotted curves show reduced connected pathway models constructed from the dominant shelving transitions and weak connector channels se- lected by the modal pathway ranking of Sec. III. Both reduced models also retain the elastic background channel needed to close the dynam- ics. The inset highlights the range where the singlet remains super- Poissonian while the triplet has already relaxed back to Poissonian behavior.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper explores how excited states in a double quantum dot cause leakage during spin-qubit readout. It demonstrates that these additional pathways can create significant low-frequency noise, potentially reversing the expected noise hierarchy between singlet and triplet states. This is crucial for designing high-fidelity readout protocols in semiconductor quantum computing.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides deep insights into the dynamics of open quantum systems, specifically how detector-induced backaction and higher-energy manifolds influence the noise statistics and fidelity of continuous quantum measurements.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how the presence of excited energy levels in a double quantum dot leads to triplet-assisted leakage during singlet-triplet qubit readout.

**Main result.** The study identifies that excited-level tunneling creates new leakage pathways that can significantly enhance low-frequency noise, potentially causing the triplet branch to become noisier than the singlet branch.

**Method.** The author uses a Lindblad master equation approach, quantum-jump simulations, and Liouvillian steady-state analysis combined with the quantum regression theorem.

**Model / system.** A lateral double quantum dot (DQD) with two single-particle levels per dot, capacitively coupled to a Quantum Point Contact (QPC) charge sensor, modeled via a Hamiltonian including interdot tunneling and on-site Coulomb repulsion.

**Key observables.** QPC current, current-noise power spectrum, Fano factor, and current contrast.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Level spacing (epsilon), QPC bias voltage, tunneling amplitude (t), and on-site charging energy (U).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Interdot Coulomb repulsion is neglected, spin is treated as a passive degree of freedom, and tunneling amplitudes are simplified to a single value.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the DQD-QPC schematic; Fig 2 illustrates charge configurations and barrier modulation; Fig 3 details the transition schemes and jump-rate matrices; Fig 4 shows quantum-jump trajectories; Fig 5 compares Fano spectra and relaxation times; Fig 6 plots the zero-frequency Fano factor versus level spacing.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the physical system and Hamiltonian decomposition, moves to the derivation of the master equation and jump operators, presents noise analysis via the quantum regression theorem, and concludes with numerical simulations and pathway analysis to identify the origins of leakage noise.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum point contact readout theory for singlet-triplet qubits in a lateral double quantum dot is extended by including tunneling of triplet configurations into a higher-energy level of the neighboring dot. This additional channel creates energetically allowed leakage pathways that modify the branch-dependent charge and current-noise signatures, even when the Pauli blockade remains effective within the ground-state manifold. The model contains two single-particle levels in each dot. The resulting singlet and triplet block structure is derived together with a Lindblad master equation. Quantum-jump simulations are then used to resolve the dynamics of individual readout events. A complementary Liouvillian steady-state analysis identifies the regime in which tunneling to the excited level qualitatively changes the readout signatures, with the crossover determined by the level spacing.

</details>

**Authors:** Aldo Tarascio, Oliver Wicki, Dominik M. Zumbühl  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02722v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · `quantum optics experiment` **2/5**

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates high-quality GaAs-based surface acoustic wave resonators operating in the GHz range at cryogenic temperatures. By optimizing geometry and material orientation, the authors achieve high quality factors, establishing GaAs as a robust platform for phonon-mediated hybrid quantum devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for quantum acoustics and hybrid quantum systems, as it provides a scalable platform for coupling phonons to other quantum degrees of freedom (like quantum dots) at high frequencies and low temperatures.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Achieving and characterizing high-quality (high-Q) surface acoustic wave (SAW) resonators at gigahertz frequencies and cryogenic temperatures using a Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) platform.

**Main result.** The researchers achieved quality factors up to 28,000 in the GHz range and demonstrated that GaAs-based SAW resonators are a viable, scalable platform for quantum acoustics with much weaker frequency-dependent loss than quartz.

**Method.** Systematic experimental study using reflection spectroscopy via a Vector Network Analyzer to measure resonance frequency, Q-factor, and reflectivity across varying cavity lengths, wavelengths, and crystal orientations.

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of GaAs-based SAW resonators acting as acoustic Fabry-Perot cavities, featuring interdigitated transducers (IDTs) and distributed Bragg reflectors (DBRs). The analysis uses a complex resonator model and a weak-reflection limit approximation for the DBRs.

**Key observables.** Internal quality factor (Q0), external quality factor (Qext), resonance frequency (f0), SAW velocity (v), single-finger reflectivity (|rs|), and attenuation coefficient (alpha_p).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Frequency range of 2.4-4-8 GHz, phonon mean free path of ~7 mm, and cavity lengths ranging from small to 200.5 lambda_0.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The DBR is modeled using a weak periodic reflector approximation; the resonator is simplified to a series RLC circuit near resonance; and the system is measured in a non-vacuum environment (liquid helium).

**Figures summary.** Fig 1a shows reflection spectra with multiple modes; Fig 2 shows Q0 vs frequency with power-law fits; Fig 3 shows Q0 vs crystal rotation angle; Fig 4 compares reflection spectra and Q0 for different etched mesa configurations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the challenge of GHz SAW resonators, describes the GaAs experimental platform and fabrication, presents systematic results on cavity size and frequency dependence, investigates the impact of crystal orientation and structural defects (mesas), and concludes with design guidelines for quantum acoustic applications.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Surface acoustic wave (SAW) resonators provide a compact platform for confining microwave-frequency phonons and are widely used in radio-frequency technologies, but their operation at gigahertz frequencies and cryogenic temperatures remains challenging. In this regime, conventional design rules do not directly apply, and achieving high-quality acoustic confinement requires careful consideration about geometry and loss mechanisms. Here, we present a systematic experimental study of SAW resonators on gallium arsenide, a platform of particular interest for hybrid quantum devices but comparatively unexplored for high-Q SAW cavities. By varying key design parameters such as cavity length, wavelength, and crystal orientation, we study resonator performance and achieve quality factors up to 28000 in the gigahertz range. In addition, we introduce mesa steps within the acoustic cavity, mimicking realistic device architectures and providing insight into scattering processes and additional dissipation channels. Our results establish practical design guidelines for GaAs-based SAW resonators and support their development as a scalable platform for quantum acoustics and phonon-mediated hybrid systems.

</details>

### [Berry-phase effect in single molecule magnets: analytical and numerical results](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01119v1)

**Authors:** Fco. Javier Anaya Garcia, Daniel Salgado-Blanco, Gabriel Gonzalez  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01119v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `Kondo & dissipative impurity` **2/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

This effect is also known as Berry’s phase interference. The parameters of the system are s = 3, K = 0.06 meV, Kz = 1 meV and Γ = 0.38.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01119_figures/2605.01119_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">

</figure>

<img src="2605.01119_figures/2605.01119_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">

</figure>

<img src="2605.01119_figures/2605.01119_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4: Comparative between the analytical and numerical solutions to the Lindblad master equation for SMMs in the Coulomb blocked regime connected to source and drain contacts, under the influence parallel and transverse magnetic field with s = 3/2, s = 2, s = 5/2, s = 3, and K = 0.06 meV, Kz = 1 meV, µS = −5eV, µD = 5eV, temperature t = 1x10−5K and Γ = 0.38.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper provides an analytical and numerical study of how Berry-phase interference can be used to manipulate and even block electronic current in single molecule magnet transistors. By using an effective Hamiltonian and the QmeQ software, the authors show that specific magnetic field configurations create destructive interference that suppresses tunneling. This work highlights the potential for using magnetic fields to control quantum transport in molecular-scale devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper explores the control of quantum transport via geometric phases (Berry phase) and the emergence of dark states in open quantum systems, which is highly relevant to the study of dissipation and quantum interference in molecular electronics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the transport signatures of Berry-phase interference in a single molecule magnet (SMM) transistor, specifically how transverse and longitudinal magnetic fields affect current flow through oppositely polarized leads.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that Berry-phase interference can lead to 'dark states' where the energy splitting vanishes, completely blocking the stationary current. It also shows that increasing the SMM spin increases the number of interference points and decreases current intensity.

**Method.** A density matrix approach using perturbation theory to derive an effective two-level Hamiltonian, combined with a Lindblad master equation in the Born-Markov and infinite-bias approximations, validated by numerical simulations using the QmeQ Python package.

**Model / system.** A single molecule magnet (SMM) transistor in the strong Coulomb blockade regime, tunnel-coupled to leads with opposite spin polarization. The Hamiltonian includes uniaxial and in-plane transverse anisotropy, longitudinal and transverse magnetic fields, and exchange interactions.

**Key observables.** Stationary average current <I>, energy splitting (delta E), and tunneling transition rate (Gamma).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Spin value (s), anisotropy constants (Kz, K), magnetic fields (Bz, Bx), tunneling rate (Gamma), and Coulomb interaction strength (U).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is in the Coulomb blockade regime; the transverse field and anisotropy are small compared to longitudinal components; the analytical model neglects temperature, lead bandwidth, and certain Coulomb effects.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows energy splitting vs. transverse field for s=3; Figure 2 provides a schematic of the SMM transistor setup; Figure 3 shows current suppression for various spin values; Figure 4 compares analytical predictions with QmeQ numerical results.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the physical problem of SMM transport, defines the Hamiltonian and effective two-level model, derives analytical results for energy splitting and current using a density matrix approach, validates these results with numerical simulations in QmeQ, and discusses the dependence of results on spin and magnetic fields.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this paper we theoretically and numerically investigate transport signatures of quantum interference on the current through a single molecule magnet transistor tunnel coupled to oppositely polarized leads in the presence of a local transverse and longitudinal magnetic field. Our calculations are based in a density matrix approach where we treat the ground state energy splitting induced by tunneling of the spin between different paths with the aid of perturbation theory. Using this approach we show that it is possible to use an effective Hamiltonian which describes the Berry phase interference as a function of the transverse magnetic field which completely blocks the current flow when we place the single molecule magnet between oppositely polarized leads. Finally, we use this effective Hamiltonian in an open source Python software (QmeQ) that allows us to calculate the current through the single molecule magnet with oppositely polarized leads tunnel coupled to the single molecule magnet. The analytical results are well reproduced by our numerical simulations.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Strong enhancement of Er3+ emission at room temperature in Si3N4 metasurfaces](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02535v1)

**Authors:** Fengkai Wei, Xinru Ji, Tobias J. Kippenberg, Duk-Yong Choi, Carsten Ronning  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02535v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum optics experiment` **3/5** · `QC/QI experiment` **2/5** · `interference shaping light` **2/5**

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** Researchers have achieved a significant boost in erbium-doped silicon nitride light emission at room temperature by using metasurfaces to create Mie resonances. By optimizing the size of nanocylinders and the depth of ion implantation, they achieved an 18-fold increase in brightness. This provides a way to integrate active light sources into standard silicon-based photonic chips.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper demonstrates significant control over the Purcell effect and local density of optical states (LDOS) in a CMOS-compatible platform, which is highly relevant to the development of integrated quantum optical components and light-matter interfaces.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Achieving efficient, room-temperature 1550 nm photoluminescence in silicon nitride (Si3N4) for active on-chip photonics, which is typically limited by low spontaneous emission and non-radiative recombination.

**Main result.** Demonstrated an 18-fold enhancement of Er3+ photoluminescence at room temperature using Mie-type resonances in Si3N4 nanocylinder metasurfaces, driven by the Purcell effect.

**Method.** The study combines ion implantation of Er3+ ions, thermal annealing, and electron beam lithography with FDTD and SRIM simulations to optimize the local density of optical states and ion distribution.

**Model / system.** The system consists of Er3+-doped Si3N4 metasurfaces made of periodic arrays of nanocylinders (height 365 nm, radius 240-465 nm) on a fused silica substrate.

**Key observables.** Photoluminescence (PL) intensity, luminescence lifetime, Purcell factor, and ion implantation depth/range.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Nanocylinder radius (optimal at 390 nm), implantation energy (50 to 350 keV), and wavelength (1530 nm/C-band).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The enhancement is primarily driven by the Purcell effect via Mie-type resonances; the system operates in a regime where thermal annealing effectively passivates defects.

**Figures summary.** The notes describe PL spectral heatmaps showing the relationship between nanocylinder radii and implantation energies, and a plot of peak PL intensity as a function of implantation energy and ion range.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the challenge of active Si3N4 photonics, describes the fabrication and doping process, presents electromagnetic simulations and ion distribution modeling, details the experimental PL and lifetime measurements, and discusses the optimization of ion depth and resonance geometry.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We report a significant enhancement of room-temperature photoluminescence from trivalent erbium-doped (Er3+) silicon nitride (Si3N4) metasurfaces at the telecommunication wavelength prepared via ion implantation. The metasurfaces, consisting of periodic nanocylinder arrays, are designed to support Mie-type resonances that tailor the local density of optical states. By systematically optimizing the nanocylinder radii, we achieve a photoluminescence (PL) enhancement factor of ~18 at a radius of 390 nm after thermal annealing, which is in excellent agreement with our simulations. Time-resolved PL measurements reveal a nearly ten-fold reduction in luminescence lifetime, confirming that the enhancement is primarily driven by the Purcell effect. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the PL intensity is strongly dependent on the Er3+ ion implantation depth, with a four-fold increase in emission observed from 20 nm to 80 nm ion range. These results provide a robust pathway for integrating efficient, active light sources into CMOS-compatible photonic device.

</details>
