<a id="paper-2605.01206"></a>
### [Phase-shift instanton approach to tunneling duality in Read--Rezayi state](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01206v1)

**Authors:** Ryoi Ohashi, Hiroki Isobe, Ryota Nakai, Kentaro Nomura  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01206v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01206_figures/2605.01206_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Illustration of the point-contact geometry in FQH systems. (a) and (b) show QPT and ET processes at a point contact, respectively. (c) highlights the tunneling duality.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01206_figures/2605.01206_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Schematic picture of the instanton and anti- instanton solutions in the periodic potential of Eq. (22). Solid (dotted) arrow represents the (anti-)instanton solution.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01206_figures/2605.01206_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Schematic picture of the phase-shift instanton pro- cess. We define ”phase-shift instanton” as the instanton pro- cess that occurs with a phase-shift of the potential. Upper figure shows the potential of Eqs. (31) and (38). Lower figure represents the details of the phase-shift instanton process in each case. Solid (dotted) line represents the potential after (before) the instanton process. (a) shows the Moore–Read state case with π-shift instanton, and (b) shows the Read– Rezayi state case with 2π/3-shift instanton.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper develops a phase-shift instanton method to establish a duality between quasi-particle and electron tunneling in non-Abelian Read-Rezayi fractional quantum Hall states. It discovers that the strong-coupling regime exhibits a universal V^4 conductance scaling across different states due to fundamental topological constraints. This result implies that simple conductance measurements cannot distinguish between these complex non-Abelian states in the strong-coupling limit.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper provides deep insights into the topological constraints of non-Abelian edge states and demonstrates how complex anyonic operators can recombine into a universal fermion, which is relevant to the study of many-body dynamics and topological order.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Extending the concept of tunneling duality from Abelian and Moore-Read states to the more complex non-Abelian Read-Rezayi states, specifically addressing the difficulty of the intricate operator algebra.

**Main result.** The authors derive an explicit dual description for the k=3 Read-Rezayi state and reveal a universal transport signature where differential conductance scales as G proportional to V^4 in the strong-coupling regime.

**Method.** A new 'phase-shift instanton' approach is introduced to incorporate phase factors from non-Abelian primary fields into an instanton gas framework, combined with Renormalization Group (RG) analysis.

**Model / system.** The system consists of fractional quantum Hall (FQH) states (Laughlin, Moore-Read, and Read-Rezayi) in point-contact geometries, modeled using effective edge actions involving U(1) charge sectors and Z_k parafermion Conformal Field Theory (CFT).

**Key observables.** Non-linear differential conductance (G) and tunneling current (I) as a function of bias voltage (V).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Filling factors (nu = 1/3, 1/2, 3/5), tunneling amplitudes (QPT and ET), and scaling dimensions (Delta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes the low-frequency limit where the frequency dependence of the Fourier coefficient can be neglected, and it relies on the instanton-gas approximation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows point-contact geometries and the concept of tunneling duality; Figure 3 illustrates the phase-shift instanton process for Moore-Read and Read-Rezayi states.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of tunneling duality, presents the phase-shift instanton method, applies it to Moore-Read and Read-Rezayi states, performs RG analysis for conductance scaling, and provides mathematical derivations in the appendices.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the duality between quasi-particle and electron tunneling in point-contact geometries of fractional quantum Hall states. To treat non-Abelian edge operators, we introduce a "phase-shift instanton" that incorporates phase factors from primary fields into the instanton gas framework. Using this method, we reformulate the Moore--Read duality and obtain an explicit dual description for the $k=3$ Read-Rezayi state. Our results clarify how quasi-particle tunneling produces characteristic phase shifts in instantons and how these shifts map strong quasi-particle tunneling to weak electron tunneling. Based on this dual description, we analytically evaluate the non-linear differential conductance in the strong-coupling regime. We reveal that, due to the physical requirement that the tunneling particle across the vacuum gap must be a true fermion, the transport behavior universally converges to a $G \propto V^4$ scaling for both the Moore--Read and Read--Rezayi states. This universal transport signature highlights a fundamental topological constraint underlying non-Abelian fractional quantum Hall edges.

</details>

**Authors:** Xianxi Zhang, Hongyu Yu, Liangliang Hong, Hongjun Xiang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01781v1>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new first-principles method to simulate the simultaneous motion of atoms and spins in magnetic materials without needing pre-fitted parameters. It successfully predicts complex magnetic phases and shows that the data generated by this method can significantly improve the accuracy of machine-learning models for magnetism.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The lack of a unified, first-principles framework capable of propagating both spin and lattice degrees of freedom simultaneously without relying on empirical parameterization.

**Main result.** The development of a fully ab initio spin-lattice dynamics (SLD) framework in VASP that accurately recovers diverse magnetic ground states and provides highly efficient, physically correlated training data for machine-learning potentials.

**Method.** A self-consistent dynamical loop integrated into VASP using constrained-moment density functional theory to obtain interatomic forces and effective magnetic fields at each time step.

**Model / system.** A coupled spin-lattice dynamics model where the lattice follows Newtonian equations of motion and spins follow the stochastic Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation, applied to magnetic materials such as CrI3, bcc Fe, Cd2Os2O7, MgCr2O4, and BiFeO3.

**Key observables.** Interatomic forces, effective magnetic fields, magnetic order parameters (e.g., AIAO order parameter), magnetization, and lattice parameters (c/a ratio).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Time step (up to 1 fs), gyromagnetic ratio, Gilbert damping parameter, and the global penalty strength for magnetic constraints.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The spin-adiabatic approximation (instantaneous adaptation of moment magnitudes) and the atomic-moment approximation (local magnetic moments are treated as discrete variables).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the dynamical loop of the SLD framework; Figure 3 benchmarks SpinGNN models using SLD-derived data against randomized-spin datasets across different training budgets and sampling strategies.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the gap in current dynamical modeling, presents the new self-consistent VASP implementation, validates the physics across diverse magnetic orders, and demonstrates the utility of the resulting data for machine learning.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Coupled spin-lattice dynamics (SLD) underlie a wide range of magnetic phenomena, yet a unified first-principles framework that propagates both degrees of freedom without empirical parameterization has remained elusive. We present a fully ab initio SLD approach integrated into VASP, in which interatomic forces and effective magnetic fields are obtained at each time step from self-consistent constrained-moment density-functional calculations. The method is validated on four materials spanning ferromagnetic, non-collinear, and geometrically frustrated orders, recovering the correct magnetic ground state in every case from random initial conditions. SLD trajectories also provide physically correlated training data for magnetic machine-learning potentials, as demonstrated for BiFeO$_3$ by a reduction of up to approximately one order of magnitude in energy MAE over training on randomized spin configurations. This framework opens a practical first-principles route to finite-temperature spin-lattice coupled phenomena in magnetic materials.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01087v1>

**Summary.** This paper investigates whether the formation of metal filaments in memristors follows non-equilibrium thermodynamic principles. Using kinetic Monte Carlo simulations, the authors demonstrate that the system minimizes entropy production and energy dissipation during the filament growth process. This suggests that the steady-state morphology of such devices might be predictable using thermodynamic extremum principles.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to identify the underlying physical principles governing the morphology and stability of metal filament formation in electrochemical metallization (ECM) memristors.

**Main result.** The researchers observe that the filament formation process follows non-equilibrium thermodynamic extremal principles, specifically the minimization of entropy production and energy dissipation rates.

**Method.** The study uses kinetic Monte Carlo (KMC) simulations combined with continuum modeling of electric potential and temperature via finite difference methods.

**Model / system.** The system is an electrochemical metallization (ECM) memristor where metal filaments form and break in a solid-state electrolyte through electrically driven ion migration.

**Key observables.** Entropy production rate (from Joule heating and heat conduction), energy dissipation rate, filament morphology, and device resistance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Applied current (3223 A), boundary temperature (300 K), ionic charge number (z=1), and various activation energies for oxidation, reduction, and diffusion.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The electric current is assumed to be ohmic and quasi-stationary, thermoelectric effects are neglected, and a linear phenomenological approximation is used for entropy production.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the physical processes in the simulation; Figure 2 displays run-averaged entropy production and energy dissipation rates; Figure 3 shows average filament growth and morphology; Figure 4 illustrates stochastic fluctuations in a single simulation run; Figure 5 shows the evolution of filament width and distance over time.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of filament morphology, describes the KMC and continuum modeling approach, presents results regarding the minimization of thermodynamic rates during formation, discusses the transition to a steady state, and concludes with the implications of extremal principles.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Electrochemical metallization (ECM) memristors have potential applications in future neuromorphic computing hardware. The set, reset, and variable-resistance features of these devices originate in the formation and breakup of metal filaments in a solid-state electrolyte. While the performance characteristics of these devices are widely investigated, the driving principles behind the morphology of the filament formation process remain unclear. In this study, we propose an approach motivated by the extremal principles found in non-equilibrium thermodynamics and observe an entropy production and energy dissipation rate minimization during the filament-forming process in kinetic Monte Carlo simulations.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01225v1>

**Summary.** This paper predicts that altermagnets can support a multidirectional 'vector' magnon spin current when electrically injected. It demonstrates that the transverse component of this current is significantly larger in altermagnets than in antiferromagnets, providing a clear experimental fingerprint to identify altermagnetic materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to identify and manipulate distinctive transport signatures of altermagnets (ATMs) for information processing, specifically focusing on how to experimentally distinguish them from conventional antiferromagnets.

**Main result.** The authors predict a 'vector' magnon spin current with both longitudinal and sizable transverse components, where the transverse response is enhanced by two orders of magnitude in altermagnets due to broken parity-time symmetry.

**Method.** The researchers use microscopic quantum kinetic equations to model electrical spin injection, diffusion, and dephasing, combined with a symmetry-based expansion of the response tensor.

**Model / system.** A heavy-metal/altermagnet (NM/ATM) interface modeled using a minimal square-lattice two-sublattice Hamiltonian including inter-sublattice exchange, anisotropy, and an applied magnetic field.

**Key observables.** Longitudinal and transverse magnon spin current components, and the transverse-to-longitudinal spin-current ratio.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Exchange coupling J1 = 1 meV, spin S = 3/2, lattice constant a = 1 nm, and relaxation time tau_m = 1 ns.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes a relaxation time tau_m that is equal for both magnon modes and focuses on the diffusion-driven transport regime.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of diffusive magnon transport and a comparison of flow in AFMs vs ATMs; Figure 2 illustrates injected magnon populations in the Brillouin zone; Figure 3 shows the dependence of longitudinal and transverse spin currents on the Neel vector orientation.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of altermagnet transport, describes the microscopic model and quantum kinetic framework, presents the symmetry analysis of the response tensor, details the results regarding vector spin currents and their enhancement, and concludes with the experimental implications for distinguishing altermagnets.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Altermagnets host chirally split magnons that promise unique functionalities for information processing. However, their distinctive transport signatures, crucial for experimental identification and manipulation, remain elusive. Here, we predict that a spin accumulation electrically injects a ``vector" or multidirectional magnon spin current into an altermagnet, comprising both longitudinal and sizable transverse components. Notably, this transverse current exhibits a sign reversal away from the source and can be switched on or off by reorienting the Néel vector. While such a transverse current is found to be not forbidden even in conventional antiferromagnets, we demonstrate through quantum-kinetic calculations that in altermagnets, the transverse response is enhanced by two orders of magnitude due to broken parity-time symmetry. This giant enhancement provides a decisive transport fingerprint for detecting magnon spin splitting and Néel-vector orientation, offering a clear criterion to experimentally distinguish altermagnets from conventional antiferromagnets.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a unified theoretical framework to describe how weak disorder and finite-size effects influence magnetization and spin transport in Heisenberg ferromagnets. It reveals that the resulting spin current follows a temperature dependence similar to the Efros-Shklovskii law found in electronic systems. The findings provide a generalized Bloch equation applicable across different dimensions.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to provide a unified description of the loss of saturation magnetization and the behavior of spin transport in weakly disordered spin-1/2 ferromagnets.

**Main result.** The author derives a unified Bloch equation for magnetization and a spin current expression that is structurally analogous to the Efros-Shklovskii law for electronic conductivity.

**Method.** The approach combines the probability distribution of segment lengths with the energy gap dependence on length to derive a gap distribution, using a modified Bloch equation approach.

**Model / system.** The system consists of weakly disordered spin-1/2 Heisenberg ferromagnets in 1D, 2D, or 3D, incorporating finite-size effects and a distribution of energy gaps due to disorder.

**Key observables.** Saturation magnetization (Ms), magnetization loss (Mloss), and spin current (j).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Disorder strength (a), gap scaling constant (c), effective dimensionality (psi), temperature (T), and external magnetic field (H).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes a weak-disorder and weak-magnetic-field regime, and assumes the magnon group velocity is independent of the magnon population.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the dependence of the mean gap on disorder and dimensionality parameters; Figure 2 shows the variation of saturation magnetization with respect to disorder, scaling, and dimensionality at different temperatures.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of magnetization loss in disordered systems, develops a mathematical framework for gap distributions, derives a unified Bloch equation, extends the derivation to spin transport, and validates the results through parameter variation analysis.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this report, a unified description of the loss of saturation magnetization in the presence of a distribution of finite-size effects is provided for weakly disordered spin-1/2 ferromagnets. This description allows us to derive a unified form of the Bloch equation for these systems. We further extend this approach to obtain a unified expression for spin transport in such systems.

</details>

**Summary.** The researchers demonstrated that two ZnO nanowire lasers can be dynamically synchronized through frequency locking when placed extremely close together. By controlling where the laser pump hits the wires, they can switch between full, partial, and no locking. This provides a robust way to control light at the nanoscale for future integrated photonic circuits.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the implementation and control of frequency locking and optical coupling in ZnO nanowire lasers at the nanoscale, specifically distinguishing between dynamic nonlinear interaction and static spectral filtering.

**Main result.** Demonstrated that closely spaced ZnO nanowire lasers (gap < 10 nm) exhibit dynamic frequency locking, including full and partial locking regimes, which can be actively controlled via spatial excitation distribution.

**Method.** Micromanipulation of ZnO nanowires into pairs, followed by spatially resolved micro-photoluminescence spectroscopy using an inhomogeneous optical pump spot.

**Model / system.** A pair of ZnO (Zinc Oxide) nanowires assembled in a staggered geometry on a SiO2/Si substrate, operating as evanescently coupled Fabry-Perot resonators in the extreme near-field.

**Key observables.** Lasing photoluminescence spectra, Free Spectral Range (FSR), group index (ng), spatio-spectral intensity maps, and side-mode suppression ratio (SMSR).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Gap distance (< 10 nm), pump power, detuning (cavity length difference), and pumping asymmetry (relative excitation intensity).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The threshold for lasing is operationally defined by the emergence of Fabry-Perot modes; the group index is approximated as a linear function of wavelength.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the nanowire assembly schematic, individual spectra before assembly, SEM images of the pair, and spatially resolved spectra. Supplementary figures show pump spot intensity maps, single nanowire spectral stability, and the derivation of the group index.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of nanoscale coupling, describes the experimental assembly and spectroscopic setup, presents the demonstration of frequency locking and its tunability, and concludes with the implications for nanophotonic systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Frequency locking between coupled laser systems provides a powerful mechanism for stabilizing and controlling coherent emission, yet its implementation and applicability down to the nanoscale remains unknown and unexplored. Here, we demonstrate optical coupling and frequency locking in closely spaced ZnO nanowire lasers operating in the extreme near field (gap < 10 nm). We observe both full and partial frequency locking, manifested as the alignment of all or a subset of the lasing modes, by spatially controlling the optical excitation. We also observe single-mode lasing in a coupled nanowire pair where the multi-mode lasing of individual nanowires is suppressed. In contrast to previously reported coupled-cavity nanowire lasers, where spectral control arises from static filtering mechanisms such as the Vernier effect, our results indicate a dynamically established relationship between actively lasing nanowires. These findings establish frequency locking as a robust and tunable mechanism in nanowire lasers, opening new routes toward stabilized and controllable nanoscale light sources for integrated nanophotonic systems.

</details>

### [Absence of Quantum-Metric-Induced Intrinsic Longitudinal Response](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02750v1)

**Authors:** Ping Tang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02750v1>

**Summary.** This paper proves that the quantum metric cannot induce an intrinsic longitudinal nonlinear conductivity in solids. It resolves theoretical discrepancies by showing that previous claims of a finite response were due to using gauge-variant energy instead of gauge-invariant energy. This suggests that reported longitudinal signals in materials like MnBi2Te4 likely stem from extrinsic, disorder-induced mechanisms.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether the quantum metric can generate an intrinsic longitudinal nonlinear transport response in solids, aiming to resolve theoretical inconsistencies in existing literature.

**Main result.** The author demonstrates that the quantum-metric-induced intrinsic longitudinal response identically vanishes due to the dissipationless nature of intrinsic currents, even though an intrinsic Hall response is allowed.

**Method.** The study employs standard quantum-mechanical perturbation theory up to the second order in an external electric field, utilizing semiclassical wave-packet dynamics and the Heisenberg equation of motion.

**Model / system.** The system consists of electrons in a crystal (metallic system) described by an unperturbed Hamiltonian H0 and subject to a uniform static electric field E.

**Key observables.** Nonlinear Hall conductivity, nonlinear longitudinal/nonreciprocal conductivity, intrinsic current density, Berry curvature, and the quantum metric.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Electric field strength E, relaxation time tau, chemical potential mu, and the quantum metric G and Berry curvature Omega.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation assumes a uniform static electric field and is truncated at the second order of the external electric field.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the problem and the Hamiltonian, proceeds to derive field-perturbed Bloch states and velocities using second-order perturbation theory, simplifies the resulting tensors using the quantum metric, and concludes by demonstrating the vanishing of the longitudinal response and discussing implications for experimental discrepancies.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Nonlinear charge transport in solids has emerged as a powerful probe of the quantum geometric properties of Bloch electrons. While the Berry curvature underlies the intrinsic anomalous Hall effect, recent studies have suggested that the quantum metric may generate both \emph{intrinsic} nonlinear Hall and longitudinal transport. Here, using standard quantum-mechanical perturbation theory, we demonstrate that the quantum-metric-induced intrinsic longitudinal response identically vanishes, even though the corresponding intrinsic Hall response is allowed. This conclusion follows from the dissipationless nature of intrinsic currents and holds independently of band structure details and to all orders in the nonlinear response. Our work resolves existing inconsistencies in the theoretical formulation of quantum-metric-induced nonlinear transport and suggests a reexamination of recently reported intrinsic longitudinal responses attributed to the quantum metric.

</details>

**Summary.** This review provides a comprehensive overview of spin caloritronics, tracing its history from fundamental spin-transport discoveries to modern applications in thermal management. It highlights how new measurement techniques and hybrid material architectures are enabling the development of efficient energy-harvesting and cooling technologies.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper reviews the historical development and future prospects of spin caloritronics, focusing on the conversion between heat, charge, and spin currents and the transition from fundamental physics to materials engineering.

**Main result.** The review highlights the evolution of the field from longitudinal to transverse effects and identifies 'magnon engineering' and hybrid structures like Artificially Tilted Multilayers (ATMLs) as key paths toward efficient energy harvesting and thermal management.

**Method.** The authors synthesize experimental progress using techniques such as lock-in thermography, time-domain thermoreflectance, and the inverse spin Hall effect to analyze various magneto-thermoelectric and thermo-spin phenomena.

**Model / system.** The systems studied include magnetic heterostructures, magnetic bilayers, and multilayers (e.g., Cu/CoFe), as well as topological materials, magnetic insulators (YIG), and ferroelectric systems.

**Key observables.** Spin Seebeck Effect (SSE), Anomalous Nernst Effect (ANE), Spin Peltier Effect (SPE), thermal conductivity, and thermoelectric coefficients (Seebeck, Nernst, Thomson).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature gradients, magnetization direction, spin/magnon diffusion lengths, and the dimensionless figure of merit (ZT).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the timeline and concept of SSE; Figure 2 classifies conversion phenomena and longitudinal vs. transverse effects; Figure 3 shows ANE publication trends; Figure 5 demonstrates lock-in thermography applications; Figure 7 illustrates hybrid structure architectures.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with a historical overview of spin caloritronics, followed by a classification of transport phenomena (magneto-thermoelectric, thermomagnetic, and thermo-spin), an analysis of experimental measurement techniques, a discussion on the transition to materials science and engineering (including hybrid structures), and concludes with future research directions and unexplored phenomena.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Since the beginning of the 21st century, novel energy conversion and control principles utilizing the spin degree of freedom have been discovered in the field of spin caloritronics, which integrates spintronics with thermal transport and thermoelectric properties. In this article, we review the history of development of spin caloritronics and experimental studies on various transport phenomena caused by heat-charge-spin interactions. We then discuss future prospects in spin caloritronics from the viewpoints of measurement techniques, physics, materials science, and engineering applications. Spin caloritronics is now at a turning point, transitioning from fundamental condensed matter physics to materials science, and further development is anticipated in both fundamental and applied research.

</details>

**Authors:** Graeme J. Ackland, Ciprian G. Pruteanu, John S. Loveday, Keishiro Yamashita  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01045v1>

The second ex- cited state is closer to an excitation of the OH stretch, Bot- tom: Contribution to Raman spectrum from this mode only, at a range of temperatures, using a range of bump heights and fundamental frequencies from 2391-2641 to represent the range of environments of the water donors and OH...</figcaption>

**Summary.** The researchers show that in CsOH.H2O, water and hydroxyl molecules are not stable entities but constantly interconvert through proton exchange. This process drives the structural transition of the crystal and provides a unique mechanism for high ionic conductivity without long-range molecular diffusion.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study explores a 2D analogue of water and the dynamics of a chemical-reaction-driven phase transition, which relates to many-body dynamics and the stability of networked systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Understanding the atomic-level dynamics, lifetime of water molecules, and the mechanism of the order-disorder transition in the crystal CsOH.H2O.

**Main result.** The order-disorder transition proceeds via a chemical reaction (proton exchange) rather than molecular rotation, where water and hydroxyl units interconvert on a picosecond scale.

**Method.** Ab initio Molecular Dynamics (AIMD) using rSCAN and PBE functionals, combined with Density Functional Perturbation Theory (DFPT) and a 1D anharmonic Hamiltonian model for Raman spectroscopy.

**Model / system.** The system is caesium hydroxide monohydrate (CsOH.H2O), featuring a quasi-2D honeycomb lattice of water and hydroxide ions that acts as a 2D analogue of water.

**Key observables.** Raman spectra (stretching, bending, and librational modes), radial distribution functions (RDF), mean squared displacements (MSD), and proton exchange barriers.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature range (200K to 700K), proton exchange barrier (~0.2 eV), and picosecond-scale molecular lifetimes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The hydrogen arrangement obeys 'ice rules'; Raman activity is modeled using a linear polarizability and a 1D harmonic well perturbed by a Gaussian term.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the crystal structure, describes the AIMD simulation setup, analyzes the symmetry-breaking and energetic stability of the lattice, investigates the dynamical network of proton exchange, and concludes with a theoretical model for Raman signatures.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We describe the atomic-level motions in caesium hydroxide monohydrate (CsOH$\cdot$H$_2$O), which is a chemical compound containing layers of water and hydroxide ions. At this composition, each oxygen is involved in three hydrogen bonds which, in the hexagonal structure, form a quasi-2D honeycomb lattice. While oxygen and caesium atoms form a typical crystal lattice, the dynamics of the hydrogen atoms are more complex. Here we show that the covalent and hydrogen bonds are continually interconverting, meaning that the water and hydroxyl are interconverting by proton exchange. The order-disorder transition of the water and hydroxyl proceeds by chemical reaction rather than rotation or diffusion of the molecules. A hydrogen can rotate out of the layer, leaving a vacant site in the 2D layer. Such a hydrogen vacancy can diffuse rapidly by single molecule rotation, leading to fast-ionic conduction. The proton exchange leads to a novel type of Raman activity combining stretch and exchange processes, for which we develop a theoretical model. This would manifest in a broad single peak associated with both H$_2$O and OH stretches and a low frequency peak appearing at elevated temperature.

</details>

**Summary.** Researchers have discovered a hidden, thermodynamically stable polymorph of the organic semiconductor DNTT that features a 3D interdigitated molecular packing. This structural change shifts charge transport from a 2D to a 3D regime and significantly boosts electron mobility. This finding demonstrates that polymorphism is a powerful tool for tuning the dimensionality and efficiency of charge transport in organic electronics.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The discovery and characterization of a previously unrecognized, thermodynamically stable bulk polymorph of the organic semiconductor DNTT and how its distinct molecular packing governs the dimensionality and efficiency of charge transport.

**Main result.** The identification of 'blue DNTT' featuring a 3D interdigitated herringbone structure that enables 3D charge transport and significantly enhances electron mobility compared to the well-known 2D 'green' phase.

**Method.** A combination of structural characterization (XRD, DSC), spectroscopy (Raman, THz, UV-Vis, PL), and computational modeling (DFT, Transient Localization Theory, and tight-binding simulations).

**Model / system.** The physical system is the organic semiconductor DNTT, specifically comparing two polymorphs: a metastable 2D 'green' phase and a stable 3D 'blue' phase. The transport is modeled using Transient Localization Theory (TLT) and a tight-binding model.

**Key observables.** Charge carrier mobility (holes and electrons), transport dimensionality (2D vs 3D), phonon spectra (low-frequency Raman/THz modes), and optical emission color.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Electron mobility in the blue phase exceeds hole mobility in the green phase by >2x; scattering time (tau_in) is approximately 250 fs; phase transition occurs between 210-240 C.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of the relaxation time approximation in TLT simulations and the assumption of a linear nonlocal electron-phonon coupling model.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Crystal structures, visual color difference, and optical spectra; Fig 2: Comparison of 2D vs 3D molecular packing; Fig 3: Temperature-dependent XRD showing phase transition; Fig 4: Raman spectra and THz-derived mobility/dimensionality analysis.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the discovery of the blue polymorph via optical properties, moves to structural and thermodynamic characterization of the phase transition, then investigates the impact of the new packing on vibrational modes and charge transport dynamics using THz spectroscopy and simulations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Organic semiconductors (OSCs) are widely explored for flexible optoelectronic technologies, with performance governed not only by molecular design, but also by solid-state packing, which can give rise to polymorphism. Dinaphthothienothiophene (DNTT) is a benchmark OSC that has long been considered monomorphic. Here, we discover, isolate, and resolve the crystal structure of a previously unrecognised bulk polymorph of DNTT, termed blue DNTT owing to its characteristic blue emission. Coexisting with the well-known (green) DNTT in commercial powders, yet previously overlooked, blue DNTT represents the thermodynamically stable form. By combining X-ray diffraction, Raman, and THz spectroscopy with simulations, we demonstrate that polymorphism in DNTT reshapes the low-frequency phonon landscape and transfer-integral network, impacting charge transport. While green DNTT exhibits two-dimensional charge transport with holes more mobile than electrons, blue DNTT shows charge transport along all crystallographic directions enabled by a distinct herringbone packing. Electron mobility along the crystallographic a and b-axes in blue DNTT exceeds twice the hole mobility in the green phase. To our knowledge, this is the first reported acene-based semiconductor exhibiting three-dimensional charge transport. Polymorphism emerges as a key lever to tune charge transport dimensionality and carrier efficiency in organic semiconductors.

</details>

**Authors:** Viren Tyagi, Geert Brocks, Shuxia Tao  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02685v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** This paper uses machine-learning-based molecular dynamics to show that methylammonium cations in perovskites can migrate as fast as iodide ions through a collective motion mechanism. This finding revises the traditional understanding of ion transport in solar cell materials and helps reconcile conflicting experimental data.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to resolve discrepancies in reported migration barriers for iodide and methylammonium defects in MAPbI3 and to understand the microscopic mechanisms of cation and anion migration.

**Main result.** The researchers discovered that MA interstitials migrate as rapidly as iodide defects due to a concerted motion of three neighboring MA ions, and they found that MA vacancy migration is negligible, providing a unified picture of ion transport.

**Method.** Molecular dynamics simulations were performed using neural network potentials (Allegro model) trained on DFT data (SCAN+rVV10 functional) and validated using the climbing image nudged elastic band method.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the hybrid organic-inorganic perovskite MAPbI3, specifically focusing on the movement of iodide (anion) and methylammonium (cation) point defects including vacancies and interstitials.

**Key observables.** Migration barriers (Eb), diffusion coefficients (D), activation energies (Ea), and pre-exponential factors (D0).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature range of 500 K to 600 K for MD simulations; migration barriers between 0.15 and 0.20 eV.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The neural network potential is assumed to accurately reproduce DFT-level accuracy for forces and energies; the study focuses on intrinsic bulk diffusion.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares energy profiles of migration paths between DFT and NNP; Figure 2 shows temperature-dependent diffusion coefficients via Arrhenius plots; Figure 3 provides a schematic of the migration mechanism.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of defect mobility, describes the development and validation of the neural network potential, presents the results of MD simulations for various defect species, and concludes by comparing findings to experimental literature.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Passivating defects and restricting defect mobilities in halide perovskites to increase device lifetimes has become a main field of research. Modeling structure and mobility of point defects is an essential contribution to this endeavor. We employ molecular dynamics, based on neural network potentials trained on density functional theory data, to model ion migration in MAPbI$_3$ triggered by I and MA vacancies or interstitials. Most of these species diffuse rapidly at room temperature, with migration barriers between 0.15 and 0.20 eV. MA interstitials are highly mobile despite their molecular nature, owing to a concerted migration mechanism involving multiple MA ions. No evidence of MA vacancy migration is obtained. Whereas diffusion of I-related defects appreciably depends on their charge state, diffusion of MA defects does not. These results revise the conventional picture of ion transport in hybrid perovskites and highlight the role of collective molecular motion in enabling fast ionic migration.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.02082"></a>
### [Ab initio evidence for spin-polarized and soft-mode instabilities in D-type carbon schwarzite C136](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02082v1)

**Authors:** Eugene Yashin  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02082v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02082_figures/2605.02082_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Phonon density of states for the submitted C136 geometry, computed from finite- displacement force constants using a 10×10×10 sampling mesh. The frequency axis is in THz; negative-frequency spectral weight indicates imaginary phonon modes in the ideal submitted structure.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02082_figures/2605.02082_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Diagnostic phonon band structure for the submitted C136 geometry. The frequency axis is in THz. Substantial branches below zero indicate soft-mode instability. The softest mode along this diagnostic path occurs near q = [0.5, 0.5, 0.5], with frequency approximately −11.90 THz.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper uses first-principles calculations to show that a specific 3D carbon network, C136, is prone to magnetic and structural instabilities due to its flat-band electronic structure. It identifies these schwarzites as candidate platforms where magnetism, lattice distortions, and superconductivity compete.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether the negative Gaussian curvature in 3D sp2-bonded carbon schwarzites (C136) generates electronic structures, such as flat-bands, strong enough to drive broken-symmetry instabilities like magnetism or structural reconstruction.

**Main result.** The study finds that C136 is not a stable metal but exhibits significant spin-polarized instability (lowering energy by ~0.50 eV) and lattice instability characterized by large imaginary-frequency phonon modes.

**Method.** First-principles Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations using the Quantum ESPRESSSE software with the PBE functional, combined with the finite-displacement method via Phonopy for phonon calculations.

**Model / system.** A 136-atom D-type carbon schwarzite (C136), which is a three-dimensional sp2-bonded carbon network with negative Gaussian curvature and non-trivial ring topology.

**Key observables.** Total magnetization (~11.01 Bohr magnetons), energy difference between spin-polarized and non-spin-polarized states, and phonon frequency (specifically the softest mode at -11.90 THz).

**Important parameters / regimes.** High density of states (DOS) near the Fermi level, flat-band features, and the wave vector q = [0.5, 0.5, 0.5] for the softest phonon mode.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The phonon calculations were performed on a 1x1x1 supercell and have not yet been fully converged with respect to supercell size; the structure has not undergone full variable-cell relaxation.

**Figures summary.** Table 1 summarizes SCF calculation results (energy and magnetization); Figure 1 shows the phonon density of states with negative spectral weight; Figure 2 shows the diagnostic phonon band structure highlighting imaginary branches.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the schwarzite system, presents DFT results for electronic and magnetic properties, details phonon calculations revealing structural instability, and concludes by discussing the competition between magnetism, lattice reconstruction, and potential superconductivity.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Carbon schwarzites are three-dimensional sp2-bonded carbon networks with negative Gaussian curvature. Because negative curvature can reorganize the pi-electron manifold, these materials provide a natural platform for flat-band physics, enhanced density of states, and competing broken-symmetry phases. Here I report first-principles calculations for a 136-atom D-type carbon schwarzite cell using density-functional theory. Non-spin-polarized calculations show a metallic electronic structure with pronounced flat-band features and high density of states near the Fermi level. A spin-polarized calculation converges to a magnetic solution with total magnetization of approximately 11.01 Bohr magnetons per 136-atom cell and absolute magnetization of approximately 12.15 Bohr magnetons per cell. The spin-polarized state is lower in energy than the corresponding non-spin-polarized solution by approximately 0.03687 Ry, or about 0.50 eV per cell. Finite-displacement phonon calculations using Phonopy and Quantum ESPRESSO, with 78 symmetry-reduced displaced configurations, further reveal substantial imaginary-frequency modes in the ideal submitted structure. The softest mode in the calculated band path occurs near q = [0.5, 0.5, 0.5], with frequency approximately -11.90 THz. These results indicate that ideal C136 is not a simple harmonically stable metallic carbon phase. Instead, it appears to be a flat-band carbon network close to spin and lattice broken-symmetry instabilities. Possible interpretations include Stoner-like flat-band magnetism and Peierls- or charge-density-wave-like structural reconstruction. Superconductivity is not claimed here as an established ground state; rather, the results identify C136 and related D-type schwarzites as candidate platforms where magnetism, soft-mode reconstruction, and possible doped or structurally stabilized superconducting phases compete.

</details>

### [Analyticity and symmetry of band extrema in gapped solids: when does the effective mass approximation hold?](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02576v1)

**Authors:** Jakob Kjærulff Svaneborg, Kristian Sommer Thygesen  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02576v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02576_figures/2605.02576_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Decision tree for the applicability of Theorem 1. If the band is analytic at k0, the effective mass tensor of Eq. (2) is well defined and Eq. (1) is the leading term of a convergent local power series for En(k). The band dispersion is therefore locally described by the effective mass approximation (1). The GW box’s ”band edges” refers to bands whose energy lies within the analyticity strip Ωdefined in Sec. IV B. For the band edge states (VBM, CBM) this is automatic in any gapped system, while for deeper bands it depends on the spectrum.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02576_figures/2605.02576_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. a) PBE band structures of relaxed and strained mono- layer TiO2. In the strained structure, the length of one of the in-plane crystal axes was increased by 0.1%. This small strain reduces the symmetry of the crystal enough to lift the degen- eracy of the valence bands, but is too small to have a visible effect on the band structure. b, c) Inverse effective mass sur- faces of the valence band maximum (VBM) of (b) relaxed and (c) strained monolayer TiO2. In the relaxed structure, the band is degenerate and warped, and the effective mass approximation does not apply. Applying the small strain, the degeneracy is lifted, and the effective mass approxima- tion now applies to each band...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02576_figures/2605.02576_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Convergence of the electron (top) and hole (bottom) effective masses with step size in the finite difference scheme for monolayer MoS2. If too large a step size is used, an in- correct anisotropy in the effective mass is found. The inset illustrates the geometry of the BZ of MoS2 around the K- point where the VBM and CBM are found. As the M and M′ points are physically equivalent, the Γ−K and K−M di- rections are related by a 180◦rotation; this provides further physical insight into the fact that the effective mass in these two directions must be equivalent for a non-warped band ex- tremum.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02576_figures/2605.02576_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Spectral structure of the correlation self-energy ΣC</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for the effective mass approximation in semiconductors, proving that band dispersion is analytic at non-degenerate extrema for standard computational methods. It also establishes that any observed anisotropy in the effective mass tensor must strictly follow the symmetry of the crystal's point group, providing a way to identify numerical errors in first-principles calculations.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the validity of the effective mass approximation (EMA) by determining whether band dispersion is real-analytic at non-degenerate extrema and establishing symmetry constraints on the effective mass tensor.

**Main result.** The authors prove that band dispersion is real-analytic at any non-degenerate extremum for standard ab initio Hamiltonians (DFT, hybrid DFT, and G0W0), implying that band warping is intrinsically tied to degeneracy. They also provide a symmetry-based classification of the effective mass tensor for all 32 crystallographic point groups.

**Method.** The study employs mathematical proofs using the Implicit Function Theorem, Weierstrass theorem, and Combes-Thomas estimates, alongside group theory for symmetry analysis and numerical finite-difference calculations.

**Model / system.** Gapped solids (semiconductors and insulators), including 2D materials like monolayer MoS2 and TiO2, modeled using DFT (LDA, GGA, meta-GGA), hybrid functionals, and G0W0 quasiparticle energies.

**Key observables.** Effective mass tensor, band dispersion E(k), band warping, and quasiparticle energies.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Non-degenerate vs. degenerate band extrema, little group symmetry (Gk0), and the analyticity strip (Omega) in the frequency domain.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system must be gapped; for G0W0, the quasiparticle correction must be smaller than the Kohn-Sham gap; the proof assumes the non-local potential decays exponentially.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 is a decision tree for the applicability of the analyticity theorem; Figure 2 shows numerical results for monolayer TiO2; Figure 4 illustrates the spectral structure and analyticity region of the correlation self-energy.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the problem of EMA validity, proceeds to a mathematical proof of analyticity for local and non-local Hamiltonians, uses group theory to derive symmetry constraints on the mass tensor, and concludes with numerical applications to MoS2 and TiO2.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The effective mass approximation is widely used across models of carrier transport, optical response, and excitons in semiconductors and insulators, but its validity hinges on the assumption that the band dispersion $E_n(\mathbf{k})$ at the relevant extremum is analytic. We prove that analyticity holds at any non-degenerate extremum for the standard ab initio Hamiltonians, including density functional theory with local or hybrid exchange-correlation functionals and for band-edge $G_0W_0$ quasiparticle energies in gapped systems. Band non-analyticity (or warping) in these settings is therefore intrinsically tied to degeneracy. We then use group theory to determine the symmetry-allowed form of the effective mass tensor for each of the 32 crystallographic point groups, providing a stringent consistency check on first-principles calculations. As a representative application, we show that the electron and hole effective masses at the $K$ point of monolayer MoS$_2$ must be strictly isotropic at the DFT and $G_0W_0$ levels.

</details>

**Authors:** Alex Hallett, Nicola A. Spaldin

The light blue dashed-dotted line is the product of the...</figcaption>

Structural Measures of Chirality A comparison of different structural parameters for chirality including the continuous chirality measure, Hausdorff distances, and helic- ity. The CCM and helicity show a parabolic trend across the pathway between enantiomers, while the Hausdorff distance scales linearly from the achiral reference phase to its maxi- mum amplitude at each enantiomer. None of the parameters are sign sensitive between enantiomers, and all necessitate the existence of an achiral reference phase.</figcaption>

</div>

**Summary.** This paper uses first-principles calculations to show that chirality in the material BTCPO is driven by the coupling of antipolar electric dipoles and electric toroidal dipoles. It identifies specific electronic multipole moments that can act as reliable order parameters to distinguish between different enantiomers. This provides a theoretical framework for quantifying chirality in complex crystal structures.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study seeks to find a rigorous, sign-sensitive quantitative measure for chirality in crystals and to identify the specific multipolar origins of the ferrochiral phase transition in BaTiOCu4(PO4)4.

**Main result.** The authors identify that chirality arises from the combination of antipolar electric dipoles and antiferroaxial electric toroidal dipoles, and demonstrate that multipole moments like the electric toroidal monopole and the w212 moment serve as effective order parameters where standard structural measures fail.

**Method.** First-principles calculations using Density Functional Theory (DFT) with the VASP package, including DFT+U for Cu atoms and non-collinear spin-orbit coupling, alongside multipole expansion of the density matrix.

**Model / system.** The material BaTiOCu4(PO4)4 (BTCPO), which features Cu4O12 cupolas that undergo an antiferroaxial rotation during a ferrochiral phase transition.

**Key observables.** Electric toroidal dipole moments, electric toroidal monopole, electric dipole moments, and the w212 multipole moment.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Hubbard U = 4 eV on Cu atoms; transition temperature of 710 degrees C.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the validity of the PBEsol GGA functional and the DFT+U approximation for describing the electronic and magnetic ground state.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the crystal structure and cupola arrangement; Fig 3 illustrates the rotational patterns of electric dipoles and the resulting toroidal moments; Fig 4 shows the hedgehog-like patterns of toroidal monopoles; Fig 5 compares various structural measures and their failure to distinguish enantiomers.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of quantifying chirality, describes the material and computational methods, presents the identification of multipolar order parameters, evaluates the success/failure of various chirality measures, and concludes with future experimental directions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a first principles study of the ferrochiral phase transition in chiral BaTiOCu$_4$(PO$_4$)$_4$, which has been shown using X-ray diffraction to proceed via the antiferroaxial rotation of antipolar structural units. We analyze the atomic-site electric and magnetic multipole moments to identify connections between these multipoles and chirality and corroborate the previous experimental interpretation. We show that antiferroically ordered atomic-site electric toroidal dipole moments act as an order parameter for the antiferroaxial rotations, and that the overall chiral order is parameterized by the composite order of the antipolar electric dipole and electric toroidal dipole moments. Finally, we evaluate the suitability of various proposed order parameters for chirality, show that some can be excluded and suggest the most promising directions for future exploration.

</details>

**Authors:** Gaolei Zhao, Juhe Liu, Jinkai Huo, Tian Han, Yunhao Tong, Hu Wang, Konstantin Kozadaev, Andrei Zheltkovich, Changsen Sun, Alexei Tolstik, Andrey Novitsky, Lujun Pan, Dawei Li

**Summary.** This paper reports the successful creation of tunable NbOI2-xClx alloys where the electrical and optical properties can be precisely engineered by adjusting the iodine-to-chlorine ratio. By controlling the composition, the researchers tuned the bandgap, ferroelectric response, and transistor characteristics, providing a versatile platform for next-generation nanoelectronics and optoelectronics.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty in achieving non-volatile, precise modulation of the optical and electrical properties in the NbOX2 (X = I, Cl) family of van der Waals ferroelectrics.

**Main result.** The researchers demonstrated that NbOI2-xClx alloys can be grown with controlled stoichiometry, allowing for the precise tuning of bandgap, lattice parameters, ferroelectric SHG signals, and FET transport properties.

**Method.** Chemical Vapor Transport (CVT) was used for crystal growth, combined with XRD, Raman, and EDX for structural characterization, DFT for electronic structure calculations, and SHG and FET measurements for optical and electrical analysis.

**Model / system.** The system consists of NbOI2-xClx, a series of 2D van der Waals ferroelectric alloys with a low-symmetry monoclinic crystal structure (space group C2) where Nb atoms are displaced from the center of the octahedra.

**Key observables.** Bandgap (Eg), Second Harmonic Generation (SHG) intensity and polarization, threshold voltage (Vth), carrier mobility (mu), and photodetector responsivity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The Cl molar ratio (x), interlayer spacing, and the nonlinear optical tensor ratio (d21/d22).

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Crystal structure, growth method, and EDX composition verification; Fig 2: XRD patterns, lattice spacing, Raman modes, and calculated band structures; Fig 3: SHG setup, thickness dependence, and polarization patterns; Fig 4: FET transfer and output characteristics; Fig 5: Photodetector photo-response and polarization sensitivity.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the synthesis and structural verification of the alloys, followed by the investigation of electronic band structure changes via DFT, then moves to nonlinear optical properties via SHG, and concludes with the electrical performance of FETs and photodetectors.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Layered niobium oxide dihalides NbOX2 (X = I, Cl), as a new family of Van der Waals (vdW) ferroelectrics, have attracted extensive attention, while achieving non-volatile modulation of their optical and electrical properties remains challenging, thereby limiting their integration into next-generation nanoelectronics and optoelectronics. Here, we report the controlled fabrication of highly crystalline NbOI2-xClx vdW alloys with composition-driven tunable optical and electrical properties via a chemical vapor transport method. Comprehensive experimental characterization combined with first-principles calculation shows that the crystal lattices, phonon modes, and band structures of NbOI2-xClx can be well tailored, which are distributed between NbOI2 and NbOCl2. Both the amplitude and polarization of second harmonic generation optical signal in NbOI2-xClx exhibit pronounced compositional dependence, offering optical evidence for tunable in-plane ferroelectric characteristic. Moreover, field-effect transistors based on NbOI2-xClx display robust n-type semiconducting behavior, with threshold voltage and carrier mobility precisely modulated through adjustment of I/Cl molar ratio. Furthermore, 2D NbOI2-xClx photodetectors across all compositions exhibit exceptional gate-tunable current on/off ratio and strong polarization-sensitive photo-response. This study thus provides a new vdW ferroelectric material platform with tunable optical and electrical properties, paving the path for its implementation in modern nanophotonics and nanoelectronics.

</details>

**Authors:** Yang Huang, Jingrun Chen  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02267v1>

**Summary.** This paper presents a new symbolic regression method that discovers simple mathematical formulas to predict material properties from chemical composition. Unlike deep learning, it provides interpretable equations that capture periodic trends in the periodic table. The model performs as well as complex black-box models while using much less computational memory and parameters.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of predicting materials properties from chemical composition with high accuracy and physical interpretability, avoiding the 'black-box' nature of traditional neural networks.

**Main result.** The framework achieves competitive accuracy with state-of-the-art models using significantly fewer parameters, providing explicit analytical expressions and recovering periodic chemical trends.

**Method.** A hybrid search algorithm combining Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with Genetic Programming (GP), supplemented by gradient-based refinement using L-BFGS.

**Model / system.** A composition-weighted symbolic regression framework that learns analytical functional forms and elemental weights directly from chemical composition without predefined descriptors.

**Key observables.** Band gap (MAE), metallicity (ROC-AUC), and glass formation (ROC-AUC).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Learnable elemental weights (w_i) for each element in the periodic table and composition-weighted latent descriptors.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes that physically meaningful governing relations are typically compact and structured, and that the optimization landscape is piecewise differentiable.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the hybrid MCTS-GP framework; Figure 2 visualizes periodic trends in learned elemental weights; Figure 3 shows the relationship between a gating factor and band gap; Figure 4 compares predicted vs. experimental band gaps for III-V alloys.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of interpretable property prediction, details the hybrid MCTS-GP architecture and optimization strategy, presents benchmarks on MatBench tasks, validates the model on III-V semiconductor alloys, and discusses limitations regarding complexity and data dependency.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce a composition-weighted symbolic regression framework for interpretable prediction of materials properties directly from chemical composition. The method jointly learns analytical functional forms and task-dependent elemental weightings without predefined descriptors. By incorporating max/min operators, it naturally enforces constraints such as non-negative band gaps and bounded classification probabilities, unifying regression and classification tasks. Efficient search is achieved through a hybrid Monte Carlo tree search--genetic programming algorithm with gradient-based refinement and parallel computation. Benchmarks on MatBench tasks show competitive accuracy relative to state-of-the-art black-box models while yielding explicit analytical expressions. Applied to III--V semiconductor alloys, the model produces smooth composition-dependent trends and learned elemental weights with chemically meaningful periodic behavior. This framework provides a scalable and interpretable route for materials discovery and property screening.

</details>

(b) Schematic of the energy conversion demonstration circuit with temperature controlled varying around Tc. 1 ○- 4 ○represents four stages of the thermo- dynamic cycle: 1 ○: heating from low temperature to Tc; 2 ○: heating from Tc to high temperature; 3 ○: cooling from high temperature to Tc; 4 ○: cooling from Tc to low temperature. (c) Latent heat of tetragonal-cubic phase transformation in BST material system and energy conversion demon- stration results of (d) Sr0.07 (e) Sr0.19 and (f) Sr0.28 bulk ceramic samples. (g) Work done on the 1 MΩload...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01791_figures/2605.01791_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. (a) Schematic of the Sr0.19 MLCC device structure. (b) Cross sectional SEM and EBSD images of the Sr0.19 MLCC device. (c) P-T curves of Sr0.19 MLCC and bulk sample with room- temperature P-E loops . (d) The current output details and (f) overview of Sr0.19 MLCC during long-cycle energy conversion demonstration. (g) Work done on the 1 MΩload resistor over 10,000 cycles of Sr0.19 MLCC.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates how tuning the order of a phase transition from first-order to second-order via chemical doping can optimize energy harvesting. By finding a 'sweet spot' in the Ba1-xSrxTiO3 system, the researchers achieved a device that is both highly efficient and capable of stable operation over 10,000 cycles.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Optimizing pyroelectric energy harvesting from low-grade heat by resolving the trade-off between high energy density (from first-order phase transitions) and high operational stability/low leakage (from second-order phase transitions).

**Main result.** The identification of a compositional 'transitional regime' in Ba1-xSrxTiO3 (specifically x=0.19) that achieves optimal lattice compatibility, suppressing leakage by two orders of magnitude while maintaining high energy conversion efficiency (5.5%) and long-term stability.

**Method.** The study uses solid-state synthesis, Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) for thermal analysis, Synchrotron X-ray Diffraction for structural characterization, and FerroAI (a deep-learning model) for phase diagram prediction.

**Model / system.** The physical system is Barium Strontium Titanate (Ba1-xSrxTiO3) ceramics, specifically focusing on the evolution of ferroelectric-to-paraelectric phase transitions as a function of Strontium doping.

**Key observables.** Phase transformation order, latent heat, lattice distortion (via peak splitting), pyroelectric coefficient (|dP/dT|), leakage current density, and energy conversion efficiency.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Sr content (x in [0, 0.3]), phase transformation temperature (Tc), lattice compatibility metric (|lambda2 - 1|), and energy density (mJ/cm3).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that the nominal Sr content accurately represents the final chemical composition and that numerical differentiation of P-T profiles is sufficient for calculating pyroelectric coefficients.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows DSC heat flow curves, predicted vs. experimental phase diagrams via FerroAI, and latent heat vs. Sr composition. Figure 5 shows the multilayer capacitor (MLCC) architecture.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the energy harvesting problem, details the material synthesis and characterization methods, presents the discovery of the transitional phase regime through thermal and structural analysis, demonstrates device-level energy conversion in an MLCC, and concludes with stability testing.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Phase-transforming pyroelectric materials have emerged as promising candidates for low-grade thermal energy harvesting. However, whether first-order transformations with large pyroelectric coefficient or second-order transformations with better reversibility are preferable remains unclear. Here we report compositionally tunable phase transformations in Ba$_{1-x}$Sr$_x$TiO$_3$ ($x \in [0, 0.3]$), revealing evolution from first-order to second-order character. We identify a transitional regime between Sr$_{0.15}$ and Sr$_{0.22}$ where transformation mechanism fundamentally changes. Within this regime, Sr$_{0.19}$ achieves optimal lattice compatibility, exhibiting electrical leakage suppressed by over two orders of magnitude while retaining substantial polarization response. Energy conversion demonstrations show the multilayer Sr$_{0.19}$ device delivers pyroelectric current of $\sim$1.6 $μ$A at 64$~^\circ$C with an energy density of 1.6 mJ/cm$^3$ per cycle and 5.5\% conversion efficiency. Remarkably, this composition operates stably over 10,000 full energy conversion cycles without external bias field or recharging, demonstrating that transitional regime compositions provide the optimal balance between energy density and operational durability for practical low-grade heat harvesting.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new way to calculate electron density in complex materials by using simple, explicit formulas derived from a model electron gas. By using a 'connector' technique, the authors can achieve high accuracy without the heavy computational cost of solving the full Kohn-Sham equations, making it a promising route for rapid materials design.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of developing simple, explicit, and computationally efficient functionals for electron charge density that depend directly on the Kohn-Sham potential, avoiding the need to solve the full Schrödinger equation.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that a hierarchy of approximations, specifically the 'Connector of the Theory' (COT) approach, can accurately and efficiently approximate charge density in highly inhomogeneous systems like cubic helium.

**Method.** The method uses a 'connector' strategy to replace higher-order Taylor expansion terms of a real system with optimized data from a Homogeneous Electron Gas (HEG) model, utilizing the Lindhard response function.

**Model / system.** The study uses the Homogeneous Electron Gas (HEG) as a fundamental building block and tests the developed functionals on cubic helium, a prototypical strongly inhomogeneous solid, under various compression and expansion states.

**Key observables.** Electron charge density n(r), total electron number (N_el), Hartree energy, and orbital-free kinetic energy functionals (TFvW, PC07).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Kohn-Sham potential (v_KS), Lindhard density-density response function, lattice constants (ranging from 2.5 to 15 Bohr), and the alpha-ansatz parameters (A and B).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The chemical potential is assumed to be zero (valid for extended systems); the study focuses on the Kohn-Sham potential as a given input rather than including explicit interaction effects in the functional itself.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares KS potentials with connector potentials; Figure 2 shows Taylor expansion accuracy; Figure 3 compares various density approximations for helium; Figure 5 shows density profiles; Figure 6 shows parameter fitting results; Figure 7 shows density under compression; Figure 9 and 10 show error distributions (MADE) and errors in physical observables.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of density functionals, defines the HEG model and the COT strategy, presents a hierarchy of approximations (LPA to COT1-alpha), tests these against benchmark KS results using cubic helium, evaluates error transferability across different densities, and discusses future extensions to non-local potentials.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

One of the most powerful strategies to address properties of real many-body systems is to incorporate data obtained for models, for example, to use data of the homogeneous electron gas in order to build the Local Density Approximation for the Kohn-Sham exchange-correlation potential. In the present work, we examine to what extent we can use model data to design functionals directly for observables of materials. In particular, we study different approximations for the charge density of real inhomogeneous materials expressed as a simple, explicit functional of a given Kohn-Sham potential, using as central building block the Lindhard density-density response function of the homogeneous electron gas. Our increasingly realistic set of approximations includes a fully nearsighted expression equivalent to the Thomas-Fermi approximation, functional Taylor expansions, and different approximations to the Connector Theory developed in [Aouina \textit{et al.}, npj Computational Materials {\bf 11}, 242 (2025)]. In all cases, the charge density is obtained without ever solving the Kohn-Sham Schrödinger equation. Results for cubic helium, a prototypical strongly inhomogeneous material, systematically improve with higher levels of approximation, indicating that this is a promising route to obtain expressions that are relatively simple to calculate and to analyze.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a method to deterministically engineer local strain and bandgap distributions in 2D materials by designing the underlying substrate geometry. By using nanostructures like rings and lobes, the researchers can precisely control multiaxial strain, providing a predictive framework for creating complex optoelectronic landscapes.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of achieving precise, deterministic, and spatially resolved control over local multiaxial strain distributions and resulting bandgap profiles in 2D materials for device applications.

**Main result.** The authors developed a material-agnostic platform using engineered nanostructures to prescribe specific strain profiles, demonstrating that a two-component analytical model can predict spatially-resolved bandgap shifts with less than 12% error.

**Method.** The study combines experimental hyperspectral photoluminescence (PL) mapping and AFM topography with Finite Element Analysis (FEA) and Density Functional Theory (DFT) to quantify strain gauge factors and validate predictive models.

**Model / system.** The platform uses suspended 2D semiconductor membranes (Ga2Se2 and WS2) draped over lithographically defined silicon-on-insulator (SOI) nanostructures, such as rings, ridges, and lobed geometries.

**Key observables.** PL peak shifts (redshift), PL intensity, in-plane strain components (biaxial and uniaxial), and AFM-measured membrane thickness and deformation profiles.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Strain gauge factors (e.g., biaxial beta approx -268 meV/% strain), nanostructure dimensions (4-14 um rings), and membrane thicknesses (75-257 nm).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analytical model assumes a linear regime for bandgap reduction and the FEA assumes a 2D membrane model with zero out-of-plane stiffness.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the nanostructure schematic; Figure 2 compares strain maps and gauge factors; Figure 3 shows PL shift predictions for rings and ridges; Figure 4 demonstrates strain cross-talk in arrays; Figure 5 shows strain gradients in lobed structures; Figure 6 shows application to WS2.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of strain control, presents the experimental platform and material characterization, establishes a quantitative strain-to-bandgap model via rings and ridges, demonstrates complex multiaxial strain in lobed structures, and generalizes the method to WS2.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Emerging classical and quantum device concepts demand precise spatial control over the optoelectronic properties of two-dimensional (2D) materials, but deterministic engineering via local multiaxial strain distributions remains challenging. Using Ga$_2$Se$_2$, we demonstrate a material-agnostic platform in which nanostructure geometry deterministically prescribes in-plane strain profiles in suspended van der Waals membranes. We first use hyperspectral photoluminescence mapping and experimentally-constrained finite element analysis to quantify the experimental biaxial and uniaxial strain gauge factors that relate strain to the change in bandgap. We next show that a two-component analytical model can predict, with less than 12% error, spatially-resolved bandgap shifts arising from multiaxial strain distributions in complex geometries, including the interactions between adjacent nanostructures. Finally, we demonstrate that this approach can be extended to other materials. The results demonstrate that nanostructure design provides a quantitative, deterministic framework for the realization of designed strain and bandgap distributions in 2D materials.

</details>
