### [Dirac Semimetal Phase in Rhombohedral $β-$Cu$_{2}$Se](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01142v1)

**Authors:** Thomas Steele, Becker Sharif, David Lederman, Xiangang Wan, Sergey Y. Savrasov  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01142v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</figure>

The Dirac points occur along kz direction and are indicated by blue dots. b) Calculated Fermi arc surface states for β−Cu2Se that are shown in the surface Brillouin Zone corresponding to the (100) surface.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the rhombohedral beta-phase of Cu2Se is a Dirac semimetal, distinguishing it from the quadratic contact point found in the cubic alpha-phase. It predicts the existence of topologically protected Fermi arcs with a spin texture that suppresses backscattering, suggesting potential for high-mobility electronic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining whether the rhombohedral beta-phase of Cu2Se realizes a Dirac semimetal state and identifying the presence of topologically protected Fermi arc surface states.

**Main result.** The study predicts that the beta-phase of Cu2Se is a Dirac semimetal with protected Dirac points and surface Fermi arcs that exhibit a helical spin texture, potentially leading to ultrahigh carrier mobility due to suppressed backscattering.

**Method.** Density Functional Theory (DFT) using the Full-potential Linear Muffin-Tin Orbital (FP-LMTO) method with spin-orbit coupling, and a tight-binding approach for surface slab calculations.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the rhombohedral beta-phase of Cu2Se, a non-stoichiometric material with space-group symmetry R3m, which can be viewed as a strained version of the cubic alpha-phase.

**Key observables.** Dirac dispersions, Fermi arc surface states, spin texture (helical-like), and carrier mobility.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Lattice parameters (a = b = 4.12 A, c = 20.45 A), c/a ratio of 4.963, and the presence of inversion and time-reversal symmetry.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The transport discussion assumes a thin-film geometry where bulk contributions are negligible, and surface calculations use a tight-binding transformation rather than full self-consistent slab calculations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the crystal structures of the alpha and beta phases; Figure 2 displays the calculated band structures for both phases; Figure 3 illustrates the Brillouin Zone with Dirac points and the calculated Fermi arc surface states on the (100) surface.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the structural difference between alpha and beta Cu2Se, presents DFT calculations of the bulk electronic structure, compares the two phases, details the surface state calculations, and discusses the implications for transport properties.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Having been extensively studied during last decades in the fields of thermoelectics and ionic conductors, the $α$ phase of Cu$_{2}$Se with antfluoride crystal structure has recently emerged as a topological zero-gap semimetal with a quadratic contact point which exists at the Fermi surface of its bulk electronic spectrum. Here we argue based on density functional electronic structure calculation that the $β$ phase of Cu$_{2}$Se realized in a recently discovered rhombohedral structure shows a Dirac semimetal behavior of the electrons near the Fermi level. These topological semimetals are currently generating a lot of interest due to unusual transport phenomena, such as strong quantum oscillations, large magnetoresistance effect and ultrahigh carrier mobilities with their Fermi velocities potentially exceeding graphene. We show that there exist Fermi arc states at the surface spectrum of $β-$Cu$_{2}$Se that are topologically protected by the bulk Dirac points. Their shape and spin properties should be resilient to the back- and side scattering effects in the surface transport, suggesting new ways for realizing high-mobility electronic devices.

</details>

</div>

**Summary.** This paper investigates how the mechanical strength of 3D-printed spinodal structures changes depending on their size and loading speed. It discovers that large-scale structures experience a massive strength boost due to inertia at high speeds, a phenomenon not seen in micro-scale structures. The authors provide a scaling framework using dimensionless numbers to predict these mechanical regimes.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Understanding how the dynamic mechanical response of spinodal architected materials scales across different length and time scales, specifically the interplay between material strain-rate sensitivity and inertial effects.

**Main result.** The study identifies that macroscale specimens transition to an inertia-dominated regime at high strain rates, causing a significant strength increase, whereas microscale specimens remain dominated by the constituent material's strain-rate sensitivity.

**Method.** High-throughput compression testing across seven decades of strain rate ($10^{-3}$ to $10^4$ s$^{-1}$) combined with extensive Finite Element (FE) simulations to decouple inertial and rate-sensitive effects.

**Model / system.** Spinodal architected materials (bicontinuous, shell-based topologies) made from rate-sensitive polymeric constituents, tested at microscale (100 $\mu$m) and macroscale (30 mm) dimensions.

**Key observables.** Peak strength (yield/compressive response), nominal stress, back-face stress, and material-point velocity distributions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Strain rate, topological length scale, constituent material strain-rate sensitivity, and dimensionless groups representing the ratio of inertial to rate-sensitive forces.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The spinodal architecture is treated as a representative topology; for numerical modeling, the constituent material is approximated as an isotropic elastic perfectly plastic solid.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the architecture and fabrication routes; Figure 2 details the experimental setups; Figure 3 shows material rate-dependence; Figure 4 compares deformation modes; Figure 5/6 present FE results isolating different physical mechanisms; Figure 7 provides dimensionless regime maps and velocity distributions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scaling problem, describes the fabrication and experimental platforms for micro/macro scales, presents experimental results for both scales, uses FE simulations to decouple inertia from rate sensitivity, and concludes with a generalized dimensionless scaling framework.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

High-throughput characterization of architected materials across a wide range of length scales enables rapid screening of topologies for engineering applications. Scaled-down specimens manufactured and evaluated in laboratory environments enable this iteration, but application scenarios may involve differing length scales and loading conditions that complicate direct comparisons. Here, we use a spinodal architected morphology to determine the interplay among the constituent material's strain-rate sensitivity, the topological length scale, and the imposed deformation rates. We report characterization spanning strain rates from $10^{-3}$ s$^{-1}$ to $10^{4}$ s$^{-1}$ on spinodal architected specimens with length scales of 100 $μ$m (microscale) and 30 mm (macroscale). The experiments show that while microscale specimens exhibit moderate increase in strength at high strain rates, macroscale specimens exhibit a nearly tenfold increase in strength at equivalent strain rates. Finite element calculations reveal that this increase is linked to a transition from a response governed by constituent material strain-rate sensitivity to inertia-dominated behavior in macroscale specimens, a transition not observed in microscale specimens at the strain rates investigated here. Using extensive finite element calculations, we develop maps to establish the parameters governing the regimes of behavior, illustrating that the transition from behavior governed by constituent material rate sensitivity to inertia-dominated behavior has analogies to fluids in that it depends on a structural length scale. Our findings provide insights into the physical parameters that govern responses across length and time scales, towards the development and design of new laboratory experiments that extract relevant dynamic properties for structural applications.

</details>

**Authors:** Matthew Frazier, Guillaume Bal  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02100v1>

The spectral flow is now modified by both JiR and JiL, i ∈{1, 3}. For the first jump Ω(1) −= −0.4, Ω(1) + = 0.8 which yields ˆω(1) e = 0.5941 and ˆω(3) e = 2.3356. For the second jump Ω(2) −= −1 and Ω(2) + = 1 yielding ˆω(1) e = 1 and ˆω(3) e = 2.5616 = E2. Therefore the blue shaded region now has a spectral flow of -1, yellow +2, and red +3.</figcaption>

Ω(y) is a linear transition between ΩS = −1 and ΩN = 1 with a jump at y = 0 as in Figures 2 and 3. Left models positive jumps and right negative jumps.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the standard Bulk-Edge Correspondence in photonic systems is sensitive to whether the interface between media is smooth or sharp. It introduces a new 'anomalable BEC' to account for edge modes that arise specifically from discontinuities in the magnetic field bias.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how the regularity (smoothness vs. discontinuity) of an interface between two different magnetic media affects the Bulk-Edge Correspondence (BEC) and the emergence of edge modes in continuum photonic systems.

**Main result.** The authors find that while continuous interfaces preserve the standard BEC, discontinuities in the magnetic field bias introduce anomalous edge modes that alter the spectral flow, necessitating a new 'anomalous BEC' framework.

**Method.** The study uses analytical derivations of asymptotic dispersion relations, spectral analysis of self-adjoint Hamiltonians, and numerical ODE-based methods to compare BDI and spatial cutoff regularization schemes.

**Model / system.** The system consists of transverse magnetic (TM) wave propagation in continuum photonic systems, specifically modeled as light-matter interacting cold plasmas (gyrotropic media) with varying magnetic field bias.

**Key observables.** Chern numbers, Bulk-Difference Invariants (BDIs), edge state spectral flow, and edge current (asymmetric transport).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic field bias (cyclotron frequency), plasma frequency, interface regularity (continuous vs. discontinuous), and wavenumber (k).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study focuses on the interface between two cold plasmas to avoid complex boundary conditions and assumes the system can be modeled via local Drude or linearized hydrodynamic approximations.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the energy spectrum for different interface profiles, demonstrating how discontinuities create new spectral branches and how regularization affects the stability of edge modes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of interface regularity, presents the local Drude and hydrodynamic models, applies regularization techniques (BDI and SC), analyzes the spectral properties of edge modes, and concludes with a new anomalous BEC framework.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this study we analyze the topological invariants and edge states of transverse magnetic wave propagation in continuum photonic systems at a finite-width interface between two gyrotropic matrials with different magnetic bias. Where previous studies have almost exclusively considered sharp transitions between two different electromagnetic media, we consider the more general geometry where the magnetic field bias is allowed to vary arbitrarily in a finite-width interface between to bulk regions. We find that when the magnetic field bias varies continuously between the two bulk regions, the Bulk Edge Correspondence (BEC) holds robustly with respect to well-defined Chern invariants. However, discontinuities in the magnetic field bias introduce edge modes which are highly localized at the associated discontinuity and whose spectral properties alter the BEC. We analyze the spectral properties of these edge modes and define a new anomalous BEC in continuum photonic systems which includes contributions from topological invariants and discontinuities in magnetic field bias.

</details>

**Summary.** This review explores how the unique electronic and nonlinear properties of graphene can be engineered to create next-generation THz technologies. It highlights how graphene-based devices, especially when integrated with metamaterials, can achieve high-speed, tunable, and scalable light generation, detection, and modulation.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of developing efficient, scalable, and integrated technologies for the generation, detection, and manipulation of terahertz (THz) frequency light (0.1–10 THz) to overcome the limitations of bulky, expensive current setups.

**Main result.** The review establishes graphene as a key disruptor for THz engineering due to its tunable and nonlinear response, demonstrating how integration with metamaterials and other semiconductors can significantly enhance modulation speed and efficiency.

**Method.** The paper provides a comprehensive review of graphene's fundamental physics, transport properties, and nonlinear optical response, alongside an analysis of various device architectures including GFETs, metamaterials, and hybrid heterostructures.

**Model / system.** The primary material is graphene, specifically focusing on monolayer, bilayer, and twisted bilayer graphene, as well as graphene-based heterostructures (e.g., with hBN) and integration with CMOS, antennas, and metamaterial matrices.

**Key observables.** Noise Equivalent Power (NEP), responsivity, modulation depth (MD), modulation/reconfiguration speed (cut-off frequency), optical activity (OA), and circular dichroism (CD).

**Important parameters / regimes.** THz frequency range (0.1–10 THz), Fermi energy (Ef), scattering rate, temperature, and photon energy (h-omega) relative to the Pauli blocking threshold (2Ef).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The review assumes the relevance of the Drude and Drude-Smith models for conductivity and focuses on the intraband-dominated regime at THz frequencies.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the graphene band structure and light-matter interaction; Figure 3 illustrates responsivity maps and modulation depth improvements in graphene/Si and graphene/Ge; Figure 5 depicts the architectural evolution of graphene modulators from large-area to resonant metamaterials.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with graphene physics (band structure and transport), moves to detection mechanisms (PTE, PB, and plasma-wave effects), discusses advanced quantum-limited detection and modulation, and concludes with the evolution of high-speed metamaterial-integrated architectures.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Graphene has been one of the most investigated materials in the last decade. Its unique optoelectronic properties have indeed raised it to an ideal and revolutionary candidate for the development of entirely novel technologies across the whole electromagnetic spectrum, from the microwaves to the x-rays, even crossing domain of intense application relevance, as terahertz (THz) frequencies. Owing to its exceptionally high tensile strength, electrical conductivity, transparency, ultra-fast carrier dynamics, non-linear optical response to intense fields, electrical tunability and ease of integration with semiconductor materials, graphene is a key disruptor for the engineering of generation, manipulation, and detection technologies with ad-hoc properties, conceived from scratch. In this review, we elucidate the fundamental properties of graphene, with an emphasis on its transport, electronic, ultrafast and non-linear interactions, and explore its enormous technological potential of integration with a diverse array of material platforms. We start with a concise introduction to graphene physics, followed by the most remarkable technological developments of graphene-based photodetectors, modulators, and sources in the 1-10 THz frequency range. As such, this review aims to serve as a valuable resource for a broad audience, ranging from novices to experts, who are keen to explore graphene physics for conceiving and realizing micro- and nano-scale devices and systems in the far infrared. This would allow addressing the present challenging application needs in quantum science, wireless communications, ultrafast science, plasmonics and nanophotonics.

</details>

**Authors:** A K M Ashiquzzaman Shawon, Eoghan Downey, Shane Smolenski, Thomas J. Hicken, Amir Henderson, Mingyu Xu, Trisha Musall, Rafael Lopes Sabainsk, Yuan Zhu, Weiwei Xie, Elena Gati, Lu Li, Zurab Guguchia, Na Hyun Jo

**Summary.** This paper provides experimental evidence for altermagnetism in Cr-doped FeSb2 by demonstrating a spin-compensated magnetic state that breaks time-reversal symmetry. The observation of a spontaneous anomalous Hall effect and bulk magnetic order supports the theoretical prediction of a d-wave altermagnetic ground state.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** To investigate the magnetic ground state of Cr-doped FeSb2 and provide experimental evidence for the existence of altermagnetic order.

**Main result.** The study identifies a spin-compensated magnetic ground state with broken time-reversal symmetry, evidenced by a spontaneous anomalous Hall effect and bulk magnetic order below 3.5 K.

**Method.** Single crystals of Fe1-xCrxSb2 were grown via the Sb-flux method and characterized using X-ray diffraction, magnetometry, electrical transport measurements, and Muon Spin Relaxation (uSR).

**Model / system.** The physical system is Cr-doped FeSb2 (specifically Fe0.85Cr0.15Sb2), which possesses an orthorhombic marcasite-type crystal structure and is a candidate for d-wave altermagnetism.

**Key observables.** Magnetic susceptibility, magnetization, longitudinal resistivity, Hall resistivity, magnetoresistance, and muon spin relaxation decay rates.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Néel temperature (TN) of approximately 3.5 K, Cr-doping concentration (x), and magnetic anisotropy along the b-axis.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The magnetic order is assumed to be intrinsic and bulk-like rather than due to impurity phases, a claim supported by uSR.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the crystal structure, XRD patterns, and lattice parameters; Fig 2 displays temperature-dependent resistivity, magnetoresistance, Hall resistivity, and magnetization; Fig 3 presents uSR spectra under zero and longitudinal fields.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with crystal growth and structural characterization, followed by magnetic and transport property measurements, then presents muon spin relaxation data to confirm bulk order, and concludes with a discussion of the altermagnetic state.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Altermagnets are a class of materials with compensated magnetic moments, in which spin sublattices are related by specific symmetries other than inversion or translation. This allows time-reversal symmetry to be broken without a net magnetization. Here, we synthesize single crystals of Fe1-xCrxSb2 and investigate their electrical transport and magnetic properties, with a focus on Fe0.85Cr0.15Sb2. Magnetization measurements suggest spin-compensated ordering below ~ 3.5 K, where magnetic moments align along the crystallographic b-direction. Transport measurements reveal a crossover from large positive to negative magnetoresistance, while an anomalous Hall response emerges below 3.5 K, indicating time-reversal symmetry breaking below TN. Muon spin relaxation measurements demonstrate bulk magnetic order below 3.5 K, confirming that the low temperature ordering is intrinsic rather than due to an impurity phase. These results support a potential altermagnetic ground state in Cr-doped FeSb2 with time-reversal symmetry breaking without net magnetization.

</details>

**Summary.** The researchers developed a way to maximize the production of high-energy photons from low-energy light by engineering the electronic structure of organic molecules. By tuning the energy levels to favor singlet formation during triplet collisions, they achieved high upconversion yields compatible with solar energy technologies.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The efficiency of sensitized triplet-triplet annihilation upconversion (sTTA-UC) is limited by a low statistical factor (f) for singlet formation, often due to energy loss through accessible triplet states.

**Main result.** By engineering the excited-state energy landscape of a naphthalene-derived annihilator, the authors achieved a singlet generation yield (f) of 0.83 and an absolute upconversion yield of 0.19.

**Method.** The study combines molecular engineering via selective substitution, DFT/TD-DFT calculations to model the energy landscape, and experimental spectroscopy (steady-state and time-resolved) to validate the TTA process.

**Model / system.** A dual-dye solution consisting of an iridium complex sensitizer, Ir(ppy)2(acac), and a naphthalene-derived annihilator, TIPS-Ph-Naph, operating via Dexter energy transfer and triplet-triplet annihilation.

**Key observables.** Photon conversion quantum yield (phi_uc), statistical factor (f), upconversion peak wavelength, excitation intensity threshold (I_th), and triplet lifetime.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The transition from quadratic to linear power dependency, the activation threshold of 0.71 mW/cm2 under broadband excitation, and the resonance between the T1T1 complex and high-energy Sn states.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The design assumes that tuning the energy landscape to favor resonances with high-energy singlets is more effective than the traditional approach of avoiding all resonances.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1a shows the sTTA-UC photophysical process; Fig 4a compares absorption and excitation spectra; Fig 4b shows the upconversion intensity vs. excitation intensity, illustrating the quadratic-to-linear transition and solar compatibility.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the sTTA-UC efficiency bottleneck, describes the molecular design strategy and synthesis of the engineered annihilator, presents computational modeling of the energy landscape, provides experimental validation of the high singlet yield and upconversion efficiency, and concludes with the system's performance under solar-simulated conditions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Photon upconverters are anti Stokes emitters capable of generating photons with higher energy than those absorbed. This behavior can be achieved through different mechanisms, which are extensively studied for applications in imaging, anticounterfeiting, information encryption and most importantly, solar energy technologies. Among these mechanisms, photon upconversion based on sensitized triplet-triplet annihilation (sTTA-UC) is particularly attractive because it operates under low intensity, incoherent light. In sTTA UC, two optically dark triplet states of a conjugated annihilator fuse upon collision to populate a higher energy fluorescent singlet, with the triplets initially generated via energy transfer from a lower energy sensitizer.   Here we introduce a general molecular design strategy to maximize singlet generation through TTA. By selective substitution, we engineered a naphthalene derived annihilator with an excited state energy landscape that strongly favors singlet formation, achieving yields up to 0.83. When combined with an appropriate triplet sensitizer, the system delivers stable UV/visible upconversion peaking at 390 nm, with an absolute upconversion yield about 0.19 and an activation excitation intensity threshold lower than 0.1 suns under non-coherent broadband excitation fully compatible with the requirements of solar powered technologies.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.01593"></a>
### [First-principles simulation of shocked H-He mixture along the principal Hugoniot](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01593v1)

**Authors:** Ammar A. Ellaboudy, Valentin V. Karasiev, S. X. Hu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01593v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable

**Summary.** This paper uses advanced first-principles simulations to study the optical properties of shock-compressed hydrogen-helium mixtures. It concludes that changes in optical reflectivity may not be a definitive signature of phase separation at low helium concentrations, challenging previous experimental interpretations of H-He demixing.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether optical reflectivity discontinuities can serve as a reliable diagnostic for H-He immiscibility (phase separation) under shock-compressed conditions relevant to Jupiter's interior.

**Main result.** The simulations show no reflectivity discontinuity at elevated temperatures and find that reflectivity predictions for a mixed system are consistent with experimental data in the demixing regime, suggesting reflectivity alone is not a sensitive diagnostic for low helium concentrations.

**Method.** Ab initio molecular dynamics simulations using the thermal Tr2SCANL meta-GGA functional for the equation of state and the RS-KDT0 range-separated thermal hybrid XC functional for optical properties via the Kubo-Greenwood formalism.

**Model / system.** A shock-compressed mixture of Hydrogen and Helium containing 11% helium (atomic fraction) under warm dense matter conditions.

**Key observables.** Optical reflectivity, equation of state, and ionic configurations.

**Important parameters / regimes.** 11% helium atomic fraction, shock-compressed regime, warm dense matter regime.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the validity of the thermal Tr2SCANL and RS-KDT0 exchange-correlation functionals for predicting properties in the warm dense matter regime.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of H-He demixing in Jupiter, describes the ab initio molecular dynamics and Kubo-Greenwood methodology, presents the calculated reflectivity and equation of state, and concludes by comparing results to experimental laser-shock data to evaluate the diagnostic utility of reflectivity.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recent laser-shock experiments on an H--He mixture containing 11~$\%$ helium (atomic fraction) have suggested the presence of an immiscibility region inside Jupiter. Reflectivity measurements were used as the primary diagnostic of H--He demixing, with discontinuities in the optical reflectivity proposed as a signature of phase separation under conditions relevant to Jupiter's interior. Here, we investigate shock-compressed H--He using \textit{ab initio} molecular dynamics simulations with optical properties evaluated within the Kubo--Greenwood formalism. The equation of state and ionic configurations were obtained using the thermal Tr$^2$SCANL meta-GGA exchange--correlation (XC) functional, while optical properties were computed using the recently developed RS-KDT0 range-separated thermal hybrid XC, which provides state-of-the-art accuracy for band-gap predictions in the warm dense matter regime. The calculated reflectivity shows overall good agreement with experimental measurements; however, no discontinuity is observed at elevated temperatures. Moreover, the reflectivity predictions for the mixed system are consistent with the experimental measurements in the temperature range where the mixture is inferred to be demixed. These results suggest that reflectivity alone may not provide a unique or sensitive diagnostic of H-He demixing at low helium concentrations under these conditions.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Generalized continuum theory of phonon angular momentum in crystals](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01678v1)

**Authors:** Mamoru Matsuo, Naoki Nishimura, Ai Yamakage, Takeo Kato  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01678v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

Schematic of the phonon dispersion relations in the long-wavelength limit for transverse phonons in (a) achiral materials and (b) chiral materials. A pair of adjacent lines in (a) indicates the two-fold degeneracy of phonon modes. The magnitude of the frequency splitting in (b) is proportional to k for optical phonons and to k2 for acoustic phonons.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01678_figures/2605.01678_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 1. Two-dimensional schematic of the microrotation field ϕ and the displacement field u in a continuum medium. The square mesh indicates the atomic displacement described by the field u, accompanied by a local rotation in the x1-x2 plane, whereas the ellipse denotes the rotation of the internal degree. Here, a is a lattice constant and ei (i = 1, 2) are unit vectors.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01678_figures/2605.01678_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3. Transverse phonon dispersions in micropolar elas- ticity. In the achiral case, the acoustic and optical branches remain degenerate, whereas in the chiral case, the optical branch is split and the acoustic branch exhibits a weaker split- ting. The curves are plotted for representative dimensionless parameters ρ = 1, I = 0.5, µ = 1, κ = 0.5, and β + γ = 1. The achiral panel corresponds to χ = 0, whereas the chiral panel uses χ = 0.2 in the convention of Eq. (57). No material- specific fit is implied.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper establishes a new continuum theory for phonon angular momentum that bridges the gap between acoustic and optical phonons. By introducing a local rotation frame, it provides a consistent way to calculate angular momentum density and explains how structural chirality leads to the splitting of phonon modes.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The lack of a unified continuum theory that can simultaneously describe both acoustic displacement modes and optical rotational modes within a single conservation law for phonon angular momentum.

**Main result.** The development of a generalized continuum theory using a local SO(3) material frame that identifies a definite phonon angular-momentum density and explains chiral phonon splitting.

**Method.** The authors use a local-frame formulation of micropolar elasticity, applying Noether's theorem to a Lagrangian density to derive conservation laws and analyzing the linearized limit and symmetry-breaking terms.

**Model / system.** A generalized continuum model of crystals/solids, specifically focusing on microstructures with rotational optical degrees of freedom represented by a local SO(3) material frame.

**Key observables.** Phonon angular momentum density (comprising orbital, displacement-polarization, and intrinsic microrotation parts), phonon dispersion relations, and chiral splitting of modes.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mass density, moment of inertia, Lamé parameters, Cosserat/wryness moduli, and the chiral coupling constant.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The theory is applied in the long-wavelength (continuum) limit and assumes a regime where torsion and curvature terms are not required; the electron-spin coupling is deferred to future work.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a 2D schematic of microrotation and displacement fields in a lattice; Figure 2 shows phonon dispersion relations; Figure 3 compares transverse phonon dispersions in achiral versus chiral media.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of unified phonon angular momentum, defines the local SO(3) material frame and co-rotated deformation gradient, applies Noether's theorem to derive angular momentum density, analyzes the linearized limit (micropolar elasticity), explores the locking limit and chiral splitting, and concludes with technical remarks on the hierarchy of continua.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We formulate a generalized continuum theory of phonon angular momentum in crystals by introducing a local SO(3) material frame in addition to the macroscopic displacement field. The local frame represents rotational optical degrees of freedom of the unit cell and brings acoustic displacement modes and optical rotational modes into a common long-wavelength continuum description. In the linearized limit, the co-rotated deformation gradient and the rotational gradient associated with the local material frame recover the Eringen microdeformation and wryness tensors; isotropic micropolar elasticity then appears as a special case. Rotational symmetry and Noether's theorem determine the continuum phonon angular-momentum density, including both the displacement-polarization contribution and the intrinsic microrotation contribution. The theory further identifies the locking limit in which microrotation reduces to lattice vorticity and the improper-symmetry-breaking terms responsible for chiral phonon splitting.

</details>

### [Geometric Percolation Threshold Defines Half-Metallic Window in Vacancy-Doped Titanium disulfides](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01754v1)

**Authors:** Shrestha Dutta, Rudra Banerjee  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01754v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01754_figures/2605.01754_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1: Spin-resolved density of states across the concentration range. (a) Dilute limit: discrete defect states deep in the gap. (b) Dead zone: states broaden but remain distinct from EF . (c) Incipient transition: the majority-spin tail grazes EF . (d) Percolation threshold: a massive majority-spin peak is pinned at EF with a clean minority-spin gap, establishing full half-metallicity.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01754_figures/2605.01754_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2: Band structure evolution from localized moments to itinerant transport. (a–c) Below threshold, defect-induced levels are flat and dispersionless (W &lt; 0.1 eV), indicating negligible wavefunction overlap. (d) At x = 12%, these levels hybridize into a 1.5 eV-wide dispersive band that crosses EF —the spectroscopic fingerprint of the percolation transition.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01754_figures/2605.01754_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3: Charge density difference (∆ρ = ρdefect −ρpristine) visualizing the percolation mechanism. (a) Dilute: the perturbation is confined to nearest-neighbour Ti atoms. (b–c) Intermediate: charge clouds remain spatially disjoint. (d) Threshold: defect orbitals coalesce into a continuous network spanning the supercell.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01754_figures/2605.01754_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4: Orbital origin of the local magnetic moment. (a) Projected DOS for a bulk-like Ti atom, showing the characteristic Oh splitting into t2g (valence) and eg (conduction) sub-bands. (b) Projected DOS for the vacancy-adjacent Ti atom. The reduced C4v symmetry collapses the eg manifold, producing a mid-gap impurity state with dz2/dx2−y2 character. (c) Schematic energy-level diagram illustrating the mechanism: the lost anion reduces repulsion on the dz2 orbital, stabilizing it within the gap.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01754_figures/2605.01754_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5: Stability and universality. (a) Thermodynamic limits: the defect formation energy (Ef) remains negative up to the jamming onset at x ≈20%. (b) Universality: the cluster size distribution n(s) from finite-size scaling (N = 6400 sites) yields τTB = 2.09 ± 0.03, confirming the 2D percolation universality class. (c) Phase diagram: the giant cluster fraction P∞(red) undergoes a discontinuous jump at xc = 12.5%, perfectly synchronized with the onset of 100% spin polarization (blue). Both order parameters collapse simultaneously at x ≈22%.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the emergence of half-metallicity in sulfur-deficient TiS2 is a geometric percolation event rather than a simple concentration-dependent effect. It identifies a specific concentration window for stable spin-polarized transport and establishes a design principle for 2D spintronics based on defect connectivity.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Resolving why defect engineering in 2D materials often produces local magnetic moments without achieving the desired itinerant half-metallic ferromagnetism.

**Main result.** The transition to half-metallicity in vacancy-doped TiS2 is driven by the geometric percolation of the vacancy network, occurring at a critical concentration of approximately 12.5%.

**Method.** The study combines Density Functional Theory (DFT) with large-scale Tight-Binding (TB) simulations and finite-size scaling analysis to study percolation universality.

**Model / system.** Monolayer 1T-phase titanium disulfide (TiS2) with sulfur vacancies, modeled using a 2D percolation framework and a large-scale tight-binding Hamiltonian on a triangular lattice.

**Key observables.** Spin polarization, giant cluster fraction (P_infinity), Fisher exponent (tau), Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), and vacancy concentration (x).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Critical concentration x_c approx 12.5%, functional window 11% < x < 15%, and geometric jamming limit at x > 20%.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The mechanism is applicable to isostructural 1T-type TMDs with extended defect wavefunctions; Hubbard-U correction is used to model electron correlation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 5 shows thermodynamic limits and defect formation energy.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the paradox of defect engineering, details the electronic and magnetic mechanism via symmetry breaking, applies percolation theory to identify the critical threshold, discusses the functional window and jamming limits, and concludes with experimental signatures.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Defect engineering of two-dimensional materials routinely produces local magnetic moments, yet itinerant half-metallic   ferromagnetism remains elusive -- experiments frequently yield paramagnetic insulators. We resolve this paradox for vacancy-doped   monolayer $1T$-\ptis~by demonstrating that the insulator-to-half-metal transition is governed by universal geometric percolation   of the defect network, extending the percolation framework established for three-dimensional diluted magnetic semiconductors into   the 2D vacancy-doped regime. Half-metallicity emerges via a two-step mechanism: crystal-field symmetry breaking   ($O_h \to C_{4v}$) selectively stabilizes the Ti $3d_{z^2}$ orbital, generating robust local moments ($0.94~μ_B$), but   spin-polarized transport requires these moments to form a spanning cluster. At critical vacancy concentration $x_c \approx   12.5\%$, a percolation transition drives the majority-spin impurity band from flat, localized levels ($W < 0.1$~eV) to a   dispersive 1.5~eV-wide band with 100\% spin polarization and a minority-spin gap of 1.0~eV. The   percolation mechanism is independently corroborated by a striking supercell-size effect: at identical concentration, $2\times2$   cells yield antiferromagnetic order while $4\times4$ cells mandate ferromagnetism, reflecting the presence or absence of a   spanning cluster. We estimate a Curie temperature exceeding 300~K from the exchange coupling, and identify a geometric jamming   instability at $x > 20\%$ that fragments the network. These results define a narrow functional window ($11\% < x < 15\%$) for   half-metallic operation and establish geometric connectivity as a quantitative design principle for defect-engineered 2D   spintronics.

</details>

### [Grain boundary segregation of light elements and their effects on cohesion in ferritic steels](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01181v1)

**Authors:** Han Lin Mai, Xiang-Yuan Cui, Tilmann Hickel, Simon P. Ringer, Jörg Neugebauer  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01181v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The shaded ranges indicate the range of studied sites for segregation, darker blue for interstitial sites, and the extended range in light blue for substitutional. Bars are coloured by the starting site type: interstitial (blue), substitutional (orange), and mixed (green).</figcaption>
</figure>

**Summary.** This paper provides a comprehensive DFT-based dataset and analysis of how light elements segregate to grain boundaries in iron. It demonstrates that the ability of a site to relax and accommodate strain is a better predictor of segregation than initial void volume, and classifies elements as either strengtheners or embrittlers.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Understanding how the segregation of light elements (H, He, B, C, N, O, P, S) to grain boundaries in ferritic steels affects interfacial cohesion and macroscale properties.

**Main result.** The study identifies B and C as cohesion enhancers, while He, O, and S are potent decohesion agents; it also reveals that segregation is driven by a site's ability to accommodate strain rather than initial Voronoi volume.

**Method.** Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations using the VASP package with the PBE functional, combined with bond-order (ANSBO) and Rice-Wang frameworks for cohesion analysis.

**Model / system.** Six specific Coincident Site Lattice (CSL) grain boundaries in bcc-Fe (including Sigma3, Sigma5, Sigma9, and Sigma11) with various light element solutes in interstitial and substitutional sites.

**Key observables.** Segregation energies (Eseg), grain boundary energy (gamma_GB), work of separation (WRGS), and bond orders (ANSBO).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Solute types (H, He, B, C, N, O, P, S), site volumes, and nearest-neighbor distances after relaxation.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of the PBE functional and the assumption that the Rice-Wang framework might be unphysical for certain elements like Helium due to rigid-grain separation assumptions.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows GB atomic structures; Figure 3 shows the lack of correlation between GB energy and segregation strength; Figure 5 shows segregation energy distributions; Figure 7 shows the correlation between nearest-neighbor distance and segregation energy; Figures 8 and 9 show elemental cohesive effects.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of light element segregation, describes the DFT methodology and site sampling strategy, presents results on segregation energy trends and cohesion modifiers, analyzes the structural drivers of segregation (volume vs. strain), and concludes with data availability.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Light elements play an important role in influencing the macroscale properties of engineering alloys through grain boundary (GB) segregation phenomena. However, the scarcity and scattered nature of ab initio datasets for light elements in steels makes reproduction and extraction of general trends from the literature difficult. Here, we present a comprehensive ab initio evaluation of the segregation energies and cohesive effects for H, He, B, C, N, O, P, S, extensively sampling both substitutional and interstitial sites in six model coincident site lattice (CSL) ferritic iron GBs using density functional theory (DFT). Cohesive effects are evaluated in both a quantum-chemistry bond-order and rigid Rice-Wang interfacial cohesive strength framework. Our calculations indicate that, compared at the same concentration, B and C enhance GB cohesion, N, P, H are mildly detrimental, and He, O, S as powerful decohesive agents/embrittlers. Sampling both interstitial and substitutional starting positions is necessary to accurately capture segregation spectra. Commonly utilised sampling criteria such as site volumes prove insufficient for identifying deepest GB binding sites. Solutes placed in either kind of site can induce large relaxations to the same final configuration, resulting in site classification ambiguity. The nearest neighbour distance of a solute to its neighbours after relaxation is shown to be a controlling factor for the lower threshold of segregation energies at sites. The freely available DFT dataset and analysis repositories are expected to advance understanding of GB segregation behaviours of light elements in steels and serve as a resource for developing machine learning interatomic potentials.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.01316"></a>
### [Impurity-Scattering Assisted Umklapp Scattering as the Origin of Low-Temperature Resistivity in the Normal-State of Cuprate Superconductors](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01316v1)

**Authors:** Xingyu Ma, Minghuan Zeng, Huaiming Guo, Shiping Feng  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01316v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01316_figures/2605.01316_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 2. (a) The antinodal spin pseudogap ¯∆(s) pg (kAN) in tem- perature T = 0.002J and (b) the corresponding antinodal spin pseudogap crossover temperature T ∗ s as a function of doping, where kAN is the wave vector at the antinode.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes that the change in resistivity power-law from T-linear to T-quadratic in cuprates is caused by the suppression of spin-excitation-mediated Umklapp scattering due to the opening of a spin pseudogap. It provides a microscopic mechanism that links the transport properties directly to the evolution of the antinodal spin dynamics with doping.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Explaining the origin of the crossover in low-temperature resistivity between the T-linear behavior in the overdoped strange-metal phase and the T-quadratic behavior in the underdoped pseudogap phase of cuprate superconductors.

**Main result.** The resistivity behavior is driven by impurity-scattering assisted electron Umklapp scattering from spin excitations; the opening of the antinodal spin pseudogap in the underdoped regime suppresses the spin excitation density of states, leading to T-quadratic resistivity.

**Method.** The authors use the Boltzmann transport equation within a microscopic pseudogap theory framework, specifically employing a fermion-spin transformation and the t-J model to derive the electron propagator and resistivity.

**Model / system.** The system is the normal-state of cuprate superconductors, modeled using a low-energy effective t-J model on a square lattice including nearest and next-nearest-neighbor hopping, chemical potential, and magnetic coupling.

**Key observables.** Low-temperature resistivity (rho), dc conductivity, antinodal spin pseudogap, and the pseudogap crossover temperature (T*)

**Important parameters / regimes.** Doping level (delta), temperature (T), impurity scattering rate (gamma_0), and the antinodal spin pseudogap magnitude

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the presence of natural impurities due to doping and focuses specifically on the low-temperature regime rather than high-temperature transport.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the electron spectral function intensity map; Figure 2 displays the antinodal spin pseudogap and its crossover temperature as a function of doping; Figure 3 and 4 show the T-quadratic and T-linear resistivity fits for underdoped and overdoped regimes respectively; Figure 5 plots the temperature scale as a function of doping.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the microscopic electronic structure and the t-J model, develops the pseudogap theory via fermion-spin transformation, applies the Boltzmann transport equation to investigate momentum relaxation via impurity-assisted Umklapp scattering, and concludes by comparing the results to different doping regimes.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The transport experiments reveal that the low-temperature resistivity in the normal-state of cuprate superconductors is quadratic in temperature (T-quadratic) in the underdoped pseudogap phase, while it is linear in temperature (T-linear) in the overdoped strange-metal phase, however, the full understanding of these different behaviours is still a challenging issue. Here starting from the microscopic electronic structure of cuprate superconductors, the low-temperature resistivity in the normal-state is investigated from the underdoped pseudogap phase to the overdoped strange-metal phase. It is shown that the mechanism requires both the impurity scattering and the umklapp scattering: the impurity scattering is needed to restrict the modification of the distribution function to at around the antinodal region,while the impurity-scattering assisted umklapp scattering from a spin excitation is at the heart of the behaviour in the low-temperature resistivity, where the doping dependence of the temperature scale exists, and presents a similar behavior of the antinodal spin pseudogap crossover temperature. In the low-temperature region above the temperature scale in the overdoped strange-metal phase, the resistivity is T-linear, however, in the low-temperature region below the temperature scale in the underdoped pseudogap phase, the opening of the spin pseudogap lowers the spin excitation density of states at around the antinodal region, which reduces the strength of the electron umklapp scattering from a spin excitation associated with the antinode, and thus leads to a T-quadratic behaviour of the resistivity.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.01031"></a>

**Summary.** This paper develops a thermodynamic model to show that coherent elastic strain is a critical factor in suppressing phase separation in lattice-mismatched alloys. By incorporating elastic compatibility into phase-diagram calculations, the authors successfully align theoretical predictions for the Nb-V system with experimental observations. The work demonstrates that elastic constraints fundamentally change the nature of phase equilibria by making coexistence compositions dependent on the total alloy composition.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how coherent elastic strain influences phase separation thermodynamics and the width of the miscibility gap in alloys with significant lattice mismatch.

**Main result.** Coherent elasticity substantially suppresses phase separation, narrows the miscibility gap, and lowers the critical temperature, while making equilibrium compositions dependent on the overall alloy composition.

**Method.** A thermodynamic framework combining DFT-derived chemical free energies with explicit coherent elastic contributions using both isotropic and biaxial strain models.

**Model / system.** The BCC Niobium-Vanadium (Nb-V) binary alloy system, modeled using a combination of CALPHAD-style chemical free energies and mechanical compatibility constraints (isotropic and biaxial).

**Key observables.** Coherent binodal and spinodal boundaries, critical temperature (Tc), miscibility gap width, and equilibrium phase compositions (x1, x2).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Lattice mismatch, temperature (T), overall alloy composition (X), and elastic parameters (bulk modulus, pressure derivative, and biaxial expansion coefficients).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Neglect of PV terms and electronic entropy; approximation of vibrational entropy as linear in composition; assumption of an ideal random-solution configurational entropy.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows composition dependence of properties; Figure 2 shows the energy surface as a function of biaxial strain; Figure 4 compares decomposition compositions in chemical-only vs. coherent-elastic models; Figure 5 analyzes the elastic origin of the suppression mechanism.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of lattice mismatch in phase separation, presents a thermodynamic framework integrating chemical and elastic energies, compares isotropic and biaxial models, validates the model against DFT and experimental data, and discusses the qualitative shift in phase boundary interpretation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Coherent elastic strain is an important but often neglected contribution to phase-separation thermodynamics in alloy systems where decomposed phases have appreciable lattice mismatch. We develop a thermodynamic framework that incorporates coherent elastic compatibility directly into phase-diagram calculations alongside conventional CALPHAD chemical free energies. Applied to the BCC Nb-V system, the framework shows that coherent elasticity substantially suppresses phase separation, narrows the miscibility gap, and lowers the critical temperature toward experimentally observed values. Beyond these quantitative effects, the coherent constraint qualitatively alters the interpretation of phase equilibria: the equilibrium decomposition compositions become functions of both temperature and overall alloy composition, so the two-phase boundary no longer represents unique coexistence compositions. These results establish coherent elasticity as a key thermodynamic factor in lattice-mismatched systems and provide a general framework for coherent phase-diagram modeling.

</details>

**Authors:** Max Rang, Paul J. Kelly  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02548v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** The study uses first-principles calculations to show that injected orbital angular momentum currents in transition metals decay much more rapidly (within a few atomic layers) than previously thought. It reveals that spin-orbit coupling plays a crucial role in converting these short-lived orbital currents into longer-lived spin currents.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the decay length scale of injected orbital angular momentum (OAM) currents in transition metals and addressing the discrepancy between theoretical predictions and experimental interpretations.

**Main result.** Orbital currents decay rapidly within a few atomic layers upon injection, unlike spin currents which have longer diffusion lengths; however, in the presence of spin-orbit coupling, injected orbital current is partially converted into a spin current.

**Method.** First-principles quantum mechanical scattering calculations using a tight-binding muffin-tin orbital (TB-MTO) basis and Ando's Wave Function Matching scheme.

**Model / system.** Transition metals (including Pt, Ti, V, Cr, and Cu) modeled as a scattering region sandwiched between two semi-infinite leads, incorporating spin and orbital Zeeman terms to create polarized leads.

**Key observables.** Charge, spin, and orbital currents (j_c, j_s, j_l); orbital Hall conductivity (OHC); spin Hall conductivity (SHC); spin-flip and orbital diffusion lengths.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Spin-orbit coupling (SOC) strength, thermal lattice disorder (temperature), and spin-flip diffusion length (l_sf).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The orbital diffusion length is assumed to be driven by electronic hopping; SOC is sometimes switched off in the scattering region to isolate orbital effects; the orbital Zeeman term is used as a proxy for orbital polarization.

**Figures summary.** Schematic of the L|S|R geometry for current generation; plots showing the decay of injected orbital polarization in Pt, Cr, and V; and visualization of current density in the leads.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of OAM decay, describes the TB-MTO scattering formalism, presents results for various transition metals (Pt, 3d metals), discusses the role of SOC and disorder, and concludes with implications for orbitronics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We use quantum mechanical scattering calculations implemented in a basis of tight-binding muffin-tin orbitals to calculate nonequilibrium spin and orbital currents in transition metals with a view to understanding the length scale on which they decay. In the case of spin currents, the relaxation length, called the spin-flip diffusion length, is reasonably well understood. We apply our experience with spin currents to study orbitally-polarized currents and find that they behave qualitatively differently. Upon injection from a lead, orbital currents decay within a few atomic layers contradicting the current interpretation of experimental results which appear to show exponential decay on the length scale of the spin-flip diffusion length and longer. When spin-orbit coupling is included, the injected orbital current is partially converted into a spin current within a few atomic layers. This insight provides a new perspective on the physics of the orbital Hall effect.

</details>

### [Interfacial charge-induced adsorption mode for electron pairing in high-temperature superconductors](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02619v1)

**Authors:** Jiu Hui Wu, Hua Tian, Kejiang Zhou  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02619v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Low-resolution page preview, page 6</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new mechanism for high-temperature superconductivity based on an interfacial adsorption mode between electrons and oxygen ions. By modeling the charge-regulated interface in YBCO, the authors provide a theoretical framework that quantitatively matches experimental measurements of the superconducting gap and symmetry. This approach offers a potential alternative to traditional strong-correlation models like RVB theory.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to resolve the unresolved electron pairing mechanism in high-temperature superconductors (specifically cuprates like YBCO), including the origins of d-wave symmetry and the pseudogap phenomenon.

**Main result.** The authors propose an 'interfacial charge-induced adsorption mode' where electrons pair by exchanging adsorption modes, successfully predicting the superconducting gap (18.37 meV) and coherence length in close agreement with experimental STM/STS data.

**Method.** The study employs a unified Hamiltonian with Bogoliubov linearization, Schrieffer-Wolff transformation, and the 1D Ginzburg-Landau equation to derive effective interaction potentials and superconducting properties.

**Model / system.** The system is the YBCO superconductor, modeled as a charge-regulated interfacial layer where electrons are confined in an adsorption space between CuO2 layers, interacting with the valence-flexible state of oxygen ions.

**Key observables.** Superconducting gap (Delta), coherence length (xi), d-wave symmetry, and pseudogap behavior.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Adsorption coupling constant (up to 43.4), superconducting gap (18.37 meV), effective superconducting component ratio (omega approx 0.0306), and adsorption potential energy window (epsilon_p).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes a charge-regulated interfacial layer induced by adsorption potential and uses the Bogoliubov linearization approximation for the chemical potential response.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the failure of BCS theory, proposes the adsorption potential framework, defines the microscopic Hamiltonian, derives the effective interaction and symmetry, and validates the theory through quantitative comparison with experimental literature.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The electron pairing mechanism by the interfacial charge-induced adsorption mode of high-temperature superconductors is revealed. For the YBCO superconductors, the coupling of electrons and valence-flexible state of oxygen ions forms a charge-regulated interfacial layer induced by the adsorption potential, and electrons are paired by sharing the optimized interfacial structure and exchanging the adsorption mode, generating strong attraction to form Cooper pairs. Then the effective interaction potential between electrons is exactly derived in details, as well as the electron-adsorption-mode coupling strength, in which the adsorption coupling constant is up to 43.4. Furthermore, we verify that d-wave come from the anisotropy of interfacial adsorption forces, and explain the pseudo-energy gap behavior. By using the one-dimensional Ginzburg-Landau equation in the absence of a magnetic field, we obtain the coherence length expression, and the coherence length calculated is very close to the literature results. By establishing the energy gap equation, we obtain the superconducting gap , which is very close to the measured result of 17meV by Scanning Tunneling Microscopy/Spectroscopy. These quantitative predictions close to the known results could verify our theoretical framework.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper presents a new generative diffusion model that jointly predicts crystal structures and local electronic properties like Bader charges and density of states. By including electronic information in the generative process, the model successfully targets specific material properties without sacrificing the physical stability or novelty of the generated crystals.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The trade-off in inverse materials design where property-conditioned generation (e.g., targeting specific band gaps) often degrades the structural quality, diversity, and physical validity of the generated crystals.

**Main result.** Jointly generating crystal structures and local electronic descriptors (Bader charges and atomic DOS) improves the success rate of property targeting and enhances structural quality (VSUN criteria) by guiding the diffusion process toward electronically plausible configurations.

**Method.** A diffusion-based framework (MatterGen-e-) that jointly denoises structural variables (lattice, positions, atom types) and site-resolved electronic descriptors through a shared score network.

**Model / system.** The system consists of inorganic crystal structures represented by lattice parameters, atomic positions, and atom types, augmented with local electronic descriptors including Bader charges and 30-dimensional latent vectors representing atomic Density of States (DOS).

**Key observables.** Band gap, formation energy, Bader charges, atomic Density of States (DOS), uniqueness, novelty, stability (energy above hull), and validity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Target band gap (e.g., 3 eV, 4 eV), target formation energy, and the 30-dimensional latent space for DOS.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The accuracy of the atomic DOS is limited by the chemical sensitivity of elements to their bonding environment; the model assumes that the 30D latent vector is sufficient to capture essential spectral features.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the MatterGen-e- framework schematic; Figure 2 displays DFT property distributions and success rates; Figure 3 compares novelty metrics; Figure 4 shows correlation between generated and DFT Bader charges; Figure 5 evaluates DOS quality via Wasserstein distance; Figure 6 illustrates the convolutional autoencoder; Figure 7 provides element-wise error analysis for charges and DOS.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of property-driven structural degradation, proposes a joint diffusion framework, details the architecture for electronic descriptor generation via autoencoders, presents a multi-criteria evaluation (VSUN) against baselines, and concludes with an element-specific analysis of descriptor accuracy.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Inverse design of inorganic crystals, in which structures are generated to satisfy a target property while preserving diversity and physical plausibility, remains more demanding than ab initio generation, as property conditioning can degrade the structural quality that current generative models otherwise achieve. We propose a diffusion framework that jointly denoises crystal-structure variables and site-resolved local electronic descriptors through a shared score network. As representative descriptors, we adopt Bader charge and atomic density of states (atomic DOS). Under both band-gap and formation energy conditioned generation, the joint models achieved higher success rates than the structure-only baseline in most target conditions, while simultaneously increasing the fraction of generated structures that satisfy uniqueness, novelty, thermodynamic stability, and physical validity (VSUN criteria). A dummy-variable control confirms that these gains originate from the electronic content of the descriptors rather than from auxiliary site-wise variables. The generated Bader charges agree with DFT references with an MAE of 5.5e-2 e on stable structures, and the generated atomic DOS captures the coarse spectral profile of the DFT reference around the modal accuracy range, although finer details and accuracy vary with elemental species. These results establish local electronic descriptors as effective generative variables that serve two complementary roles: broadening the explored materials space through increased structural diversity, and mitigating the trade-off between property targeting and structural quality by guiding the structural trajectory toward electronically plausible configurations during joint denoising.

</details>

**Authors:** Alena Samsonova, Viacheslav Dremov, Oleg Klimenko, Nikolai Brilliantov, Vladimir N. Antonov  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02432v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** This paper uses conductive AFM to map the spatial distribution of metallic filaments in amorphous silicon memristors. It demonstrates that transport is driven by discrete filaments rather than uniform conduction and provides a trap-assisted tunneling model to explain the coexistence of volatile and non-volatile switching.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study addresses the high stochasticity and device-to-device/cycle-to-cycle variation in Ag/Cu memristors caused by the random nature of filament formation and rupture.

**Main result.** The researchers found that charge transport is dominated by discrete, spatially distributed filaments with a mean surface density of ~3200 per um2, and that both volatile and non-volatile filaments coexist within a single device.

**Method.** Conductive Atomic Force Microscopy (c-AFM) was used for spatial resistance mapping, combined with the Johnson-Kendall-Roberts (JKR) model for contact area estimation and a trap-assisted tunneling (TAT) model for transport analysis.

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of Ag/Cu cation-based memristors embedded in an amorphous silicon (a-Si) insulating matrix. The transport is modeled using multiple trap-assisted tunneling (TAT) between discrete metallic filaments.

**Key observables.** I-V curves, resistance maps, filament surface density, and filament types (volatile vs. non-volatile).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mean filament density (~3200 per um2), effective electron mass (0.3 me), energy gap (2.00-2.75 eV), and filament lengths (1.1 nm for non-volatile, 2.3 nm for volatile).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes conductance is limited by tunneling between the last trap and the bottom electrode and that only one filament contributes to the resistance of a single pixel.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the device cross-section and AFM topography; Fig 2 presents cumulative and typical I-V curves; Fig 3 compares volatile and non-volatile I-V curves and shows resistance maps at different voltages; Fig 4 plots calculated resistance vs. energy gap.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of stochasticity in memristors, describes the a-Si/Ag/Cu device fabrication and c-AFM experimental setup, presents the spatial mapping of filaments, applies a trap-assisted tunneling model to explain the observed volatility, and concludes with the implications for device uniformity.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Cation-based Ag/Cu filaments formed in an insulating $α$-Si matrix are widely used as memristors in crossbar arrays for efficient in-memory computing. However, the stochastic nature of filament formation and rupture gives rise to device-to-device and cycle-to-cycle variation. Despite successful implementation of large-scale memristor arrays, systematic studies of filament parameters and their spatial distribution in the memristors are scarce. In this work, we use conductive atomic force microscopy (c-AFM) to probe the spatial distribution of conductive filaments in $α$-Si memristors. The charge transport is dominated by a limited number of discrete filaments rather than by uniform conduction across the device area. The systematic analysis of the experiment gives the mean surface density of the conductive filaments $\sim$3200 per $μ\text{m}^2$. Both volatile and non-volatile filaments can be found within one memristor. The experimental data and the nature of volatile and non-volatile filaments may be explained within the model of multiple trap assisted tunnelling. The model yields reasonable estimates for physical properties for both types of filaments.

</details>

**Authors:** Friedrich Schöppler, Lukas Stumpf, Lucas Fuhl, Elena Behr, Charlotte Allard, Christoph Lambert, Richard Martel, Tobias Hertel  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01906v1>

While spectral positions and line shapes vary between dyes, encapsulated species consistently exhibit signatures distinct from their solution counterparts. Most AFM-visible objects also appear as PL-active rods or, for very short nanotubes, as diffraction-limited spots. The comparison provides a qualitative impression that a substantial fraction of the deposited nanotubes is emissive af- ter purification, although it is not intended as a quantitative determination of the filling yield.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper investigates the optical properties of dyes encapsulated inside boron nitride nanotubes using both ensemble and single-nanotube techniques. It demonstrates that the confinement effect is highly dependent on the guest molecule, acting as a dielectric tuner for some dyes and a conformational regulator for others. The findings reveal that these 1D systems form ordered but structurally heterogeneous ensembles.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining how 1D confinement within boron nitride nanotubes (BNNTs) modifies the optical, kinetic, and structural properties of encapsulated pi-conjugated dyes.

**Main result.** The study finds that BNNT encapsulation induces guest-specific responses, such as dielectric tuning in Nile Red and length-dependent conformational/excitonic modifications in oligothiophenes, resulting in structurally heterogeneous but highly polarized ensembles.

**Method.** Liquid-phase wet-chemical encapsulation followed by ensemble spectroscopy (absorption, PL, PLQY, TCSPC) and polarization-resolved single-nanotube microscopy.

**Model / system.** A nanoconfined 1D system consisting of various pi-conjugated dyes (oligothiophenes 4T, 6T, 8T and Nile Red) encapsulated within wide-bandgap, optically transparent boron nitride nanotubes (BNNTs).

**Key observables.** Absorption/extinction spectra, photoluminescence (PL) spectra, PL quantum yield (PLQY), fluorescence lifetimes, radiative and non-radiative decay rates, and degree of linear polarization.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Oligomer length, BNNT inner/outer diameters, dielectric constant of the environment (DMF vs. BNNT interior), and van der Waals cross-sections of the dyes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The BNNT host is assumed to be electronically inactive and optically transparent, acting primarily as a dielectric and geometric constraint.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Chemical structures and dye classification; Fig 2: BNNT diameter distributions and comparison to dye dimensions; Fig 3: Normalized absorption and PL spectra for 4T, 6T, 8T, and NR; Fig 5: AFM/PL correlation for 6T; Fig 6: Polarization-resolved PL traces; Fig 7: Spatial contrast maps of single nanotubes; Fig 8: Histograms of polarization degree and angle.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the experimental protocol for dye encapsulation, followed by a systematic study of different dye classes (oligothiophenes vs. Nile Red), an analysis of the kinetic decay rates, and concludes with single-nanotube microscopy to probe structural heterogeneity and orientational order.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Boron nitride nanotubes (BNNTs) provide wide-bandgap, optically transparent one-dimensional hosts for molecular dyes, limiting direct electronic participation of the host. Whether dye@BNNT systems produce bright, well-defined J- or H-aggregates or instead heterogeneous emissive ensembles whose character depends on chain length and local packing remains only partly resolved. We address this question using ensemble extinction, photoluminescence, quantum-yield measurements, and TCSPC-derived radiative and non-radiative rates, together with polarization-resolved single-nanotube microscopy on encapsulated quaterthiophene, sexithiophene, octithiophene, and Nile Red, selected from a ten-dye screening. In the oligothiophene series, confinement modifies spectra and excited-state dynamics in a length-dependent manner, with all three oligothiophenes forming weakly emissive ensembles with suppressed effective radiative rates and 6T showing the strongest redistribution between effective radiative and non-radiative decay. The absence of radiative-rate enhancement or fluorescence-lifetime shortening across the series disfavors bright J-aggregate assignments. Polarization-resolved single-nanotube microscopy reveals strongly polarized emission, but with tube-to-tube and intratube variations, identifying oligothiophene@BNNTs as ordered yet structurally heterogeneous confined ensembles. Nile Red provides a complementary case in which the dominant response is dielectric tuning of a solvatochromic charge-transfer state rather than oligothiophene-like aggregate formation. These findings establish dye-filled BNNTs as optically quiet nanoconfined systems in which molecular ordering, dielectric confinement, and guest-guest coupling can be distinguished through combined ensemble and single-nanotube spectroscopy.

</details>

### [Metallic crossover through the tilt-free transition in La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ at high pressure and temperature](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01651v1)

**Authors:** Bastien Michon, Yingpeng Yu, Beatrice D'Alò, Elena Stellino, Gergely Németh, Bosen Wang, Jianping Sun, Jinguang Cheng, Paolo Postorino, Ferenc Borondics, Francesco Capitani
