Black and green triangles indicate the onset temperatures of the DW- like order (TDW ) and superconductivity (Tc), respectively, with filled symbols for single crystals [6] and open symbols for polycrystals [7]. Pink and purple circles represent onset pressures and temperatures from our Raman data. The pink, purple and blue regions correspond to the Amam (bad metal), coexistence (Amam + tilt-free) and pure tilt-free (good metal) phases, respectively. A color gradient in the superconducting region highlights the crossover from filamentary to bulk su- perconductivity [7, 17]. Schematic...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that a structural transition from a tilted to a tilt-free phase in La3Ni2O7 leads to a massive increase in charge carrier density. By using Raman and infrared spectroscopy, the authors establish a link between the lattice structure and a metallic crossover from a bad metal to a good metal. This provides a unified picture of the electronic and structural evolution in this high-Tc superconducting candidate.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Determining the precise phase boundaries and the impact of structural changes on the electronic properties of the bilayer nickelate La3Ni2O7 under high pressure and temperature.

**Main result.** The study identifies a structural transition from a tilted Amam phase to a tilt-free phase, which drives a metallic crossover from a bad metal to a good metal with a two-order-of-magnitude increase in carrier density.

**Method.** High-pressure and high-temperature Raman spectroscopy combined with synchrotron-based infrared and visible reflectivity measurements using a diamond anvil cell.

**Model / system.** The physical system is La3Ni2O7, a bilayer nickelate with a Ruddlesden-Popper structure, studied under pressures up to 17 GPa and temperatures up to 544 K.

**Key observables.** Raman phonon modes, Fano line shape (electron-phonon coupling), plasma frequency (carrier density), and infrared reflectivity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Pressure (0.4 to 17 GPa), Temperature (up to 544 K), and the structural phases (Amam, coexistence, and tilt-free).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The reliability of the signal in the diamond phonon interference region is limited, requiring the exclusion of certain spectral ranges from fitting.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 shows phonon disappearance, softening, and increased Fano parameter; Figure 3 shows infrared reflectivity spectra and the shift of the plasma edge; Supplementary figures show the evolution of phonon FWHM, intensity, and the massive enhancement of carrier density (omega_p^2) with pressure.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the nickelate system and the problem of structural-electronic coupling, describes the experimental setup for high-pressure/temperature spectroscopy, presents Raman results tracking structural transitions and electron-phonon coupling, presents infrared results demonstrating the metallic crossover, and concludes with a unified phase diagram.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$, a bilayer nickelate with Ruddlesden-Popper structure, undergoes a pressure-induced structural transition from a tilted Amam phase to an untilted Fmmm (or I4/mmm) phase near 10-15 GPa, concomitant with the emergence of high-T$_c$ superconductivity (T$_c$ $\sim$ 80 K). Despite intense interest, the phase boundaries and the impact of structural changes on the electronic properties remain unclear. Here, we combine high-pressure and high-temperature Raman and synchrotron-based infrared spectroscopies to map the structural and electronic evolutions. Raman measurements confirm the pressure-driven structural transition and reveal the emergence of Fano line shapes, indicating enhanced electron-phonon coupling. High-temperature data show analogous spectral signatures above 544 K, suggesting an unreported upper temperature limit of the Amam phase within the T-P phase diagram of this system. Infrared reflectivity measurements evidence a concomitant metallization, with a tremendous two-order-of-magnitude increase in carrier density, marking a crossover from a bad metal to a good metal. These results establish a unified picture of the structural transition and its strong coupling to the electronic properties.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that growing MnBi2Te4 in a magnetic field allows for the creation of a metastable ferromagnetic state with a different electronic structure than the standard antiferromagnetic version. This method provides a new way to tune the topological properties and magnetic phases of intrinsic magnetic topological insulators.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to find ways to control and reconfigure the magnetic ordering and electronic properties of the magnetic topological insulator MnBi2Te4 beyond its native A-type antiferromagnetic state.

**Main result.** By using magnetic-field-assisted synthesis, the researchers successfully stabilized a metastable ferromagnetic ground state in MnBi2Te4, which exhibits a different Fermi surface and potential type-II Weyl semimetal phase.

**Method.** The researchers used a self-flux growth method in a 9 T magnetic field and characterized the resulting crystals using X-ray diffraction, magnetometry (VSM, torque), transport measurements (resistivity, Hall effect), and first-principles DFT calculations.

**Model / system.** The physical system is MnBi2Te4, a layered van der Waals intrinsic magnetic topological insulator. The study compares the native A-type antiferromagnetic (AFM) state with a field-induced metastable ferromagnetic (FM) state.

**Key observables.** Magnetic susceptibility, magnetization, magnetic torque, electrical resistivity, Hall resistivity, de Haas-van Alphen oscillations, and specific heat.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Curie temperature (Tc approx 12.5 K), magnetic field strength (up to 35 T), and the transition from S=5/2 to S=1/2 spin state.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that the observed changes in electronic properties are primarily driven by the reconfiguration of the magnetic ground state and orbital contributions rather than spin scattering.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the crystal structure, XRD patterns, and peak shifts; Figure 2 displays temperature-dependent magnetic susceptibility; Figure 3 presents resistivity and Hall effect data.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of magnetic control, describes the field-assisted synthesis method, presents structural and magnetic characterization of the new phase, details the transport and quantum oscillation measurements, and concludes with DFT-supported analysis of the electronic structure.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Magnetic topological insulators provide a unique platform to explore the interplay between magnetism and topology. MnBi$_2$Te$_4$, known for its A-type antiferromagnetic (AFM) ground state, undergoes a striking transformation when single crystals are grown in an applied magnetic field. Despite retaining the same crystal structure, field-grown MnBi$_2$Te$_4$ exhibits a ferromagnetic (FM) ground state with a Curie temperature of $\sim$ 12.5 K, confirmed by magnetization, magnetic torque, electrical resistivity, and specific heat measurements. First-principles calculations support these findings, revealing that magnetic-field-assisted synthesis can effectively reconfigure the ground-state spin order and thereby modify the material's electronic properties, as reflected in the de Haas-van Alphen oscillation seen in the magnetic torque.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

**Authors:** Nidhin George Mathews, Erkka J. Frankberg, Vivek Devulapalli, Chandan Kumar, Barbara Putz, Aloshious Lambai, Sergei Khakalo, Mattia Cabrioli, Bjarke Holl Christensen, Janne-Petteri Niemelä, Arnold Milenko Müller, Fabio Di Fonzo, Ivo Utke, Erkki Levänen, Gaurav Mohanty  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01917v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper explores how different deposition methods affect the mechanical ductility and fracture of amorphous alumina films at the microscale. It reveals that while defects in sputter-deposited films cause brittle failure, high-quality PLD and ALD films can undergo significant plastic deformation. These findings are crucial for developing damage-tolerant amorphous oxide coatings for electronics.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the origin and limits of room-temperature microscale bending plasticity and fracture behavior in amorphous aluminum oxide (a-Al2O3) films.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that PLD and some ALD a-Al2O3 films exhibit substantial microscale bending plasticity (strains > 10%), while SD films fail brittly due to columnar defects; however, notched tests show identical fracture toughness across all methods, ruling out crack-tip plasticity.

**Method.** In situ SEM microcantilever bending (MCB) and notched microcantilever bending (NCB) experiments, supplemented by nanoindentation, TEM, XRD, and 3D Finite Element Modeling.

**Model / system.** Amorphous aluminum oxide (a-Al2O3) thin films (~2 micrometers thick) deposited via three different routes: Pulsed Laser Deposition (PLD), Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD), and Sputter Deposition (SD).

**Key observables.** Total strain, fracture toughness (KIC), hardness (H), elastic modulus (E), yield stress, and microstructural features like columnar voids.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Yield stress (~5.6 GPa), fracture toughness (3.1 +/- 0.2 MPa.m0.5), and film thickness (~2-10 micrometers).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The material is modeled as an isotropic elastic-plastic material without hardening in FEM simulations; the electron beam is assumed not to induce plasticity.

**Figures summary.** Figures include microcantilever geometries, XRD spectra and TEM images confirming amorphous structure, RBS/ToF-ERDA elemental profiles, stress-strain curves, and FEM-predicted stress distributions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of plasticity in amorphous oxides, details the three deposition methods and characterization techniques, presents mechanical testing results (unnotched vs. notched), discusses the role of defects and microstructure, and concludes with technological implications.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recent work has demonstrated microscale compressive plasticity in pulse laser deposited (PLD) amorphous alumina (a-Al2O3). This work explores microscale bending plasticity and fracture behavior of a-Al2O3 films deposited using three different methods-PLD, atomic layer deposition (ALD) and sputter deposition (SD). The three deposition routes produced amorphous films with similar stoichiometric compositions. We demonstrate, for the first time, bending plasticity in PLD and ALD a-Al2O3 films at microscale using in situ microcantilever bending experiments at room temperature. All tested PLD a-Al2O3 microcantilevers showed substantial ductile behavior in bending by accommodating total strains >10% without fracture. Half of the tested ALD a-Al2O3 cantilevers exhibited elastic brittle fracture while the other half showed bending plasticity, indicating that the observed deformation behavior is strongly influenced by the presence and distribution of defects within the tested volume. All SD a-Al2O3 microcantilevers showed elastic brittle failure attributed to their columnar growth microstructure. The microscale bending response was found to be highly dependent on the film deposition method highlighting the role of defects in suppressing plasticity mechanisms. Notched microcantilever bending tests on all three films showed brittle failure with similar fracture toughness value of 3.1 +/- 0.2 MPa.m0.5, effectively ruling out any localized crack tip plasticity. These findings underscore the importance of minimizing defects during fabrication in order to develop damage tolerant amorphous oxides. Nonetheless, the observation of bending plasticity in both PLD and ALD microcantilevers, which include a tensile stress component as well, suggests that the plastic deformation mechanisms in amorphous alumina are more general and are not exclusively governed by the deposition method.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper clarifies the physical principles and practical applications of mid-infrared photo-induced force microscopy (IR-PiF-IR). It provides strategies for accurate chemical imaging by decoupling field-coupling effects and demonstrates the technique's ability to resolve nanometer-scale chemical changes in biological structures like bacterial cell walls.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Addressing technical and physical questions regarding Mid-infrared photo-induced force microscopy (IR-PiF-IR), specifically its physical background, practical handling, and potential applications in biological and chemical imaging.

**Main result.** The paper demonstrates that IR-PiF-IR can resolve chemical composition (e.g., peptidoglycan vs. membrane) by comparing intensities at different frequencies to decouple field-coupling effects, and shows its utility in visualizing antibiotic-induced changes in bacteria.

**Method.** An AFM-based method using tip-enhanced mid-infrared illumination (via QCLs) and heterodyne side-band detection to measure mechanical forces induced by thermal expansion.

**Model / system.** The experimental platform involves an AFM tip interacting with various systems including Bacillus subtilis bacteria, PMMA nanoparticles, and molecular monolayers on gold or CaF2 substrates.

**Key observables.** PiF contrast, spectral line shapes (dissipative vs. dispersive), chemical maps, and spectral shifts in amide bands.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Lateral resolution < 5 nm, spectral range 800-1800 cm-1, and penetration depth ranging from volume to surface-sensitive.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes the tip-sample interaction force is the sum of conservative and non-conservative forces and that the heterodyne PiF signal is proportional to the gradient of the tip-sample force.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Comparison of PiF-IR scans of B. subtilis using RGB merge for chemical mapping. Fig 2: Classification of AFM-IR methods by resolution/depth and PMMA refractive index plots. Fig 4: Chemometric analysis of bacterial surfaces and spectral signatures of hydrogen bond formation.

**Paper structure.** The paper is structured as a response to specific technical questions, covering physical mechanisms, comparison of AFM-IR modes, practical application to biological samples (bacteria/exosomes), and discussions on sensitivity and substrate effects.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Mid-infrared photo-induced force microscopy (IR-PiFM/PiF-IR) enables high-resolution chemical imaging of surfaces with lateral resolution less than 5 nm. Here are some answers to questions about the physical background, practical handling and potential applications of PiF-IR including its use in the context of studying antimicrobial interaction. Such questions had been addressed to me during the Faraday Discussions on Vibrations at Interfaces which took place in April 2026 in Manchester/UK. The discussion was part of the theme "What is the question, what is the technique?" in the context of which I presented our recent work [James et al., Faraday Discussions, 2026, doi: 10.1039/d6fd00003g]. A modified version of this manuscript will be published in the themed collection "Vibrations at Interfaces" in Faraday Discussions.

</details>

### [Multiscale computational approaches to magnetic behaviour in Cobalt Ferrite (CoFe$_2$O$_4$) nanostructures](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02065v1)

**Authors:** Soham Chandra, Soumyajit Sarkar  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02065v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

(a) Workflow linking first-principles DFT+U calculations to atomistic spin modeling and statistical-mechanics simulations. Interatomic parameters (exchange interactions, anisotropy, magnetoelastic terms) are used to construct a spin Hamiltonian on the inverse spinel lattice, followed by Monte Carlo and spin-dynamics approaches to obtain thermodynamic and dynamic magnetic properties. (b) Crystal and magnetic structure of CoFe2O4, showing tetrahedral (A) and octahedral (B) sublattices. Dominant antiferromagnetic A–B exchange leads to ferrimagnetic ordering with antiparallel...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02065_figures/2605.02065_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Monte Carlo simulation workflow for atomistic spin models of CoFe2O4 inverse spinel ferrites. The procedure includes initialization of the lattice and model parameters, construction of the spin Hamiltonian, Metropolis-based Monte Carlo updates with energy evaluation, and measurement of thermodynamic and magnetic observables after equilibration.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents a unified computational framework to predict the magnetic properties of Cobalt Ferrite nanostructures across multiple length and time scales. By linking first-principles electronic structure calculations to atomistic and micromagnetic models, it enables the rational design of materials for spintronics and hyperthermia. The approach demonstrates how microscopic features like cation inversion and surface disorder dictate macroscopic device performance.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of bridging the gap between Angstrom-scale electronic interactions and macroscopic magnetic observables in Cobalt Ferrite nanostructures, specifically accounting for the nonlinear coupling between cation inversion, strain, and surface effects.

**Main result.** The establishment of a predictive multiscale computational framework that successfully transfers parameters from DFT+U to atomistic and micromagnetic models to predict size-dependent anisotropy, Curie temperature, and switching dynamics.

**Method.** A hierarchical multiscale pipeline integrating Density Functional Theory with Hubbard corrections (DFT+U), atomistic spin dynamics (Heisenberg Hamiltonian, Monte Carlo, and LLG equations), and continuum micromagnetic modeling.

**Model / system.** Cobalt Ferrite (CoFe2O4) nanostructures, including nanoparticles and thin films, modeled using an inverse spinel lattice with tetrahedral (A) and octahedral (B) sublattices and a generalized classical Heisenberg Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Curie temperature (Tc), coercivity (Hc), saturation magnetization (Ms), magnetocrystalline anisotropy (K1), magnetostriction, and hyperthermia response (SAR).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Exchange constants (Jij), magnetocrystalline anisotropy (K1), magnetoelastic coefficients (B1), cation inversion parameter (lambda), and Hubbard U parameter.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of a classical approximation for spins (neglecting quantum fluctuations at low temperatures) and the reliance on the accuracy of the Hubbard U parameter in DFT+U.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of the multiscale workflow from DFT to micromagnetics; Figure 2 illustrates the Monte Carlo simulation workflow including lattice initialization and Metropolis updates.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the need for multiscale modeling, establishes the electronic-scale foundation via DFT+U, develops the atomistic spin Hamiltonian and statistical mechanics approach, applies the framework to case studies (nanoparticles and thin films), and discusses future directions like machine learning and DFT+DMFT.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Cobalt ferrite (CoFe$_2$O$_4$) is a prototypical ferrimagnetic spinel oxide whose exceptional magnetic anisotropy, magnetoelastic coupling, and thermal stability underpin applications in spintronics, magnetic hyperthermia, energy harvesting, and catalysis. This chapter presents a comprehensive computational framework that integrates electronic$-$structure calculations with atomistic spin modeling, statistical mechanics, and continuum micromagnetics to predict magnetic functionality across length and time scales. Starting from density functional theory with Hubbard corrections (DFT$+$U), we derive exchange constants J$_{ij}$, magnetocrystalline anisotropy K$_1$, and magnetoelastic coefficients B$_1$, accounting for cation inversion, strain, and correlation effects. These parameters feed into generalized Heisenberg Hamiltonians, enabling Monte Carlo and Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert simulations of finite-size effects, hysteresis, coercivity, and hyperthermia response in nanoparticles and thin films. Coarse-graining strategies bridge to micromagnetic modeling, ensuring consistent parameter flow without empirical fitting. Computational case studies demonstrate size-dependent anisotropy enhancement, surface spin disorder, strain-tunable switching, and doping trends, revealing design principles inaccessible to experiment alone. Validation against benchmarks, e.g. Curie temperature, anisotropy constants, coercivity, magnetostriction, confirms predictive accuracy. Current challenges, e.g., U$-$parameter sensitivity, realistic surface chemistry, spin-lattice coupling, and large-scale integration are discussed alongside emerging directions including DFT$+$DMFT, coupled dynamics, and machine-learned potentials.

</details>

### [On the role of crack electrolyte wetting in the degradation and performance of battery active particles](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01806v1)

**Authors:** S. Luza-Vega, Y. Zhao, E. Martínez-Pañeda  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01806v1>

</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that electrolyte infiltration into battery particle cracks significantly alters electrochemical reaction rates and mechanical stress profiles. By using a coupled electro-chemo-mechanical model, the authors show that standard models neglecting crack-electrolyte coupling systematically underestimate both battery capacity and fatigue-inducing stresses.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how electrolyte wetting within cracks in Ni-rich cathode particles redistributes electrochemical reactions and affects battery degradation and performance.

**Main result.** The study finds that electrolyte wetting causes a 8x amplification of lithium flux at crack tips and that neglecting this coupling leads to a 25-32% underestimation of delivered capacity and a 10% underestimation of tensile stresses.

**Method.** A controlled comparison between a fully coupled electro-chemo-mechanical finite element model and a simplified single-particle model using a uniform flux assumption.

**Model / system.** A cracked Ni-rich layered oxide cathode particle (radius 5 um) embedded in a composite electrode containing a conductive carbon binder and liquid electrolyte.

**Key observables.** Lithium concentration, electrostatic potential, stress fields, interfacial reaction rates (flux), delivered capacity, and tensile stresses.

**Important parameters / regimes.** 1C and 3C discharge rates, lithiation/delithiation-induced volumetric changes, and crack geometry (length 1.77 um, width 0.078 um).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The electrolyte is treated as a binary liquid; cracks are assumed to be stationary; the electrolyte domain is a homogenized representation of liquid and carbon binder; no CEI formation is considered.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the problem overview and model geometries; Figure 2 shows equilibrium potential and molar volume vs stoichiometry; Figure 3 shows electrolyte potential distribution; Figure 4 shows spatial reaction rate and overpotential distribution; Figure 5 shows lithium concentration and hydrostatic stress; Figure 6 shows lithium flux evolution; Figure 8 shows stress and chemical strain ratios.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of particle cracking, presents the two modeling frameworks (Realistic vs. Idealized wetting), details the governing physics and numerical implementation, compares results regarding flux redistribution and concentration gradients, evaluates the impact on capacity and stress, and concludes with the implications for battery degradation modeling.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Cathode particle fracture is widely recognised as a major degradation mechanism in lithium-ion batteries, yet cracking also permits electrolyte wetting of newly exposed internal surfaces, modifying interfacial reaction pathways. The mechanistic role of electrolyte wetting in redistributing reactions within cracked particles remains unclear. Here, we isolate this effect through a controlled comparison between (i) a fully coupled electro-chemo-mechanical model resolving lithium concentration, electrostatic potential, and stress fields in both the active material and the electrolyte inside and outside cracks, and (ii) a single-particle chemo-mechanical model employing the conventional uniform flux assumption. The coupled model predicts strong spatial heterogeneity in interfacial reaction rates, with flux amplification approximately 8x relative to the imposed uniform flux at the crack tip. Reaction redistribution, and thus lithium flux, is governed predominantly by local solid-state lithium concentration and stress variations, while electrolyte potential gradients inside cracks remain secondary under the conditions considered. Uniform flux models can underpredict delivered capacity by 25% at 1C-rate; this discrepancy increases at higher rates. They also underestimate tensile stresses throughout the delithiation process by 10%, directly affecting crack driving conditions. These results demonstrate that neglecting crack-electrolyte coupling leads to systematic underestimation of both utilisation limits and fatigue-relevant stress histories.

</details>

**Authors:** Koushik Ghorai, Sayan Sarkar, Amit Agarwal  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01812v1>

A lon- gitudinal charge current Jx in a d-wave altermagnet generates a pure transverse orbital current JLz y , without any accompa- nying charge flow. Notably, when the field is aligned along the ±x and ±y directions, the longitudinal components of both spin and orbital currents vanish, providing characteristic signatures of the spin-splitter and orbital-splitter currents, respectively. Calculations are performed at T = 50 K with a scattering time τ = 0.1 ps.</figcaption>
</figure>

The orbital response does not change sign under reversal of Jz, confirming the dominant T -even contribution in the system. (b) Schematic of an altermagnet (AM)–ferromagnet (FM) heterostructure, where the AM produces a transverse spin current as well as an accompanying transverse orbital current. Within the FM, spin–orbit coupling transforms part of this orbital current into spin current, contributing to spin torques that switch the ferromagnetic magnetization (M). (c) Time evolution of the FGT magnetization components under the combined spin-splitter and orbital-splitter current induced...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01812_figures/2605.01812_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. (a) Spin-splitter current and three components of the orbital current in FeSb2. In the absence of spin–orbit coupling, the transverse orbital currents are comparable to the spin-splitter current, whereas the longitudinal component σLz x;x vanishes. (b) Due to mirror symmetries, the Drude contribution to σLz x;y is absent, and the response is entirely governed by the orbital Berry curvature. The same behavior holds for σLz y;x. (c) The charge current from both Drude and Berry-curvature contributions also vanishes. This is consistent with the above-discussed symmetry restrictions.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces the orbital-splitter current in altermagnets, an orbital analogue to the spin-splitter current. It demonstrates that in FeSb2, this current is purely intrinsic and can be much larger than the spin-splitter current, potentially enabling much faster magnetization switching in spintronic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the existence and properties of an orbital-splitter current (OSC) in collinear altermagnets, specifically looking for an orbital analogue to the known spin-splitter current.

**Main result.** The authors identify a purely intrinsic, highly anisotropic OSC in the d-wave altermagnet FeSb2 that can exceed the spin-splitter current by nearly four times and significantly accelerate magnetization switching in heterostructures.

**Method.** The study uses a density-matrix formalism and the quantum Liouville equation to derive orbital conductivity, combined with systematic crystallographic symmetry analysis of magnetic point groups.

**Model / system.** The primary system is the d-wave altermagnet FeSb2, modeled using a minimal tight-binding Hamiltonian that captures sublattice-dependent spin splitting and spin-orbit coupling.

**Key observables.** Orbital-splitter current (OSC), spin-splitter current (SSC), orbital Hall conductivity, orbital Berry curvature, and magnetization switching time.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Néel order parameter (Jz), spin-orbit coupling strength (lambda), relaxation time (tau), and chemical potential (mu).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The derivation assumes a steady-state solution to the quantum Liouville equation and utilizes the weak-scattering limit for the intrinsic contribution.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the schematic of the orbital-splitter effect; Figure 3 displays polar plots of spin and orbital currents; Figure 4 presents orbital conductivity dependence on the Néel vector; Figure 5 compares spin and orbital current components; Figure 6 shows AHE conductivity; Figure 7 compares magnetization switching times.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the OSC concept, provides a symmetry-based derivation of orbital conductivity, analyzes the specific case of FeSb2, explores the angular dependence of currents, and concludes with the application to magnetization switching in heterostructures.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In collinear altermagnets, the real-space rotational symmetry of opposite spin sublattices generates a large nonrelativistic spin-splitter current. Orbital transport in this setting has remained largely unexplored. Here, we introduce the orbital-splitter current (OSC), an orbital analogue of the spin-splitter current, and derive its Drude and orbital Berry curvature contributions using a density-matrix framework. We show that the $d$-wave altermagnet $\mathrm{FeSb}_2$ realizes a purely intrinsic OSC because mirror symmetries suppress the Drude channel by forcing the orbital magnetic moment to vanish. The OSC response is strongly anisotropic and, for selected field orientations, exceeds the spin-splitter current by nearly a factor of four. We further show that the OSC generates a damping-like torque in an altermagnet-ferromagnet heterostructure and, when combined with the spin-splitter current, significantly reduces the magnetization switching time.

</details>

**Authors:** Manish Kumar Mohanta  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02368v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the AgI (110) surface can host a robust, symmetry-protected persistent spin texture, expanding the known materials platform for spintronics from chalcogenides to halides. It shows that these spin textures are highly tunable via electric fields and strain, offering a new way to control spin and orbital transport.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether halide semiconductors can host symmetry-protected persistent spin textures (PST) and exploring the tunability of spin-orbital Hall responses via strain and electric fields.

**Main result.** The AgI (110) surface is found to host a robust PST that is resistant to strain and structural distortion, but can be transitioned to a Rashba-type texture by a vertical electric field.

**Method.** First-principles DFT calculations using Quantum Espresso and Wannier90, combined with analytical modeling using effective spin-orbit coupled Hamiltonians and the Kubo formula.

**Model / system.** The (110) surface of gamma-phase Silver Iodide (AgI), a non-centrosymmetric and nonsymmorphic zinc-blende structure, modeled via an effective Hamiltonian capturing anisotropic spin splitting.

**Key observables.** Spin Hall conductivity (SHC), orbital Hall conductivity (OHC), spin-projected band structure, spin-projected Fermi surface, and momentum offset (Q).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Bandgap of 1.85 eV, spin-orbit coupling constants (0.3-0.48 eV-Angstrom), biaxial strain (up to 4%), and vertical electric field strength.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the stability of the surface structure and relies on the accuracy of the pseudoDojo library pseudopotentials and the density functional approximation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows surface geometry, phonon stability, and electronic band structures; Figure 2 displays spin-projected bands and Fermi surfaces; Figure 6-8 illustrate the modulation of bands and Hall conductivities under strain and electric fields; Figure 9 details multilayer AgI properties.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with structural and stability analysis, moves to the identification of the PST and its electronic properties, develops analytical models for the spin-orbit interaction, investigates the effects of external perturbations (strain and electric fields), and concludes with the study of multilayer configurations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

A systematic investigation of the structural, electronic, and spin-orbital transport properties of the AgI (110) surface is presented using first-principles calculations combined with analytical modelling. The non-centrosymmetric and nonsymmorphic nature of the system gives rise to a robust persistent spin texture (PST), characterized by a unidirectional spin configuration and suppressed spin relaxation, enabling an effectively infinite spin lifetime. Unlike previously reported PST materials, which are predominantly based on chalcogen compounds, this work demonstrates that a halide semiconductor can host PST, thereby significantly expanding the materials platform for spintronic applications. The underlying mechanism is captured using an effective spin-orbit coupled Hamiltonian, which reproduces the anisotropic spin splitting and momentum shift observed in the band structure. This work introduces two new analytical models describing PST and compares them with existing models, offering new perspectives on PST arising from spin-orbit interaction. In addition, the system exhibits sizable intrinsic spin Hall conductivity (SHC) and orbital Hall conductivity (OHC), highlighting its potential for efficient charge-to-spin and charge-to-orbital conversion. The PST is found to be robust against biaxial strain, structural distortion, and multilayer formation, while a vertical electric field breaks the symmetry protection and drives a transition to a Rashba-type spin texture. These findings establish AgI (110) as a promising platform for realizing long-lived spin transport and tunable spin-orbit functionalities in the low-dimensional halide systems.

</details>

### [Photovoltaic creation of charged domain walls in barium titanate](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02407v1)

**Authors:** P. S. Bednyakov, P. V. Yudin, A. K. Tagantsev, J. Hlinka  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02407v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02407_figures/2605.02407_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1 The geometry of the experiment (a). The orientations of charged and neutral domain walls are marked with red and blue lines, respectively. The sample is illuminated with UV light and observed from the [001] direction; the electric field is applied along the [110] direction. (b) A photograph of the (001) surface of a typical sample with ferroelectric CDWs. Domain walls appear as colored stripes due to light interference when viewed slightly off from a top-down position.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02407_figures/2605.02407_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2 The (001) plane view of CDW formation. NDWs are seen as the horizontal lines in a-f, CDWs - as the vertical lines in f-h. (a) The domain struc- ture under an applied voltage of 300 V, which is identical to that initial (no UV). (b–g) The evolu- tion of the domain structure when the voltage is kept while the sample is UV illuminated. (h) Final configuration with conductive CDWs. The arrows show the direction of the spontaneous polarization.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02407_figures/2605.02407_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3 The time evolution of current during the NDW-to-CDW transformation, with the schematics of the domain structures. (a) Region 1: The voltage is applied and no illumination is present. No change in the domain structure is observed. Region 2: The voltage is kept. Under UV illumination, bending of NDWs and the formation of zigzag patterns are observed. Region 3: The system of CDWs is formed. I–V curves for the sample with numerous NDWs without UV illumination (b) and with it (c).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02407_figures/2605.02407_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 4 The photovoltaic mechanism for the desta- bilization of NDWs and the growth of CDWs.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02407_figures/2605.02407_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 5 Schematic for the stages of CDW growth: (a) appearance of original domain inclinations due to the asymmetry of the screening of sample edges with positive and negative free carriers; (b) growth of kinks due to the photovoltaic effect and their alignment into vertical columns; (c) formation of zigzag CDWs; (d) the final structure containing vertical CDWs. The view is taken in the same direction as in Fig. 2.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates how UV light and an electric field can be used to create conductive channels (charged domain walls) in insulating barium titanate. The researchers show that the bulk photovoltaic effect is the key driver for this structural transformation, offering a way to create reconfigurable electronic and opto-electronic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Understanding the mechanism behind the transformation of neutral domain walls (NDWs) into conductive charged domain walls (CDWs) in barium titanate under UV illumination and an electric field.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that the bulk photovoltaic effect (BPVE) drives the conversion of NDWs to CDWs by providing necessary charge screening at the walls, a process supported by both experimental current measurements and phase-field simulations.

**Method.** The study combines experimental optical observation and current-voltage measurements on BTO crystals with phase-field simulations using the finite element method (COMSOL) and a Landau-Ginzburg-Devonshire framework.

**Model / system.** The physical system is barium titanate (BaTiO3) single crystals with an initial structure of neutral domain walls parallel to the (110) plane, subjected to UV illumination and a DC electric field.

**Key observables.** Domain wall configuration (NDW vs. CDW), photocurrent (J), photovoltaic coefficient, and current-voltage (I-V) characteristics.

**Important parameters / regimes.** UV wavelength (365 nm), electric field strength (~4 kV/cm), light intensity (~10 mW/cm2), and time scales for CDW formation (10 minutes to 2 hours).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The simulation assumes homogeneous, through-the-sample generation of photo-carriers and treats light polarization as random and uniform.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows experimental geometry and the resulting CDW stripes; Fig 2 shows the time-series evolution of domain structures; Fig 4 and 5 illustrate the schematic mechanism and stages of CDW growth; Fig 6 shows phase-field simulation results of domain evolution.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with experimental setup and domain engineering, moves to the observation of current increases and domain transitions, proposes a mechanism based on the bulk photovoltaic effect, validates this via phase-field simulations, and concludes with a theoretical derivation of the photovoltaic coefficients.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The optical control of domain structures in ferroelectrics is of great interest. In the present work, we demonstrate the reliable creation of charged domain walls - conductive channels in otherwise insulating barium titanate - by a combined effect of light and electric field. We propose a scenario for the documented process, in which the bulk photovoltaic effect plays the key role, providing charge screening at the walls. Our scenario is supported by the results of phase-field simulations. The results are of interest for future reconfigurable electronic and opto-electronic devices.

</details>

**Authors:** Gabriel T. dos Santos, Roberto dos Reis, Vinayak P. Dravid  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01697v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Solid lines represent the ensemble mean (N = 5), with shaded re- gions indicating ±1σ uncertainty. The C–O and C–C channels exhibit the largest loss of latent integrity. The π–π∗-labelled trajectory should be read as a mixed low-energy response be- cause the measured 1–3 eV descriptor increases with dose. (b) Heatmap representation of latent channel-integrity values Ci across the measured dose range (152–1368 e−/˚A2).</figcaption>

The shaded regions indicate the fixed integration windows used for the nominal π–π∗, C–C, C–O, and M–O channels. The strongest spectral evolution occurs in the surface-plasmon region (∼15– 25 eV), while the low-energy region shows a smaller but non-negligible dose response. (b) Evolution of the window- integrated low-loss descriptor ˜neff as a function of cumulative dose for each channel. The descriptors are relative window areas, not absolute f-sum-rule effective electron numbers.</figcaption>

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents a machine learning framework using Physics-Informed Neural Networks to quantify how electron-beam irradiation degrades the chemical structure of MOFs. By encoding kinetic power-law equations into the neural network, the authors can extract meaningful degradation rates from sparse, noisy spectroscopic data. This approach allows for distinguishing between direct bond breaking and complex spectral redistribution during material damage.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Quantifying the dose-dependent degradation of beam-sensitive Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) during in situ electron microscopy characterization.

**Main result.** A Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) successfully models the kinetic degradation of C-O and C-C bonds in MIL-101(Fe), identifying a half-integrity threshold near 1.0e3 e-/A2 and revealing oscillator-strength redistribution in the pi-pi* window.

**Method.** The researchers use a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) constrained by a power-law ordinary differential equation, monotonicity, and a hierarchy prior to model spectral evolution from EELS data.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the Metal-Organic Framework MIL-101(Fe) studied via in situ low-loss Electron Energy-Loss Spectroscopy (EELS) under 300 kV electron-beam irradiation.

**Key observables.** Window-integrated low-loss spectral areas for pi-pi* (1-3 eV), C-C (4-7 eV), C-O (10-15 eV), and M-O (20-25 eV) energy windows.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Degradation rate constants (ki), kinetic exponents (pi), and the half-integrity dose threshold (Phi_1/2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The descriptors are relative window-integrated areas rather than absolute electron numbers; the model is applied to a very sparse dataset of only nine dose frames.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the PINN framework; Figure 2 displays in situ EELS spectra; Figure 3 presents inferred latent integrity trajectories and heatmaps; Figure 4 shows inferred degradation rates and exponents; Supplementary figures show ensemble predictions and ablation studies against unconstrained models.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of beam damage, describes the experimental EELS setup, details the PINN architecture and physics-based loss functions, presents the results of bond-specific degradation, and concludes with an ablation study comparing the PINN to standard neural networks.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Electron-beam irradiation limits atomic-resolution characterization of beam-sensitive hybrid materials, yet quantitative models that connect \textit{in situ} spectroscopy to dose-dependent degradation remain scarce. Here we use a physics-informed neural network (PINN) to model beam-induced spectral evolution in MIL-101(Fe) from an in situ low-loss electron energy-loss spectroscopy (EELS) dose series. Each spectrum is reduced to fixed-window low-loss descriptors, $\tilde n_{\mathrm{eff},j}(Φ)=\int_{\mathcal{W}_j}S(E,Φ)\,dE$, evaluated over nominal $π$--$π^{*}$, C--C, C--O, and M--O windows. These descriptors are relative window-integrated low-loss spectral areas, not absolute f-sum-rule effective electron numbers. For each spectral channel, a latent integrity variable $C_i(Φ)$ obeys the same uncoupled power-law degradation equation in normalized dose space, $dC_i/dφ=-k_i C_i^{p_i}$, regularized by monotonicity, boundedness, and a single hierarchy prior $k_{\mathrm{C\text{-}O}}\geq k_{\mathrm{C\text{-}C}}$. Applied to nine dose frames spanning 152--1368~e$^-$/Å$^2$, the ensemble PINN identifies C--O and C--C as the most strongly dose-sensitive linker-associated channels, with half-integrity thresholds of approximately $1.0\times10^3$~e$^-$/Å$^2$. The 1--3~eV $π$--$π^{*}$-labelled window increases with dose and is therefore interpreted as a mixed low-energy response, likely involving oscillator-strength redistribution rather than direct monotonic loss of a single bond population. The framework provides a dose-dependent, spectroscopy constrained description of MOF degradation while also defining the limits of what fixed-window low-loss EELS can assign without independent chemical-state validation.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01881v1>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new model for 2D amorphous materials, suggesting they are composed of stable, distinct crystalline motifs embedded in a random network rather than being purely disordered. Using machine learning potentials and kinetic Monte Carlo, the authors demonstrate that this polymorphic crystallite model is a general and accurate way to describe various multi-element monolayers.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The challenge of accurately modeling the atomic-scale structure and stability of monolayer amorphous materials, which lack long-range order and involve complex, competing structural motifs.

**Main result.** The authors propose a 'polymorphic crystallite model' where amorphous 2D materials consist of stable, randomly distributed crystallites (e.g., o-B2N2 and o-B4N4 in maBN) within a continuous random network, a model validated across maBN, maLiCl, and maBCN systems.

**Method.** An energy-driven kinetic Monte Carlo (EDKMC) framework combined with an active-learning-based machine learning potential (MLP) trained from first-principles DFT energies.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on multi-element 2D amorphous monolayers, specifically amorphous boron nitride (maBN), lithium chloride (maLiCl), and boron carbon nitride (maBCN), modeled via a polymorphic crystallite paradigm.

**Key observables.** Degree of disorder (DOD), ring statistics (5-, 6-, and 7-membered rings), density of states (DOS), and atomic forces/energy differences.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Simulation temperature (reference parameter), kBT (0.5 eV to 3 eV), and the degree of disorder (DOD).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The EDKMC approach assumes the melting process must be avoided to prevent irreversible transitions to 3D structures.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the EDKMC workflow and MLP accuracy validation; Figure 2 compares atomic configurations of different models and identifies specific structural motifs; Supplementary figures show convergence, stability, and electronic properties.

**Paper structure.** Introduction of the modeling challenge; description of the EDKMC active-learning framework and MLP training; application to maBN to propose the polymorphic crystallite model; validation of the model in maLiCl and maBCN; discussion of stability and electronic properties.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Modeling the atomic structure of amorphous materials has long been a critical challenge in materials science. Recent advances in monolayer amorphous materials enable direct observation of their atomic structures, paving the way for a better understanding of their atomic-scale models. Here, we investigate amorphous multielement monolayers using machine learning potential from first-principles total energies via energy-driven kinetic Monte Carlo based active-learning framework. A polymorphic crystallite model is proposed to describe the atomic configuration of monolayer amorphous boron nitride, as it consists of coexisting crystallite of $o-B_2N_2$ and $o-B_4N_4$ structural motifs. Generality of the polymorphic crystallite model is further validated in two other multielement monolayer amorphous systems. Monolayer amorphous LiCl shows coexisting hexagonal and tetragonal crystallites, while monolayer amorphous BCN contains a combination of graphene-like, h-BN-like, and borophene-like crystallites. These findings expand the classical picture of amorphous structure models and offer new insight into the microscopic structure of amorphous materials.

</details>

**Authors:** Michał A. Stróżyk, Jong-Dae Hong, JaeYong Kim, Iwona Jóźwik, Ryszard Diduszko, Jerzy Zagórski, Witold Chromiński, Kinga Suchorab, Katarzyna Ciporska, Marcin Brykała, Jacek Jagielski

**Summary.** This study investigates Chromium-coated Zircaloy-4 as a potential material for accident-tolerant nuclear fuel cladding. The researchers found that the Cr coating effectively delays oxidation at high temperatures, though thermal excursions cause complex interfacial changes like Fe segregation and nano-bubble formation.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Evaluating the effectiveness of Chromium (Cr) coatings on Zircaloy-4 cladding to prevent oxidation and hydrogen release during high-temperature nuclear accidents.

**Main result.** The Cr coating significantly reduces mass gain and delays oxide formation (above 600C vs 200C for uncoated), though it leads to Fe segregation and the formation of a Zr(Fe,Cr)2 Laves phase at the interface.

**Method.** High-temperature X-ray diffraction (HT-XRD), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), SEM, and TEM were used to study phase evolution and oxidation kinetics up to 1100C.

**Model / system.** A Zircaloy-4 (Z4) cladding substrate with a 10 +/- 2 micrometer Chromium (Cr) layer deposited via Arc Ion Plating (AIP).

**Key observables.** Oxidation thresholds, mass gain, lattice constants, thermal expansion coefficients, and microstructural features like nano-bubbles and Laves phase formation.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature range from RT to 1100C, coating thickness of ~10 micrometers, and oxidation environments (air, steam, and vacuum).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study is described as a preliminary structural study; long-term stability in complex reactor environments is not fully addressed.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares Z4 microstructure before/after coating and shows Cr grain structure; Figure 2 shows surface morphology and cross-sections of uncoated vs coated samples; Figure 3 presents TGA mass gain curves; Figure 6 and 7 show TEM/SEM of phase evolution and interface features.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of ATF cladding, describes the AIP deposition process, presents structural characterization of the coating, details high-temperature oxidation and phase transformation results, and concludes with interface analysis.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Following the Fukushima Daiichi disaster, an increasing number of studies concentrate on the development of Accident Tolerant Fuel (ATF) cladding materials for nuclear fuel, aiming to prevent the oxidation of zirconium during incidents such as Loss of Coolant Accident (LOCA) and to effectively lower the amount of heat and hydrogen released during emergency core cooling (ECC). Zirconium alloy cladding with a protective chromium (Cr) coating is considered one of the promising candidates, largely due to its relatively short timeline for deployment in nuclear power plants. In this study, Cr-coated and uncoated Zircaloy-4 claddings were evaluated using high temperature X-ray diffraction (HT-XRD) in vacuum over a temperature range from RT to 1100oC. The temperatures corresponding to the formation of oxide phases are >200oC and >600oC for the uncoated and Cr-coated samples, respectively. SEM and TEM characterisation of the sub-surface in Cr-coated specimen after HT-XRD revealed Fe segregation, formation of Zr(Fe,Cr)2 Laves phase and nano-bubbles at the former Cr / Z4 interface.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.02295"></a>
### [Quantum Limits of Electronic Transport in Nanostructured Macroscopic Conductors](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02295v1)

**Authors:** Agnieszka E. Lekawa-Raus, John S. Bulmer, Teresa Kulka, Magdalena Marganska, Nick Papior, Dwight G. Rickel, Fedor F. Balakirev, Jacek A. Majewski, Krzysztof Koziol, Karolina Z. Milowska  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02295v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable

**Summary.** This paper provides a new atomistic framework to explain how quantum interference at junctions governs the macroscopic electrical properties of carbon nanotube networks. By combining theory with high-field measurements, the authors show that transport is driven by junction-level physics rather than simple defects or doping. This offers a way to bridge the gap between nanoscale electronic properties and device-scale performance.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The microscopic principles governing charge transport in macroscopic assemblies of 1D and 2D materials remain unresolved, as current phenomenological models fail to connect electronic structure to macroscopic magnetotransport.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that macroscopic transport in disordered low-dimensional networks is governed by junction-level quantum interference, where positive magnetoresistance is controlled by junction overlap length and negative magnetoresistance arises from lattice-mismatched heterojunctions.

**Method.** The authors develop a unified atomistic framework linking quantum-coherent transport, thermal disorder, and magnetic-field effects, which is then validated against ultrahigh-field magnetotransport measurements up to 60 T.

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of macroscopic assemblies of carbon nanotube fibres, treated as disordered networks of one- and two-dimensional materials.

**Key observables.** Magnetoresistance (positive and negative), conductivity, and junction transport properties.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic field strength (up to 60 T), temperature range, junction overlap length, and lattice mismatch.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of transport in macroscopic 1D/2D networks, presents a new atomistic theoretical framework, describes ultrahigh-field experimental measurements on carbon nanotube fibres, and concludes with a statistical analysis of the data to identify the origin of magnetoresistance.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Macroscopic assemblies of one- and two-dimensional materials promise to translate nanoscale electronic properties into device-scale performance, yet the microscopic principles governing charge transport in such networks remain unresolved. In these systems, conductivity is often interpreted using phenomenological models that do not explicitly connect electronic structure to macroscopic magnetotransport. Here we develop a unified atomistic framework that links quantum-coherent transport, thermal disorder and magnetic-field effects, and combine it with ultrahigh-field magnetotransport measurements up to 60 T over a broad temperature range on carbon nanotube fibres. We show that positive magnetoresistance is controlled by junction overlap length, whereas negative magnetoresistance arises predominantly from lattice-mismatched heterojunctions rather than weak localisation alone. Statistical analysis of a large-scale numerical dataset reveals that the experimentally observed positive quadratic magnetoresistance originates from junction transport. These results show that macroscopic transport in disordered low-dimensional networks is governed primarily by junction-level quantum interference rather than solely by defects or doping.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.01156"></a>
### [Rational Mechanics of Material Strength in Brittle Solids](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01156v1)

**Authors:** Arash Yavari, Aditya Kumar  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01156v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01156_figures/2605.01156_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Schematic illustration of spatial and material covariance. The mappings ϕ and ϕ′ describe the same physical deformation process, and are related by a material relabeling Ξ and a spatial reparametrization ξ. The blue conﬁgurations represent the undeformed, stress-free body, while the red conﬁgurations represent the same deformed, stressed body. Restricting to Ξ = idB gives spatial covariance, while restricting to ξ = idS gives material covariance. The map ξ may be viewed as a warped lens through which the same deformed body is described. Covariance of a continuum ﬁeld theory means that any tensor ﬁeld, including scalar and vector ﬁelds, transforms under these maps via push-forward...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01156_figures/2605.01156_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Schematic illustration of the constitutively-admissible stress manifold A (bounded by the black ellipse) and the strength hypersurface S (red curve) in principal stress space. The strength hypersurface is a hypersurface of the constitutively-admissible stress manifold and separates safe and unsafe stress states.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01156_figures/2605.01156_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Plot of various isotropic strength surfaces in principal stress space under plane stress conditions. (a) Mohr-Coulomb surface, (b) Hoek-Brown surface, (c) 3D Hoek-Brown or GZZ surface, and (d) Mogi-Coulomb surface.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper establishes a rigorous mathematical framework for defining material strength in brittle solids undergoing large deformations. It shows that for the theory to be physically consistent (covariant), strength must be a function of both stress and strain rather than stress alone. The work provides a unified description that encompasses classical linear elasticity, anisotropy, and the effects of residual stresses.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The lack of a mathematically consistent and representation-independent definition of material strength in the framework of finite elasticity, particularly regarding how strength depends on both stress and strain.

**Main result.** The authors prove that a covariant strength function must depend on both stress and strain measures to ensure spatial covariance, and they demonstrate that for isotropic solids, the safe domain is star-shaped under a proportional-reduction hypothesis.

**Method.** A geometric and symmetry-theoretic approach using rational mechanics, finite elasticity, and invariant theory under spatial diffeomorphisms.

**Model / system.** Brittle solids (ranging from glass/ceramics to elastomers) modeled within the framework of nonlinear finite elasticity, including isotropic, anisotropic, and anelastic regimes.

**Key observables.** Strength function, strength hypersurface, and the safe domain.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Stress measures (Cauchy, Piola-Kirchhoff), strain measures, material symmetry groups, and eigenstrains.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The theory assumes a hyperelastic framework, focuses on strength-based criteria, and assumes that sufficiently large stresses are inadmissible.

**Figures summary.** A figure showing the eigenvalues of the Biot stress for isotropic solids.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the problem of strength in finite elasticity, moves to a covariant formulation using stress and strain pairs, analyzes the geometric properties of the strength hypersurface and safe domain, extends the theory to anisotropic and anelastic solids, and finally shows the reduction to classical linear elastic criteria in the small-strain limit.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Material strength is a classical concept with renewed importance in fracture mechanics, particularly in crack nucleation in brittle solids. We formulate material strength in finite elasticity and examine its geometric, constitutive, and symmetry-theoretic foundations. Spatial covariance requires a strength function to depend on both stress and the corresponding strain measure, so that strength is governed by the pair (stress,strain), not stress alone, and only then can representations based on different stress measures be consistently related, with classical stress-based criteria recovered as a special case. We analyze covariance under spatial diffeomorphisms and relate formulations based on the first Piola--Kirchhoff, second Piola--Kirchhoff, and Cauchy stresses. For stress-based criteria, we define the strength hypersurface as a subset of the constitutively admissible stress manifold and study the associated safe domain. Under standard regularity assumptions and the requirement that sufficiently large stresses are inadmissible, the strength surface is a smooth compact hypersurface of this manifold. For isotropic solids, we show that the safe domain is star-shaped under a proportional-reduction hypothesis. We extend the formulation to anelastic brittle solids, showing that residual stresses and eigenstrains modify the strength surface through the material metric, and discuss anisotropic strength via material symmetry. Finally, in the small-strain limit, the theory reduces to classical stress-based criteria.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper develops a multiscale method to monitor surfactant phase transitions in real-time using plasmonic sensing. By linking molecular-scale hydration to macroscopic optical shifts, the researchers can identify specific interfacial morphologies and their formation kinetics. This provides a robust, label-free way to study complex soft-matter interfaces.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of resolving the structural morphologies and transition kinetics of surfactant phases at solid-liquid interfaces in situ.

**Main result.** The establishment of a multiscale framework that uses plasmonic sensing to identify surfactant phases via dielectric fingerprints, specifically identifying a spectral shift reversal during the transition to a water-permeable phase.

**Method.** A multiscale approach combining atomistic Molecular Dynamics (MD) for morphology, Density Functional Theory (DFT) for dielectric properties, and Finite-Difference Time-Domain (FDTD) simulations for optical response, validated by in-situ LSPR sensing.

**Model / system.** The system consists of CTAB surfactant adsorption on a silica-coated gold nanodisk array in an aqueous environment, modeling three distinct phases: an impermeable bilayer, a capped bilayer, and a permeable cylindrical phase.

**Key observables.** Localized Surface Plasmon Resonance (LSPR) extinction peak shifts (delta lambda), effective permittivity, and transition rate constants.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Surfactant concentration, water volume fraction, layer height, and apparent rate constants (kappa_app).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The continuum model assumes an effective-medium approximation for the surfactant layers based on water volume fractions and molecular packing.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows CTAB phase schematics and MD snapshots of molecular/water distribution; Fig 2 shows continuum models for FDTD; Fig 3 shows predicted LSPR shifts and sensitivity analysis; Fig 4 shows experimental LSPR peak shifts and kinetic relaxation curves.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of interfacial sensing, describes the multiscale simulation framework (MD, DFT, FDTD), presents the experimental LSPR platform, details the results of phase identification and kinetic extraction, and concludes with the generalizability of the approach.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Surfactant self-assembly at solid-liquid interfaces governs interfacial stability, transport, and reactivity across many technologies, yet resolving interfacial surfactant phases and their transition kinetics in situ remains challenging. Here, we establish an atomistically grounded plasmonic framework that quantitatively maps interfacial surfactant phases and phase transitions onto optical signatures. Distinct morphologies differ in packing and hydration, modifying the effective permittivity within the optical near field and producing surfactant phase-specific plasmonic extinction peak shifts. Using cetyltrimethylammonium bromide on silica as a prototypical surfactant-surface system, we combine atomistic simulations, electronic-structure calculations, and continuum electrodynamics to translate molecular morphologies into predicted spectral shifts for literature-reported surface phases. We experimentally confirm the predicted ordering and magnitude of steady-state peak shifts during stepwise concentration changes, and extract transition kinetics from exponential relaxations of the time-resolved peak shift. A key mechanistic signature is reversal of the spectral shift direction upon transition from an impermeable bilayer to a water-accessible, channel-containing phase, consistent with hydration-driven reduction of the local effective permittivity. Because the approach relies on dielectric contrast in the plasmonic near field and works through a dielectric overlayer, it provides a broadly applicable route for real-time identification of interfacial surfactant phases and their kinetics in aqueous conditions.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that applying uniaxial strain to 2D altermagnets can break magnetic symmetries to induce a fully spin-polarized ferrimagnetic state. By controlling the direction of the strain, researchers can reversibly switch the spin polarization, providing a new framework for developing efficient, strain-controlled spintronic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of achieving controllable and reversible full spin polarization in symmetry-compensated magnets, such as altermagnets, where crystalline symmetries enforce spin degeneracy.

**Main result.** Uniaxial strain can break the symmetry between magnetic sublattices, driving a transition from an altermagnetic state to a fully compensated ferrimagnetic state that enables 100% spin polarization and reversible spin switching.

**Method.** The study uses symmetry and spin-group analysis, a minimal tight-binding model, first-principles DFT calculations, Boltzmann transport theory, and ab initio molecular dynamics.

**Model / system.** The study focuses on 2D altermagnetic (AM) and ferroelastic fully compensated ferrimagnetic (fFIM) systems, specifically identifying monolayer Mn2SeO and V2SO as candidate materials.

**Key observables.** Spin polarization percentage, spin-polarized band structure, integrated density of states (IDOS), spin-dependent electronic occupation, and spin-resolved longitudinal conductivities.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Uniaxial strain (compressive/tensile), strain direction (orthogonal x and y), and the transition from AM to fFIM states.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The mechanism is assumed to be general for 2D systems where uniaxial strain can induce sublattice inequivalence.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the illustration of strain engineering and Brillouin zones; Fig 2 displays the atomic structure, spin density, and spin-polarized transport for Mn2SeO; Fig 3 demonstrates the reversal of spin polarization in Mn2SeO under different strain directions; Fig 4 compares the ferroelastic configurations of V2SO.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of spin degeneracy in compensated magnets, presents a symmetry-based mechanism using a tight-binding model, validates the mechanism with first-principles calculations on Mn2SeO and V2SO, and concludes with the implications for spintronic applications.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Achieving controllable spin polarization and its reversal in symmetry-compensated magnets. Here we demonstrate, using symmetry analysis and a minimal tight-binding model, that uniaxial strain removes these constraints by inducing inequivalence between magnetic sublattices in two-dimensional (2D) system, driving an altermagnetic (AM) state into a fully compensated ferrimagnetic (fFIM) state and enabling fully spin polarization. Furthermore, strain along orthogonal directions gives rise to two energetically degenerate fFIM states with opposite spin polarization, enabling reversible spin switching. More importantly, the two symmetry-related fFIM states can be regarded as distinct ferroelastic variants, suggesting that this model or mechanism can be extended to ferroelastic fFIM systems. The generality of this mechanism is confirmed by combining spin-group analysis, first-principles calculations, and Boltzmann transport theory in representative candidates, including AM Mn$_2$SeO and ferroelastic fFIM V$_2$SO. Our results reveal a universal symmetry-driven framework for strain-controlled and -reversible fully spin-polarized transport and identify strain-engineered AM and ferroelastic fFIM systems as a promising platform for volatile and nonvolatile spintronic applications.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

**Summary.** This paper explains why the zero-energy peak in p-wave superconductors remains sharp despite surface roughness, unlike in d-wave superconductors where it broadens into a V-shape. The difference is attributed to the fact that d-wave states involve scattering between two different edge modes, whereas p-wave states involve only a single mode.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the physical origin of why Midgap Andreev Edge States (MAES) in p-wave superconductors are robust against surface roughness, while those in d-wave superconductors undergo significant broadening and develop a V-shaped density of states.

**Main result.** The authors show that d-wave fragility arises from inter-mode scattering between two distinct MAES modes, whereas p-wave robustness is due to the presence of only a single MAES mode, which prevents mode-mixing broadening.

**Method.** The study uses the quasiclassical theory of superconductivity via the Eilenberger equation and a random S-matrix model to simulate diffuse surface scattering.

**Model / system.** The system consists of 2D nonchiral p-wave and d-wave superconductors with a surface at y=0, modeled using a uniform gap approximation and a random S-matrix to represent varying degrees of surface roughness.

**Key observables.** Surface Density of States (SDOS) and the midgap peak at the Fermi energy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Roughness parameter W (ranging from 0 for specular to 1 for diffuse), specular reflection probability R, and the gap symmetry index m.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Fermi surface is assumed to be isotropic (circular), the surface is macroscopically flat, and the analysis focuses on the nonchiral, flat-band regime.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 relates roughness W to reflection probability R; Figure 2 shows the angular dependence of the gap sign; Figure 3 shows numerical SDOS for d-wave; Figure 7 shows the increase of scattering parameters with W in p-wave states.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of MAES sensitivity to roughness, establishes a mathematical framework using Eilenberger equations and S-matrix theory, performs a comparative analysis of p-wave and d-wave modes, and concludes by identifying inter-mode scattering as the mechanism for d-wave broadening.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We revisit the effect of surface roughness on midgap Andreev edge states (MAES) in p- and d- wave superconductors. For a perfectly specular surface, MAES form a flat band at the Fermi energy, which manifests as a sharp midgap peak in the surface density of states (SDOS). Previous theoretical studies have shown that MAES in p- and d-wave superconductors respond markedly differently to surface roughness. In the d-wave state, diffuse surface scattering significantly broadens the midgap peak in the SDOS, accompanied by the emergence of a V-shaped structure centered at the Fermi energy. In contrast, the midgap peak in the p-wave state remains robust against diffuse scattering. In this work, we clarify the physical origin of this contrasting behavior. A key aspect of our analysis is that the flat band in the d-wave state consists of two distinct types of MAES modes. We show that inter-mode diffuse scattering leads to substantial broadening of the midgap peak and to the formation of the V-shaped structure. By contrast, the robustness of MAES in the p-wave state arises from the presence of a single MAES mode in the flat band. These results provide new insight into the response of MAES to surface roughness.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Spin-orbit coupling effects in altermagnets: Interplay of weak spin and orbital ferromagnetism with relativistic splitting of electron states](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01780v1)

**Authors:** L. M. Sandratskii, K. Carva, V. M. Silkin  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01780v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
