**Summary.** This paper explores how spin-orbit coupling induces weak ferromagnetism and complex 3D magnetic structures in altermagnets like MnTe. It shows that the relativistic splitting of electron states and the formation of avoided crossings are central to the nonmonotonous behavior of induced magnetic moments. The work highlights the critical role of metal-ligand hybridization in defining altermagnetic properties.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how spin-orbit coupling (SOC) drives relativistic effects in altermagnets, specifically the interplay between weak spin/orbital ferromagnetism and the relativistic splitting of electron states.

**Main result.** The study reveals a nonmonotonous dependence of the weak ferromagnetic (WFM) moment on SOC strength and demonstrates that SOC transforms collinear spin-MSES into complex 3D noncollinear magnetic structures through avoided crossings.

**Method.** Relativistic Density Functional Theory (DFT) using the Augmented Spherical Waves (ASW) method, incorporating LDA, GGA, and LDA+U, combined with symmetry analysis based on spin-space groups.

**Model / system.** The prototypical altermagnet MnTe (NiAs crystal lattice) and CrSb, focusing on the transition from nonrelativistic to relativistic regimes.

**Key observables.** Spin and orbital atomic magnetic moments, weak ferromagnetic (WFM) moments, and the Magnetic Structure of Electron States (MSES).

**Important parameters / regimes.** SOC strength (scaled by gamma), Néel vector orientation, Hubbard U parameter, and metal-ligand hybridization.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The local atomic z-axis is assumed to be parallel to the direction of the atomic spin moment in the SOC operator; use of the quasisymmetry approximation to decouple spin and orbital components.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the MnTe unit cell and magnetic canting; Figures 2-6 illustrate the dependence of spin/orbital moments on SOC strength; Figures 10-12 and 17-20 show the evolution of MSES and band structure features like avoided crossings.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from an atomistic analysis of magnetic structures and WFM to an electronic-level study of electron state magnetic structures (MSES), utilizing symmetry hierarchies (NR to QS to Relativistic) to explain the emergence of complex 3D magnetism.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The aim of the paper is to contribute to reaching a deeper understanding of the formation of relativistic effects in altermagnets. The focus of the paper is on the phenomena of weak ferromagnetism (WFM) and relativistic DFT calculations combined with the symmetry analysis on the basis of spin space groups. The consideration is performed on two different levels. On the first level, the atomistic magnetic structure of weak ferromagnetic state is calculated. Both spin and orbital atomic moments are taken into account. We study the dependence of the WFM moment on the strength of the SOC and obtain a peculiar nonmonotonous type of dependence. An interesting result is obtained in quasisymmetry (QS) calculation where only the component of the SOC collinear to the Néel vector is taken into account. In QS calculation the spin WFM is absent while the orbital WFM is present. This reveals a principal difference in the formation of the spin and orbital magnetic moments. On the second level, the study is focused on the properties of individual electron states. We introduce the notion of the magnetic structure of the electron state (MSES). It is shown how the collinear spin-MSESs of both metal and ligand atoms and compensated orbital-MSES of the ligand obtained in the nonrelativiatic calculation transform into complex noncollinear 3D MSESs of both spin and orbital nature. An important role in the formation of MSESs is played by the relativistic splitting of the accidental spin degeneracies at general {\bf k} points filling the volume of the Brillouin zone. The formation of the regions of avoided crossings in the relativistic band structure is related to the nonmonotonous behavior of the WFM moment. The importance of the metal-ligand hybridization in the formation of the AM properties is discussed. Most of the calculations are performed for MnTe.

</details>

**Authors:** Wenjun Zhao, Yuri Fukaya, Pablo Burset, Jorge Cayao, Yukio Tanaka, Bo Lu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02140v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The black and red ellipses represent the two spin species of the al- termagnetic spin bands in momentum space and ˆn1(2) = (cos χ1(2), sin χ1(2), 0) denotes the in-plane N´eel vector in AM1 (AM2), i.e., the tilt angle of the spin-band lobe relative to the x axis. <figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. (a) Spin supercurrent Is as a function of the super- conducting phase difference φ with a fixed crystal misorien- tation χ2 −χ1 = π/2. (b) Spin supercurrent Is at zero phase difference (φ = 0) as a function of the relative orientation χ2 −χ1. (c) Maximum spin supercurrent Imax s versus the junction length L for χ2 −χ1 = π/2. (d) Maximum spin su- percurrent Imax s as a function of the misorientation χ2 −χ1. In all panels, J = 0.25t, the black curves correspond to the dx2−y2 case with χ1 = 0 and the red curves to the dxy case with χ1 = π/4. For panels (a), (b), and (d), the junction length is fixed at L = 22a.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a way to create a field-free platform for superconducting spintronics using altermagnetic layers. By tuning the orientation of the altermagnetic Neel vectors, one can control the magnitude and polarity of spin-polarized supercurrents and eliminate unwanted 0-pi transitions. This provides a highly tunable mechanism for generating dissipationless spin currents.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study aims to explore how inhomogeneous altermagnetic interlayers can be used to tailor superconducting spin currents and manipulate 0-pi transitions in Josephson junctions without the need for external magnetic fields.

**Main result.** The researchers found that a pi-misorientation of Neel vectors in altermagnetic layers suppresses pair-breaking and eliminates 0-pi transitions, while non-collinear alignment generates a tunable, spin-polarized Josephson current.

**Method.** The study employs a tight-binding model on a 2D lattice and uses the recursive Green's function method combined with the Mobius transformation to calculate the nonlocal part of the Matsubara Green's function.

**Model / system.** A 2D Josephson junction consisting of two superconducting leads connected by two altermagnetic layers and a normal region (SC1-AM1-N-AM2-SC2), featuring anisotropic spin splitting with d-wave symmetry.

**Key observables.** Josephson charge current (Ic), spin-polarized Josephson current (Is), and the current-phase relation (CPR).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Altermagnetic strength (J), misorientation angle between Neel vectors (delta chi), junction length (L), and interface transparency (tint).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system assumes translational invariance along the y-direction, a small number of sites for the normal region, and the use of a conventional BCS pairing potential.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides a schematic of the junction setup, illustrating the spin-band lobes and Neel vectors; Figure 4(d) shows the complex oscillatory behavior of the maximum spin supercurrent as a function of crystal misorientation.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of field-free spintronics, describes the altermagnetic Josephson junction model and Hamiltonian, details the Green's function numerical method, presents results regarding charge and spin currents under different Neel vector orientations, and concludes with experimental implications.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The pursuit of dissipationless spin supercurrents is a central theme in superconducting spintronics. We propose a field-free Josephson junction using an inhomogeneous altermagnetic interlayer with in-plane Néel vectors. We show that the current-phase relation and critical Josephson current is highly sensitive to the misorientation angle between the altermagnetic layers' Néel vectors. Specifically, at a $π$ misorientation with equal layer thicknesses the spatial oscillations of the superconducting pair amplitude, governed by the center-of-mass momentum, undergo mutual cancellation. This compensation suppresses individual layer pair-breaking, significantly enhancing the critical current and eliminating $0$-$π$ transitions. Furthermore, the non-collinear alignment of the Néel vectors facilitates the emergence of a net spin-polarized Josephson current. This spin current serves as a distinct signature of spin-triplet pair correlations, generated by the spin-dependent momentum shifts inherent to the altermagnetic exchange field. Our results establish a highly tunable, field-free platform for the realization of dissipationless spintronic devices.

</details>

### [SR-CGCNN: Shared Recurrent Convolution in Crystal Graph Neural Networks for Materials Property Prediction](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01304v1)

**Authors:** Satadeep Bhattacharjee  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01304v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01304_figures/2605.01304_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Schematic comparison between the standard three- layer CGCNN and the proposed shared-recurrent CGCNN (SR-CGCNN). In the standard architecture, three message- passing steps are performed using independently parameter- ized convolutional blocks with parameters θ1, θ2, and θ3. In SR-CGCNN, the same message-passing transformation with shared weights θ is applied recurrently for the same number of propagation steps. The graph construction, pooling oper- ation, and prediction head are kept unchanged. In the imple- mentation used in this work, the main convolutional weights are shared across recurrent steps, while normalization param- eters are allowed to be step-specific.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01304_figures/2605.01304_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Test-set parity plots for the standard three-layer CGCNN and the proposed SR-CGCNN. (a) Formation- energy prediction. (b) Band-gap prediction. The dashed line denotes ideal agreement between reference and predicted values. The three-step SR-CGCNN closely follows the stan- dard CGCNN-3 prediction trend for both targets while re- ducing the number of convolutional parameters from 66,432 to 22,912.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper proposes a more efficient version of Crystal Graph Neural Networks that uses shared weights across recurrent steps. This approach allows for deeper information propagation in crystal structures without a massive increase in the number of trainable parameters. It demonstrates that weight-tying can maintain high predictive accuracy for material properties like band gap and formation energy.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The coupling between message-passing depth and parameter count in Crystal Graph Convolutional Neural Networks (CGCNNs) makes increasing the structural receptive field computationally expensive.

**Main result.** The proposed SR-CGCNN achieves accuracy comparable to standard CGCNNs while using only 34.5% of the trainable convolutional parameters by using weight-tying across recurrent steps.

**Method.** The authors introduce a shared-recurrent architecture where the same convolutional weights are applied repeatedly across multiple message-passing steps to decouple depth from parameter count.

**Model / system.** The model operates on crystalline materials represented as graphs, where atoms are nodes and interatomic distances define edges, using datasets from the Materials Project.

**Key observables.** Formation energy (eV/atom) and band gap (eV).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of message-passing steps (T) and number of independent convolutional layers (L).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary benefit of deeper networks can be approximated by the repeated application of a single learned local update.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides a schematic comparison between the architecture of standard stacked CGCNNs and the proposed recurrent SR-CGCNN.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the parameter-depth coupling problem, describes the SR-CGCNN architecture, presents benchmarking results on formation energy and band gap datasets, and discusses limitations and future directions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Crystal graph neural networks predict materials properties by propagating information through local atomic environments. In conventional crystal graph convolutional neural networks (CGCNNs), this propagation depth is increased by stacking independently parameterized convolutional layers. This coupling between message-passing depth and parameter count raises a simple question: can repeated application of the same learned local update recover most of the benefit of a deeper CGCNN? We address this question by introducing a shared-recurrent CGCNN (SR-CGCNN), in which the main crystal-graph convolutional weights are tied across recurrent message-passing steps. The graph construction, pooling operation, and prediction head are kept unchanged, allowing a controlled comparison with standard CGCNN baselines. On Materials Project-derived formation-energy and band-gap datasets, a three-step SR-CGCNN approaches the accuracy of a standard three-layer CGCNN while using only $34.5\%$ of its trainable convolutional parameters. The formation-energy test mean absolute error changes from $0.0945$ to $0.0986~\mathrm{eV\,atom^{-1}}$, while the band-gap error changes from $0.4346$ to $0.4503~\mathrm{eV}$. These results indicate that repeated shared message passing can provide a parameter-efficient approximation to stacked CGCNN depth, offering a compact recurrent interpretation of crystal graph convolution.

</details>

### [Stochastic Cluster Expansion for Excited State Energies](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01064v1)

**Authors:** Annabelle Canestraight, Russell Miller, Libor Veis, Vojtech Vlcek  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01064v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

Error bars denote the standard error of the mean for 25 samples. (b) The Naphthalene–TCNE complex, with the Naphthalene HOMO and TCNE LUMO shown.</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. (a) Gap renormalization for Benzene–TCNE and Naphthalene–TCNE, compared with full DMRG. Error bars denote the standard error of the mean for 25 samples. (b) Standard error of the mean as a function of the number of stochastic samples. (c) Standard error of the mean per electron.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01064_figures/2605.01064_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. (a) Singlet–triplet gap as a function of n-acene length. Results are shown for the FCS-only calculation (HOMO and π∗), the SCE method, and DMRG+PDFT calculations[12]. (b) Dimension of the full Hilbert space for the complete π subspace compared to the largest active space used within the SCE method.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a stochastic cluster expansion method to calculate excited-state energy gaps in large, strongly correlated molecular systems. By treating a small active space exactly and sampling the rest of the orbital environment stochastically, the method achieves high accuracy with a massive reduction in computational complexity. It demonstrates success on benchmark systems like polyacenes and charge-transfer complexes.

**Why it may be interesting.** The method provides a scalable way to handle large-scale many-body problems by using stochastic sampling to reduce the effective dimension of the Hilbert space, which is highly relevant for simulating large-scale quantum dynamics and electronic structure.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Calculating excited-state electronic structures in strongly correlated systems is difficult due to the exponential scaling of the many-body Hilbert space and the challenge of constructing controlled active spaces.

**Main result.** The Stochastic Cluster Expansion (SCE) framework successfully extends to excitation gaps, providing accurate singlet-tricept gaps for polyacenes and charge-transfer complexes with a significantly reduced Hamiltonian dimension.

**Method.** An extension of the stochastic cluster expansion (SCE) that expresses energy differences as a hierarchy of orbital-space cluster contributions, using a minimal frontier chemical subspace (FCS) treated exactly and a stochastic sampling of the remaining orbital environment.

**Model / system.** Strongly correlated electronic systems, specifically charge-transfer complexes (e.g., Naphthalene-TCNE) and polyacenes (up to heptacene), modeled using many-body Hamiltonians in an orbital-based framework.

**Key observables.** Excitation gaps, specifically singlet-triplet (S0-T1) energy gaps.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of orbitals in the Frontier Chemical Subspace (FCS), number of stochastic samples (N_zeta), and the cluster expansion order.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes correlation is sufficiently localized for truncated expansion; assumes the excitation is predominantly captured within the defined FCS; assumes the energy gap is evaluated between states sharing nearly the same eigenvectors across stochastic samplings.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows gap renormalization vs FCS size for Naphthalene-TCNE; Fig 2 illustrates gap renormalization for Benzene-TCNE, error convergence vs number of samples, and error per electron; Fig 3 compares singlet-triplet gaps across polyacene lengths and compares Hilbert space dimensions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of excited-state scaling, presents the SCE formalism for energy differences, describes the partitioning into FCS and stochastic rest-space, demonstrates the method on charge-transfer complexes and polyacenes, and discusses error convergence and limitations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Excited-state electronic structure in strongly correlated systems remains challenging due to the exponential scaling of the many-body Hilbert space and the difficulty of constructing systematically controlled active spaces. Building on the stochastic cluster expansion (SCE) framework previously developed for ground-state correlation energies, we extend the formalism to excitation gaps by expressing energy differences directly as a hierarchy of orbital-space cluster contributions. In this formulation, excitation energies are reconstructed from reduced-rank calculations involving a minimal frontier chemical subspace (FCS), treated exactly, together with stochastic sampling of the remaining orbital environment. This approach eliminates the need for large or chemically preselected active spaces. We demonstrate the method on charge-transfer complexes and polyacenes, where accurate singlet-triplet gaps are obtained that agree with full-system results. The method converges with low-order cluster terms and provides a systematically improvable framework for excited states in correlated systems.

</details>

**Summary.** This paper examines how the thickness of Ti/Cu layers affects the growth and structural quality of multilayers. It finds that larger periods promote better crystallinity and regularity, while very thin layers suffer from interdiffusion and loss of periodic contrast.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating how the bilayer period thickness affects the structural evolution, morphology, crystallization, and texture development of Ti/Cu multilayers.

**Main result.** Increasing the period thickness leads to more regular structures with enhanced crystallization and texture, whereas very small periods (4 nm) exhibit significant intermixing and reduced periodic contrast.

**Method.** Magnetron sputtering was used for synthesis, followed by characterization using TEM (STEM-HAADF, HRTEM), SAED, XRD, and X-ray reflectometry.

**Model / system.** Ti/Cu multilayer coatings with periods ranging from 4 nm to 52.5 nm, deposited on Si(111) substrates via magnetron sputtering.

**Key observables.** Crystallinity, texture, layer roughness, layer waviness, interplanar spacings, and periodic contrast.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Period thickness (d) from 4 to 52.5 nm, layer thickness (l) from 1.00 to 26.25 nm, and total stack thickness of ~420 nm.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study uses periodic multilayers as model objects to separate basic mechanisms from the complexity of aperiodic supermirrors; XRD background was approximated by an exponential function.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows residual gas composition; Figure 2 shows STEM-HAADF cross-sections; Figure 3 and 5 show SAED and HRTEM for specific periods; Figure 8 shows XRD peak decomposition; Figure 9 shows XRR curves.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scientific problem of structural evolution, describes the experimental synthesis and characterization setup, presents results for various period thicknesses, analyzes phase evolution and lattice expansion, and concludes with a discussion on growth-induced instabilities.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Ti/Cu multilayers with periods ranging from 4 to 52.5 nm were synthesized by magnetron sputtering to examine how the period thickness affects morphology, crystallization, texture development, and preservation of periodicity. The structural evolution was analyzed using complementary transmission electron microscopy techniques with X-ray diffraction and reflectometry. The results show that the period thickness governs the balance between interfacial transition-region formation, crystallization, and growth-induced morphological instabilities. At the smallest period of 4 nm, the structure has strongly reduced periodic contrast, which may be attributed to extended interfacial regions, partial Cu-Ti intermixing, and suppression of well-defined layer formation. At 10 nm, the multilayer structure is preserved but remains affected by accumulated roughness and layer waviness; local modification of the Cu lattice is suggested to be significant relative to the bilayer period, likely due to mixed Cu-Ti interfacial regions and/or coherent strain. With increasing period, the multilayers become more regular, and the layers show progressive crystallization and texture development during growth. Cu crystallizes predominantly in the fcc structure with a strong Cu(111) contribution, whereas Ti exhibits a more complex structural evolution, from an amorphous or weakly crystallized state near the substrate toward a more ordered and textured state closer to the surface.

</details>

### [Surface segregation of liquid metal plasma-facing component alloys: A ReaxFF investigation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01863v1)

**Authors:** Md Adnan Mahathir Munshi, Abdul Aziz Shuvo, Mike Kotschenreuther, Adri C. T. van Duin, Bladimir Ramos-Alvarado  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01863v1>

Left panels: Depth-resolved probability density functions (PDFs) of Sn (deep teal), Al (light brown) and O (red) atoms along the z-axis, illustrating their spatial distributions across the slab. Right panels: Atomic snapshots showing the mixed Sn-Al and Sn-Al-O structures at each temperature.</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 3(b) demonstrates strong chemical separation compared to the more homogeneous</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 4. (a) Depth profile of Sn and Al atomic percentages in Sn–Al slab at 1300 K. (b) Same slab after oxygen exposure, showing Sn, Al, and O along the z-axis. Oxygen penetrates near-surface regions and creates interfacial zones with abrupt Sn and Al changes, marking reactive boundaries.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper uses reactive molecular dynamics to show that oxygen and hydrogen can be used to engineer the surfaces of liquid metal alloys for nuclear fusion. By driving low-Z elements to the surface, these impurities create a protective layer that reduces sputtering while maintaining a stable high-Z bulk. This provides a predictive method for designing self-healing materials for extreme fusion environments.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The study addresses the challenge of designing liquid metal plasma-facing components (PFCs) for fusion reactors that can handle extreme heat while minimizing sputtering. Specifically, it investigates how non-metal species (O and H) drive the surface segregation of low-Z elements (Al, Li) to create self-adaptive, low-sputtering surfaces.

**Main result.** The researchers demonstrate that oxygen and hydrogen induce strong surface segregation of Al and Li in Sn-based alloys, with the simultaneous presence of O and H producing the most pronounced segregation. They established a mechanistic link between non-metal chemistry and interfacial structure, providing a predictive framework for alloy design.

**Method.** The study uses Reactive Molecular Dynamics (MD) with a newly developed and validated ReaxFF reactive force field. The simulations involve high-temperature ramping and the introduction of reactive species to observe compositional evolution.

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of liquid metal alloy slabs (Sn-Al and Sn-Li) with free surfaces, exposed to oxygen (O) and hydrogen (H) radicals. The model uses a slab-based nanostructure with periodic boundary conditions and vacuum layers to simulate the interface.

**Key observables.** Probability density functions (PDFs), local atomic composition profiles, radial distribution functions (RDF), and a new overlap-based segregation index (S) to quantify species separation.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperatures ranging from 300 K to 1300 K; alloy compositions including Sn-Al (7.4 wt% Al) and Sn-Li (9, 20, and 26 at% Li); presence of O and H radicals.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The diamond-cubic starting lattice is assumed to be a neutral starting point as high-temperature ramping removes structural memory; the ReaxFF potential is assumed to be accurate for the studied condensed-phase configurations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the slab nanostructure topology; Figure 2 details the ReaxFF workflow; Figures 3-5 show PDFs and snapshots for Sn-Al systems; Figures 6-12 show depth profiles and snapshots for Sn-Li systems under various O and H exposures; Figure 13 shows temperature-dependent profiles.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the development and validation of the ReaxFF potential, followed by the investigation of binary Sn-Al and Sn-Li alloys, then explores the effects of oxygen, hydrogen, and their synergistic combination on surface segregation, concluding with a unified view of segregation regimes.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Engineering liquid metal alloys offers a transformative pathway for plasma-facing components (PFC) by enabling chemically tailored surfaces that can simultaneously optimize plasma-material interactions, reduce divertor heat flux, and enhance core plasma confinement, thereby advancing the commercial viability of nuclear fusion power plants. This study, employing an atomistic simulation framework, provides direct evidence that incorporating non-metal surface-active agents (such as O and H, or their combination) enables strong surface segregation. This capability makes tin-aluminum (Sn-Al) and tin-lithium (Sn-Li) alloys, with suitable compositions, good candidates for PFC applications. Specifically, the presence of low-Z solutes (Li, Al) leads to preferential surface enrichment, which imparts low-Z sputtering characteristics, while the Sn solvent maintains thermophysical stability. To systematically examine this behavior, we first optimized ReaxFF parameter sets for Sn-Al, Sn-Al-O, Sn-Li, Sn-Li-O, Sn-Li-H, and Sn-Li-O-H systems. We validated them using formation energies and elastic constants. We then employed reactive molecular dynamics simulations to resolve the coupled effects of surface segregation and impurity-driven chemistry at fusion-relevant temperatures. We also introduced an overlap-based segregation index that captures interfacial compositional separation directly from atomistic density distributions. This metric reveals a clear hierarchy of segregation regimes across all systems and presents a unified view of segregation across all observations reported herein. Together, these findings establish a mechanistic link between non-metal chemistry and interfacial structure, providing a predictive framework for designing self-adaptive, low-sputtering liquid metal alloys for fusion applications.

</details>

**Authors:** Subhadeep Chakraborty, Aabir Mukhopadhyay, Udit Khanna, Sourin Das  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02095v1>

**Summary.** This paper explores the topological properties of three-terminal Josephson junctions, revealing a hierarchy of Chern and dipolar phases. It shows that at specific chiral points, synthetic flat bands emerge that suppress Josephson currents and create a 'sweet plateau' of phase insensitivity. This discovery provides a blueprint for a new class of Andreev qubits that are intrinsically protected against phase-bias noise.

**Why it may be interesting.** It presents a novel paradigm for quantum information protection by moving from local 'sweet spots' to global 'sweet plateaus' using intrinsic Hamiltonian symmetries, which is highly relevant for the development of noise-resilient superconducting quantum architectures.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the challenge of mitigating dephasing and decoherence in superconducting qubits caused by fluctuations in superconducting phase biases.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that breaking time-reversal symmetry in a three-terminal Josephson junction creates synthetic flat bands that provide a 'sweet plateau' of phase insensitivity, enabling a robust architecture for symmetry-protected Andreev qubits.

**Method.** The study uses an analytical approach involving the Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) spectrum, Berry curvature integration for Chern numbers, and Wilson loops for dipole polarization, supplemented by numerical simulations of transconductance and density of states.

**Model / system.** A three-terminal Josephson junction (3-TJJ) realized with semiconducting nanowires and proximity-induced s-wave superconductivity, modeled using a scattering matrix approach in a synthetic Brillouin zone defined by superconducting phase differences.

**Key observables.** Chern numbers, dipole polarization, time-averaged transconductance, Berry curvature, and Andreev bound state (ABS) energy spectrum.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The scattering parameter zeta, superconducting phase biases (phi2, phi3), chirality parameter delta_G, and the chiral points (zeta = 2pi/3, 4pi/3).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the short-junction limit for determining Andreev bound state energies.

**Figures summary.** Figures show Berry curvature contours, dipole polarization magnitude, and the identification of the dipolar phase; plots of time-averaged conductance and the gradient of ABS energy; and a comprehensive phase diagram mapping gap closings and topological transitions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the 3-TJJ system, analyzes the hierarchical topology (Chern vs. dipolar) of the BdG spectrum, demonstrates the emergence of synthetic flat bands at chiral points, explores the resulting quantized transconductance, and concludes with implications for robust qubit architectures.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We uncover hierarchy of topological phases within the synthetic Brillouin zone of a three-terminal Josephson junction's (3-TJJ's) Bogoliubov-de Gennes spectrum. We demonstrate that the above-gap continuum realizes a Chern insulator phase with quantized monopole charges (\pm 1), while the subgap Andreev bound states (ABS) are characterized by a quantized dipolar invariant. By breaking time-reversal symmetry at the junction, we induce synthetic flat bands that suppress DC Josephson currents across the entire phase-bias space. Furthermore, under voltage bias, the junction exhibits a robust quantization of the time-averaged transconductance that is reminiscent of a quantized Hall conductance plateau owing to the flat band limit and its dipole phase. As a byproduct, the flat band produces a global "sweet plateau" of phase insensitivity, surpassing localized sweet spots of conventional superconducting qubits and enabling a robust architecture for symmetry-protected Andreev qubits.

</details>

**Authors:** R. Weht, A. A. Aligia, M. Nunez-Regueiro  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01115v1>

</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** The paper demonstrates that reversing the stacking order in rhombohedral graphite creates a unique interface hosting four topological flat bands. This discovery provides a theoretical mechanism for stable, high-density electronic states that could potentially host superconductivity.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the emergence of topological flat bands at the interface of inverted rhombohedral graphite stacking (ABC to CBA) and their potential link to high-Tc superconductivity.

**Main result.** The study finds that inverting the rhombohedral stacking order creates four robust topological flat bands at the interface, with charge density concentrated at the inversion layer.

**Method.** First-principles Density Functional Theory (DFT) using VASP and a tight-binding model mapped onto a Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) chain.

**Model / system.** Various graphite stacking configurations, specifically focusing on the interface between ABC and CBA rhombohedral sequences, modeled via a 1D SSH-like chain Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Energy bands, density of states (DOS), and charge density distribution.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Interlayer separations (3.25-3.53 A), K and K' points of the Brillouin zone, and the topological condition |qa| < sqrt(2/3) |tz/t|.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is modeled using nearest-neighbor intralayer and interlayer hopping; the SSH mapping is used for physical interpretation.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows flat bands in an ABC slab; Fig 2 shows charge localization in Bernal/rhombohedral interfaces; Fig 3 shows four flat bands in the inverted ABC/CBA interface; Fig 4 illustrates the stacking inversion structure.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by investigating rhombohedral slabs, moves to Bernal/rhombohedral interfaces, then presents the central result of inverted rhombohedral interfaces, supported by an SSH model mapping.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Motivated by the indications of high-Tc superconductivity in natural graphite enriched in the rhombohedral phase, we study the band structure of several stacking configurations that combine two of the three graphite structures as well as modifications of the rhombohedral sequence (from ABCABC... to CBACBA...), using first-principles calculations. We focus in particular on the possible emergence of flat bands near the Fermi level. When the two different rhombohedral orderings are combined, flat bands of topological origin emerge at the interface between the two domains, near the K and K' points of the Brillouin zone. Mapping a simple tight-binding model of a rhombohedral slab along the direction perpendicular to the graphene layers onto a Su-Schrieffer-Heeger chain provides a transparent understanding of the underlying physics.

</details>

**Authors:** Eliza Wieman, Nejc Nagelj, Ethan Curling, Larry Chen, Jin Yu, A. Paul Alivisatos, Aaron Lindenberg, Benjamin T. Diroll, Jacob H. Olshansky, Jihong Ma, Burak Guzelturk, Benjamin L. Cotts

**Summary.** This paper presents a contact-free method using time-resolved X-ray diffraction to track how heat moves through quantum dot thin films. It discovers that heat dissipates much more slowly in solid films than in liquids, highlighting a significant bottleneck for thermal management in future optoelectronic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The thermal properties and heat dissipation dynamics of colloidal quantum dot (QD) thin films are poorly understood compared to their electronic properties, which is critical for preventing device degradation.

**Main result.** The study reveals a massive four-order-of-magnitude difference in thermal relaxation timescales between liquid dispersions (~180 ps) and solid thin films (~2.3 us), with the thin-film thermal conductivity found to be as low as 0.55 W m^-1 K^-1.

**Method.** Time-resolved X-ray diffraction (TR-XRD) using GIWAXS and WAXS geometries to monitor changes in Bragg peak intensities (Debye-Waller Factors) following pulsed optical excitation.

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of CdSe/CdS core/shell quantum dots in two environments: close-packed thin films on fused silica and liquid dispersions in dodecane. Thermal transport is modeled using 1D transient heat transfer and Finite Element Modeling (FEM).

**Key observables.** Bragg peak positions (lattice expansion), Bragg peak intensities (Debye-Waller Factors), and amplified spontaneous emission (ASE) spectra.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Thermal conductivity (0.55 W m^-1 K^-1), interfacial thermal conductance (~15 MW m^-2 K^-1), thermal relaxation lifetimes (ps to us scales), and excitation fluence.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analytical model assumes a semi-infinite slab and full conversion of laser energy to heat; the FEM model is used when thermal penetration depth exceeds film thickness.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 shows ASE spectra indicating optical gain; Figure 3 displays 2D diffraction images and intensity changes; Figure 5 shows simulations of depth-averaged temperature evolution and comparison between experimental data and FEM/analytical models.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of thermal management in QDs, describes the TR-XRD experimental setup and the QD systems used, presents the validation of the device-like environment via optical gain, details the extraction of thermal parameters via DWF and FEM, and concludes with the comparison of liquid vs. solid-state dynamics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Colloidal quantum dots (QDs) and their thin-films are increasingly used in electronic and photonic devices replacing traditional bulk semiconductors. However, thermal properties of the QDs are comparatively underexplored relative to device development efforts. This study shows the use of time-resolved X-ray diffraction as a contact-free method to probe the thermal response of QDs in device-like environments, providing in-situ insights for future thermal management strategies. Through the extraction of Debye-Waller Factors on a sub-nanosecond timescale, we use time-resolved X-ray diffraction to directly capture the heating and cooling of core/shell CdSe/CdS QDs following pulsed optical excitation. In a QD thin-film that actively provides optical gain, the thermal conductivity is found to be as low as 0.55 $\mathrm{W\,m^{-1}\,K^{-1}}$, because of the poor heat flow within close-packed QD solids. For QDs dispersed in liquids, interfacial thermal conductance is found to dominate the thermal relaxation with a conductance on the order of 15 $\mathrm{MW\,m^{-2}\,K^{-1}}$.

</details>

### [Two distinct superconducting regimes in Ti4Co2O under pressures](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01893v1)

**Authors:** Lifen Shi, Keyuan Ma, Binbin Ruan, Zhen Wang, Pengtao Yang, Zhian Ren, Jianping Sun, Gang Li, Fabian O. von Rohr, Bosen Wang, Jinguang Cheng  
**Type:** both · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01893v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Summary.** This paper explores how high pressure can tune the superconducting properties of Ti4Co2O, revealing two distinct phases. It demonstrates that the superconducting transition temperature changes non-monotonically because pressure shifts the electronic density of states. This work provides insight into how external stimuli can control superconductivity in materials with strong electron correlations.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the pressure-induced evolution of superconductivity and the origin of the non-monotonic behavior of Tc and Bc2(0) in the Ti4Co2O superconductor.

**Main result.** The study identifies two distinct superconducting regimes (SC1 and SC2) driven by electronic reconstruction, where Tc shows a non-monotonic dependence due to the shifting density of states relative to the Fermi level.

**Method.** High-pressure electrical transport and magnetic susceptibility measurements using piston-cylinder and diamond anvil cells, combined with synchrotron X-ray diffraction and DFT calculations.

**Model / system.** The physical system is the single-crystal eta-carbide type superconductor Ti4Co2O, which possesses a cubic structure (space group Fd-3m) and exhibits strong electron correlations with weak spin-orbit coupling.

**Key observables.** Superconducting transition temperature (Tc), upper critical field (Bc2(0)), resistivity exponent (n), density of states at Fermi level (N(Ef)), and bulk modulus.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Low-pressure phase (P < 12.3 GPa), high-pressure phase (P > 20.5 GPa), Pauli paramagnetic limit (BpBCS), and pressure coefficient (dTc/dP).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The non-monotonic Tc is attributed to the evolution of the density of states under compression, assuming the structural integrity of the lattice up to 55.8 GPa.

**Figures summary.** The figures likely illustrate the pressure dependence of Tc and Bc2(0), the evolution of the resistivity exponent n, and the density of states (DOS) calculated via DFT.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the material context, describes the experimental high-pressure setup, presents the transport and structural results, analyzes the two superconducting regimes through transport exponents and DOS, and concludes with the implications for electron-phonon coupling.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We report on the pressure dependence of superconducting transition temperature Tc and upper critical field Bc2(0) through electrical transport of the Ti4Co2O superconductor (eg.,the superconducting transition temperature Tc = 2.5 K and the Bc2(0)=7.2T=2.9Tc). We find that the Tc exhibits non-monotonic pressure dependence:it rises monotonically at first with a pressure coefficient of dTc/dP=0.034 K/GPa, but rapidly decreases around 10-20 GPa, and then increases with the dTc/dP = 0.023 K/GPa, up to= 4.31 K at 69.7 GPa. Concurrently, the Bc2(0)exhibits a dome shaped pressure dependence, with its maximum at 5 GPa of almost twice the value at ambient pressure, exceeding the weak-coupling Pauli paramagnetic limit Bp throughout the whole pressure range. By comparing the normal-state and superconducting properties, we identify two distinct superconducting regimes, with a low-pressure superconducting phase characterized by an enhanced Bc2(0)values and Fermi-liquid normal-state electrical transport (the exponent n = 2), and a high-pressure superconducting phase with a monotonically increased Tc and an enhancement in phonon scatterings (the exponent n = 4). Room-temperature synchrotron X-ray diffraction indicates that there is no structural transition up to 55.8 GPa, which gives a relatively large bulk modulus of 192 GPa in comparison with other alloy superconductors. First-principles calculations suggest that the nonmonotonic Tc maybe closely related to the evolution of the density of states of Ti4Co2O upon compression, which is different from those of isostructural superconductors Ti4Ir2O and Nb4Rh2C. Our results show that even in the Ti4Co2O with weak spin-orbit coupling, superconductivity remains highly sensitive to the external stimuli such as pressure.

</details>

### [Two-Photon-Induced Direct 3D Printing of Freeform High-Index Phase-Change Sb2S3 Nanostructures](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01054v1)

**Authors:** Abhrodeep Dey, Andrea Dellith, Anne Sauer, Uwe Hübner, Henrik Schneidewind, Markus A Schmidt, Astrid Bingel, Volker Deckert, Jer-Shing Huang, Wei Wang  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01054v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2. Optical response and structural characterization of Sb–BDCA. (a)</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01054_figures/2605.01054_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 4. DITPS-based printing and imaging of holographic metasurface. (a) (Left</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper presents a new 3D printing technique called DITPS that allows for the direct, maskless fabrication of complex 3D nanostructures using high-index phase-change materials. By using a liquid precursor, the method overcomes the geometric limitations of traditional thin-film deposition and lithography. This enables rapid prototyping of advanced photonic devices like 3D metasurfaces and holograms.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Current fabrication methods for high-index chalcogenide phase-change materials (PCMs) are limited to 2D or quasi-3D thin films, which restricts the ability to rapidly prototype complex, freeform 3D nanostructures.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrated a solution-phase direct printing method (DITPS) that enables the maskless, fast, and cost-effective 3D printing of high-index Sb2S3 structures, including helices, Fresnel zone plates, and holographic metasurfaces.

**Method.** Dip-in two-photon-induced solidification (DITPS) using a femtosecond laser to trigger the local decomposition of an Sb-BDCA precursor solution into solid amorphous Sb2S3.

**Model / system.** The experimental platform involves a Nanoscribe GT2 two-photon printer using an Sb-BDCA precursor solution and high-NA objectives, printing Sb2S3 structures on Au-coated and ITO-coated SiO2 substrates.

**Key observables.** Refractive index change, optical loss, lateral and axial resolution, elemental composition (via EDX), and optical functionality of printed structures (via transmission and microscopy).

**Important parameters / regimes.** 780 nm laser wavelength, sub-micron lateral resolution (~280 nm), refractive index change (delta RI > 0.7), and high-NA (1.4) objective lens.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The simulation of voxel shape assumes a consistent X-polarized, 780 nm, NA 1.4 excitation source configuration.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the workflow for Sb2S3 synthesis; Figure S2 displays a gallery of 3D printed helical structures; Figure S3 shows EDX spectra for elemental verification.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the limitation of current PCM fabrication, describes the chemical synthesis of the precursor, details the DITPS printing process and voxel simulation, demonstrates structural versatility (helices and 2D patterns), proves optical functionality (FZPs and CGHMs), and compares the method to EBL and wet-etching.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Chalcogenides have recently emerged as an important class of phase-change materials (PCMs) for nanophotonics, owing to their very high refractive index (RI) and low optical loss in the visible to near-infrared range. They exhibit an ultralarge RI change (> 0.7) upon phase transition, which can be triggered by multiple stimuli such as electrical bias, laser illumination or thermal heating. These properties make them highly appealing materials for flat optics and metasurface applications. Current nanophotonic implementations of chalcogenide PCMs mostly rely on two-dimensional (2D) or quasi three-dimensional (3D) thin film patterning based on the coating of chalcogenide materials from a solid-state target. This limits fast prototyping of 3D freeform micro- and nanostructures, thus restricting geometric design freedom and device functionality. Here, we demonstrate a solution-phase direct printing of chalcogenide PCMs into functional structures. The method is based on dip in two photon-induced solidification (DITPS) of a specially synthesized antimony trisulfide (Sb2S3) precursor solution. Direct printing with DITPS is simple, maskless, fast and cost effective, enabling true freeform 3D printing of photonic devices with sub micron resolution. We show direct writing of Sb2S3 helices with different wire cross section profiles on gold and ITO substrates, as well as functional planar Fresnel zone plates (FZPs) and computer generated hologram metasurfaces (CGHMs) in a single printing step. This freeform DITPS approach thus enables rapid 3D prototyping of high index metasurfaces and opens a route to integrating high-index PCMs into existing photonic architectures and device platforms.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2605.02688"></a>
### [Unified Mapping of Multi-Site Electrocatalytic Activity Using a Single Descriptor](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02688v1)

**Authors:** A. Dana, D. Terrones, S. Gelin, I. Dabo  
**Type:** both · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02688v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02688_figures/2605.02688_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Effect of lateral adsorbate-adsorbate interactions on volcano plot and electrochemical observables. Repulsive interactions flatten the volcano peak, whereas attractive interactions narrow the peak, resulting in a three-dimensional volcano depiction with lateral interactions as the third dimension. For the cyclic voltammetry (CV) response, repulsive interactions shift the CV peak to lower potentials and reduce its intensity. Conversely, attractive interactions result in the opposite pattern. The coverage-potential curve becomes steeper for attractive interactions, while repulsive interactions cause a more gradual shift.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02688_figures/2605.02688_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Parity plot comparing adsorption energies obtained from the coverage-dependent model with corresponding predicted values for Pt(111), including a representative monolayer with 6/12 coverage.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02688_figures/2605.02688_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Hydrogen coverage as a function of electrode potential on Pt(111) and Pt3Ni(111) surfaces (dashed lines represent experi- mental data and solid lines are model predictions). The consistency between model predictions and experimental coverage-potential mea- surements [24] demonstrates that the model reliably accounts for ad- sorption energetics and lateral interactions on these surfaces.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02688_figures/2605.02688_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4: Simplified catalytic volcano plot constructed using the effective hydrogen adsorption free energy descriptor, ∆˜GH∗, which incorporates both coverage dependence and lateral adsorbate- adsorbate interactions. This descriptor enables the unification of activity trends across different surface coverages into a single curve. The calculated volcano illustrates the relationship between the effec- tive adsorption free energy and catalytic activity, demonstrating how the inclusion of interaction effects modifies the position and shape of the activity maximum. The volcano is obtained using a transfer coefficient of α = 0.5.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.02688_figures/2605.02688_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5: Parity plot comparing model-predicted adsorption energies with first-principles calculations, along with a representative atomic configuration with 6/12 coverage for Pt3Ni(111).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new analytical framework to predict the catalytic activity of complex alloys by accounting for both lateral adsorbate interactions and site heterogeneity. By projecting high-dimensional energy landscapes onto a single effective descriptor, the authors provide a way to unify complex multi-site activity trends into a single, interpretable volcano plot. This allows for more efficient screening of multi-component materials for hydrogen production.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Traditional Sabatier-type volcano plots fail for complex multi-site alloy catalysts because they ignore coverage-dependent lateral interactions and site heterogeneity.

**Main result.** The authors developed a unified descriptor mapping that projects a multidimensional activity landscape onto a single effective coordinate, successfully capturing multi-peaked activity trends in alloys.

**Method.** The study combines Density Functional Theory (DFT) for energetics with a mean-field statistical mechanics model using a grand canonical ensemble and a regular mixing model.

**Model / system.** The system focuses on platinum-based alloys (e.g., PtNi) for the Hydrogen Evolution Reaction (HER). The model uses a lattice-based Hamiltonian incorporating site-specific binding energies and pairwise lateral interaction energies.

**Key observables.** Hydrogen coverage, voltammetric (CV) peaks, exchange current density, and catalytic activity (HER).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Binding energy (I), interaction strength (J), hydrogen coverage (theta), and electrode potential (U).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Uses a mean-field approximation for configurational entropy and assumes adsorbed hydrogen is in thermodynamic equilibrium with a reservoir of protons and electrons.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows how lateral interactions modify isotherms and CV peaks; Figure 2 is a parity plot of predicted vs. DFT energies; Figure 3 compares predicted vs. experimental coverage; Figure 4 shows the new effective descriptor volcano plot; Figure 7 shows the polynomial mapping and resulting multi-peaked volcano.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by identifying the limitations of single-site descriptors, introduces a mean-field model for coverage-dependent interactions, extends this to multi-site alloy systems, proposes a reduced descriptor mapping, and validates the framework against DFT and experimental data.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a precise and general method to map the activity of electrocatalysts across multiple sites. Starting from a mean-field statistical mechanics model, we introduce an effective adsorption free energy descriptor that explicitly incorporates lateral adsorbate-adsorbate interactions, enabling the construction of coverage-consistent volcano relationships. Extending this approach, we show that adsorption energetics and interaction strength define a two-dimensional activity landscape that gives rise to a "volcano ridge" that captures the coupled influence of binding and interactions on catalytic performance. For multi-site systems, we demonstrate that the inherently nonlinear coupling between distinct adsorption environments leads to multi-peaked activity trends that cannot be represented by conventional single-site descriptors. To address this, we introduce a reduced descriptor mapping that projects the multidimensional activity landscape onto a single effective coordinate while preserving the underlying physics of site heterogeneity and lateral interactions. The resulting framework generalizes Sabatier-type analysis to complex alloy catalysts and provides a physically interpretable route for screening electrocatalytic materials of arbitrary compositional complexity.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.01724"></a>
### [Universal Design Principles for High-Quality Persistent Spin Textures](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01724v1)

**Authors:** Cheng-Ao Ji, Lingling Tao, James M. Rondinelli, Xue-Zeng Lu  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.01724v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01724_figures/2605.01724_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1. Schematic illustrations of spin textures in two-dimensional slices of the BZ. The (a)- (c) three typical classes of spin textures: Rashba-type (R), Dresselhaus-type (D) and Weyl-type (W), represented by purple, green and blue shading, respectively. Expressions for the three corresponding spin-orbit ﬁelds B(k) are shown as well. The (d)-(i) six kinds of spin textures described by our models generated by pairs of the typical fields: (d) WW, (e) DD-1, (f) WD, (g) RR, (h) DD-2, and (i) RD spin texture. The regions of PSTs are enclosed with orange lines.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01724_figures/2605.01724_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2. (a) Spin texture of Na2Sn2O3 parameterized with the WW model. Here the parameters 𝛼1, 𝛽1 and 𝛼2, 𝛽2 are set as 0.43 eV Å and -1.28 eV Å, respectively, which are obtained by fitting band structures and spin textures around Γ and H (Table S8, Supporting Information). (b)-(d) The DFT+SOC spin textures for additional candidate compounds. PST regions with spin deviation less than 5° are indicated by orange arrows, while the remaining regions are marked with blue arrows. The location of the CBM is indicated by the green circle.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2605.01724_figures/2605.01724_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3. DFT+SOC band structures and spin textures in kx-ky plane of (a, b) Na2Sn2O3 and (d, e) AgClO4. Spin textures based on the k∙p models around (c) the CBM of Na2Sn2O3 and (f) the VBM of AgClO4. The high-quality PST regions for carrier doping are highlighted with the blue dashed circle. The location of CBM/VBM is indicated by the green circle.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Summary.** This paper introduces a universal principle for designing materials with high-quality persistent spin textures by leveraging the interaction of multiple spin-orbit fields. It identifies specific compounds that can host extremely long spin lifetimes, providing a roadmap for developing next-generation spintronic devices.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** The scarcity of materials exhibiting high-quality Persistent Spin Textures (PST) characterized by large Brillouin zone areas, minimal spin deviation, and exceptionally long spin lifetimes.

**Main result.** The authors establish a universal design principle based on the interplay of spin-orbit fields from different k-points, identifying new classes of high-quality PSTs and predicting specific materials like Na2Sn2O3 and AgClO4 with long spin lifetimes.

**Method.** The study combines symmetry analysis, k.p effective Hamiltonian modeling, and first-principles Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations with high-throughput screening of the Materials Project database.

**Model / system.** The research focuses on noncentrosymmetric crystals (polar, nonpolar, chiral, and achiral) using a universal k.p effective Hamiltonian framework that captures the interaction of Rashba, Dresselhaus, and Weyl-type spin-orbit fields.

**Key observables.** Spin deviation (angle), PST area in the Brillouin zone, and spin lifetime (nanoseconds).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Spin-orbit coupling coefficients (alpha, beta), spin deviation threshold (< 5 degrees), and wavevector range (~0.1 A^-1).

**Assumptions / limitations.** A linear-in-k approximation is used, which is justified by the small wavevector range of the high-quality PST regions.

**Figures summary.** Simulations of spin textures showing PST and non-PST regions; plots of PST area changes relative to doping and spin deviation thresholds; and illustrations of 3D spin texture characteristics in momentum space.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of PST quality, presents a universal model for spin-orbit field interplay, classifies new PST classes via symmetry, identifies specific candidate materials through database screening, and demonstrates engineering strategies like pressure and doping.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Persistent spin texture (PST) describes a unique spin-momentum locking in momentum space that maintains a uniform spin orientation through portions of the Brillouin zone (BZ), enabling exceptionally long spin lifetimes which are essential for applications in spintronics. However, materials exhibiting large BZ regions of high-quality PST, characterized by minimal spin deviation and long spin lifetimes, remain scarce. Here a universal model is introduced to capture the formation of superior PST regions arising from the interplay of spin-orbit fields at different k points. Within this framework, high-quality PSTs are identified in several systems belonging to various point groups. Notably, the nonpolar-chiral compound Na2Sn2O3 exhibits ~0.02 Å-2 high-quality PST region, which can be reversed by the switching of geometric chirality, while AgClO4 (D2d symmetry) exhibits a 0.016 Å-2 PST region. Significantly, Na2Sn2O3 and AgClO4 host persistent spin helices with spin lifetimes of 0.5-7.4 ns and 0.9-2.5 ns, respectively, among the longest reported for PST materials. In addition, both chemical substitutions and the application of pressure are demonstrated as effective routes for engineering high-quality PST. Our findings not only establish a universal principle for high-quality PST, but also provide promising materials across various point groups for the next-generation spintronic devices.

</details>

<a id="paper-2605.02677"></a>
### [Vortex Transport in Ni/Bi Bilayer Superconductor with Strong Spin-Orbit and Exchange Interaction](http://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02677v1)

**Authors:** Laxmipriya Nanda, Sohini Guin, Yasen Hou, Rajib Sarkar, Naresh Shyaga, Souvik Banerjee, A. Sundaresan, N. S. Vidhyadhiraja, Jagadeesh S. Moodera, Dhavala Suri  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.02677v1>  
**Analysis basis:** abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable

**Summary.** This paper examines the superconducting properties of Ni/Bi bilayers, which feature strong spin-orbit coupling and exchange interactions. The researchers observed vortex transport driven by competing forces and concluded that the system primarily exhibits conventional s-wave superconductivity.

**Why it may be interesting.** not directly relevant

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Main problem.** Investigating the nature of superconductivity and vortex dynamics in Ni/Bi bilayers, specifically looking for evidence of unconventional pairing in a strong spin-orbit and exchange-coupled environment.

**Main result.** The study finds that vortex transport is dominated by competing Magnus and viscous forces, and that the superconductivity is well-described by a conventional s-wave order parameter.

**Method.** Magneto-transport studies near the superconducting transition temperature (Tc) and control experiments using a ferromagnetic insulator.

**Model / system.** A bilayer system consisting of ferromagnetic Nickel (Ni) coupled to Bismuth (Bi), a strong spin-orbit metal.

**Key observables.** Transverse resistance peaks, Tc (superconducting transition temperature), and vortex dynamics.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Transition temperature (Tc) of 3 to 4 K, out-of-plane magnetic fields, and exchange proximity effects.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the Ni/Bi bilayer system, describes magneto-transport measurements near Tc, analyzes vortex dynamics through transverse resistance, presents control experiments with ferromagnetic insulators, and concludes with an assessment of the superconducting order parameter.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Nickel/bismuth (Ni/Bi) bilayers are a promising platform for exploring unconventional superconductivity. Ferromagnetic Ni is coupled to Bi, a strong spin orbit metal that only becomes superconducting below approx 10 mK, forming a bilayer exhibits superconductivity at a much higher temperatures, a Tc of 3 to 4 K. Such a bilayer thus makes an ideal system to probe Cooper pairing in strong spin orbit coupled magnetic environments. Magneto transport studies near Tc reveal the behavior of vortex dynamics and exchange proximity effects. It is seen that isolated vortices of the bilayers respond sensitively to out of plane fields, producing antisymmetric transverse resistance peaks attributable to competing Magnus and viscous forces. Control experiments using a ferromagnetic insulator confirm that superconductivity extends throughout the bilayer, not just confined at the interface. Overall, the results provide a unified picture of transport dominated by vortex dynamics and show that a conventional s wave order parameter accounts for the observations, with any likely unconventional contributions being only subtle.

</details>
